Most of these questions are recent past real examination questions – either as actually set or adjusted for any subsequent changes in legislation, regulation or market practice. 

The answers are either based on the Chief Examiner’s Report or they are our suggested answer to cover the areas highlighted as relevant by the examiner. You should note that the Chief Examiner’s Report discusses the issues raised in a question; it does not necessarily provide a ‘fair copy’ answer, and does not therefore reflect an answer that a real candidate could reasonably be expected to produce. 

## **Section B** 

Quote from the Examiner: 

_‘Section B problems are intended to raise more practical or operational issues that arise in specific factual situations which candidates may have to deal with on a day-to-day basis in professional practice. Factual problems are created using fictional characters to explore the application and operation of particular sets of legal and regulatory provisions. The questions and sub-questions are set to allow candidates to move through the situation and identify and resolve particular problems in order. Structured answers are expected with the degree of detail expected depending upon the mark allocation set for the particular sub-question asked. Candidates are expected to identify and explain the relevant point of law or regulation concerned in each case and then resolve the issue raised. Higher marks are awarded where additional levels of detail are provided and informed and original conclusions produced._ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

In Section B, the compulsory question (which in practice is always Question 11 in the exam) generally relates to fundamental ethical and regulatory issues. 

The compulsory Section B questions that are included in this book are as follows: 

B14, B16, B18, B21, B26, B32, B35, B38, B42, B50, B52, B55, B58, B61, B65, B67, B69, B73, B78, B80, B83, B86, B89, B93 and B95. 

## **Section C** 

Quote from the Examiner: 

_‘In answering section C questions, candidates have to explain the background to the relevant subject concerned. This includes explaining the principal legal and regulatory provisions and requirements that would apply. They then have to comment on the specific issue raised and answer the particular question set. Marking can be flexible with all relevant points of detail being considered. Additional marks are awarded where candidates can demonstrate a strong command of the relevant law and regulation involved and for informed and original opinion’._ 

It is therefore worth bearing in mind that the answers provided for section C questions are to be used as a revision tool. They rarely reflect an answer that could realistically be prepared in a real exam. It is possible to produce an answer that appears quite different to that provided in this pack, and yet be awarded a strong mark by the marker. 

Some of the Section C (essay) questions (for example, those set during the 2007-2009 financial crisis) quickly go out of date. With these questions the date of the exam has been noted, and the question has been left in its original form. This is intended to provide an idea of the type of topical essay question that may be set and of the approach to answering these questions that the examiner would expect candidates to take.

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **Introductory note** 

For convenience of reference, the Section A Questions have been grouped into the following categories: 

1. UK regulatory structure 

2. MiFID regulations and international aspects 

3. High level standards 

4. Financial promotions 

5. Conduct of business 

6. Client assets 

7. Market conduct 

8. Enforcement and discipline 

9. Complaints and compensation 

10. Takeover Code and Listing Rules 

11. Money laundering and other financial crime 

12. Markets and exchanges 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **1. UK regulatory structure** 

- **A1.1** Explain the difference between authorisation (s19) and permission (s20) under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (FSMA) as amended. 

## _(2 marks)_ 

All persons are required to be **authorised** or **exempt** if they carry on a regulated activity in the United Kingdom under s 19(1) of the Financial Services and Markets Act (FSMA). This is referred to as the “general prohibition” under s19 (2). 

Authorisation is dealt with under s31. The following persons are authorised under FSMA: 

- a) Persons holding a Part 4A permission; 

- b) EEA passport firms (under Schedule 3) _[N.B. No passporting rights currently exist,_ 

_though they could be restored under a future UK-EU trade agreement];_ 

- c) Treaty firms (under Schedule 4); or 

- d) Any other persons otherwise authorised under FSMA (s31 (1)). 

Authorisation constitutes a form of **status** or **condition** for the carrying on of any regulated activity in the UK. 

Authorised persons must also hold the relevant **permission** to carry on the specific regulated activity or activities conducted in the UK (s 20(1) FSMA). Applications for permission are made under s 55A and Part 4A generally. Breach is not an offence as such, although it is subject to FCA disciplinary action. Permission is accordingly concerned with the specific **legal right** , **authority or entitlement** to carry on the particular financial activity or activities concerned. 

**A1.2** Explain the difference between authorisation and exemption under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (FSMA). _(2 marks)_ 

Under the “General Prohibition” (s19) of FSMA a person carrying on a regulated activity must be either authorised or exempt. Authorisation includes persons holding permission under Part 4A of FSMA. Also Treaty firms (under Schedule 4) and other persons otherwise authorised under FSMA (s31 (1)).) 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

Exemption is dealt with under s38 and the Financial Services and Markets Act (Exemption) Order 2001. This generally restates the earlier categories of exempt persons under the previous financial legislation in effect in the UK. These include: 

- a) Appointed Representatives; 

- b) Members of professions; 

- c) Members of Lloyd’s; 

- d) Recognised Investment Exchanges and Recognised Clearing Houses; 

- e) Other persons as listed under the 2001 Exemption Order. 

**A1.3** List the general functions of the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA). _(2 marks)_ 

The general functions of the FCA are: 

- a) Making rules under FSMA; 

- b) Preparing and issuing codes under FSMA; 

- c) Giving general guidance under FSMA; 

- d) Determining the general policy and principles by reference to which it performs its 

   - functions. 

**A1.4** Explain the purpose of the Regulated Activities Order (RAO). 

_(2 marks)_ 

The purpose of the RAO is to specify the scope of the regulated activities covered by FSMA including the nature of the investments and the activities covered. The RAO is necessary as the general prohibition applies to regulated activities which are defined as being specified by the Treasury under the RAO. 

The general nature of the investments and activities covered by the FSMA are listed in Schedule 2 to the FSMA although these are only indicative or illustrative as the legal meanings are set out in detail in the RAO. The purpose of the RAO is to legally define the scope of the investments and investment activities covered by the general prohibition. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A1.5** Identify the main sanctions imposed for breach of the general prohibition against carrying on regulated activities without being authorised or exempt under the Financial Services and Markets Act (FSMA). _(4 marks)_ 

Any person who breaches the general prohibition is liable to up to two years imprisonment or a fine or both (on indictment) or six months imprisonment or a fine not exceeding the statutory maximum (on summary conviction) (s23(1)(a) and (b) FSMA). Breach of the general provision is referred to as an authorisation offence (s23 (2)). 

Agreements entered into in breach of the general prohibition are also unenforceable against the other party with the other party being entitled to recover any money or property paid or transferred or compensation for loss sustained (s26(2)(a) and (b)). 

Reference could also be made to the defence of all reasonable precaution and exercise of due diligence (s23 (3)) or the separate offence of falsely claiming to be an authorised or exempt person (s24 (1)). 

**A1.6** List the four categories of Authorised Person set out in the FSMA. _(4 marks)_ 

The four categories of authorised person listed in s 31(1) are: 

a) Persons holding a Part 4A permission to carry on one or more regulated activities; 

b) EEA firms (authorised under Schedule 3); 

c) EU Treaty firms (authorised under Schedule 4); or 

- d) Any person otherwise authorised under FSMA (s31 (1)). 

[N.B. Since Brexit and the loss of passporting rights, category (b) above is now obsolete.] 

- **A1.7** Describe the main requirements that apply to becoming an exempt appointed representative of an authorised person. _(3 marks)_ 

A person (other than an authorised person) may act as an appointed representative where: a) They are a party to a contract with an authorised person (the principal); 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

   - b) The contract permits or requires the person to carry on business of a prescribed 

      - description and complies with any requirements that may be prescribed under relevant Regulations; 

   - c) The principal accepts responsibility in writing for the carrying on of those activities in 

      - respect of the whole or part of that business s39 (1) (a) (i) and (ii) and (b)). 

- **A1.8** Outline the strategic and operational objectives of the FCA. 

_(3 marks)_ 

The FCA has the strategic objective of ensuring that relevant markets function well. It also has three operational objectives (ss 1B, C, D and E FSMA), which are to: 

- a) secure an appropriate degree of protection for consumers 

- b) protect and enhance the integrity of the UK financial system, and 

- c) promote effective competition in the interests of consumers. 

An additional continuity objective is inserted under the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 in connection with ring fencing (s1EA FSMA2000 as amended). 

- **A1.9** List the various panels that the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has a statutory duty to consult. _(2 marks)_ 

The FCA is required to consider representations that are made to it by any of the four panels set out below: 

- a) The  FCA Practitioner Panel; 

- b) The Smaller Business Practitioner Panel; 

- c) The Markets Practitioner Panel; 

- d) The Consumer Panel. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A1.10** Identify the general functions of the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) under FSMA. 

_(2 marks)_ 

The general functions of the PRA are: 

   - a) Making rules under FSMA; 

   - b) Preparing and issuing codes under FSMA; 

   - c) Determining the general policy and principles by reference to which it performs particular functions. 

- **A1.11** What is the general objective of the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) and how is the PRA required to advance this objective? _(3 marks)_ 

The PRA’s general objective is promoting the safety and soundness of PRA-authorised 

persons (which include banks, insurers and other systemically important firms). 

The PRA is required to advance its general objective primarily by: 

   - a) Seeking to ensure that the business of PRA-authorised persons is carried on in a way which avoids any adverse effects on the stability of the UK financial system; and 

   - b) Seeking to minimise the adverse effect that the failure of a PRA-authorised person could be expected to have on the stability of the UK financial system. 

- **A1.12** Explain the nature and significance of an “authorised person” and an “exempt person” under the FSMA 2000. _(4 marks)_ 

The general prohibition set out in s19 (1) of FSMA requires that any person carrying on a regulated activity within the UK is either an authorised or an exempt person. The requirements with regard to authorised persons are developed in ss.31-39. 

An authorised person is set out in s31 (1). An authorised person generally includes a person holding a Part 4A Permission, a treaty firm (qualifying under Schedule 4) or someone otherwise authorised under the FSMA. 

Authorisation refers to the general status of being able to carry on any regulated activity. The need for the separate designation arose as a result of the separate availability for UK 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

firms and European Treaty and single market firms. Entitlement to carry on a particular activity is conferred under the permission regime (s20 (1)). 

Exempt firms are those excluded for public service entities (such as the Bank of England or international financial institutions) which did not carry on commercial business for financial profit alone or other statutory bodies that were separately regulated under the founding Acts. 

The list of relevant exemptions is now set out in the “Exemption Order” which is issued under s 38(1) of FSMA. 

**A1.13** List four of the regulated activities and four of the investments covered by the FSMA 2000. 

_(2 marks)_ 

The list of “regulated activities” set out in Schedule 2 consists of: 

- a) Dealing in investments; 

- b) Arranging deals in investments; 

- c) Deposit taking; 

- d) Safeguarding and administration of assets; 

- e) Managing investments; 

- f)   Investment advice; 

- g) Establishing collective investment schemes; 

- h) Using computer-based systems for giving investment instructions; 

- i)   Activities relating to information about persons’ financial standing; 

- j)   Activities relating to the setting of benchmarks. 

The investments covered consist of: 

- a) Securities; 

- b) Investments creating or acknowledging indebtedness; 

- c) Government and public securities, 

- d) Instruments giving entitlements to investments; 

- e) Certificates representing securities 

- f) Units in collective investment schemes; 

- g) Options; 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

   - h) Futures; 

   - i) Contracts for differences; 

   - j) Contracts of insurance; 

   - k) Participation in Lloyds’ syndicates; 

   - l) Deposits; 

   - m) Loans and other forms of credit; 

   - n) Contracts for hire of goods; 

   - o) Rights in investments. 

- **A1.14** List four of the ‘relevant persons’ who must be represented on the FCA’s Markets Practitioner Panel. _(2 marks)_ 

The FCA must appoint to its Market Practitioner Panel persons to represent the interests of the following ‘relevant persons’: 

- a) Authorised firms; 

- b) Persons who issue financial instruments (e.g. listed companies) 

- c) Sponsors (of listed companies); 

- d) RIEs; 

- e) Primary information providers (PIPs). 

**A1.15** Explain the content and purpose of the “general prohibition” under the FSMA 2000. 

_(2 marks)_ 

The general prohibition in s19 of FSMA creates an offence of undertaking regulated activities unless the person is either authorised or exempt. The penalty for breach is two years imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine. In addition, contracts may be unenforceable. The purpose of the general prohibition is to provide investor protection by making sure that those who conduct regulated activities are fit and proper persons to do so, which will include an assessment of the business’s competence. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A1.16** Explain the purpose of permission under the FSMA 2000. 

## _(2 marks)_ 

To comply with the General Prohibition under s.19 of FSMA a firm may become authorised by seeking permission under Part 4A of the FSMA. To obtain permission to perform one or more regulated activities the person must meet the threshold conditions. This process ensures that those who undertake regulated activities are fit and proper persons and competent to undertake those activities. A business may not undertake activities other than those for which it has permission, which restricts the broad authorisation to comply with s.19 to the more specific activities that an authorised person may undertake. 

## **A1.17** Explain what is meant by the Bank of England’s ‘financial stability strategy’. 

_(3 marks)_ 

The Bank of England (the Bank) has been given the objective of protecting and enhancing the stability of the UK financial system. The court of directors of the Bank is required to determine the Bank’s strategy in relation to its financial stability objective (known formally as its ‘financial stability strategy’). This strategy must be reviewed at least every three years. Before determining or revising the Bank’s financial stability strategy, the court of directors must consult the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) and the Treasury. The Bank must also publish its financial stability strategy. 

**A1.18** Explain the functions of the Financial Policy Committee (FPC). 

_(4 marks)_ 

The functions of the FPC cover the following range of activities: 

- Monitoring the stability of the UK financial system with a view to identifying and assessing systemic risks; 

- Giving directions to the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) or the Prudential Regulatory Authority (PRA), requiring the regulator to achieve the implementation of a specified macro-prudential measure; 

- Making recommendations to any of the following: 

   - Within the Bank of England (the Bank) (about the provision by the Bank of financial assistance to financial institutions or about the exercise by the 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

Bank of its functions in relation to payment systems, settlement systems and clearing houses); 

   - To the Treasury (about the exercise of the Treasury power to make various statutory orders, such as in relation to regulated activities); 

   - To the FCA or the PRA (about the exercise of their respective functions); or 

   - To any other person. 

- Preparing and publishing two reports each year relating to financial stability. 

**A1.19** Give eight examples of a regulated activity. 

_(4 marks)_ 

- Managing investments 

- Advising on investments 

- Dealing in investments as principal 

- Dealing in investments as agent 

- Arranging deals in investments 

- Regulated mortgage administration and provision 

- Effecting and carrying out contracts of insurance 

- Safeguarding and administering investments 

- Providing funeral plans 

- Accepting deposits 

- Lloyd’s activities 

- Sending dematerialised instructions 

- Establishing/operating/winding up a collective investment scheme 

- Agreeing to carry on regulated activities 

- Activities relating to information about persons’ financial standing 

- Activities relating to the setting of benchmarks 

**A1.20** Give FOUR examples of excluded activities. 

_(2 marks)_ 

- Acting as a trustee, nominee or personal representative 

- Activities in the course of carrying on a profession or business 

- Activities in connection with the supply of goods or services 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- Groups and joint enterprises 

- Activities in conjunction with the sale of a body corporate 

- Employee share schemes 

**A1.21** Give EIGHT examples of specified investments. _(4 marks)_ 

- Deposits 

- Electronic money 

- Contracts of insurance 

- Shares etc. 

- Instruments creating or acknowledging indebtedness 

- Government and other public securities 

- Instruments giving entitlements to investments 

- Certificates representing certain securities 

- Units in a collective investment scheme 

- Rights under a stakeholder pension 

- Options 

- Futures 

- Contracts for differences 

- Lloyd’s syndicate capacity and syndicate membership 

- Rights to or interests in investments 

**A1.22** Give a brief description of a collective investment scheme. _(2 marks)_ 

FSMA defines a collective investment scheme as any arrangement where property of any description is pooled and not managed on a day-to-day basis by the investors. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A1.23** The PRA has the power to restrain a proposed action to be taken by the FCA. What are the three conditions that must be met for the PRA to be able to exercise this power? 

_(3 marks)_ 

Firstly, the FCA is proposing to exercise one of its regulatory powers in relation to a PRAauthorised person. Secondly, the PRA considers that the exercise of this power by the FCA may threaten the stability of the UK financial system or result in the failure of a PRAauthorised person in a way that would adversely affect the UK financial system. 

Thirdly, the PRA considers that the giving of the direction is necessary in order to avoid these possible consequences. 

**A1.24** List three types of collective investment schemes covered by the FSMA. 

_(3 marks)_ 

Part XVII FSMA applies with regard to the following types of collective investment schemes: 

- a) Authorised or unauthorised unit trust schemes; 

- b) Open-ended investment companies; 

- c) Recognised overseas schemes including: 

   - i) Designated country or territory schemes; and 

   - ii) Individually recognised overseas schemes. 

**A1.25** Briefly explain how senior manager functions are managed by the appropriate regulator under the FSMA. _(3 marks)_ 

All authorised persons are required to ensure that any person carrying on a controlled function must be approved by the appropriate regulator (s 59(1) FSMA). 

Senior manager functions must be fit and proper before they can be approved (s 61(1) 

FSMA). In determining whether a senior manager function applicant is fit and proper, regard is had to: 

- a) Honesty, integrity and reputation; 

- b) Competency and capability; and 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## c) Financial soundness. 

Reference may also be made to the list of controlled functions maintained by the appropriate regulator (under s 59(4)) or the Conduct Rules (COCON) with which approved persons have to comply. Reference may also be made to SYSC if necessary. 

**A1.26** List six categories of exempt person under the FSMA 

_(3 marks)_ 

Exempt persons are set out in the Exemption Order issued by the Treasury under s 38(1) FSMA. This includes: 

- a) Government organisations and related bodies (including the Bank of England and National Savings Bank); 

- b) Other central banks; 

- c) Supra-national organisations (such as International Monetary Fund and World Bank); 

- d) Municipal banks and local authorities; 

- e) Charities; 

- f) Members of the profession; 

## g) Members of Lloyd’s; 

- h) Appointed representatives; 

- i) Recognised Investment Exchanges (RIEs), Recognised Overseas Investment 

   - Exchanges (ROIEs) and Recognised Clearing Houses (RCHs). 

**A1.27** What are the six consumer outcomes of the FTOC initiative? _(3 marks)_ 

_[Tutor note: The Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) initiative initially applied to any business carried out by any firm with any customer – but now is restricted to business which is_ _**out of scope** of the Consumer Duty regulations created because of s9 Financial Services Act 2021, which came into effect in July 2023.]_ 

The FTOC Consumer Outcomes are as follows; 

- Consumers can be confident that they are dealing with firms where the fair treatment of customers is central to the corporate culture. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- Products and services marketed and sold in the retail market are designed to meet the needs of identified consumer groups and targeted accordingly. 

- Consumers are provided with clear information and are kept appropriately informed before, during and after the point-of-sale. 

- Where consumers receive advice, the advice is suitable and takes account of their circumstances. 

- Consumers are provided with products that perform as firms have led them to expect and the associated service is both of an acceptable standard and as they have been led to expect. 

- Consumers do not face unreasonable post-sale barriers imposed by firms to change product, switch provider, submit a claim or make a complaint. 

**A1.28** Explain the general purpose of the FCA’s Fair Treatment of Customers initiative and its relationship to the FCA’s operational objectives. _(4 marks)_ 

_[Tutor note: The Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) initiative initially applied to any business carried out by any firm with any customer – but now is restricted to business which is_ _**out of scope** of the Consumer Duty regulations created as a result of s9 Financial Services Act 2021, which came into effect in July 2023.]_ 

The purpose of the FTOC initiative is to ensure that firms that are out-of-scope of the Consumer Duty deal with their customers in a fair and appropriate manner at all times. This is based on PRIN 6 under which firms must pay due regard to their customers’ interests and treat them fairly. 

Fair treatment of customers can be considered to fall within the FCA’s integrity objective (protecting and enhancing the integrity of the UK financial system) under FSMA. 

Product providers must ensure that all products are properly designed for their target market and sold with clear, understandable information through appropriate distribution channels. Distributors must ensure that customers have all of the information they require, that products are suitable and that post sales services comply with expectations. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A1.29** Briefly explain the different roles and functions of the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) and the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) under the regulatory regime bought into effect under the Financial Services Act 2012. _(3 marks)_ 

The PRA is responsible for the regulation of banks, insurance companies and large, systemically important investment firms. Operating as the PRA is one of the functions of the Bank of England. 

The FCA is responsible for conduct regulation generally as well as the direct regulation of other firms not subject to PRA control and for the recognition and oversight of stock markets and exchanges. It operates as an independent agency, replacing the former Financial Services Authority (FSA). 

**A1.30** Describe the general relationship between ss19, 20 and 21 FSMA. _(3 marks)_ 

Any person carrying on a regulated activity in the UK must be authorised (or exempt) under s 19(1) FSMA. Authorised persons are listed in s 31(1) – (a) anyone holding a Part 4A permission, (b) Treaty firms (Schedule 4) and (c) a person otherwise authorised under the FSMA. Authorisation constitutes a form of status or condition for the carrying on of any regulated activity in the UK. 

Authorised persons must also hold the relevant permission to carry on the specific regulated activity or activities conducted in the UK (s 20(1) FSMA). Breach is not an offence as such although it is subject to disciplinary action by the appropriate regulator. Applications for permission are made under s 40 and Part 4A generally. Permission is accordingly concerned with the specific legal right, authority or entitlement to carry on the particular financial activity or activities concerned. 

Candidates should have referred separately to s 21 of the FSMA and the need for financial promotions to be issued or approved by an authorised person and with such promotions being fair, clear and not misleading. 

One mark for each general correct explanation up to three marks although detailed responses are not required. Half market for any other additional correct relevant information where necessary up to three marks in total. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A1.31** Identify six of the Regulatory Guides included within the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(3 marks)_ 

The Regulatory Guides within Block 9 of the Handbook include: 

   - a) the Collective Investment Scheme Information Guide; 

   - b) the Enforcement Guide; 

   - c) Financial Crime: a guide for firms; 

   - d) Financial Crime Thematic Reviews; 

   - e) the Perimeter Guidance Manual; 

   - f) the Responsibilities of Providers and Distributors for the Fair Treatment of Customers; 

   - g) the Unfair Contract Terms Regulatory Guide; 

   - h) Wind-down Planning Guide; and 

   - i) MiFID II Guide. 

- **A1.32** Explain the purpose and importance of macroprudential policy in ensuring the stability of financial markets. _(3 marks)_ 

Macroprudential policy is concerned with monitoring the build up of risks and exposures across the financial system as a whole and not simply within one or more specific regulated sector. 

This was regarded as being a major omission before the recent financial crisis and especially with the failure by the authorities to deal with asset price bubbles, in particular, in the property market and the build of debt and leverage across the financial system. 

A number of new bodies have been set up to attempt to develop this form of oversight. These include the Bank of England’s Financial Policy Committee (FPC), set up by the Coalition Government to replace the previous Council for Financial Stability (CFS) in the UK, as well as the Financial Stability Oversight Council (FSOC) in the US and European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB) in the EU. Equivalent work is carried out by the Financial Stability Board (FSB) at the international level with the International Monetary Fund (IMF). 

A number of issues still remain in developing appropriate forms of information and data collection, assessment and processing and appropriate tools to respond to any identified 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

difficulties as well as appropriate cooperation mechanisms between all of the other agencies involved at a national and on a cross-border basis. 

- **A1.33** List eight of the Regulated Activity Groups (RAGs) provided for under SUP 16 of the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(4 marks)_ 

The Regulated Activity Groups (RAGs) listed in SUP 16.12.4 of the Handbook consist of: 

- a) RAG 1 – accepting deposits, meeting of repayment claims and managing dormant account funds 

- b) RAG 2.1 – effecting contracts of insurance, carrying out contracts of insurance and entering as provider into a funeral plan contract 

- c) RAG 2.2 – managing the underwriting capacity of a Lloyd’s syndicate as a managing agent at Lloyds, advising on syndicate participation at Lloyd’s and arranging deals in contracts of insurance written at Lloyd’s 

- d) RAG 3 – dealing in investments as principal, dealing in investments as agent, advising on investments (excluding retail investment activities), and arranging (bringing about) deals in investments (excluding retail investment activities) 

- e) RAG 4 – managing investments and establishing, operating or winding up a collective investment scheme, a stakeholder pension scheme or a personal pension scheme 

- f) RAG 5 – home finance administration or home finance providing activity 

- g) RAG 6 – acting as trustee of an authorised unit trust, safeguarding and administration of assets (without arranging), arranging safeguarding and administration of assets and acting as depositary or sole director of an OEIC 

- h) RAG 7 – retail investment activities, advising on pensions transfers & opt-outs and arranging (bringing about) deals in retail investments 

- i) RAG 8 – making arrangements with a view to transactions in investments and operating a multilateral trading facility (MTF) 

- j) RAG 9 – home finance mediation activity and insurance mediation activity (noninvestment insurance contracts) 

- k) RAG 10 – the activities of an RIE 

- l) RAG 11 – bidding in emissions auctions 

- m) RAG 12 – credit-related regulated activity 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A1.34** Explain the consequences of breach of s20 FSMA. 

_(3 marks)_ 

Breach of s20 is stated not to constitute an offence directly, transactions are not unenforceable and a right of action will not immediately arise unless otherwise specified (s20(2) and (3)). Breach will nevertheless constitute a disciplinary matter subject to FCA enforcement including a fine or censure (public or private) or possibly a restriction or variation of permission. 

**A1.35** Where are regulated activities defined under the FSMA? Give four examples. _(4 marks)_ 

The principal regulated activities are listed in **Schedule 2 FSMA** although this does not contain the legal definition of regulated activities. Regulated activities are ‘ **as specified’** (under s 23(4) FSMA) which means by Statutory Instrument. The legal definition of regulated activities is then set in the **Regulated Activities Order** ( **RAO** ) issued under the FSMA as amended. 

- **A1.36** The Banking Act 2009 created a Special Resolution Regime (SRR) to deal with banks and other depositary institutions at risk of failure. Summarise **three** powers of the SRR when faced by a failing bank. _(3 marks)_ 

After the great financial crisis, a permanent regulatory framework was established to consider cases of banks and other financial intermediaries at risk of failure. This framework established under the Banking Act 2009 was termed the Special Resolution Regime (SRR). The Banking Act established both objectives to guide decision making by the different bodies involved and a number of powers. These powers (as outlined in the workbook) include: 

- To place a bank into temporary public ownership. 

- To place a bank into receivership and allow payment to insured depositors. 

- To use administration procedures on all or part of the bank. 

- To transfer part or all of the bank to a third-party private buyer or as an intermediate step to the Bank of England pending a future sale. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A1.37** Explain the nature of macro-prudential policy and how this is principally managed under the regulatory system in the UK. _(3 marks)_ 

Macro-prudential policy is concerned with the monitoring of the build up of risks and exposures across the financial system as a whole. This was identified as one of the key failures in the UK, EU and US before the global financial crisis. This has allowed the formation of major asset price bubbles, in particular, in the property market and the build of debt and leverage across the financial system. Macro-prudential policy is managed by the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) within the Bank of England. 

- **A1.38** Identify SIX of the supervisory principles that apply under the FSMA as amended. 

_(3 marks)_ 

The supervisory principles set out in s 3B FSMA as amended consist of the following: 

   - a) efficient and economic use of resources; 

   - b) proportionate restrictions; 

   - c) desirability of sustainable growth in the UK economy; 

   - d) consumer responsibility; 

   - e) senior management responsibility; 

   - f) the regulatory authorities take into account the differences in the nature and objectives of the separate businesses carried on by persons under FSMA; 

   - g) information disclosure; 

   - h) transparency. 

- **A1.39** Identify the main types of firms subject to Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) supervision and control under FSMA. _(3 marks)_ 

The PRA is responsible for the prudential regulation of banks, building societies and credit unions, insurance businesses (including general insurers, life insurance companies, wholesale firms on the London market and mutual insurers including friendly societies) as well as designated investment firms. 

There is no need to specify the types of insurance companies covered. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A1.40** Identify the regulatory activities for which the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) is responsible under FSMA. _(3 marks)_ 

The question may either have been answered with reference to the FCA’s objectives or residual regulatory activities not conferred on the PRA. 

The FCA’s strategic objective is protecting and enhancing confidence in the UK financial system and its operational objectives are consumer protection, integrity efficiency and choice. 

Reference may alternatively have been made to regulated activities of a specified kind and by order (s22(1) and (5) FSMA) and to Schedule 2 and the RAO 2001/No 544 as amended. The FCA is strictly responsible for anything not reserved to the PRA under the FSMA 2000 (PRA-regulated Activities) Order 2013 No 556 (s 22A FSMA). 

**A1.41** Identify FOUR of the principal statutes that govern financial services regulation in the UK. 

_(2 marks)_ 

Four of the principal statutes that govern financial services regulation in the UK are: 

- (a) Financial Services and Markets Act 2000; 

- (b) Banking Act 2009; 

- (c) Financial Services Act 2012; 

- (d) Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013. 

**A1.42** Identify six of the principal means through which the regulatory authorities are held accountable under FSMA. _(3 marks)_ 

The regulators may generally be held accountable through any of the following means: 

- (a) HM Treasury appointments; 

- (b) Publication of annual report; 

- (c) Maintenance of appropriate internal governance structure; 

- (d) Appointment of complaints investigator under FSMA; 

- (e) Competition scrutiny; 

- (f) Judicial review; 

- (g) Consultation with Practitioner Panel; 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- (h) Consultation with Consumer Panel; 

- (i) Consultation with Small Business Practitioner Panel; 

- (j) Upper Tribunal; 

- (k) Conduct of independent review; 

- (l) Conduct of formal Treasury enquiry. 

**A1.43** Explain the role and function of the Upper Tribunal under the FSMA. 

_(3 marks)_ 

The role and function of the Upper Tribunal is to consider financial services appeals. The Upper Tribunal was established in 2008 under the Tribunal, Courts and Enforcement Act 2007 and is managed by Her Majesty's Courts and Tribunals Service. This replaced the Financial Services and Markets Tribunal in April 2010 which had originally been set up under s 132 FSMA. 

The Upper Tribunal has four Chambers with financial services matters being considered by the Tax and Chancery Chamber. The chamber specifically considers appeals against decision notices issued by the FCA and PRA. This may include decisions to discipline authorised firms or approved persons, withdrawal of approval, vary permission, market abuse or issue prohibition orders. 

**A1.44** Explain the role and function of the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) within the UK financial and regulatory architecture. _(4 marks)_ 

The Financial Policy Committee (FPC)  was set up under the Financial Services Act 2012 (s 9B Bank of England Act 1998 and s4 FSA 2012). 

Macro prudential policy is concerned with the monitoring of the build-up of risks and exposures across the financial system as a whole. This was identified as one of the key failures in the UK, EU and US before the global financial crisis. This has allowed the formation of major asset price bubbles, in particular, in the property market and the build of debt and leverage across the financial system. 

The function of the FPC is to examine issues that may affect financial stability with specific reference to the identification and monitoring of systemic risks and taking action to remove 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

or reduce such risks to protect and enhance the resilience of the UK financial system (s 9C Bank of England Act 1998). The FPC has been given specific powers to manage housing market debt including limiting the proportion of high loan income mortgages that banks can offer or prohibiting new lending above specific loan to income ratios. 

The FPC has examined such other issues as sovereign debt exposures, forbearance and provisioning practices, bank capital levels, earnings retention (bonus payments) and opaque funding structures, such as with regard to collateral swaps or similar transactions with exchange traded funds (ETFs). 

**A1.45** Explain the role and function of the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA). 

_(4 marks)_ 

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) is responsible for strengthening business competition and preventing and reducing anti-competitive practices within the UK. The CMA was set up on 15 March 2012 and was established under Part 3 of the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013. 

The CMA works to promote competition for the benefit of consumers inside and outside the UK and to ensure that markets work well for consumers, businesses and the economy. The CMA is an independent, non-ministerial government department with around 700 staff. 

The CMA specifically investigates mergers that could restrict competition, conducts market studies and investigations, investigates anti-competitive conduct and abuses of dominant positions, enforces consumer protection legislation, cooperates with other sector regulators and considers regulatory references and appeals. 

**A1.46** Explain the general requirement imposed under s21 FSMA on financial promotions. 

_(3 marks)_ 

s 21 FSMA requires that a financial promotion, in the form of an invitation or inducement to engage in an investment activity, must be issued by an authorised person or the content approved by an authorised person (s 21(1) and (2)). All financial promotions must be clear, 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

fair and not misleading (COBS 4.2). This is subject to the exemptions provided for under the Financial Promotion Order (FPO) as amended. 

The restrictions on financial promotions do not apply to purely factual communications nor to eligible counterparties with regard to MiFID business. 

A person who breaches the restrictions on financial promotions is guilty of an offence and liable for up to two years’ imprisonment (six months on summary conviction) or a fine or both (s 25(1)) subject to a reasonable belief or reasonable precautions defence ((s 25(2)). 

**A1.47** Outline the statutory objectives of the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA). 

_(3 marks)_ 

The PRA's general objective is to promote the safety and soundness of PRA authorised persons (s2B(2) FSMA as amended). This is to be advanced by seeking to ensure that the business or failure of a PRA authorised person does not have any adverse effect on the stability of the UK financial system and that the continuity of service of ring-fenced bodies is protected (s 2B(3)(a)-(c) FSMA). 

The PRA's insurance objective is to contribute to the securing of an appropriate degree of protection for those who are or may become policyholders (s2C(2) FSMA as amended). 

The PRA’s general objective was revised in 2013 to ensure that in discharging its general functions, the business of ring-fenced bodies are carried on in a way that avoids any adverse effect on the continuity of the provision of core services in UK and protected from risks that could adversely affect the continuity of core services with the risk that failure of the body, or a member of its group, could affect the continuity of core services (s2B(3)(c) inserted under the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013). 

The PRA was also given a secondary competition objective (SCO) on 1 March 2014 of acting, in so far as reasonably possible, in a way that facilitates effective competition in the markets for authorised firms carrying out regulated activities (s 130 Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A1.48** In a financial regulation context, define what is meant by the term ‘financial supervision’ and give **three** examples of supervisory principles used to enhance supervision of financial firms. _(4 marks)_ 

The term financial supervision relates to the monitoring of a firm’s behaviour and compliance with financial regulations. There are many supervisory principles introduced to ensure the enhanced supervision of financial firms by financial regulators. These principles (as outlined in the Official Workbook) include: 

- Be forward looking and pre-empt poor conduct to limit the effects on markets and customers. 

- Focus on strategy and business models to identify any future risks. 

- Focus on culture and governance to determine what drives behaviours in the organisation. 

- Focus on individual and firm accountability through holding senior managers and firms to account. 

- Follow proportionate and risk-based measures in its actions, such as using its wider market knowledge to identify problematic firms and individuals. 

- Engage in two-way communication with firms and consumers. 

- Clearly communicate the expected behaviours for firms and individuals. 

- Apply these principles in a co-ordinated manner, within the FCA and with other appropriate external bodies. 

- Rectify any systematic harms identified and work to prevent their reoccurrence. 

- Where appropriate refer action/activities to the Enforcement division. 

**A1.49** Identify and explain three of the key themes within the FCA’s Environmental, Social and 

Governance (ESG) strategy. _(3 marks)_ 

The FCA’s Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) strategy is based on the 5 following core themes: 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

(1) Transparency - promoting transparency on climate change and wider sustainability along the value chain. 

(2) Trust - building trust and integrity in ESG-labelled instruments, products and the supporting ecosystem. 

(3) Tools - working with others to enhance industry capabilities and support firms’ 

management of climate-related and wider sustainability risks, opportunities and impacts. 

(4) Transition - supporting the role of finance in delivering a market-led transition to a more sustainable economy. 

(5) Team - developing strategies, organisational structures, resources and tools to support the integration of ESG into FCA activities. 

_[Tutor note: Candidates performed badly on this question. Few could name three of the five themes. Most candidates wrote in very general terms about the FCA’s expectations concerning a business’s approach to ESG.]_ 

**A1.50** Explain the extent to which regulated financial institutions will be subject to a general duty of care to consumers under Section 29 (1) of the Financial Services Act 2021. 

_(3 marks)_ 

s 29(1) of the Financial Services Act 2021 required the FCA to consult on whether it should make general rules providing that authorised persons owe a duty of care to consumers. A consultation paper titled A New Consumer Duty (CP 21/13) was produced on 14 May 2021. The Consumer Duty came into effect in July 2023. This applies to retail clients, including non-professional customers and small and medium sized enterprises (SMEs), and consists of three key elements: 

(a) A new ‘Consumer Principle’ (a firm must act to deliver good outcomes for retail clients); 

(b) Overarching ‘Cross-cutting Rules’ (a firm must take all reasonable steps to avoid causing foreseeable harm and to enable customers to pursue their financial objectives and to act in good faith); and 

(c) Four Outcomes (based on communications, products and services, customer service and price and value). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A1.51** List six principal areas of financial business which are affected by the Financial Services Act 2021. _(3 marks)_ 

The UK Financial Services Act 2021 was an attempt to re-regulate financial services after 

the UK’s exit from the European Union. A wide range of business areas are affected by this act. These financial business areas include: 

- part 4A permissions, 

- duty of care, 

- money laundering offences, 

- forfeiture of money and overseas trustees, 

- debt respite schemes, 

- help to save, 

- buy-now-pay-later products, 

- financial collateral, 

- payment services, and 

- the provision of cash. 

**A1.52** Outline the role of the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) in the authorisation and supervision of firms. _(4 marks)_ 

The FCA has been tasked with maintaining confidence in the UK financial system by regulating the conduct of firms. Two of its core regulatory functions are authorisation and supervision. Authorisation is the gateway through which firms and individuals must pass before they are permitted to carry out regulated activities in the UK. Firms must meet threshold conditions, such as having adequate financial resources, being appropriately organised, and demonstrating that their senior managers are fit and proper. This process helps to ensure that only firms who are capable of acting responsibly and in the interests of consumers are allowed to participate in the market. 

Supervision is the FCA’s ongoing process of monitoring firms to ensure they continue to meet regulatory standards. The FCA uses a proactive, risk-based approach to supervision, focusing more closely on firms that pose higher potential risks to consumers or market 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

integrity. Supervision may involve thematic reviews, firm-specific assessments, and routine or targeted communications and inspections. Where necessary, the FCA may intervene, impose restrictions, or take enforcement action. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **2. MiFID regulations and international aspects** 

**A2.1** Identify eight activities that may be passported under the Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID). _(4 marks)_ 

The following activities that may be passported under the Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID): 

- (a) Receiving and transmitting orders; 

- (b) Execution orders on behalf of clients; 

- (c) Dealing on own account; 

- (d) Managing portfolios on a discretionary basis; 

- (e) Investment advice; 

- (f) Underwriting financial instruments; 

- (g) Placing financial instruments; 

- (h) Operating a Multilateral Trading Facility (MTF). 

**A2.2** Give a brief description of MiFID. 

## _(2 marks)_ 

The Markets in Financial Instruments Directive replaced and extended the scope of the Investment Services Directive (ISD). Amongst other things MiFID provides the regime for ‘passporting’ of financial services firms authorised in one member state to be able to conduct business in other member states. MiFID also extended the scope of ISD core activities to include advising on investments and also included commodity derivatives as well as transferable securities in the enlarged scope. 

**A2.3** List four financial instruments that are covered by MiFID. 

## _(2 marks)_ 

MiFID instruments include: 

- Transferable securities 

- Money market instruments 

- Units in collective investment schemes 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- Derivatives relating to securities, currencies, interest rates or yields 

- Commodity derivatives 

- Financial contracts for differences 

- Other derivatives (relating e.g. to climate variables or other statistics). 

**A2.4** List four ancillary services that may be provided by a firm under MiFID. _(2 marks)_ 

Ancillary services under MiFID include: 

   - Safekeeping and administration of financial instruments, including custody and related services 

   - Granting loans to an investor (to carry out a transaction in which the firm is involved) 

   - Advice to undertakings on capital structure and on mergers and acquisitions 

   - Foreign exchange services 

   - Investment research and financial analysis 

   - Services related to underwriting 

- **A2.5** Explain the general purpose and content of the Capital Requirements Directive (CRD) IV requirements within the UK. _(3 marks)_ 

The general purpose of the CRD 4 is to restate the core European measures that apply with regard to banks and investment firms. This consists of both a Capital Requirements Directive (CRD) and Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) and replaces all existing measures in this area. This is supported by the separate Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID II) for investment firms. 

The CRD contains all of the core provisions with regard to definitions, competent authorities, market access, initial capital, freedom of establishment and services, reciprocity, prudential supervision, disclosure and ancillary provisions. The CRR contains the principal measures governing own funds and capital adequacy for credit risk, market risk and operational risk as well as large exposures, liquidity and leverage. 

The specific objective of CRD 4 was to impose higher requirements with regard to credit risk, liquidity and leverage following the global financial crisis beginning in 2007-08. These 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

provisions were implemented under the Basel 3 measures adopted by the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS) in 2010. This, in particular, increased Core Equity Tier 1 capital (CET 1) from 2% to 4 ½% and imposed a separate Conservation Buffer (2 ½%), Countercyclical Buffer (0-2 ½%) and Systemic Risk Buffer (0-2 ½%) with a short 1-month Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) and 12-month Net Stable Funding Requirement (NSFR) and 3% leverage ratio. 

**A2.6** Explain the general purpose and nature of the Basel III and revised capital requirements. _(4 marks)_ 

The Basel Committee on Banking Supervision imposes basic minimum common, international requirements in relation to the capital adequacy position of banks. The Committee's first standards were issued in 1988 (Basel I) with revised provisions in 2004 (Basel II) and following the recent global financial crisis in 2010 (Basel III). 

Basel III builds on the three pillars introduced under Basel II with a minimum requirements (pillar one), supervisory review (pillar two) and market disclosure (pillar three). 

Basel III revises the basic minimum requirements and imposes two new liquidity ratios and a minimum leverage ratio for the first time. The minimum capital requirements increase core two year one capital (essentially paid-up share capital and retained earnings) from 2% to 4.5% with an additional conservation buffer (2.5%), countercyclical buffer (0-2.5%) and systemically important institution buffer (0-3%). 

Basel III was implemented in 2019. 

The cumulative effects of imposing these requirements will be to increase the stability of banks by allowing them to absorb additional losses in the event of a major crisis and to withstand short-term lending restrictions through the new liquidity rules with overall debt levels being limited through the new leverage ratio. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A2.7** Identify any TWO of the three European Supervisory Authorities and summarise the types of firms or activities for which they are responsible. _(4 marks)_ 

The three European Supervisory Authorities consist of: 

- a) the European Banking Authority (EBA) is responsible for banking policy; 

- b) the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) is responsible for securities policy; and 

- c) the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EOPIA) is responsible for insurance and pension policy. 

**A2.8** Explain the role and function of the Financial Stability Board (FSB) at the international level. _(4 marks)_ 

The Financial Stability Board (FSB) was established at the G20 London Summit in April 2009 following the global financial crisis to extend and replace the earlier Financial Stability Forum (FSF) which had been set up following the Asian financial crisis beginning in Thailand in July 1997. The FSB consists of representatives from national financial ministries, central banks and supervisory authorities as well as other international financial agencies. This includes inter alia the BCBS, the International Organisation of Securities Commissions (IOSC), International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) and the Committee on Payments and Market Infrastructures (CPMI). 

The purpose of the FSB is to coordinate the work of national financial authorities and international standard agencies, and to promote the implementation of effective regulatory, supervisory and other financial sector policies. The FSB has produced a large number of important reports in various policy areas since the global financial crisis beginning in 20072008. The FSB has specifically produced an online or virtual Compendium of Standards which is effectively a global financial rulebook/ This includes a specific list of 16 Key Standards (https://www.fsb.org/work-of-the-sb/about-the-compendium-ofstandards/key_standards/). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A2.9** How did Brexit affect the ability of UK firms to provide financial services to EU clients? 

_(3 marks)_ 

Valid points include: an explanation of passporting, transition, the end of passporting, and postBrexit options for UK firms. 

The term passporting arose from the Investment Services Directive (ISD) 1993. In this directive passporting considers situations where a firm authorised to provide investment services in one member state, could provide this same service in other member states without additional authorisation. 

Following notice given by the UK government to the EU and various deadline extensions, the UK left the EU on 31 January 2020, but stayed in the EU due to transitional provisions that ended at 11pm on 31 December 2020. During the transitional time period, UK firms had to comply with EU legislation, including implementing new legislation. The government introduced legislation that converted existing directly applicable EU legislation (Regulations) into UK law and preserved existing UK laws which implement EU obligations. 

obligations. 

Following the end of the transitional period, UK firms lost their ability to access the EU single market on the basis of ‘passporting’; this means that UK firms are limited in their ability to provide financial services to EU clients, likewise for EU firms to UK clients. 

UK firms may look to exemptions in local law that allow for access by firms from non-EU countries or apply for permission from the European Commission, or other EU bodies under the access regimes for third countries provided in EU legislation. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **3. High level standards** 

- **A3.1** Specify four of the threshold conditions which an FCA-regulated firm has to meet to obtain Part 4A permission under FSMA. _(2 marks)_ 

The threshold conditions consist of: 

- a) Location of offices; 

- b) Effective supervision; 

- c) Appropriate resources; 

- d) Suitability; 

- e) Business model. 

**A3.2** List four of the Principles for Businesses set out in PRIN. 

_(4 marks)_ 

The twelve Principles for Businesses are: 

**1.** Integrity 

**2.** Skill, care and diligence; 

**3.** Management and control; 

**4.** Financial prudence; 

**5.** Market conduct; 

**6.** Customers’ interests; 

**7.** Communications with clients; 

**8.** Conflicts of interest; 

**9.** Customers and relationships of trust; 

**10.** Clients’ assets; 

**11.** Relations with regulators; 

**12.** Consumer Duty. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A3.3** Name two of the sections within the FCA Handbook that set out the key obligations in relation to management functions. _(2 marks)_ 

Management functions under the FCA Handbook are dealt with in: 

1. PRIN 

2. SYSC 

3. SUP 

4. GENPRU and BIPRU 

**A3.4** List four of the FCA’s individual (‘tier 1’) conduct rules. 

_(2 marks)_ 

COCON 2.1.1: 

**1.** You must act with integrity. 

**2.** You must act with due skill, care and diligence. 

**3.** You must be open and co-operative with the FCA, PRA and other regulators 

**4.** You must pay due regard to the interests of customers and treat them fairly 

**5.** You must observe proper standards of market conduct 

**6.** You must act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers. 

**A3.5** Explain the “senior managers” regime. 

_(3 marks)_ 

Before undertaking a senior manager function on behalf of an authorised firm a person must first receive approval from the appropriate regulator to undertake that function. The firm seeks approval for the individual, and before granting approval the regulator must be satisfied that the candidate is fit and proper to perform the senior manager function to which the application relates. The regulator will have regard to a number of things when assessing the fitness and propriety of a person to perform a particular senior manager function. In particular these will include the person’s: 

- Honesty, integrity and reputation; 

- Competence and capability; 

- Financial soundness. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

Once approved an individual is subject to the provisions of COCON and the conduct rules (both Tier 1 and Tier 2) and is also subject to discipline by the regulator for failing to comply with relevant rules or principles. Penalties for this can include fines and withdrawal of approval. 

- **A3.6** Explain how the FCA ensures that those who seek to become authorised are Fit and Proper. _(4 marks)_ 

Schedule 6 of FSMA sets out threshold conditions which represent the minimum conditions which a firm is required to satisfy and continue to satisfy, in order to be given and retain Part 4A permission. There are five of these conditions as follows: 

- Location of offices 

- Effective supervision 

- Appropriate resources 

- Suitability 

- Business model 

The FCA will therefore consider where the firm is established, but also who has influence over running the business. The firm must demonstrate through its business plan that it will comply with the requirements of BIPRU and SYSC. The firm must demonstrate that its financial and other resources are appropriate for the likely risks that it will face. 

In assessing suitability the FCA must be satisfied by the firm that it is fit and proper person having regard to all of the circumstances, including connections to any person, the nature of the business and the need to ensure that its affairs are conducted soundly and prudently. 

The FCA will have regard to whether the firm will conduct its business with integrity and in compliance with proper standards, has competent and prudent management and can demonstrate that it conducts its affairs with due skill, care and diligence. 

In addition the FCA will look at whether the governing body of the firm is made up of individuals with an appropriate range of skills and experience to understand, operate and manage the firm’s regulated activities. Also that the firm has in place adequate systems of 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

control to comply with requirements under SYSC. These should include areas such as recruitment of only employees who are honest and competent and committed to high standards of integrity. 

- **A3.7** Give FOUR examples of matters the FCA would have regard to when determining a person’s honesty, integrity and reputation. _(2 marks)_ 

   - Whether the person has been convicted of a criminal offence (particularly of fraud, dishonesty or otherwise relevant to financial services). 

   - Adverse findings or settlement in civil proceedings. 

   - Whether the person has been the subject of, or interviewed during an investigation by the FCA or other regulatory body. 

   - Previous contravention of any requirements and standards of a regulatory system. 

   - Previous justified complaints about the individual. 

   - Whether they have been dismissed or asked to resign from previous employment. 

   - Whether they been disqualified from acting as a director or involved with a company that has been refused (or had withdrawn) registration or similar to perform a trade or business, or been involved with a company that has failed within a year of their involvement. 

**A3.8** Identify six of the key requirements set out in SYSC. 

_(3 marks)_ 

The main requirements imposed under SYSC are: 

- Senior management arrangements (SYSC 2) 

- General organisational requirements (SYSC 4) 

- Employees, agents and other relevant persons (SYSC 5) 

- Compliance, internal audit and financial crime (SYSC 6) 

- Risk control (SYSC 7) 

- Outsourcing (SYSC 8) 

- Record-keeping (SYSC 9) 

- Conflicts of interest (SYSC 10). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A3.9** Explain briefly the general purpose and structure of the High Level Standards contained in the FCA’s Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(4 marks)_ 

The High Level Standards are set out in Block 1 of the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. The purpose is to establish the general minimum standards of conduct with which regulated firms and their key personnel (approved persons) must comply at all times. 

The High Level Standards consist of the: 

   - a) Principles for Businesses (PRIN); 

   - b) Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls (SYSC); 

   - c) Code of Conduct (COCON) 

   - d) Threshold Conditions (COND); 

   - e) Statements of Principle and Code of Practice for Approved Persons (APER); 

   - f) Fit and Proper Test for Approved Persons (FIT); 

   - g) Financial Stability and Market Confidence (FINMAR); 

   - h) Training and Competence (TC); and 

   - i) General Provisions (GEN). 

- **A3.10** List three of the main requirements governing supervision of outsourcing relationships under SYSC. _(3 marks)_ 

Outsourcing is dealt with as an aspect of operational risk under SYSC 8. Firms must maintain robust governance arrangements and adequate internal control mechanisms over material and non-material outsourcing arrangements. Non-material outsourcing may be dealt with in a manner that is appropriate and proportionate to the firm's operations. 

The FCA may also object within one month where an MiFID investment firm intends to outsource portfolio management outside the EEA unless: 

- a) the other service provider is authorised or registered in its home country to provide the services covered and subject to effective supervision; and 

- b) an appropriate cooperation agreement has been entered into between the FCA and the overseas authority concerned. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A3.11** List eight of the high level standards in the sourcebooks contained in the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(4 marks)_ 

The manuals or sourcebooks contained within the high level standards are as follows: 

|(a) Principles for Businesses;|PRIN|
|---|---|
|(b) Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls;|SYSC|
|(c) Code of Conduct|COCON|
|(d) Threshold Conditions;|COND|
|(e) Statements of Principle and Code of Practice for Approved Persons;|APER|
|(f) Fit and Proper Test for Approved Persons|FIT|
|(g) Financial Stability and Market Confidence Sourcebook;|FINMAR|
|(h) Training and Competence;|TC|
|(i) General Provisions;|GEN|
|(j) Fees Manual.|FEES|



**A3.12** Explain the different ways in which firms can manage conflicts of interest . 

_(3 marks)_ 

A firm must manage conflicts of interest fairly between itself and customers and between customers under FCA PRIN 8. Conflicts of interest can generally be dealt with either through declining to act, Chinese walls or maintaining an independence policy and disclosure as a last resort. 

Conflicts of interest are dealt with under SYSC 10 with firms being required to take all reasonable steps to identify conflicts between the firm and a client or between clients (SYSC 10.1.3R) with types of conflicts being set out in SYSC 10.1.4R. Firms must maintain and operate effective organisational and administrative arrangements to prevent conflicts creating a material risk of damage to clients (SYSC 10.1.7R) failing which the conflict must be disclosed to the client (SYSC 10.1.8R). 

Common platform firms and management companies must maintain an effective conflicts of interest policy (SYSC 10.1.10R) with specific provisions applying with regard to the content of the policy (SYSC 10.1.11R). Firewalls and Chinese walls are dealt with under (SYSC 10.2). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A3.13** List six of the PRA’s Fundamental Rules. 

_(3 marks)_ 

The Fundamental Rules (FRs) imposed under the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) Rulebook apply with regard to: 

- (a) Integrity; 

- (b) Due skill care and diligence; 

- (c) Act in a prudent manner; 

- (d) Maintain adequate financial resources; 

- (e) Have effective risk strategies and risk management systems; 

- (f) Organise and control its affairs responsibly; 

- (g) Deal with regulators in an open and cooperative way; 

- (h) Prepare for resolution. 

**A3.14** Explain the principal purpose of the Senior Managers Regime (SMR). 

_(3 marks)_ 

The objective of the **Senior Managers Regime (SMR)** is to ensure that senior managers are held accountable for any **misconduct** within their specific areas of responsibility. Firms have to prepare **Responsibility Maps** to allocate all relevant Senior Management functions (SMFs) with each manager adding a **Statement of Responsibility (SOR)** (s60(2A) FSMA). 

Authorised persons must vet candidates before putting them forward for approval to ensure that they are fit and proper in terms of relevant qualifications, training, competence and personal characteristics (s60A). Conditions such as time limits may be attached to approvals (s61(2B)) and approval can be varied subsequently (s 63ZB). The regime applies to persons approved under s59 FSMA to carry out a designated senior management function (SMF) as provided for under s 59ZA FSMA as amended. 

**A3.15** Explain the general purpose and content of the Financial Conduct Authority’s conduct risk control framework. _(3 marks)_ 

This question can be answered in a number of different ways. The FCA has generally been made responsible for conduct regulation with the PRA managing prudential regulation 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

under the revised institutional structure established under the FSA 2012. The FCA is specifically responsible for conduct risk although this is a difficult issue to define precisely. The term conduct risk is not referred to in the FSMA as amended. Conduct risk is not separately defined in the FCA Glossary nor is the term risk separately defined. The objectives of the FCA are referred to under the FSMA in terms of acting as a financial function authority with the specific operational objectives of promoting consumer protection, system integrity and competition (s1B (2) and (3) FSMA). Conduct risk is not separately defined and it would generally appear that this refers to anything that is not of a prudential regulation nature in practice. 

The FCA has been developing its approach to conduct risk under its 2013 and 2014 Risk Outlook documents with a series of priority actions being set out in its Business Plans for 2013 and 2014. Reference may also have been made to various speeches. 

The new policy risk model is generally made up of two parts with ‘Drivers of Risk’ (Part A) consisting of Underlying drivers (inherent factors and Structures and business conduct features) and Environmental developments) and ‘Evolving risk landscape’ (Part B) consisting of Cross-market pressures and related risks and Forward-looking areas of focus (2014 Risk Outlook P7). 

Candidates may also have referred to relevant high level regulatory provisions covering conduct risk including PRIN 1 (integrity), 5 (market conduct), 6 (customers' interest), 7 (client communication), 8 (conflicts of interest), 9 (trust), 10 (asset protection) and 12 (consumer duty) as well as COCON 1 (integrity) and 5 (market conduct), SYSC 10 (conflicts of interest) and 19 (remuneration) as well as COND and FIT more generally. Training requirements may also have been referred to under TC as well as the Consumer Duty, Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) and the Retail Distribution Review (RDR). 

- **A3.16** Explain the purpose and content of the offence of causing a financial institution to fail, imposed under section 36 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013. 

_(3 marks)_ 

s36 creates a new offence of causing a financial institution to fail. This applies where the person is a senior manager in relation to an institution and takes (or agrees to take) a 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

decision (or fails to act) and was aware at the time of the decision that this could cause the failure of a group institution in circumstances where the conduct would fall below that which would be reasonably expected and the decision (or failure to act) led to the failure of the institution (s36(1)). 

The person is guilty on summary conviction for up to 12 months imprisonment or seven years on indictment or a fine or both (s36(2)). Relevant terms are defined in s37. 

**A3.17** Explain the principal purpose of the Certification Regime (CR) established under the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013. _(3 marks)_ 

The Certification regime was adopted with the Senior Managers regime following recommendation by the Parliamentary Commission for Banking Standards (PCBS) on ‘Changing Banking for Good’ in June 2013. The Certification regime was introduced for deposit taking firms (other than credit unions) and applies to personnel other than Senior Managers that may pose a risk of significant harm to the firm or a customer. Firms are to certify the fitness and propriety of staff every year (s 63E FSMA) rather than have preapproval by the authorities as under the earlier approved persons regime. The same conditions apply with regard to certification as approval including qualifications, training, competence and characteristics (s 63F). A new set of Conduct Rules (COCON) were also adopted (under s 64A FSMA) which replace the earlier APER rules. 

**A3.18** List eight of the sections within Senior Management Arrangements (SYSC) within the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(4 marks)_ 

SYSC 1 Application & Purpose 

SYSC 2 Senior Management Arrangements SYSC 3 Systems and Controls 

SYSC 4 General Organisational Requirements 

SYSC 5 Employees, Agents & Other Persons & Electronic Communications 

SYSC 6 Compliance, Internal Audit & Financial Crime SYSC 7 Risk Control 

SYSC 8 Outsourcing [Financial Technology] 

SYSC 9 Record-Keeping 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

SYSC 10 Conflicts of Interest SYSC 10A Recording Telephone conversations SYSC 11 Liquidity Risk Systems & Controls SYSC 12 Group Risk Systems & Controls SYSC 13 Operational risk Systems & Controls SYSC 14 Risk Management for Insurers SYSC 18 Whistleblowing SYSC 19A IFPRU Remuneration Code SYSC 19B AIFM Remuneration Code SYSC 19C BIPRU Remuneration Code SYSC 19D Dual Regulated Firms Remuneration Code SYSC 19E UCITS Remuneration Code SYSC 19F Remuneration & Performance Management SYSC 20 Reverse Stress Testing SYSC 21 Risk Control: Additional Guidance SYSC 22 Regulatory References SYSC 23-27 Senior Managers & Certification SYSC 28 Insurance Distribution: Specific Knowledge 

**A3.19** Identify the four Senior Manager Conduct Rules within Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) COCON 2.2. _(2 marks)_ 

The Senior Manager Conduct Rules set out within FCA COCON (2.2) consist of the following: 

(a) You must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which you are responsible is controlled effectively. 

(b) You must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which you are responsible complies with the relevant requirements and standards of the regulatory system. 

(c) You must take reasonable steps to ensure that any delegation of your responsibilities is to an appropriate person and that you oversee the discharge of the delegated responsibility effectively. 

(d) You must disclose appropriately any information of which the FCA and PRA would reasonably expect notice. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A3.20** Explain why it is important for a firm to keep accurate records of past business undertaken (SYSC 9). _(3 marks)_ 

SYSC 9 stipulates that keeping accurate records of past business is critical for effective regulation and compliance. Often it is required to keep records for seven years. Clear and accurate records allow a regulator to effectively monitor the firms’ behaviours, ensure they are compliant and have fulfilled all their obligations to their customers and clients. This involves record keeping both within the firm of internal procedures and of external actions in an accessible manner which may be stored for future reference by any appropriate regulatory authority. 

Critically, and to satisfy the demands of UK MiFID (as outlined in the workbook), the firm needs to ensure: 

1. The record can be reconstituted into each key stage of the processing of each transaction. 

2. It must be possible for any corrections or other amendments, and the contents of the records prior to such corrections and amendments, to be easily ascertained. 

3. It must not be possible for the records otherwise to be manipulated or altered. 

**A3.21** Name and outline three of the principles within the CISI Code of Conduct. _(3 marks)_ 

The principles are: 

- Personal Accountability 

- Client Focus 

- Conflict of Interest 

- Respect for Market Participants 

- Professional Development 

- Aware of Capabilities 

- Respect Others and the Environment 

- Speak Up & Listen Up. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A3.22** Firms trading in non-investment products in UK wholesale financial markets are subject to many voluntary codes of conduct. Outline the scope and focus of two such codes. _(3 marks)_
