Firms trading in non-investment products in UK wholesale financial markets are subject to many voluntary codes of conduct. This question asked candidates to outline the scope and focus of two such codes. Within a strong answer we expected candidates to outline any two of the following: 

• The FX Global Code has no legal or regulatory bearing and covers topics such as ethics, governance, information sharing, as well as risk management and compliance and confirmation and settlement. This code was developed by a partnership between central banks and market participants from 16 jurisdictions around the globe. The Code promotes a robust, fair, liquid, open, and transparent marketplace in which participants can effectively and confidently transact. a partnership between central banks and market participants from 16 jurisdictions around the globe. The Code promotes a robust, fair, liquid, open, and transparent marketplace in which participants can effectively and confidently transact. 

• The UK Money Market Code is a voluntary code, written by market participants which sets out best practice expected from participants in UK deposit, repo and securities lending markets. The code promotes high standards to build trust and certainty. 

, written by market participants which sets out best practice expected from participants in UK deposit, repo and securities lending markets. The code promotes high standards to build trust and certainty. 

• The Global Precious Metals Code was introduced by the London Bullion Market Association and applies to all precious metals market participants. The Code sets out best practice in the market including compliance, governance, risk management and pre- and post-trade execution. 

**A3.24** Define and explain **one** of the cross-cutting obligations under the Consumer Duty. 

_(3 marks)_ 

• Acting in Good Faith Towards Retail Customers – Firms must act with honesty, fairness, and openness in all dealings with retail customers. Actions should align with what customers reasonably expect, and this principle should be embedded at the design stage of products and services to ensure fairness from the beginning. 

• Avoiding Foreseeable Harm – Firms are responsible for ensuring that retail customers understand and accept the inherent risks in products and services. They must take proactive 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

steps to prevent foreseeable harm within their control and must not exploit customers' vulnerabilities, behavioural biases, or lack of financial literacy. 

• Enabling and Supporting Retail Customers to Pursue Their Financial Objectives – Firms should provide relevant information and understanding to support customers in making their own financial decisions. This approach empowers customers to take responsibility for their choices, fostering an environment of trust and confidence in the firm's products and services. 

**A3.25** What is the purpose of the Consumer Credit Sourcebook (CONC), and how does it protect consumers in the credit market? _(3 marks)_ 

The FCA first introduced the Consumer Credit Sourcebook (CONC) in April 2014. The purpose of CONC is to set out the detailed obligations that are specific to credit-related regulated activities and activities connected to those activities carried on by firms. 

CONC protects consumers by making sure that firms carry out affordability checks before offering credit, provide clear information about the costs and terms of loans, and don’t engage in aggressive or misleading sales practices. It also sets rules for dealing with customers in financial difficulty, such as offering appropriate repayment plans instead of taking immediate action like repossession. These rules help prevent consumers from taking on credit they can't afford and ensure that they are treated fairly if they face problems repaying a loan. 

**A3.26** What steps should firms take to achieve good outcomes for vulnerable customers? _(3 marks)_ 

To achieve good outcomes for vulnerable customers, firms should take appropriate action to understand the needs of their target market/customer base. Firms should make sure staff have the right skills and capability to recognise and respond to the needs of vulnerable customers. Additionally, firms should respond to customer needs throughout product design, flexible customer service provision and communications; and monitor and assess whether they are meeting and responding to the needs of customers with characteristics of vulnerability, and make improvements where this is not happening. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **4. Financial promotions** 

**A4.1** Explain the difference between a “real time” and a “non-real time” financial promotion. _(3 marks)_ 

The meaning of “real time” and “non-real time” is set out in Article 7 of the Financial Promotion Order. 

Real time generally refers to instantaneous or immediate forms of financial promotion involving direct contact between the customer and a regulated firm. This will generally include personal meetings as well as telephone or other interactive forms of communication. 

Non-real time refers to more static forms of advertisement not involving any immediate contact between the potential customer and the firm. This will generally include mail shots as well as magazine and TV advertising as well as internet promotions to the extent that no direct communication is involved. 

Relevant factors to be taken into account in determining whether a communication is nonreal time include: 

   - a) More than one person is involved using a standard format; 

   - b) The communication creates a record; 

   - c) It does not require any immediate response. 

- **A4.2** The Financial Promotion Rules on the communication of unsolicited real time financial promotions (cold calls) contain a number of requirements that must be complied with. List three of these requirements. _(3 marks)_ 

Cold calls may only be made in the following cases: 

- a) The communication falls within one of the exemptions set out in the  Financial Promotions Order (such as certified sophisticated investor); 

- b) The communication falls within one of the COBS exceptions (such as eligible counterparty or professional client); 

- c) Existing retail client and the relationship has been agreed; 

- d) Certain marketable packaged products (such as regulated non-derivative collective investment schemes); 

- e) Services relating to readily realisable securities other than warrants. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A4.3** Identify six exceptions under the Financial Promotions Order for breach of the restriction on the issuance of financial promotions under s 21 of the FSMA. _(3 marks)_ 

The main exceptions contained in the FPO include: 

   - a) Generic promotions; 

   - b) Investment professionals; 

   - c) Overseas recipients; 

   - d) Deposits and insurance; 

   - e) One-off communications; 

   - f) Certified high net worth individuals (net income of over £100,000 or net assets of over £250,000); 

   - g) Certified sophisticated investors; 

   - h) High net worth companies. 

- **A4.4** Identify and explain the main regulatory requirements that apply with regard to financial promotions under the FSMA 2000 and the FCA Handbook and the source of the main exemptions available. _(3 marks)_ 

The main obligation on financial promotion is imposed under s21 (1) and (2) of the FSMA. This requires that a financial promotion must only be communicated by an approved person or have been approved by an authorised person. 

This is supported by the exemptions that may be issued under either the Financial Promotions Order (issued by the Treasury) or set out by the FCA in the COBS (Conduct of Business Sourcebook) within the FCA Handbook. 

- **A4.5** Give three examples of financial promotions to which the FCA’s Conduct of Business (COBS) Rules do not apply. _(3 marks)_ 

The Financial Promotions Order provides a number of exemptions, for example, for communications directed at investment professionals, to certified high net worth individuals and certified sophisticated investors. Also excluded are a company’s statutory accounts and 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

directors’ reports, and disclosures/announcements required by the listing rules or the rules of an exchange or market. 

The COBS Rules do not apply to communications to a person outside the UK made from a place outside the UK. 

**A4.6** Describe the regime relating to financial promotions. 

## _(4 marks)_ 

A financial promotion is an invitation or inducement to engage in investment activity. Under section 21 of FSMA 2000 it is an offence in the course of business to communicate an invitation or inducement to engage in investment activity unless either the person doing so is an authorised person or the content of the financial promotion is approved by an authorised person 

The Financial Promotions Order sets out a number of exemptions from section 21 for example for promotions directed at authorised persons or certified high net worth or sophisticated investors. There are also exemptions for promotions required by statute such as a company’s annual report or by the rules of an exchange or market. 

Where a firm issues or approves the content of a financial promotion then it must ensure the person who does so is competent to perform that role and confirms that the promotion complies with the requirements of the rules in COBS 4. Like all communications with clients a financial promotion must be fair, clear and not misleading. 

**A4.7** Give four examples of exempt financial promotions. 

## _(2 marks)_ 

The Financial Promotions Order specifies certain exemptions from s21 as follows: 

Communications directed at: 

- Investment professionals 

- Certified high net worth individuals and associations of high net worth individuals 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

Also the following: 

- Company accounts 

- Directors’ reports 

- Listing particulars and prospectuses 

- Promotions by overseas persons to overseas persons 

- One off non-real time financial promotions and one-off solicited real time promotions 

- A financial promotion which contains only one or more of the following, name of the firm, name of an investment, a contact point, a logo, a brief factual description of the firm’s activities, fees or investment products, the price or yields of investments and the charges. 

- A personal quotation or illustration form 

- A financial promotion subject to the Takeover Code 

- A financial promotion in the form of a decision tree for a stakeholder pension scheme 

**A4.8** What is meant by a solicited financial promotion? 

## _(2 marks)_ 

Where contact is initiated by the customer or the financial promotion takes place in response to an express request from the recipient, then it is described as solicited: if it is initiated by the firm, then it is unsolicited. A person is not treated as having expressly requested a call, visit or dialogue because he either omits to indicate that he does not wish to receive such contact or because he agrees to standard terms that state such visits etc. will take place. Therefore a customer must positively indicate that he wants such contact or specifically request it. 

- **A4.9** In what circumstances may a firm make an unsolicited real time financial promotion (“cold call”)? _(2 marks)_ 

A firm cannot make a cold call unless: 

- a) the recipient is an existing customer and envisages receiving cold calls; or 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

   - b) the financial promotion relates to a generally marketable packaged product (which is not a higher volatility fund); or 

   - c) the financial promotion relates to readily realisable securities other than warrants). 

- **A4.10** What rules apply when a firm communicates to clients, or potential clients, information which contains an indication of the past performance of an investment? 

_(3 marks)_ 

Where past performance is indicated, the following rules apply: 

   - That indication must not be the most prominent feature of the communication. 

   - Performance information must cover at least the last five years (or the whole available period, if less), and must be based on complete 12 month periods. 

   - The reference period and source of information must be stated. 

   - There must be a prominent warning that the figures refer to the past and that past performance is not a reliable indicator of future results. 

   - The currency must be clearly stated (if different from that of the EEA state where the retail client is resident), together with a warning about the effect of currency fluctuations. 

   - If the indication is based on gross performance, the effect of commissions, fees or charges must be disclosed. 

- **A4.11** Specify the consequences of breach of the restriction on financial promotion imposed under section 21 of the FSMA. _(2 marks)_ 

The consequences of breach of the restriction on the issuance of unauthorised or unapproved financial promotions are as follows: 

- a) the person is guilty of an offence and liable for up to six months imprisonment (on summary conviction) or two years (on indictment) or a fine or both (s 25(1)); 

- b) agreements entered into in consequence of an unlawful communication are unenforceable against the other party who is also entitled to recover any money or property paid and compensation for loss sustained (s30(2)(a) and (b)); 

- c) a public or private censure may be imposed (s 205); 

- d) a general financial penalty may be imposed (s 206(1)); 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

   - e) an action for damages may possibly be available (s 138D); 

   - f) an injunction may be issued (s 380(1)); 

   - g) the FCA may apply for a restitution order (s382(1)). 

- **A4.12** List six of the general requirements that apply with regard to the publication of past performance information under COBS within the FCA Handbook. _(3 marks)_ 

The FCA requirements with regard to past performance information under COBS 4.6 include the following: 

- a) information must not be the most prominent feature; 

- b) information should cover, at least, the five preceding years; 

- c) information should cover 12 month periods; 

- d) information should state the reference period and source; 

- e) a prominent warning should be included against reliance on past information; 

- f) specify the relevant currency and warn that currencies may fluctuate; 

- g) disclose the effect of commissions, fees and charges on gross performance. 

**A4.13** Explain the general requirement imposed under s21 Financial Services and Markets Act (FSMA) on financial promotions. _(3 marks)_ 

As 21 FSMA requires that a financial promotion, in the form of an invitation or inducement to engage in an investment activity, must be issued by an authorised person or the content approved by an authorised person (s 21(1) and (2)). All financial promotions must be clear, fair and misleading (COBS 4.2). This is subject to the exemptions provided for under the Financial Promotion Order (FPO) as amended. 

The restrictions on financial promotions do not apply to purely factual communications nor to eligible counterparties with regard to MiFID business nor to incoming MiFID communications with these regulated in the home EEA state. 

A person who breaches the restrictions on financial promotions is guilty of an offence and liable for up to two years imprisonment (six months on summary conviction) or a fine or both (s 25(1)) subject to a reasonable belief or reasonable precautions defence ((s 25(2)). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **5. Conduct of business** 

- **A5.1** Explain the purpose and content of the aggregation and allocation rule under the Conduct of Business (COBS) section of the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(4 marks)_ 

Aggregation is concerned with the grouping together of customers’ orders for execution either with those of other clients or with the firm’s.  A firm may not aggregate orders unless there is likely to be no disadvantage to the customer and any possible disadvantage has been disclosed to the customer either orally or in writing. 

Fair allocation is concerned with ensuring that no unfair preference is given to any one by a firm in its dealings having regard to transaction price and size. Firms must, in particular, ensure that customer orders are prioritised unless the firm can show on reasonable grounds that the transaction could not otherwise have been effected. 

- **A5.2** Explain what is meant by the best execution obligation imposed on firms, and identify the execution factors. _(2 marks)_ 

When executing an order for a client, a firm must take all sufficient steps to obtain the best possible result for its client taking into account the execution factors. 

The execution factors are: the price, costs, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of an order. 

**A5.3** What are the best execution criteria, and what are they for? _(2 marks)_ 

The best execution criteria are used for the purpose of determining the relative importance of the execution factors (such as price, costs, speed, etc). 

The best execution criteria are: 

- The characteristics of the client (including their categorisation as retail or professional); 

- The characteristics of the client order. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

      - The characteristics of financial instruments that are the subject of the client order. 

   - The characteristics of the execution venues to which the order can be directed. 

   - (Execution venues include regulated markets, MTFs, OTFs, systematic internalisers, market makers or other liquidity providers.) 

- **A5.4** What is the general rule with regard to the timing of executing client orders? In what circumstances may this rule be varied? _(2 marks)_ 

In general, the rule on client order handling requires a firm to have in place procedures for the prompt, fair and expeditious execution of client orders. Otherwise comparable client orders should be executed in chronological order of receipt by the firm. 

Where client orders are otherwise comparable, they should be carried out sequentially and promptly, unless: 

- The characteristics of the order or prevailing market conditions make this impossible; or 

- The interests of the client require otherwise. 

**A5.5** What is the “quantitative test” for elective professional client status? _(2 marks)_ 

The “quantitative test” (which applies only in relation to MiFID business) requires that at 

least two of the following criteria are satisfied: 

- The client has carried out significant transactions on the relevant market at an average frequency of 10 per quarter over the previous four quarters. 

- The size of the client’s portfolio exceeds €500,000 (this includes cash deposits as well as financial instruments). 

- The client works, or has worked, in the financial sector for at least one year in a professional position, which requires knowledge of the transactions or services envisaged. 

**A5.6** List six per se eligible counterparties. 

_(3 marks)_ 

- An investment firm 

- A credit institution 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- An insurance company 

- A UCITS scheme (or its management company) 

- A pension fund (or its management company) 

- Another authorised or regulated EEA financial institution 

- Certain own account dealers in commodities or commodity derivatives 

- A national government (including a body dealing with public debt) 

- A central bank 

- A supranational organisation 

## **A5.7** 

What are the general obligations of a firm under the rule on suitability? _(3 marks)_ 

A firm must take reasonable steps to ensure that a personal recommendation, or a decision to trade, is suitable for its client. 

In order to assess suitability, the firm must obtain appropriate information about the client’s: 

- Investment knowledge and experience; 

- Financial situation; and 

- Investment objectives. 

The firm must obtain this information from the client, and it must be sufficient to give the firm 

a reasonable basis for believing that a proposed transaction: 

- Meets the client’s investment objectives; 

- Is such that the client is able financially to bear any related investment risks consistent with his investment objectives; and 

- Is such that the client has the necessary experience and knowledge to understand those risks. 

The firm is entitled to rely on the information provided by the client (unless it is aware that the information is manifestly out of date, inaccurate or incomplete). 

- **A5.8** Explain the FCA’s requirements on inducements. _(2 marks)_ 

Principle 1 (Integrity), Principle 6 (Customers’ interests) and PRIN 12 (Consumer Duty) require a firm to conduct its business with integrity, to pay due regard to the interests of its customers and to treat them fairly. The purpose of the rule on inducements is to ensure that 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

a firm does not conduct business under arrangements that might give rise to a conflict with its clients. 

A firm must not pay to, or accept from, a person other than a client any fee, commission or benefit, unless: 

- The fee, commission or benefit is designed to enhance the quality of the relevant service to the client and does not impair compliance with the firm’s duty to act in the best interests of the client; and 

- The firm clearly discloses to the client the existence, nature and amount of the fee, commission or benefit before providing the service. 

**A5.9** Explain the purpose of a Chinese wall. 

## _(2 marks)_ 

Chinese walls are used in circumstances where the FCA would consider it appropriate for a firm to withhold or not to use information that would otherwise be disclosed to, or used for the benefit of, a client. A Chinese wall provides an artificial barrier to the flow of information between the parts of a firm (provided that the arrangements remain effective and are adequately monitored). This assists the firm in complying with Principle 8 (Conflicts of interest) which requires a firm to manage a conflict of interest fairly both between itself and its clients and between one client and another. 

**A5.10** What is the ‘qualitative test’ for elective professional client status? _(2 marks)_ 

The ‘qualitative test’ requires the firm to undertake an adequate assessment of the expertise, experience and knowledge of the client that gives reasonable assurance, in light of the nature of the transactions or services envisaged, that the client is capable of making his own investment decisions and understands the risks involved. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A5.11** Apart from the ‘qualitative test’, what procedures must a firm follow before it may treat a client as an elective professional client? _(2 marks)_ 

The procedures require that: 

- The client must state in writing that it wishes to be treated as a professional client (either generally or in respect of a particular service or transaction or type of transaction or product); 

- The firm must give the client a clear written warning of the protections and investor compensation rights the client may lose; and 

- The client must state in writing that it is aware of the consequences of losing such protections. 

**A5.12** What information must a firm provide to a client about itself, its services and its remuneration? _(4 marks)_ 

A firm must provide a retail client and (in the case of MiFID business) any client with prescribed information. This includes: 

- Information about the firm and its services: 

   - Name and address of the firm and appropriate contact details 

   - Statement that the firm is authorised (and the name of its competent authority) 

   - Whether the firm is acting through an appointed representative (or a tied agent) 

   - Details of occasional and periodical reporting 

   - Conflicts of interest policy 

- Information about safeguarding of designated investments and client money 

- Information about costs and associated charges 

This information must be provided in a durable medium (or via a compliant website), either before any service is provided or (if this is impossible, as where an agreement is concluded by distance communication) immediately after starting to provide the service. 

A firm carrying on MiFID business must also make available information to enable a client to identify any relevant investor compensation scheme. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A5.13** What were the main changes brought in by the Retail Distribution Review (RDR) and when did these become effective? _(2 marks)_ 

The three main changes brought in by the RDR are as follows: 

- When dealing with retail clients, a firm is now required to describe its services as either ‘independent’ advice or ‘restricted’ advice; 

- For new advice to a retail client commission-based remuneration has been ended. Firms are now required to charge retail clients an agreed fee for the advice they give; 

- The professional standards of investment advisers have been increased (to QCF Level 4). 

The changes brought in by the RDR became effective on 31 December 2012. 

- **A5.14** What are the two circumstances, in terms of the rule on appropriateness, when a firm may be required to give a warning to its client? _(2 marks)_ 

A firm is required to give a warning to the client in two circumstances (these warnings may be provided in a standardised format): 

   - Where the firm considers, on the basis of the information received, that the product or service is NOT appropriate for the client, it must warn the client of that fact. 

   - Where the client does not provide information to enable the firm to assess appropriateness (or where the client provides insufficient information regarding his knowledge and experience), the firm must warn the client that it is UNABLE TO DETERMINE whether the product or service is appropriate for him. 

- **A5.15** For the purpose of the rule on appropriateness, what are non-complex financial instruments? _(2 marks)_ 

Non-complex financial instruments must satisfy the following criteria: 

- They are not derivatives (or similar products) 

- There are frequent opportunities to trade in them at independently determined publicly available prices 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- They do not involve a potential liability that exceeds their cost 

- There is publicly available information on their characteristics that is likely to be readily understood by the average retail client for the purpose of making an investment decision. 

**A5.16** Outline the main conduct of business rules in respect of a firm’s order execution policy. 

_(4 marks)_ 

In order to satisfy its obligation to obtain the best possible result for its clients, a firm is required to establish and operate an order execution policy. This policy must include, for each class of financial instrument, information about the different execution venues that the firm will use to execute client orders and the factors affecting the choice of execution venue. 

A firm must provide appropriate information to its clients about its order execution policy. In the case of retail client, the firm must provide the following details about its execution policy before executing any transaction: 

- An account of the relative importance the firm assigns to the execution factors; 

- A list of the main execution venues used by the firm; 

- A clear and prominent warning that any specific instructions from the client may prevent the firm from taking the steps that it would otherwise have taken under its order execution policy to obtain the best possible result for the client. 

Where the order execution policy provides for the possibility that client orders may be executed outside a regulated market, an MTF or an OTF, the firm must inform its clients about this possibility. 

A firm must obtain the prior consent of its clients to its order execution policy. 

A firm must obtain the prior express consent of its clients before executing their orders outside a regulated market, an MTF or an OTF. This may be by means of a general agreement or transaction-by-transaction. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

A firm must regularly monitor the effectiveness of its order execution arrangements and execution policy, and it must review them annually. 

A firm must be able to demonstrate to its clients, at their request, that it has executed their orders in accordance with its execution policy. 

- **A5.17** What are the requirements in respect of a client limit order that is not immediately executed? _(2 marks)_ 

Where a client places a limit order in respect of shares traded in a regulated market and the order is not immediately executed under prevailing market conditions, then, unless the client expressly instructs otherwise, the firm must make the order public with a view to having it executed as soon as possible. The firm can do this by transmitting the order to a regulated market, an MTF or an OTF that operates an order book trading system. 

This obligation to make public a limit order will not apply to a limit order that is large in scale compared with normal market size (large in scale for this purpose is a size greater than approximately 10 per cent of the share’s average daily turnover). 

**A5.18** What information must be included in a periodic statement provided by a firm to a retail client? _(3 marks)_ 

The information to be included in a periodic statement comprises: 

- Name of firm 

- Name of retail client’s account 

- Statement of the contents and the valuation of the portfolio, including: 

   - Market value (or fair value) of each investment 

   - Cash balance at the beginning and end of the reporting period 

   - Performance of the portfolio during the reporting period 

- Total fees and charges 

- Comparison of performance with benchmark (if any) 

- Total of dividends and interest received 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

   - Information about corporate actions (e.g. bonus issues) 

   - Relevant trade confirmation information (if not provided on a transaction-bytransaction basis) 

- **A5.19** Identify four of the ways in which conflicts of interest can be dealt with under the Conduct of Business Rules (COBS) contained in the FCA’s Handbook. _(4 marks)_ 

A firm must manage conflicts of interest fairly between itself and customers and between customers under PRIN 8. 

Conflicts of interest can generally be dealt with either through Disclosure, Declining to act, Chinese Walls or maintaining an Independence policy. Special rules apply with regard to common platform firms under SYSC 10 and investment research under COBS 12. Disclosure is now only to be used as a measure of last resort under SYSC 10. 

- **A5.20** Explain the two different types of professional client covered by the Conduct of Business 

Rules (COBS) contained in the FCA’s Handbook of Rules and Guidance. 

_(2 marks)_ 

## A client may either a **per se professional client** or an **elective professional client** 

(under COBS 3.5.1 R). 

A **per se professional client** includes any of the following (unless it is an eligible counterparty or is given a different categorisation under the chapter): 

- 1) an entity required to be authorised or regulated to operate in the financial markets; 

- 2) in relation to MiFID or equivalent third country business a large undertaking meeting two of the following size requirements on a company basis: (a) balance sheet total of EUR 20,000,000; (b) net turnover of EUR 40,000,000; and (c) own funds of EUR 2,000,000; 

- 3) in relation to business that is not MiFID or equivalent third country business a large undertaking meeting certain conditions; 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- 4) a national or regional government, a public body that manages public debt, a central bank, an international or supranational institution (such as the World Bank, the IMF, the ECP, the EIB) or another similar international organisation; or 

- 5) another institutional investor whose main activity is to invest in financial instruments (in relation to the firm's MiFID or equivalent third country business) or designated investments (in relation to the firm's other business) including securitisation of assets or other financing transactions entities. 

## **Elective Professional Client** 

A firm may treat a client as an **elective professional client** if it falls under 1) and 3) or, where applicable, 2): 

- 1) the firm undertakes an adequate assessment of the expertise, experience and knowledge of the client that gives reasonable assurance, in light of the nature of the transactions or services envisaged, that the client is capable of making his own investment decisions and understanding the risks involved **(the ‘qualitative test’)** ; 

- 2) in relation to MiFID or equivalent third country business in the course of that assessment, at least two of the following criteria are satisfied: a) the client has carried out transactions, in significant size, on the relevant market at an average frequency of 10 per quarter over the previous four quarters; b) the size of the client's financial instrument portfolio, defined as including cash deposits and financial instruments, exceeds EUR 500,000; or c) the client works or has worked in the financial sector for at least one year in a professional position, which requires knowledge of the transactions or services envisaged **(the ‘quantitative test’)** ; or 

- 3) the following procedure is complied with: a) the client must state in writing to the firm that it wishes to be treated as a professional client either generally or in respect of a particular service or transaction or type of transaction or product; b) the firm must give the client a clear written warning of the protections and investor compensation rights the client may lose; and c) the client must state in writing, in a separate document from the contract, that it is aware of the consequences of losing such protections. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A5.21** List four ways in which a transaction may be considered unsuitable for a client under COBS 9. _(2 marks)_ 

The following transactions would be considered unsuitable for a client: 

- a) A transaction may be unsuitable for a client because of the **risks** of the designated investments involved, the **type** of transaction, the **characteristics** of the order or the **frequency** of the trading. 

- b) In the case of managing investments, a transaction might also be unsuitable if it would result in an **unsuitable portfolio** (COBS 9.3.1). 

Reference may also be to the following to the extent necessary: 

   - a) **Churning and switching –** frequency and agreed investment strategy (COBS 9.3.2); 

   - b) **Income withdrawals and short-term annuities –** all the relevant circumstances (COBS 9.3.3); 

   - c) **Loans and mortgages** - suitability of the overall transaction (COBS 9.3.2). 

- **A5.22** List the three main types of information that must be obtained to comply with the suitability requirement imposed under COBS 9 of the FCA Handbook. _(3 marks)_ 

To comply with the suitability obligation, firms must obtain information with regard to: 

- a) Knowledge and experience in the investment field; 

- b) Investment objectives; 

- c) Financial situation. 

One mark for each correct response up to three marks. 

**A5.23** List six of the sourcebooks contained in the Business Standards section of the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(3 marks)_ 

The sourcebooks contained the Business Standards section of the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance consist of: 

- a) Conduct of Business sourcebook (COBS); 

- b) Insurance: Conduct of Business sourcebook (ICOBS); 

- c) Mortgages and Home Finance: Conduct of Business sourcebook (MCOB); 

- d) Banking: Conduct of Business sourcebook (BCOBS); 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

e) Client Assets (CASS); 

f) Market Conduct (MAR); 

g) Product Intervention and Product Governance sourcebook (PROD). 

**A5.24** State the three principal client categories under the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) Handbook and PRA Rulebook. _(3 marks)_ 

MiFID introduced three classes of client categories: 

- (a) Eligible counterparty and either per se or elective eligible counterparties; 

- (b) Professional clients and per se or elective professional clients; and 

- (c) Retail clients. 

**A5.25** What information and content needs to be provided within a suitability report when providing a client with investment advice? _(3 marks)_ 

The suitability report is a central procedure in ensuring appropriate advice is provided to clients. Briefly this report should include details of the client’s investment objectives, knowledge, education, profession, experience of investing and the risk tolerance they can bear and be consistent with their investment objectives. 

- _[Tutor note: This is all the examiner’s report requires for a ‘strong answer’.]_ 

**A5.26** Outline three circumstances where a conflict of interest may arise in the provision of financial services. _(3 marks)_ 

Conflicts of interest in financial services have significant regulatory implications. The workbook defines such conflicts of interest and they include situations where a person: 

- is likely to make a financial gain, or avoid a financial loss, at the expense of the client, 

- has an interest in the outcome of a service provided to the client, 

- has a financial, or other incentive to favour the interest of another client(s) over the interest of the client, 

- carries on the same business as the client, 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

• receives, or will receive, from a person other than the client, an inducement in relation to a service provided to the client. 

**A5.27** How should a firm charge for investment advice provided to retail customers? 

_(2 marks)_ 

• Advisers must charge consumers directly for the advice and services they provide. They cannot be remunerated by the product provider for making personal recommendations to consumers. The fee must be agreed upon between the adviser and the consumer prior to the service being provided. Firms must disclose in writing to retail clients the nature of the advice service, whether it is independent or restricted. If providing restricted advice, the firm must explain the nature of the restriction. This disclosure must be made in good time before providing personal recommendations or basic advice related to retail investment products. 

• The charging structure should improve the clarity of how firms describe their services to consumers, address potential distortions in consumer outcomes due to adviser remuneration, and increase the professional standards of advisers. This includes the total price, related fees, commissions, charges, expenses, and any taxes payable via the firm. If costs cannot be indicated at the time, the basis on which they will be calculated should be provided to the client so they can verify them. 

**A5.28** Explain what a ‘key investor information document’ (KIID) is and the information that must be included within it. _(3 marks)_ 

A key investor information document (KIID) needs to be issued for each UCITS or mutual fund (Undertakings for the Collective Investment in Transferable Securities) scheme managed by an authorised fund manager (AFM). These documents should include the essential characteristics of the UCITS scheme. These documents should ensure investors understand the risks of the investment product and are able to make investment decisions on an informed basis. This requires that the KIID is fair, clear, not misleading and consistent with relevant parts of the prospectus. The KIID must include: 

- identification of the UCITS scheme 

- a short description of its investment objectives and investment policy 

- past performance or, where relevant, performance scenarios 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- costs and associated charges, and the risk/reward profile of the investment 

- appropriate guidance in relation to the risks associated with investments in the scheme. 

**A5.29** What individual requirements must an investment adviser meet in order to be qualified to provide regulated investment advice to retail customers? _(4 marks)_ 

To provide advice to a retail customer, an investment adviser must possess a valid statement of professional standing (SPS) from an FCA-accredited body. These statements of professional standing confirm that an adviser: 

- has successfully completed a Level 4 (or above) qualification listed in the FCA Appropriate Qualification Tables; 

- has completed 35 hours of CPD annually; 

- be deemed to be ‘fit and proper’ in accordance with the FCA’s APER (Approved Persons in 

Appointed Representatives) and/or the SM&CR (Senior Management and Certification Regime); 

- and has agreed to adhere to a code of ethics. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **6. Client assets** 

- **A6.1** Describe the main requirements that apply to a firm becoming authorised to provide custody services. _(3 marks)_ 

Custody is provided for under CASS. The custody rules generally require that assets are held safely and securely. 

General rules apply with regard to the following: 

   - a) segregation of safe custody investment from designated investments; 

   - b) registration and recording title in the name of the client, a nominee company, a custodian, the firm or another person subject to the specified rules; 

   - c) firms must hold documents of title to safe custody investments either in the possession of the firm or custodian, the firm or another person subject to specified rules; 

   - d) relevant terms and conditions must be set out in an appropriate client agreement; 

   - e) appropriate risk disclosures must be made; 

   - f) comply with restrictions applicable with regard to stock lending; 

   - g) appropriate reconciliations must be made. 

- **A6.2** The FCA’s client money and assets rules require that client money is segregated from a firm’s assets. Provide three exceptions to this general rule. _(3 marks)_ 

Segregation exceptions include: 

- a) Opt outs by professional clients (for non-MiFID business) or eligible counterparties subject to appropriate disclosure and documentation; 

- b) Money due and payable to the firm (such as fees and commissions); 

- c) Money from an affiliated company; 

- d) Money paid in connection with a delivery versus payment transaction (DVP); and 

- e) Money held by an approved bank on behalf of a client in an account with itself. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A6.3** Explain the purpose of the ‘client money’ rules. _(2 marks)_ 

Principle 10 (clients’ assets) requires a firm to arrange adequate protection for clients’ assets when the firm is responsible for them. An essential part of that protection is the proper accounting and handling of client money The rules are designed to restrict the comingling of clients’ and firms’ money, to minimise the risk of clients’ money being treated as the firm’s assets in the event of the insolvency of the firm and to minimise the risk of clients’ money being used without the consent of or against the wishes of the clients. 

- **A6.4** Briefly explain the parts of the CASS sourcebook within the FCA's Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(3 marks)_ 

CASS is the Client Assets sourcebook which contains the requirements concerning the holding of client assets and client money in the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. This includes specific provision with regard to Collateral (CASS 3), Client money: insurance mediation activity (CASS 5), Custody rules (CASS 6), Client money rules (CASS 7), Client money distribution (CASS 7A) and Mandates (CASS 8). 

- **A6.5** Explain the general purpose and content of the CASS sourcebook within the FCA's Handbook. _(3 marks)_ 

CASS is the Client Assets sourcebook which contains the requirements concerning the holding of client assets and client money in the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. This includes specific provision with regard to Application and Classification (CASS 1 & 1A), Collateral (CASS 3), Client Money: Insurance Mediation Activity (CASS 5), Custody Rules (CASS 6), Client Money Rules (CASS 7), Client Money distribution (CASS 7A), Mandates (CASS 8), Information to Clients (CASS 9), Cass Resolution Pack (CASS 110), Debt Management Client Money (CASS 11), Commodities Futures Trading Commission Part 30 Exemption Order (CASS 12) and Claims Management: Client Money (CASS 13). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **7. Market conduct** 

**A7.1** List any six types of conduct that may constitute market abuse under the UK Market Abuse Regulation (MAR). _(3 marks)_ 

Types of market abuse behaviour prohibited by MAR are: 

   - (a) engaging and attempting to engage in insider dealing (MAR Art 8(1)) 

   - (b) recommending that another engages in insider dealing (MAR Art 8(2)) 

   - (c) unlawfully disclosing inside information (MAR Art 10(1)) 

   - (d) engaging or attempting to engage in market manipulation which includes: 

   - (i) false or misleading behaviour 

   - (ii) securing the price of financial instruments at an abnormal or artificial level 

   - (iii) deceptive behaviour 

   - (iv) disseminating false or misleading information through media (e.g. the Internet) 

   - (v) benchmark manipulation (MAR Art 12(1)). 

- **A7.2** Specify the penalties that apply for breach of the prohibition on market abuse in the UK Market Abuse Regulation (MAR). _(3 marks)_ 

In the event of breach of MAR, the FCA may: 

- impose an unlimited fine or financial penalty (s 123(1) FSMA); 

- publish a public censure (s 123(3)); 

- apply for an injunction (s 381); or 

- apply for a restitution order (s 383). 

The FCA can also commence other disciplinary proceedings for breach of the High Level Principles or other provisions in the Handbook. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A7.3** Identify **six** pieces of information which need to be included in a transaction report for the purpose of market surveillance. _(3 marks)_ 

The question asks for the information requirements outlined for transactions reporting within the FCA’s Supervision sourcebook. The specific information required is for the case of market surveillance. A wide range of information requirements (reported in the workbook) can be included in such an answer including those listed below. 

- a)  reporting firm’s identity – legal entity identifier (LEI) 

- b)  trading venue identifier code 

- c)  executing entity identifier code 

- d)  trading date and time 

- e)  whether the transaction is a buy/sell 

- f)  instrument identification code (name and ISIN of the instrument) 

- g)  trading capacity 

- h)  quantity – number of shares/nominal/amount of stock purchased/sold 

- i)  settlement date 

- j)  identity of the counterparty (other party to the transaction) 

- k)  price and currency 

- l)  unique transaction identifier 

- m)  buyer or seller/decision-maker details and identification code 

**A7.4** Explain what is meant by a market sounding in the context of the UK Market Abuse Regulation (MAR). _(3 marks)_ 

A market sounding comprises the communication of information, prior to the announcement of a transaction, in order to gauge the interest of potential investors in a possible transaction and the conditions relating to it such as its potential size or pricing, to one or more potential investors by: 

a) an issuer; 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

   - b) a secondary offeror of a financial instrument, in such quantity or value that the transaction is distinct from ordinary trading and involves a selling method based on the prior assessment of potential interest from potential investors; 

   - c) an emission allowance market participant; or 

   - d) a third party acting on behalf or on the account of any person referred to above. 

- **A7.5** Outline the principal exemptions available for breach of the provisions on market abuse under FSMA. _(3 marks)_ 

The FSMA contains two due diligence defences to the imposition of a financial penalty of: a) believing, on reasonable grounds, that the behaviour did not amount to market abuse; and 

b) taking all reasonable precautions and exercising all due diligence to avoid market abuse (s 123). 

Additionally, safe harbours include: 

   - a) Acting in conformity with the FCA’s rules on buy-back programmes and stabilisation; 

   - b) Acting in conformity with the FCA’s rules on Chinese walls; 

   - c) Disclosures in accordance with the FCA Primary Market’s Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules (DTR); 

   - d) Disclosures in accordance with the Takeover Code’s rules on disclosure during a takeover bid; 

   - e) Market soundings. 

- **A7.6** Explain the general purpose and content of sections 89 and 90 of the Financial Services Act 2012 on misleading statements. _(3 marks)_ 

The Financial Services Act (FSA) 2012 created two offences of making misleading statements (s 89) and misleading impressions (s 90). 

s89 applies with regard to the making of a statement with regard to a relevant agreement that is known to be false or misleading in a material respect or being reckless as to whether 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

it is or not or dishonestly concealing any material facts in connection with the statement or otherwise. Specifically, section 89 of FSA 2012 makes it a criminal offence to engage in market manipulation through: 

- a) Knowingly making a statement that is false or misleading; 

- b) Dishonestly concealing any material facts; 

- c) Recklessly (dishonestly or otherwise) making a false or misleading statement. 

s90 applies where a person acts or engages in any course of conduct that creates a false or misleading impression as to the market or the price or value of a relevant investment and intended to create the impression to induce another person to acquire, dispose of, subscribe for or underwrite an investment (or refrain from doing so) or knew that the impression was false or misleading or was reckless as to whether it was or not. 

A person guilty of an offence is liable on summary conviction for up to 12 months imprisonment or for up to ten years on indictment or a fine or both (s 92). 

**A7.7** Define and explain what is meant by the term ‘insider dealing’ _. (3 marks)_ 

Insider dealing involves the use of ‘inside’ or privileged information to trade for profit. Such inside information would include non-public information pertaining directly or indirectly to a financial asset or which may be useful to someone trading: i.e., the information would likely influence the price of the financial asset. There are many types of inside information, be this client trades, or information as to an upcoming corporate transaction. Morally such use of information is a form of theft. Under UK Market Abuse Regulation insider trading is when an individual possesses such inside information and uses this or facilitates (or recommends to) a third party using this information for trading, or limiting, cancelling or altering trading activities. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A 7.8** Provide **three** examples of activities which would be considered market manipulation behaviours. _(3 marks)_ 

The UK Market Abuse Regulation (UK MAR) provides a number of different situations in which market manipulation is believed to exist. These include and are defined under the MAR legislation as the following. 

- A person or persons acting in collaboration, to secure a dominant position over the supply of or demand for a financial instrument. 

• Buying or selling of financial instruments, at the opening or closing of the market, which has or is likely to have the effect of misleading investors acting on the basis of the prices displayed. 

• The placing of orders to a trading venue, including cancellation and/or modification, by any available means of trading, including by electronic means, such as algorithmic and highfrequency trading strategies having the effects referred to above or by: 

- Making it more difficult for other persons to identify genuine orders on the trading 

- system of a trading venue. 

- Creating or being likely to create a false or misleading signal about the supply of, 

- or demand for, or price of, a financial instrument. 

• Taking advantage of occasional or regular access to electronic media by voicing an opinion about a financial instrument (while having a position in that financial instrument) and profiting from the impact of the price of that instrument without having disclosed the conflict of interest to the public in a proper and effective way. 

**A7.9** What is meant by the term ‘insider information’? 

_(3 marks)_ 

Broadly, the _Market Abuse Regulation_ (MAR) defines inside information as information of a precise nature which: 

- has not been made public; 

- directly or indirectly relates to one or more issuers, or to one or more financial instruments; and 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- if it were made public, would be likely to have a significant effect on the prices of those financial instruments, or on the price of related derivative financial instruments. 

MAR also sets out specific definitions for inside information for commodity derivatives and emission allowances. (See MAR, Article 7 (Inside Information), Article 7(1)(b) for commodity derivatives and Article 7(1)(c) for emission allowances). There are only a few limited and highly regulated circumstances where insider information may be used legally, such as for market sounding. 

**A7.10** Explain what is meant by a ‘market sounding’. _(2 marks)_ 

A market ‘sounding’ is where a firm is hired by an issuer that wishes to raise market funds, yet initially wishes to identify if investors have an appetite for such an issue. This would also include discovering the possible interest rate or price of this fundraising. A sounding can include the disclosure of ‘non-public’ information which is sensitive and considered to be ‘insider information’. 

Subsequently the disclosing firm must comply with the following obligations: 

- Obtain the consent of the person receiving the market sounding to receive the inside information. 

- Inform the person receiving the market sounding that they are prohibited from using that information for acquiring or disposing of financial instruments. 

- Ensure the confidentiality of the information. 

- Maintain records of all information given and to whom. 

- Advise the recipients of the sounding when the information provided ceases to be 

considered as inside information. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **8. Enforcement and discipline** 

- **A8.1** List six supervisory tools set out in SUP within the FCA’s Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(3 marks)_ 

The main supervisory tools set out in SUP include: 

- desk based reviews; 

- liaison with other agencies and regulators; 

- management meetings or representative meetings; 

- on-site inspections; 

- reviews and analysis of periodic returns and notifications; 

- past business reviews; 

- transaction monitoring; and 

- use of auditors and skilled persons. 

Specific risks may also be dealt with through a number of additional tools including: 

   - making recommendations for preventative or remedial action; 

   - providing individual guidance; 

   - imposing requirements; or 

   - varying a firm’s permission including cancellation. 

- **A8.2** List six of the redress or enforcement options available to the FCA under the FSMA against regulated institutions for breach of the FSMA or FCA rules. _(3 marks)_ 

Disciplinary powers include: 

- a) Fines; 

- b) Private or public censure; 

- c) Injunction; 

- d) Varying or withdrawing approval; 

- e) Varying or cancelling permission; 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

   - f) Information gathering; 

   - g) Investigations; 

   - h) Reports by skilled persons; 

   - i)   Restitution; 

   - j)   Bankruptcy or insolvency. 

- **A8.3** Name four types of administrative sanction against a firm available to the FCA under FSMA. _(2 marks)_ 

The FCA may exercise any of the following administrative sanctions against a firm: 

   - a) Fine; 

   - b) Public censure; 

   - c) Variation, refusal or restriction of permission; 

   - d) Restitution order. 

- **A8.4** Explain the purpose of a warning notice, a decision notice, a final notice and a supervisory notice under the Decision Procedure and Penalties Manual (DEPP). _(4 marks)_ 

The purpose of the notices is set out in DEPP: 

- a) Warning Notice – This provides the recipient with details about action the FCA/RDC proposes to take and the recipient’s right to make representations. 

- b) Decision Notice – This provides the recipient with details about action the FCA/RDC has decided to take subject to the RDC complying with its formal procedures. 

- c) Final Notice – This confirms the terms of the action that the FCA/RDC has decided to take and the date from which it takes effect. 

- d) Supervisory Notice – This provides the recipient with details about action the FCA/RDC has taken or proposes to take generally with immediate effect and before any formal procedures have been taken. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A8.5** Explain the role of the Regulatory Decisions Committee (RDC). 

## _(3 marks)_ 

When an investigation is launched by the FCA, the process is administered by the RDC. The RDC is accountable to the board of the FCA, but it is outside the FCA management structure. RDC members represent the public interest and are drawn from practitioners and non-practitioners. The only FCA employee is the chairman of the RDC. 

The FCA will normally refer more significant disciplinary matters to the RDC. Thus, the RDC will usually take the final decision on the following actions: 

   - To refuse, restrict or modify a Part 4A permission application; 

   - To refuse an application for approved person status; 

   - To make a prohibition order; 

   - To exercise the FCA’s powers to impose financial penalties or public censure; 

   - To seek a court order against any person. 

- **A8.6** Explain the general purpose and outline content of the DEPP Manual within the FCA’s Handbook of Rules and Guidance. _(4 marks)_ 

The purpose of the FCA’s Decision Procedure and Penalties manual (DEPP) is to set out the procedures for issuing decisions with regard to statutory notice, the FCA's policy on the imposition and amount of penalties and the conduct of interviews under s 169(7) FSMA. The FCA enforcement powers are set to under Part XI FSMA and DEPP. 

The provisions of DEPP generally apply to: 

|a) Application and purpose|DEPP 1|
|---|---|
|b) Statutory notices|DEPP 2|
|c) Nature and procedure of the Regulatory Decisions||
|Committee (RDC)|DEPP 3|
|d) Decisions by FCA staff under executive procedures|DEPP 4|
|e) Settlement decision procedure|DEPP 5|
|f) Penalties|DEPP 6|
|g) Power to impose a suspension or restriction|DEPP 6A|
|h) Statement of policy on interviews conducted on||



**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

behalf of overseas and EEA regulators DEPP 7 i) Variation of SMF managers’ approval DEPP 8 

**A8.7** Outline how appeals are conducted against regulatory decisions under the FSMA as amended. _(3 marks)_ 

The question may have been answered either in terms of appeals against individual decisions or appeals against the conduct of the regulatory authorities. 

Hearings and appeals against regulatory decisions are dealt with under Part 9 FSMA as amended under s 23 FSA 2012. Appeals are managed under the Upper Tribunal (under the transfer of Tribunal Functions Order 2010 SI 2010/22). This applies with regard to decisions by the regulators, the Bank of England or in respect of compensation orders under the Banking (Special Provisions) Act 2000 or Banking Act 2009. 

Part 9 FSMA contains specific provisions on proceedings, decisions and supervisory notices, offences as well as legal assistance and an appeal on a point of law (ss 132-137). 

Appeals against regulatory authorities are dealt with under Part 6 Financial Services Act 2012. The authorities are required to make arrangements for the investigation of complaints in connection with the exercise of relevant functions with an independent investigator being responsible for conducting the investigations under the complaints scheme (s 84 FSA 2012). The FSA 2012 contains further specific provisions on relevant functions, consultation and publication of the complaints scheme, the investigation of complaints, with the investigator being exempt from liability in damages (ss 84 FSA 2012). 

**A8.8** Explain the principal features of the FCA’s Firm Systematic Framework (FSF). 

_(3 marks)_ 

The FCA’s Firm Systematic Framework (FSF) is provided for under SUP1A.3.4 with the FCA Supervision Model being based on the three pillars of the FSF, Event Driven Work and Issues and Products. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

Purpose of the FSF is to assess whether a firm is, currently and prospectively, being run in a manner that results in the fair treatment of customers, minimises risks to market integrity and does not impede effective competition. The FCA asks whether the firm has the interests of customers and the integrity of the market at the core of the way its business is run. 

The FSF will identify and mitigate the key drivers of conduct behaviour and ensure that firms behave in a way that minimises the risk that they present to the FCA’s objectives. 

**A8.9** Describe and explain the appeals process following receipt of a decision notice by the FCA. _(3 marks)_ 

Any person receiving a decision or supervisory notice from the FCA has the right to appeal this decision. This involves a referral to the Upper Tribunal (or the Tax and Chancery Chamber of the Upper Tribunal). The Upper Tribunal is independent of the FCA and is appointed by the government’s Ministry. 

This appeal must be undertaken in the 28-day grace period provided by the regulator after the notice has been issued. During this period the FCA cannot take the action it has proposed. 

An appeal at the Upper Tribunal involves a rehearing of the FCA’s decision and can include new evidence that was not available to the FCA at the time. The Tribunal’s decision is binding on the FCA. 

**A8.10** Briefly describe the FCA’s approach to enforcement action. _(3 marks)_ 

The FCA follows a proportionate and risk-based approach to enforcement, which includes credible deterrence by ensuring firms and individuals comply with regulations through disciplinary action. It also emphasises restorative measures by encouraging remediation and cooperation from firms. In addition, the FCA promotes transparency by publicising enforcement actions to maintain market confidence. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **9. Complaints and compensation** 

**A9.1** Explain the difference between the Ombudsman Scheme and the Financial Services Compensation Scheme under the FSMA. _(2 marks)_ 

Both the Ombudsman Scheme and the Financial Services Compensation Scheme are provided for under FSMA (Parts XVI and XV). The objective of each is to provide a single integrated cross sector support mechanism for clients of regulated firms. 

The purpose of the Ombudsman Scheme is to provide a mechanism for clients to make complaints concerning the conduct of regulated firms to an independent party. (This is distinct from judicial review with claims being referred to the Upper Tribunal (Tax and Chancery Chamber) under Part IX of the FSMA.) The relevant provisions are now set out within the DISP section of the FCA Handbook. 

The purpose of the compensation scheme is to make payment to former clients of regulated firms after the firm has failed and been closed down. This replaces three earlier compensation schemes maintained in the banking, securities and insurance areas. (In practice, the earlier three funds are now operated as sub-funds within the new single scheme arrangements.) The relevant provisions are now set out within the COMP section of the FCA Handbook. 

**A9.2** Specify the main financial limits on compensation that may be payable to customers in connection with deposit taking and investment business. _(4 marks)_ 

The compensation limits set out in COMP are as follows: 

- a) Deposits (£120,000); 

- b) Investments (£85,000); 

- c)  Long term insurance (100% of value attributed to policy); 

- d) General insurance (100% of valid claim (compulsory) and 90% of valid claim (noncompulsory)). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A9.3** Outline the time limits that apply in handling client complaints under DISP. _(3 marks)_ 

The timetables set out in DISP are as follows: 

- a) Acknowledgement of receipt – prompt notice (formerly five days); 

- c) Holding or Final response – eight weeks. 

- d) Notice of 6 months time limit to refer to FOS. 

Records of complaints and of the measures taken for their resolution must be retained for: 

a) Five years, for MiFID business 

- b) Three years, for other complaints 

**A9.4** Explain the role and function of the Office of Complaints Commissioner. _(4 marks)_ 

The FCA and PRA are required under Part 6 of the Financial Services Act 2012 to maintain a complaints scheme for the investigation of complaints arising in connection with the exercise of, or failure to exercise, any of their relevant functions and to appoint an independent person (referred to as the Complaints Commissioner) to be responsible for the conduct of investigations in accordance with the complaints scheme (the Scheme) (http://www.fscc.gov.uk/). The Scheme came into effect on 1 April 2013. 

The Complaints Commissioner considers grievances against the FCA, PRA, the Payment System Regulator and the Bank of England’s oversight of clearing houses and settlement systems. Complaints about financial firms rather than one of the regulators, should be referred to the company concerned and then the Financial Ombudsman Service. 

Complaints can be made by anyone who is directly affected by the way in which the regulators have carried out their functions, or anyone acting directly on such a person’s behalf, provided that the complaint meets the requirements of the Scheme. To be eligible to make a complaint under the Scheme, a person must be seeking a remedy (which for this purpose may include an apology) in respect of some inconvenience, distress or loss which the person has suffered as a result of being directly affected by the regulators’ actions or inaction (http://fscc.gov.uk/complaints-scheme/). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

Complaints should be made within 12 months of the date on which the complainant first became aware of the circumstances giving rise to the complaint. Complaints made later than this will be investigated under the Scheme only if the complainant can show reasonable grounds for the delay (http://fscc.gov.uk/complaints-scheme/). 

**A9.5** Explain the role and function of the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). _(4 marks)_ 

The purpose of the Ombudsman scheme under the FSMA is to provide for the resolution of ‘certain disputes’ quickly and with minimum formality by an independent person (s 225(1)). The scheme is to be administered by a body corporate with further direction being provided with regard to jurisdiction and operation in Part XVI and Schedule 17 FSMA. 

Complainants should generally use the financial institution’s internal scheme first before making a separate reference to the Ombudsman. The Ombudsman may direct financial institutions to take any necessary action or make financial awards up to £355,000. 

The Ombudsman scheme is generally dealt with under the Dispute Resolution: Complaints sourcebook (DISP) with compensation under COMP within the Redress block of the FCA Handbook. 

**A9.6** Identify the three sourcebooks within the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance governing Redress. ( _3 marks_ ) 

The three sourcebooks within the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance governing Redress consist of: 

- a) DISP Dispute Resolution: Complaints 

- b) CONRED Consumer Redress Schemes sourcebook 

- c) COMP Compensation 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- **A9.7** List three principle sets of limits payable under the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). _(3 marks)_ 

   - The limits on **deposit claims** are **100** % of up to **£120,000** per person per firm and **£240,000** on joint accounts (from 1 December 2025). 

   - The limit for **mortgages (mortgage advice and arranging)** is **£85,000** (from 1 April 2019). 

   - The limit for **debt management** is **£85,000** (from 1 April 2019). 

   - The limit on **investments is £85,000** per person per firm (for claims against firms declared in default from 1 April 2019). 

   - The limit on **compulsory insurance, long-term insurance, professional indemnity insurance and dealt or incapacity is 100% and 90% on all other claims from 8 October 2020.** 

The limits are set out on http://www.fscs.org.uk/what-we-cover/. 

**A9.8** Describe the purpose of the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). _(2 marks)_ 

The FSCS is designed to protect consumers by providing compensation when authorised financial firms fail. One of its main functions is depositor protection, offering up to £120,000 per person, per firm if a bank or building society collapses. It also covers investment protection, compensating individuals up to £85,000 for losses caused by the insolvency of an authorised investment firm, helping to maintain trust in the financial system. In addition, the FSCS provides insurance and mortgage protection, ensuring policyholders and mortgage customers are compensated if their insurer or provider fails, safeguarding them from significant financial harm. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **10. Takeover Code and Listing Rules** 

**A10.1** List the general principles (in summary) contained in the Takeover Panel’s Takeover Code. 

_(3 marks)_ 

There are six general principles, as follows; 

- All holders of the securities of an offeree company of the same class must be afforded equivalent treatment; moreover, if a person acquires control of a company, the other holders of securities must be protected. 

- The holders of the securities of an offeree company must have sufficient time and information to enable them to reach a properly informed decision on the bid; where it advises the holders of securities, the board of the offeree company must give its views on the effects of implementation of the bid on employment, conditions of employment and the locations of the company’s places of business. 

- The board of the offeree company must act in the interests of the company as a whole and must not deny the holders of securities the opportunity to decide on the merits of the bid. 

- False markets must not be created in the securities of the offeree company, of the offeror company or any other company concerned by the bid in such a way that the rise or fall of the prices of the securities becomes artificial and the normal functioning of the markets is distorted. 

- An offeror must announce a bid only after ensuring that he/she can fulfil in full any cash consideration, if such is offered, and after taking all reasonable measures to secure the implementation of any other type of consideration. 

- An offeree company must not be hindered in the conduct of its affairs for longer than is reasonable by a bid for its securities. 

**A10.2** Outline two of the listing principles firms are required to follow when listing on the London Stock Exchange (LSE). _(2 marks)_ 

To list a firm on the London Stock Exchange it is expected that a firm follows a wide range of different principles. These are established in the Listing Rules Chapter 7 (LR7) and are enforceable by the UK regulator. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

Listing principles include: 

   - A listed company must establish and maintain adequate procedures, systems and controls to comply with its UKLR obligations 

   - A listed company must take reasonable steps to enable its directors to understand their responsibilities and obligations as directors. 

   - A listed company must deal with the FCA in an open and cooperative manner. 

   - A listed company must act with integrity towards the holders and potential holders of its premium listed securities. 

   - A listed company must ensure that it treats all holders of the same class of its listed equity shares that are in the same position equally in respect of the rights attaching to such listed equity shares. 

   - A listed company must communicate information to holders and potential holders of its listed equity shares in such a way as to avoid the creation or continuation of a false market in such listed equity shares. 

- **A10.3** Explain the principal functions of the FCA as the competent authority for the listing of securities (‘Primary Markets’) under FSMA 2000. _(4 marks)_ 

The FCA is appointed the UK Listing Authority under s72 FSMA, and has recently started referring to itself as ‘FCA Primary Markets’ in this role. The FCA is required to maintain the ‘Official List’ under s74 FSMA and in so doing to issue the ‘Listing Rules’. Penalties may be imposed for breach of the Listing Rules under s91 FSMA with a supporting ‘Statement of Policy’ being issued by the FCA under s93 FSMA. 

The general functions of the FCA with regard to listing are set out in s 73(2): 

- issue rules with regard to listing under Part 6; 

- issue general guidance with regard to listing; 

- determine the general policy and principles governing the listing regime. 

The FCA is separately required to maintain the official list (s 70(1)) with applications for 

listing only being accepted where they satisfy the listing rules or any other requirements imposed (s 75(4)). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

The former FSA replaced the London Stock Exchange as the UK Listing Authority following the LSE’s demutualisation in the early 2000s. The Official List is a list of securities that may be offered to the general public subject to compliance with the relevant requirements with regard to content and disclosure. 

Admission to listing is distinct from admission to trading which is concerned with entry to a specific market or exchange. 

- **A10.4** Identify the three sourcebooks within the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance governing Listing and their general purpose or content. _(3 marks)_ 

The three sourcebooks within the Handbook governing Listing and their general purposes are: 

- a) Listing Rules (LR) – conditions for issuers seeking a listing on the official list of the LSE ; 

- b) Prospectus Regulation Rules (PRR) – conditions for and contents of prospectuses where offers of securities are to be made to the public; 

- c) Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules (DTR) - controls on inside information and the timely disclosure of price sensitive information to the market as well as notifications of holdings in a company's shares. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **11. Money laundering and other financial crime** 

**A11.1** Outline four of the principles which shape how a firm limits issues of bribery. 

_(4 marks)_ 

A range of principles are proposed in the UK to tackle the issue of bribery. Key legislation in this includes, for example, the UK Bribery Act 2010. The principles include the following from the workbook. 

- Principle 1 Proportionate procedures. The procedures to prevent bribery are 

- proportionate to the risk of bribery within the organisation. 

- Principle 2 Top-level commitment. The upper echelon management needs to be 

- committed to preventing bribery throughout the organisation. A culture where bribery is unacceptable needs to be developed. 

- Principle 3 Risk assessment. A periodic, documented and informed assessment of 

- the internal and external risks posed by bribery needs to be undertaken within the organisation. 

• Principle 4 Due diligence. The organisation needs to undertake diligent procedures to limit identified bribery risks. 

• Principle 5 Communication (including training). Policies and procedures to limit bribery need to be developed and communicated within the organisation. This may involve the training of staff. 

• Principle 6 Monitoring and review. It is expected the firm will monitor and review its policies and procedures to limit the incidence of bribery. 

Other appropriate material can be included in an answer and can also be acknowledged. 

**A11.2** How is terrorism defined in the Terrorism Act 2000? _(3 marks)_ 

The definition of terrorism covers the use or threat of use of ‘action’ which is: 

- Designed to influence the government (UK or overseas) or to intimidate the public (UK or overseas); and 

- Made for the purpose of advancing a political or religious or ideological cause. 

‘Action’ is relevant for the offence if it: 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

- Involves serious violence against a person.
