- Involves serious damage to property. 

- Endangers a person’s life. 

- Creates a serious risk to the health and safety of the public; or 

- Is designed to seriously interfere with or disrupt an electronic system. 

**A11.3** Explain the role and function of the Money Laundering Reporting Officer (MLRO) within a regulated institution. _(2 marks)_ 

The MLRO is a controlled function and the person is a senior manager function. Firms (other than sole traders) are required to appoint an MLRO to oversee compliance with the FCA rules on systems and controls against money laundering, with the MLRO having the necessary authority, independence and ability to access sufficient resources and information, in order to allow the MLRO to carry out their responsibility (SYSC 6.3.9). 

The functions of the MLRO can be listed as follows: 

a) to receive reports of transactions giving rise to suspicious transactions; 

b) to determine whether a suspicious transaction report should be filed; and c) to make a suspicious transaction report to NCA in appropriate cases. 

The MLRO should also report at least annually to the firm’s governing body on the operation and effectiveness of the firm’s systems and controls in relation to money laundering. 

**A11.4** Outline the principal money laundering offences under UK law. 

_(3 marks)_ 

The main UK money laundering offences are now restated under Part VII of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (PCA). The PCA creates three main offences relating to Concealing (s 327), Arranging (s 328) and Acquisition, Use and Possession (s 329) as well as attempting, conspiring or inciting to commit any such offence (s340(11)(a) and (b)). Money laundering 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

includes Aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring the commission of any of these offences (s 340(11)(c) and (d)). 

Two separate offences are also created of Failing to disclose (ss 330-332) and Tipping-off (s 333). 

**A11.5** Explain the role and function of the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). _(3 marks)_ 

The Financial Action Task Force (FATF) was set up in 1989 as an inter-governmental agency to develop international standards governing money laundering, terrorist financing and fraud. The FATF publishes 40 core recommendations  covering Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing, as well as typologies of money laundering and financial crime. The FATF conducts mutual evaluations and reviews of country implementation and compliance. The FATF also identifies high risk and non-cooperative jurisdictions. 

## **A11.6** Define financial crime and explain two examples of financial crimes regulated by the FCA. _(3 marks)_ 

The FCA defines financial crime as any criminal activity involving money, financial markets, or financial services. This includes insider trading, which is the illegal buying or selling of securities based on non-public, material information. It also covers money laundering, the process of disguising the origins of illegally obtained money. 

Market manipulation is another form of financial crime and involves creating artificial or misleading market conditions, such as through "pump and dump" schemes. Fraud, including misrepresentation or Ponzi schemes, also falls under this definition. Bribery and corruption are included, referring to the offering, giving, receiving, or soliciting of something of value to influence a financial decision. Lastly, sanctions evasion is considered financial crime and involves circumventing international or domestic sanctions to carry out prohibited transactions or business dealings. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A11.7** Briefly explain the role and function of the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). 

_(3 marks)_ 

FATF was established at the G7 Summit in Paris in 1989. Its membership has subsequently been enlarged and now includes many more countries than the original G7 members. FATF is an inter-governmental body whose purpose is the development and promotion of policies, both at national and international levels, to combat money laundering and terrorist financing. It is a policy-making body, which works to generate the political will to bring about national legislative and regulatory reforms in these areas. 

FATF monitors the progress of its member states in implementing necessary measures, reviews money laundering and terrorist financing techniques and counter measures, and promotes the adoption and implementation of appropriate measures globally. In performing these activities, FATF collaborates with other international bodies involved in combating money laundering and the financing of terrorism. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

## **12. Markets and exchanges** 

- **A12.1** The FSMA includes provisions with regard to RIEs and RCHs. Provide three examples of 

an RIE and one example of an RCH. _(2 marks)_ 

The recognised investment exchanges (RIEs) are as follows: 

- a) London Stock Exchange (LSE); 

- b) London Metal Exchange (LME); 

- c) ICE Europe; 

- d) AQUIS Exchange; 

- e) Cboe Europe Equities. 

The Recognised Clearing Houses (RCHs) are: 

- a) Euroclear UK and Ireland (which incorporates CREST); 

- b) LCH.Clearnet (which incorporates the London Clearing House); 

- c) LME Clear; 

- d) ICE Clear Europe. 

**A12.2** Explain the nature of and principal activity carried out by a systematic internaliser. 

_(3 marks)_ 

A systematic internaliser is a financial firm that matches customer orders internally on his own book. It is effectively an internal market. Firms may go on to formal markets at the end of the day to settle outstanding positions. 

A systematic internaliser is formally recognised under MiFID and MiFID II and MiFIR as an investment firm which deals on its own account, on an organised, frequent, systematic and substantial basis, by executing client orders outside regulated markets, multilateral trading facilities (MTF) or organised trading facilities (OTF). 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A12.3** Explain the role of a market maker in the context of Markets in Financial Instruments Directive ll (MiFID II). _(2 marks)_ 

A market maker is a financial institution or individual which aids the provision of liquidity to certain financial markets. In this role the market maker may trade on their own account to ensure that transactions in the market of interest can be undertaken by other market participants. Through ensuring sufficient transactions are undertaken in a financial market, an effective price for financial assets can be established. These market maker roles are generally undertaken by larger financial institutions such as banks. 

Reflection as to the role of MiFID II in this context would be helpful. Other appropriate material can be included in an answer and can also be acknowledged. 

**A12.4** What is meant by the term ‘initial public offering’? How is such an offering undertaken? _(4 marks)_ 

An initial public offering or IPO is a first floatation of shares/equity within a company to be traded on a public exchange. There are multiple ways in which primary offerings can be undertaken: 

- An _offer for sale_ , whereby the firm the company offers shares for sale to the public and institutional investors. This will generally be coordinated by an investment bank. 

• _An offer for subscription_ involves the company soliciting potential investors as to their interest in investing within an IPO. When these subscriptions have been received the new shares can be allocated and issued. 

- An _intermediaries offer_ involves selling shares to financial institutions. These institutions can then re-sell the shares to the public. 

- _Placing_ involves an investment bank placing blocks of shares with their current clients and 

- contacts. 

- An _introduction_ does not involve an offer of shares for sale; rather, it allows a company’s existing shareholders to trade their shares on a stock exchange. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A12.5** Define **four** different types of financial benchmark classified under UK financial regulations. _(3 marks)_ 

UK benchmark regulation defines a benchmark as a publicly available index. These benchmarks are developed from an assessment of underlying values of pertinent variables such as prices, interest rates or alike. The UK benchmark regulation classifies benchmarks into six different types: 

- _Critical benchmarks_ – where the value of contracts underlying the benchmark is at least 

- €500 billion. 

• _Significant benchmarks_ – where the value of contracts underlying the benchmark is at least €50 billion, and/or where there are no or very few market-led substitutes for the benchmark and/or there would be an impact on financial stability if the benchmark ceased to be produced. 

- _Commodity benchmarks_ – where the underlying asset of the benchmark is a commodity 

- as defined by UK MiFID. 

- _Regulated data benchmarks_ – where the input data to the benchmark is provided directly 

- from regulated venues. 

- _Interest rate benchmarks_ – where the benchmark is determined on the basis of the rate at 

- which banks may lend to, or borrow from, other banks or agents in the money markets. 

• _Non-significant benchmarks_ – where the value of contracts underlying the benchmark is less than €50 billion, and the benchmark is not a commodity or interest rate benchmark. 

**A12.6** What is a systematic internaliser? _(2 marks)_ 

A systematic internaliser is an investment firm which deals on its own account by executing client orders outside a regulated market (RM), multilateral trading facility (MTF) or organised trading facility (OTF). In this role the systematic internaliser provides liquidity to markets. 

The systematic internaliser regime, or trading on own account acting on a ‘principal’ basis, was introduced in 2007 as part of the MiFID regime, applies to shares and was introduced to enhance market transparency. This test was subsequently amended to include posttrade reporting requirements to assist in the price discovery process for investors. MiFID II also applies to the systematic internaliser regulatory regime when non-equity financial instruments such as bonds and derivatives are traded. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section A: Questions and Answers** 

**A12.7** What is the Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA)? Name **two** types of assets in scope of MiCA. _(3 marks)_ 

The Markets in Crypto Assets Regulation (MiCA) is an EU regulatory framework for the issuance of, intermediating and dealing in, cryptoassets. It introduces licensing and conduct of business requirements as well as a market abuse regime with respect to cryptoassets. MiCA applies with respect to ‘cryptoassets’, which are defined very broadly as ‘a digital representation of a value or a right that uses cryptography for security and is in the form of a coin or a token or any other digital medium which may be transferred and stored electronically, using distributed ledger technology or similar technology’. This definition is aimed at capturing not only cryptocurrencies, such as Bitcoin and Ethereum, but also stablecoins and utility tokens. 

**A12.8** What is the FCA's Early and High Growth Oversight initiative, and what are its objectives? _(3 marks)_ 

The FCA's Early and High Growth Oversight initiative provides enhanced regulatory supervision and tailored support to newly authorised firms during their first few years of operation, particularly those experiencing rapid growth. The primary objective is to ensure that these firms establish strong governance and risk management frameworks early on, which are critical for long-term sustainability and regulatory compliance. The objectives of Early and High Growth Oversight are to: 

- identify and address harm or misconduct more swiftly; 

- raise the standards of adequate systems and controls from the starts; 

- promote competition; and 

- provide firms with a better supervisory experience. 

**==> picture [153 x 30] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## _**Each Question carries 20 marks in total.**_ 

## **B1.** 

You have recently taken a new compliance role in a UK financial firm which sells investment products with advice. In your first few weeks at the firm, you observe a range of practices which raise concerns, as to the form and content of advice provided to clients and specifically how advice is provided on future investments. On reflection you feel it is important to develop a training course as to the needs and requirements for providing investment advice and whether this advice is suitable and recorded in an appropriate manner. 

- **a)** What are the regulatory requirements and expectations for providing advice on investments and to ensure the advice provided is suitable for the client? 

   - _(5 marks)_ 

- **b)** What factors determine whether the investment advice provided to a client is suitable and accounts for the risk tolerance of the client? _(5 marks)_ 

- **c)** What is a more suitable course of action for a compliance professional when discovering a range of practices which raise concerns as to the form and content of advice provided to clients? _(5 marks)_ 

- **d)** The market for providing financial advice is dynamic and there have been many developments in its financial regulation. Summarise the developments to the market for financial advice indicated within the Financial Advice Market Review (FAMR) (2015). _(5 marks)_ 

## **B2.** 

Mark is a sales representative at a large insurance provider. His role involves selling life insurance and critical illness policies to consumers. Mark is under pressure to meet aggressive sales targets, and the firm offers significant commission incentives for selling premium policies. One day, he meets Olivia, a single mother who is looking for a life insurance policy to provide financial security for her child. Olivia has a limited budget and is not familiar with insurance products. Instead of recommending a basic policy that meets her 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

needs, Mark persuades her to buy an expensive policy with additional features she does not fully understand. He assures her that it is the best option, downplaying the long-term costs and the cancellation penalties. A year later, Olivia struggles to afford the premiums and realises the policy is far more expensive than she was led to believe. She files a complaint, stating that she was misled about the costs and suitability of the policy. 

- a) Which of the FCA key principles have been potentially breached by Mark and the firm? 

   - ( _7 marks_ ) 

- b) What steps should the firm take to ensure compliance with Consumer Duty in situations like this? 

   - ( _7 marks_ ) 

- c) What are the potential regulatory consequences for the firm and Mark if they are found to have failed in their duty to Olivia? 

   - ( _6 marks_ ) 

## **B3.** 

A rich far eastern domiciled individual approaches a small private securities firm in London to discuss possible investment opportunities. The individual claims to be a sophisticated investor. Consider each of the following: 

- a) How would you categorise the prospective client for FCA Conduct of Business (COBS) purposes? Under which types of headings may the client be categorised and what information would you require to make any proper determination? _(5 marks)_ 

- b) If the prospective client is assigned one status initially, in what circumstances may this be adjusted subsequently? Comment, in particular, on the possibility that the prospective client may wish to have either more, or less protection than that already provided. _(5 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c) What documentation requirements generally apply under the COBS rules before you can begin to conduct any regulated activities on behalf of the client? _(5 marks)_ 

- d) If the prospective client is interested in investing in financial derivatives on either a discretionary or execution-only basis, do any further requirements have to be complied with? _(5 marks)_ 

## **B4.** 

A US investment management company wants to set up an operation in London to launch a new pooled product under the terms of which investors will own a proportionate share in the portfolio of business property holdings across Europe. You are asked to advise on each of the following: 

- a) Explain the main requirements that apply with regard to the setting up of a new financial business in the UK under FSMA. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Comment on the extent to which the proposed arrangements might fall within the definition of a collective investment scheme under the FSMA. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Identify any other investment activities that may be involved. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Explain the main restrictions that apply with regard to the sale and promotion of such schemes in that UK. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) If the investment management company chose to base itself in the EEA and seek local authorisation there instead, comment on any passporting issues that will arise under European legislation. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B5.** 

Sophie is the Head of Compliance at a mid-sized investment firm. The firm has recently expanded its operations, onboarding a large number of new clients and offering a broader range of financial products. However, the compliance team remains small, and Sophie is concerned that existing oversight processes may not be sufficient to monitor 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

regulatory risks effectively. In recent internal audits several compliance breaches were identified, including failures in transaction monitoring, inadequate staff training, and a lack of clear accountability within the compliance function. Senior management has expressed concerns about increased regulatory scrutiny and wants Sophie to propose a strategy to strengthen the firm’s compliance oversight. 

- a) What are the key compliance risks resulting from compliance breaches identified by Sophie`s firm, and why is effective governance important in managing these risks? 

   - _(5 marks)_ 

- b) What steps should Sophie take to enhance compliance oversight and ensure that regulatory obligations are met? 

_(5 marks)_ 

- c) How can senior management support the compliance function, and why is a strong compliance culture essential for the firm? 

_(5 marks)_ 

- d) What potential regulatory and reputational consequences could the firm face if it fails to improve its compliance framework? 

_(5 marks)_ 

## **B6.** 

You are the Head of Compliance at Megabank, a large integrated securities house. Megabank has recently appointed Roy “Brass” Neck to re-invigorate its Equities Division, which includes both Equity Research and Equity Sales. Neck’s reputation is as a practical, hands-on manager, with aggressive ideas about the ways to generate business and motivate staff. 

After two weeks in the post, he comes to you with the following proposals, which he wants implemented without delay: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) Whenever the Corporate Finance Department goes to “pitch” for a new client they must be accompanied by an analyst from the Equities Department. He explains that this will show the potential client that Megabank has a strong team of experts; it will also enable the analysts to provide a “reality check” to control over-enthusiastic Corporate Financiers and turn down unsuitable new clients. 

- b) The specialised dealers who currently work in the Private Client Department must be redeployed within the Equity Department and report to Neck. This is so that he can control the dealers’ activities and ensure that all the order flow in equities is retained within the firm. At present, too many trades are being executed with other houses via SETS and Retail Service Providers. He will also be able to give the dealers large lines of stock, which they can place with Private Clients. 

- c) If a Corporate Client is going to make an announcement, the Corporate Finance Department must brief the Equity Division well before the announcement is made, in order for them to be prepared for the inevitable questions from clients that will arise when the announcement is made. 

- d) The Nominee and Safe Custody Department must notify all Equities Division staff of every “S793 Notice” they receive. (s793 of the Companies Act gives companies the power to demand details of the underlying shareholders within a reasonable time. Company secretaries usually write to nominee companies to find out who the beneficial owners are.) 

What advice would you give to Mr Neck with regard to each of the arrangements that he wants to put in place? _(20 marks)_ 

## **B7.** 

- a) Ruth Kendall, a retail client, telephoned Bell Jones, a firm of brokers and a member of the London Stock Exchange and regulated by the FCA. She asked the firm to sell shares on her behalf. Bell Jones effected the deal by buying some of the shares as principal and selling the remainder in the market as agent. The total due to Ruth amounted to £28,000. Two days after effecting these transactions Bell Jones sent Ruth a letter and contract confirming the deal together with the transfer documents. The latter were 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

completed and returned promptly by her with the relevant share certificates. Two weeks elapsed during which time Ruth heard nothing from Bell Jones. She then telephoned the firm and was told that her funds were available. She was asked whether she would prefer to reinvest the money in a combination of gilts and options or alternatively whether she would prefer the cash. The firm’s employee with whom she spoke emphasised several times that as a widow she should have her money working for her on the market. Ruth requested Bell Jones to pay a quarter of the sum into her bank account and to retain three quarters to invest on her behalf. The firm sent Ruth a cheque for £7,000 drawn on its current account and placed £21,000 into a client bank account (designated as the Ruth Kendall account). Bell Jones subsequently purchased from that account gilts and when it received the certificates the firm used them as collateral for its bank overdraft. Bell Jones has since been put into receivership by the bank for defaulting on loan payments. 

Advise Ruth about the following matters: 

   - (i) What rule breaches have, or may have, occurred? _(8 marks)_ (ii) What are her prospects of recovering her money? _(2 marks)_ 

- b) ABC Co is a member of the London Stock Exchange and regulated by the FCA. It acts in a broker dealer capacity. It is a wholly owned subsidiary of XYZ Co, a registered market maker in all leading and many smaller company shares. One of ABC retail clients, Brown, a non-discretionary retail client, has a modest portfolio of ‘blue chip’ investments and wants to buy some smaller AIM shares as long-term investments. He has also been reading about LIFFE equity options and thinks that they might offer him a ‘bit of excitement’. Brown tries to contact his usual sales executive at ABC Co to discuss his intentions as he has not dealt in these investments before. However, the executive is out at lunch and so Brown is put through to another executive, Smith, a trader, with whom he has not previously spoken. Without hesitation Smith recommends several AIM company shares and traded options to Brown. Brown accepts the recommendations and instructs Smith to deal in the AIM shares. Smith goes to lunch and returns just before the close of business. He executes hurriedly buying orders with XYZ Co in several AIM company shares. The following morning, without further consultation with Brown, he transacts a traded option deal for him through another broker. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Analyse and discuss the regulatory issues arising from these transactions under the Conduct of Business Rules, and identify any breaches you suspect may have occurred. 

_(10 marks)_ 

## **B8.** 

- a) Imperial Computers is a leader in the manufacturing of computer chips. They have just invented the “superchip” which will have a faster rating than any other chip on the market. If they can market this product successfully, and make it cheaply, they will eclipse Intel as the market leader. The stock market knows that Imperial Computers has made great strides in this area and investors generally expect that the company will soon begin their marketing efforts on a world beating computer chip. 

Investments Unlimited is regulated by the FCA. The firm’s “star” electronics industry analyst anticipates that Investments Unlimited will wish to publish current research on Imperial Computers. He telephones Imperial Computers’ head office to arrange a meeting with their Chief Executive, Frank Merchant. Mr Merchant’s secretary says, “Sorry, Frank is in hospital. I don’t know what is wrong but he asked me to call for an ambulance about an hour ago.” 

Consider whether the analyst has inside information and also whether Mr Merchant’s secretary has acted lawfully. _(6 marks)_ 

- b) A day or two later Imperial Computers issues a press release giving details of Frank Merchant’s physical condition. He has not suffered a heart attack but has been treated for stress and chest pains. As the analyst walks across the trading floor towards his office, he stops at the desk of one of Investments Unlimited’s principal traders. In the ensuing conversation he tells the trader that he is updating his research, that he will publish his report first thing in the morning and that he now thinks investors should sell their positions in the short term. In response to this intelligence, the trader immediately sells stock so that by the end of the afternoon the book is short sufficient shares that the trader is comfortable that he will be able to meet customer selling demand. 

Analyse the respective positions of the analyst and the trader. 

_(7 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c)   After his conversation with the trader, the analyst decides to put the sales force in the know. He approaches a private client fund manager for the firm and has a conversation similar to that held with the trader. 

The fund manager has a discretionary retail client who has a significant holding in shares of a different computer company. Indeed, the fund manager purchased those shares for the customer two days ago on the grounds that the company was a good long-term investment that would produce the income and growth that the client expects from his investments. The fund manager has now made seven trades on this account this month, each of which has shown a profit. Appropriate client documentation is in place. 

The fund manager, knowing his client’s account has no spare buying power, sells the shares in the other computer company to generate sufficient margin to support a short sale of Imperial. The fund manager feels this is a fairly riskless way to generate some additional trading commission. In fact, his strategy works and his client makes a substantial profit on the short sale before the fund manager covers it and repurchases the other computer shares 

Ignoring the question whether or not the fund manager is dealing on the basis of inside information, analyse the fitness and propriety of his behaviour. _(7 marks)_ 

## **B9.** 

You are the Compliance Officer in the corporate finance division of Agrobank, a large UK investment bank.  Sharp, a corporate finance director, has just been to see you to ask your advice on various questions arising out of a rights issue by Technoco plc, on which Agrobank has been advising and in relation to which it has acted as sponsor. 

The nil-paid dealings period has now closed. Only 30% of the issue has been taken up by shareholders. Agrobank now has 48 hours (the “placing period”) in which to place the remaining 70% before it goes to sub-underwriters. Surprisingly, the market appears to be unaware of the poor take up and the market price stands at a reasonable premium to the price at which new shares are being offered pursuant to the rights issue. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Agrobank is confident that it will be able to place the remaining rights. However, it would like its market maker to take 15% and has decided to allocate up to 7% to Agro Fund Management, its discretionary fund manager. It will retain 2% in house as principal and place the remaining 46% in the market. Neither the full amount taken up by the shareholders nor the amount allocated within the Agrobank group will be disclosed to places. Any amounts remaining unplaced at the end of the placing period will go to the sub-underwriters. 

- a) What legislation would you advise on at this stage, and why? _(7 marks)_ 

- b) What, if any, disclosure obligations arise in respect of the proposed allocations to the Agrobank group? _(2 marks)_ 

Sharp would also like Agrobank to invest its own funds in Technoco’s shares to prevent a major fall in the share price should news about the size of the rump leak out before the rump has been placed. He believes that the purchase would be a shrewd investment – he cannot understand why the rights issue was so badly supported and has shown you some management figures he received this morning from Technoco’s finance director, showing how well the company is doing. The figures are considerably better than the projections in the rights issue circular. He was so impressed when he received them that he suggested the finance director send them to a number of Technoco’s institutional shareholders. As a result of the information, one of the institutions has been in touch with Agrobank and would like to take an option over shares in Technoco acquired by Agrobank, rather than buying the shares in the market itself. 

- c)  Identify whether any criminal offences have been committed, or may have been committed, on the latest facts as described by Sharp. _(7 marks)_ 

- d) At the end of the fully paid dealing, members of the Agrobank group have in aggregate the following for registration: 

Market maker                      9.0% 

Agro Fund Management     9.0% 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Principal funds                    2.9% 

Which of these will require disclosure to Technoco? _(4 marks)_ 

## **B10.** 

You are the compliance officer of Osprey Private Bank, a UK-incorporated bank which is regulated by the FCA for the conduct of investment business. Osprey is a subsidiary of Global Bank Inc, a US-based universal bank, which provides a variety of banking services to both retail and wholesale clients. 

- a) Global Bank Inc. is considering setting up a global website for the group. The website will be hosted in the US, with its content managed by Global Bank Inc. in the US. The website will advertise Osprey’s personal and corporate banking services. You are asked to advise on anything required to ensure that the website does not “raise any UK compliance issues”. _(6 marks)_ 

- b) Mr Corleone, a native of Sicily, but now resident for most of the year in the Caribbean, has an account with Osprey through which he mainly conducts share dealing activities. You are approached by an officer from the National Crime Agency asking for a print-out of all the transactions on Mr Corleone’s account for the last twelve months. 

   - (i) Should you give the information to the officer? 

   - (ii) Should you notify Mr Corleone of the approach and/or the disclosure? 

Please give reasoned answers. _(6 marks)_ 

- c) Mr Corleone introduces his Russian friend, Mr Staksovcashsky, to Osprey telling Osprey Mr Staksovcashsky is dissatisfied with the performance of his current bankers. Mr Staksovcashsky proposes to ask his old bankers to transfer the balance on his account (worth several hundred thousand pounds in cash and securities) to Osprey. Mr Green, a young account executive at Osprey, is keen to add Mr Staksovcashsky to his client list and asks you what he needs to do in order to take on Mr Staksovcashsky as a customer. Please advise. _(8 marks)_

## **B11.** 

You are the compliance officer of an investment bank, which has just taken on as a client, Crimpleton Bains plc, a company whose shares are admitted to trading on the London Stock Exchange. The company is generally beset by scandal. Some concern has been expressed in relation to the manner in which Crimpleton Bains plc’s officers and employees and advisers have conducted themselves in the past. You are asked to consider whether any market abuse issues, or indeed other regulatory or legal issues, arise from the following circumstances: 

   - a) Crimpleton Bains plc makes widgets. It has recently been disclosed that your client may have sold widgets in breach of UN Sanctions against Iraq. As a result of this disclosure and the possibility of prosecution, the share price has slumped. It turns out that, one week before this information was made public, an employee of Crimpleton Bains plc sold all his shares, and then resigned. He tells you that he sold the shares as soon as he found out what his employers were up to and that his behaviour had nothing to do with a desire to avoid loss and everything to do with his personal morality. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) On two occasions, Crimpleton Bains plc gave an informal preview of a profit warning, which it was shortly going to announce to the market, to a journalist who had previously written favourable articles about the company. On the second occasion, the journalist was asked to sign a confidentiality agreement and was given a warning to the effect that he should not deal on the basis of that information until it had been announced to the market. On neither occasion did the journalist deal in the securities of the company. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) You review the text of the announcements submitted by Crimpleton Bains plc to its previous corporate finance advisers and in comparing the text which, ultimately, was published by the regulatory information services, it is clear that some significant inaccuracies have arisen. You conclude that the error has been that of the previous corporate finance advisers. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Crimpleton Bains plc’s profitability is closely related to the price of fuel on the world markets, which has generally affected the share value of most similar manufacturing 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

companies, including your client. It is clear to you that earlier statements relating to the sales growth of the company are no longer accurate. However, an internal revision of Crimpleton Bains plc’s sales growth forecasts has not been announced to the markets. 

_(4 marks)_ 

- e) Crimpleton Bains plc has in the past held breakfast briefings with selected analysts. They have discontinued them, but can’t remember whose idea it was to stop. They would like to start hosting them again. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B12.** 

You are the head of a division within a London investment bank which covers a broad range of activities including Merger and Acquisition (‘M&A’) business and a separately incorporated Private Equity (‘PE’) operation. 

You are aware of the current high level of regulatory scrutiny attached to conflicts of interest by the FCA, especially in the area of investment banking and some of the recent scandals that have occurred. You have also recently participated in a training video, which emphasised the ethical culture of the bank and reminded colleagues of the need for trust and integrity in dealing with clients whose interests must always be considered of paramount importance. 

a) Explain the importance of the need to maintain high standards of behaviour and integrity under the FCA Handbook and how this is generally secured in practice. 

_(4 marks)_ 

You become aware that your M&A team are advising client X on the acquisition of ABC Limited which is the subject of an auction process. You are also aware through the conflict management escalation process that your PE colleagues are also participating in the auction process. The potential conflict has been managed on the basis of Chinese walls and independence policies and you have satisfied yourself on the reputational issues through disclosure. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

b) Explain the FCA Handbook requirements that apply with regard to independence policies and disclosure of conflicts of interest. _(4 marks)_ 

c) Explain what action should be taken to ensure the integrity of the Chinese walls in place and that they are effectively monitored and maintained. _(4 marks)_ 

The M&A director responsible for advising client X comes to see you to provide an update on the transaction and confirms that his client is through to the final round of the auction process with one other bidder, which is believed to be the affiliated PE operation. During the discussion you learn that following a review of the information made available to all potential bidders in the data room, the M&A team conducted its own comprehensive due diligence exercise which resulted in an assessment that the value of ABC Limited is significantly less than generally believed. The M&A director questions whether this will affect the bid strategy of his PE colleagues. You are also concerned to know whether PE have picked up on the over-valuation of ABC Limited and are not about to commit the Group and its Fund Investors to an investment of several hundred million dollars on the basis of erroneous information. 

d) What courses of action are open to you and what factors would you take into account in deciding how to act? _(4 marks)_ 

The next day, the M&A director responsible for advising client X comes to you in a state of excitement and informs you that their due diligence has uncovered certain accounting irregularities at ABC Limited. 

e) What are your responsibilities to report such issues? _(4 marks)_ 

## **B13.** 

You are the FCA member of staff responsible for supervising General Research and Advisory Brokers Ltd (GRAB). The Financial Promotions Unit has drawn your attention to an advertisement published by GRAB in a major national newspaper about the shares in an unlisted oil services company. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

The promotion states that: 

- (i) The shares have grown by 10% more than the shares of listed companies in the same industry; 

(ii) The company has total assets of £128 million, and sales in the last year were £514 million; 

(iii) GRAB will provide a commission-free dealing service in the company’s shares; 

(iv) Investors can expect to make a 25% return on the shares within 12 months. 

a) Explain what issues the advertisement raises concerning compliance with relevant FCA rules and with relevant statutory provisions. _(5 marks)_ 

b) In view of your findings in (a) above, describe the measures which you will now take to address the issues which you have discovered. Refer to the powers available to the FCA under statute and to the tools and policies referred to in the Regulatory Processes Block of the FCA Handbook. Also explain how your proposed course of action complies with the duties of FCA and the regulatory objectives set out in FSMA. 

_(5 marks)_ 

You subsequently find out that GRAB: 

- (v) Has no records of who confirmed that the promotion could be issued, when or why; 

- (vi) Was dealing in the shares as principal at a bid-offer spread of 20% of the bid price; 

- (vii) Did not have permission to deal as principal in any type of investment. 

The firm’s chief executive believes that it was issued on the approval of an account executive who specialised in the sale of life assurance and collective investment schemes. This person has since been asked to leave the firm, because he has provided a poor standard of service to clients, giving rise to frequent complaints. 

You also find out that: 

(viii) The oil-services company has liabilities of £350 million; 

- (ix) GRAB has invested £22 million from clients’ discretionary portfolios in the company’s shares, and look unlikely to recover more than 20% of their investments; 

- (x) The chief executive of GRAB is also a director of the oil-services company. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c) Describe the regulatory issues arising from your new findings. _(5 marks)_ 

- d) Explain your views on the powers, tools and policies to be used on GRAB in view of the wider range of regulatory issues that you are aware of as a result of the information set out in c). Also set out your views on the measures to be taken in respect of key approved persons at the firm and state whether you would take more stringent measures than in your answer to (b). Explain the reasons for your decision. 

_(5 marks)_ 

## **B14.** 

Andy, Head of Equities Trading, is holding his weekly team meeting.  The team is not performing well and Andy’s boss has made it clear that he expects Andy to turn things around. 

Andy: ‘OK – main point for our team meeting today – we must come up with some bright revenue generating ideas and quickly.  Any thoughts?’ 

Bob (Head of Proprietary Trading): ‘We need to get more insight into the market and capitalise on that.  Why don’t we Premises to re-locate us so that we are more in the swim of client order flow?  Right next to the market makers would make best business sense.  And we could arrange to see the list of client indications of interest – they’re not confirmed orders at that stage and the clients would value the additional liquidity that would be provided in the market.’ 

Charles (Deputy Head of Prop Trading and formerly a Research analyst): ‘Better still, get us all access to the pending research recommendation changes and take advantage of the window after they have been approved by the research committee, but before they have been released to clients.  Don’t let on, but I still have systems access to the draft reports being prepared in the Utilities sector from my time there as an analyst, and I intend to keep it that way!’ 

Dave (Head of Sales): ‘I’m on the board of one of our best clients – maybe I could exercise some influence there to bring more business to us?’ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Ed (Head of Syndication, Capital Markets): Personally, I think we should allow personal account shareholdings in the shares of our clients – that way we align our objectives with those of our clients.  Anyway, these are all good ideas guys, they could really help the firm.  Andy, why don’t you give a prize for the best idea – a week’s skiing for the winner and his best customer would incentivise us most – we should use the classiest resort in the US, with travel, accommodation and all incidental expenses paid?’ 

Fred (aside to George): ‘I’m not entirely comfortable with some of this, are you?  I think we should run it by Compliance and definitely escalate Charles’s continuing access to draft research if Andy won’t take it up with him.’ 

George (aside to Fred): ‘Fred, listen, you are a long-standing close personal friend of Charles.  20 years working together and he’s given you a lot of support in your career. How could you even think of putting him in a difficult position and potentially jeopardising his job?  Have a heart for him and his family and don’t go there.  And remember, the rest of the team’s jobs will be on the line if you block our route to improved performance. What’s more, if previous experience is anything to go by, you’ll very likely be disadvantaged or sidelined if you rock the boat right now.’ 

## From the above scenario: 

- a) Identify four potential conflicts of interested, explain their regulatory and ethical nature and indicate appropriate tools for their management and control. 

   - _(12 marks)_ 

- b) Why is appropriate management of conflicts important to firms? 

_(4 marks)_ 

- c) Comment on George’s and the firm’s apparent attitude to whistle blowing.  What provisions to facilitate whistle blowing would you expect within a firm? 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B15.** 

You provide Compliance advice on a part time basis to Stockwell Corporate Finance Ltd (SCFL) a small corporate finance firm with permission to advise and arrange deals in investments.  Its business has always consisted solely of giving corporate finance advice and arranging corporate finance deals.  SCFL does not deal in investments directly and has no dealing or settlement systems.  Its three account executives are all Certified Persons. 

SCFL recently arranged the flotation of a business (Smallco) for a long-standing client, realising £40 million in cash for its 4 directors.  The grateful directors of Smallco want to entrust the money to SCFL to invest for the long term in whatever way it thinks most appropriate.  They offer to pay an annual fee of 1% of the value of the amount invested. Time Robbins, the Managing Director of SCFL, thinks that this is an excellent opportunity to earn income, diversify the business of the firm and support a good client relationship. He appears unconcerned about the regulatory implications, saying that the firm must _“strike while the iron is hot”_ and that he is not prepared to await your return from holiday which was long delayed while the flotation of Smallco took place. 

- a) What are the regulatory implications of accepting the offer from the directors of Smallco to place their cash with SCFL? _(6 marks)_ 

Although you learn, in due course, that it was suggested to Robbins at an executive meeting that there might be regulatory problems if the firm started to carry out this new business without changing the way it operates, he decided that the opportunity was too good to miss and that the firm must go ahead immediately, in view of the risk that another firm will get the business if SCFL delayed “just to conform with yet more red tape”. 

Robbins emphasised the importance of providing a first rate service to the client, which he is confident of providing, because one of the account executives has past experience in investing funds for clients.  He also instructed all members of staff to ensure that no one outside SCFL and Smallco find out that the business is being done. 

- b) What regulatory issues could result from this proposed course of action? 

_(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

When you return to the office on Monday you are called in immediately to Robbins’ office where he tells you what has been going on whilst you were away and he says that although he was reluctant to do so, he had been persuaded by the other executives not to take any precipitate action but to await your return. 

Robbins tells you that he is determined that the firm must undertake this new business from the directors of Smallco, as he sees it being the start of important new opportunities for SCFL but, before being irrevocably committed, he must know the regulatory implications of doing so, in particular what the likely requirements of the FCA would be. 

- c) What do you tell Robbins? 

_(10 marks)_ 

## **B16.** 

You are a compliance officer working for a financial services firm focused on the sale of investment products to retail customers. A new chief executive officer (CEO) appoints a friend to become Head of Marketing for the firm. This new Head of Marketing joins the firm after a successful marketing career working in supermarkets and industrial products. After a first week at the firm, the new Head of Marketing suggests that current approaches to marketing the firm’s financial service are insufficiently market focused and involve far too much bureaucracy. They propose that the form of advertising and the sales processes need to be slim-lined and become more focused on meeting sales targets and profitability metrics. As the compliance officer, this proposal causes you some professional concerns. 

After several months have passed, you discover that several advertisements have been placed on the firm’s website and distributed over social media for an investment product you were unfamiliar with. You were not part of this development and weren’t granted sight of any of the documentation in the development of these proposed investments. The advertisement relates to investing in a ‘holiday time share’ in a non-EU eastern European holiday resort. The investment scheme claims to provide investors with a ‘rock solid investment’, ‘guaranteed returns’, and access to a holiday home for a few weeks of the year. In the scheme it is proposed the investors can choose to stay at the holiday resort in an eastern European non-EU nation at certain times of year or choose to sell-on the right to 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

stay at the property to a third party. It is stated this investment will provide a ‘regular and reliable source of income for all investors’. The investment scheme is presented in a very upbeat and positive manner with the suggestion that any investment in the scheme will result in 10% annual returns and has no downside risk. Indeed, no mention of the risks of the investment or any downside of the investment are contained in the advertisement. The advertisement also includes sentences stating that the investment is ’only for qualified and discerning investors’ and is ‘approved under section 21 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000’. 

On seeing these advertisements, you are alarmed and write to the CEO outlining your concerns as to the appropriateness of this advertisement. The CEO sets up a meeting with you, the Head of Marketing and the other senior managers. In the discussion, the Head of Marketing declares the advertisement and associated business have been very profitable for the firm, with several clients already signed up. They also state that compliance was not included in the new product development as compliance acts as an ‘anti-business’ element within the firm. After a fraught discussion of these issues, it is reported the CEO was aware of these sales. Eventually, the CEO decides that these advertisements and taking on clients for these investment schemes must stop immediately and asks the Head of Marketing to step down. You are asked if you can further explore the ramifications of this investment scheme and can address some of the outstanding regulatory questions which it has raised for the firm. 

a) Discuss the key points which would need to be raised in response to discovering this investment scheme has already been marketed and sold to clients. In your answer, outline and discuss the regulatory priorities in the sale of investment services. _(6 marks)_ 

b) The advertisement of the time share investment has several deficiencies. What are these failings and why do they raise concerns? _(4 marks)_ 

c) After discovering the non-EU eastern European time share scheme has accepted funds from clients, what are the next steps the firm should follow? As the compliance officer how would you resolve this issue? _(4 marks)_ 

d) How might you judge the behaviour and actions of the compliance officer and the Head of Marketing in relation to the CISI Code of Conduct? _(6 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B17.** 

You are a compliance officer in a firm of hedge fund managers and become aware that one of your firm’s most senior traders (Rapier) has been approached by a salesman from an investment bank, who is seeking market feedback on a forthcoming issue of US$ securities issued by a Brazilian Bank in New York. 

Although you have not been approached for advice as to the impact that these discussion could have on Rapier’s trading activities, which generate a significant portion of the firm’s income, a number of concerns spring to mind. 

## a) What might these concerns be and why? 

## _(5 marks)_ 

You discuss these concerns with your boss who says that he will have a word with Rapier. As a result of this, you are approached by Rapier, who tells you that he has been an active borrower and short seller of stock in the Brazilian bank for some time and suggest that there is no reason why he should not continue to trade in this manner. 

## b) What advice would you give and why? 

## _(5 marks)_ 

Rapier also suggests to you that he believes his trading activities are permitted as he is not trading in the UK. 

## c) How would you respond and why? 

_(5 marks)_ 

Shortly after your conversation with Rapier, the Brazilian securities are issued and as a result of his short position, Rapier has booked the firm a substantial profit. 

Nevertheless, you remain concerned at the position in which Rapier may have placed both himself and the firm. 

- d) Explain what further issues may arise and their possible regulatory consequences. 

_(5 marks)_

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B18.** 

Emma is a senior financial adviser at a wealth management firm. One of her long-standing clients, David, has expressed interest in a high-risk investment opportunity that promises substantial returns. Emma is aware that David has limited investment experience and a lower risk tolerance. However, her firm has an internal sales target for this investment, and senior management has encouraged advisers to promote it aggressively. Emma is facing pressure to meet her targets and is aware that she may receive a significant bonus if she successfully sells this product to David. 

Michael, the firm’s compliance officer, learns about the situation and raises concerns. He reminds Emma of the firm's responsibility to adhere to FCA regulations and ensure that all investment recommendations align with clients' risk profiles and financial goals. Michael warns that proceeding with the sale without fully assessing suitability could have regulatory and ethical repercussions. 

- a) Identify and evaluate the ethical issues present in this scenario. 

_(8 marks)_ 

- b) How should Emma apply the CISI Code of Conduct in handling this situation? 

   - _(6 marks)_ 

- _c)_ What potential consequences could Emma, Michael, and their firm face if she proceeds with selling the product to David? _(6 marks)_ 

## **B19.** 

You have recently started work as the Compliance Officer of a small city firm which has three different business units: Research, Brokerage and Corporate Finance. 

After carrying our a review of the firm’s business practices during your first couple of weeks you are now ready to present your findings to the Chief Executive and there are a number of matters that you wish  to bring to his attention. Specifically, you are concerned about the following observations. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

The Head of Research appears to report to the Head of Brokerage, although you cannot find a formal staff organisational chart to confirm this. You have also been told that in the interests of transparency, all of the Broking team attend the weekly Research team meeting in which Research discuss the companies that they are currently covering, the views that they have on them and the nature of the reports that will be issued the following week. 

As a result of these close links between Brokerage and Research, it is possible for the Research staff to be remunerated in accordance with the number of transactions that are generated from their research reports. Considerable emphasis is placed on this direct link between research reports and client transactions and as a result the heads of the two departments have agreed that the most active clients of the previous six months should receive research reports a couple of hours before other clients, to give them time to react to it before the market changes. The Head of Research has explained to you that in order to fully monitor the impact of research reports on client transactions it is necessary for the Brokerage and Research teams to sit next to each other, and in fact the dividing wall that used to separate the two teams has recently been taken down. 

You also have some concerns about the Corporate Finance department. It appears to be very successful and has recently expanded to take on two new trainees who you are told have been such quick learners that they have already been able to give some sound advice to clients despite the fact that they have not yet had the time to sit their exams and that that Head of the Department has been too busy to give them much attention as he is busy working on a hostile takeover. 

When you asked the Head of Corporate Finance more about the hostile takeover mandate that is keeping him busy he explained proudly that he is so well regarded in the market that two firms bidding to buy the same company have given him a mandate to work for them on the transaction. He has not felt the need to inform the two client firms of the two mandates as he tells you that the situation is adequately managed by the use of a Chinese Wall – the Corporate Finance team is in a restricted-access area of the office. 

During the course of your review work, however, you have noted that even though the Corporate Finance team is indeed in a separate office, the door is normally kept open as it 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

can get very stuffy. And anyway, with the door locked it makes it more difficult for the Heads of Research and Brokerage to get in, which you have seen them do on a regular basis, although you are told that it is only to discuss golf! 

Comment briefly on the soundness of the regulatory control infrastructure within this firm with special reference to: 

- a) Any practices you consider to be inappropriate; _(5 marks)_ 

- b) Any controls that you consider should be implemented or strengthened; _(5 marks)_ c) Any rule breaches that you think might have taken place; _(5 marks)_ 

- d) The potential consequences of any rule breaches you have identified. _(5 marks)_ 

## **B20.** 

One of the rich private clients of a leading United Kingdom investment bank has become increasingly dissatisfied with the quality of the service provided. You are instructed to advise the client on any rights of redress or recovery that may be available against the firm. 

- a) The client considers that it has been given the wrong advice with regard to some of the investments made as a result of which a significant amount of money has been lost especially in Asian equity and derivatives holdings. Advise the client on what action should initially be taken. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Having obtained further information from another client of the firm as to the firm’s knowledge of the weak prospects of the markets concerned and following the firm’s failure to provide satisfactory explanation, you are asked to explain if any further rights of complaint are available. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) The client is then informed that the losses suffered have actually resulted from the fraudulent dealings of the unsupervised office manager in Bangkok. How should the client proceed? _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- d) Following a separate investigation into the activities of the office manager, the FCA becomes aware of the extent of the loss. Can it assist the client in any way with regard to the losses suffered? _(4 marks)_ 

- e) In the event of the firm’s subsequent collapse and closure, does the client have any separate right of claim apart from against the firm directly which is now in insolvency? _(4 marks)_ 

## **B21.** 

A new divisional director (Herbert) has been hired from overseas to head up your Equities division. He will be required to be certified by the firm to allow him to direct the division and advise clients. 

When he joins, as the firm’s Compliance Officer you state that, despite his considerable experience, he will need to pass the regulatory module of an appropriate UK qualification before he can start taking investment decisions or provide investment advice. It is not considered feasible for him to be supervised given his seniority and experience. 

Both the Divisional Managing Director and Herbert indicate that this should not be a problem and that, while studying for the examination, Herbert will restrict himself to strategy and non-trading activities. 

Herbert begins to study for the examination and, over the following period, the performance of the Equities division improves substantially. You learn from an unattributable, but reliable, source that Herbert has, in fact, been providing detailed direction to his department including taking investment decisions and offering investment advice for which he is not currently approved. 

On raising these concerns with both Herbert and his Managing Director, they reassure you that he is not taking investment decisions or providing any investment recommendations or advice. However, your source remains adamant that, despite the reassurances that you have received, Herbert is conducting regulated activities while performance within the Equities division continues to strengthen. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Herbert is scheduled to take the regulatory examination in the near future and is expected to pass, which will allow him to be judged to be competent and therefore registered for the appropriate certified functions. 

- a) Outline the nature and operation of the regime for certified persons. 

_(8 marks)_ 

- b) Given the above facts, what action would you take from a compliance perspective, if any, and what factors would you take into account? _(10 marks)_ 

- c) Would your answer differ if you were aware that the FCA were due to visit and have indicated that they will look at your firm’s internal compliance procedures and TC arrangements? _(2 marks)_ 

## **B22.** 

You are the compliance officer of a medium sized hedge fund. On Friday morning you are heading back to your desk and are approached in the corridor by the fund’s top performing portfolio manager who asks for a “quick chat”.  During your conversation you discover that on Monday the fund manager had been contacted by an investment bank about the refinancing plans of ABC Company, an international company with its headquarters in Germany.  ABC Company has issued corporate securities in the form of bonds which are to be listed on the Luxembourg Stock Exchange. 

The investment bank had contacted the portfolio manager for his views on the refinancing which included the tender for certain of ABC Company’s bonds (“the ABC bonds”). During the course of this conversation, the portfolio manager was informed about the size of the refinancing deal (EUR 0.7 billion), the date of the announcement (the following Thursday), and that the ABC bonds would form part of the tender offer. 

Later on Monday afternoon the portfolio manager gave instructions to a junior broker in the firm to purchase 1.4 million of the ABC bonds. On Thursday ABC Company made a regulatory announcement that it had commenced a cash tender offer for certain bonds, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

including the ABC bonds. The ABC bonds rose sharply in value, and shortly after the portfolio manager gave instructions to sell which realised a healthy profit for the fund. 

On Thursday evening while toasting the profit for the fund, one of the portfolio manager’s friends from another hedge fund, who had recently attended a course on market abuse, commented that the portfolio manager may have committed market abuse. After a sleepless night, the portfolio manager decides to speak with you in the morning. 

You immediately request the recordings of the telephone conversation between the portfolio manager and the investment bank. To your horror, you discover that the conversation was not recorded. You further discover that the portfolio manager’s notes on the refinancing deal had been left open on his desk before the announcement was made. 

- a) You immediately contact the fund’s Chief Executive Officer (CEO) and inform her about the situation. She requests an analysis from you on whether any market abuse has been committed, including details of the relevant possible offences that may have been involved. How would you respond? _(6 marks)_ 

- b) Having given your analysis to the CEO, she further asks what corrective action the fund should take and what are the likely steps that the FCA may take, should the fund notify the regulator. What would you advise? _(4 marks)_ 

- c) The CEO then requests an immediate review of the fund’s anti-market abuse systems and controls. In order to undertake this review, you prepare an outline of the potential risks to the firm and applicable internal policies and procedures that may be adopted to mitigate these. Explain the general content of the outline. _(6 marks)_ 

- d) On the Saturday morning, the worried portfolio manager calls you on your mobile. He asks you what the regulatory penalties are for his conduct and the consequences for him and the firm. What would you advise? _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers**

## **B25.** 

You have taken over as Compliance Officer of a firm which trades securities with professional clients and eligible counterparties. The Chief Executive has appointed you because he feels that your predecessor has not done an adequate job and he is looking for you to resolve the problems and to ensure that they do not happen again. He is particularly concerned that there have been material thefts of client money, made possible by inadequate segregation of duties in the accounts department. This has been exacerbated by infrequent reconciliation of client money bank accounts. The Finance Director is to be sacked. 

- a) The Chief Executive has explained the above to you at a preliminary meeting in his office. He continues: “It is so embarrassing that this has happened – I mean, it’s not as if I don’t care about protecting our clients’ assets but I don’t want the FCA to find out about this. They get so uptight about client money you know, and if they ever find out, remember they are judge, jury and executioner – they can have both of us thrown out of the industry at the drop of a hat. So make sure that our clients don’t lose a penny, and see to it that the back office work out some sound procedures to make sure that this doesn’t happen again.” 

What advice would you give to the Chief Executive on the regulatory implication of this proposed course of action? _(6 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- b) As you start to investigate the client money rule breaches, you find out that the firm holds client money in respect of on-exchange futures transactions. However, the firm’s Part 4A permission does not include dealing in futures. 

What are the regulatory implications of this, and what action do you recommend to the Chief Executive? _(6 marks)_ 

- c) The Finance Director is currently holding the Finance Function, and the Chief Executive asks you to give him a “clean” withdrawal _on Form C – Notice of ceasing to perform controlled functions_ , on the grounds that dismissal is adequate punishment. Advise the Chief Executive of the regulatory implications of this course of action. 

## _(3 marks)_ 

- d) This incident leads you to conduct an overall review of client files, to see what clients have opted out of client money protection. You find out that, not only do you have no documentation on file for some of the clients, but that the firm does not even have their addresses. The clients in question have all been classified as professional clients. 

What are the main regulatory implications of this? 

_(5 marks)_ 

## **B26.** 

You work in the Far East for PB Private Bank which is a part of a large multinational financial services group (MSG) with your firm’s client profile being principally ultra high net worth individuals and families. 

Among this group of clients is the Chang family whose wealth derives from a highly successful manufacturing business. Five years ago, the founder of the business died leaving his 3 sons to run the business and with a financial interest each but with his wife holding a controlling financial interest although taking no part in the management. 

Under the direction of the sons, the business has continued to prosper to the point at which they wish to expand significantly although this has produced tensions with regard to the way in which this could be achieved. Due to the close nature of their relationship with 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

the Private Bank, the Changs have maintained their commercial banking interests within your group at MSG and have only taken advice on their financing discussions from MSG until now. 

PB’s relationship manager responsible for the Changs’ non-commercial interests is JM who has been based in the Far East for many years. Since JM’s wife is a family friend of the Changs, you believe that the relationship with the Changs is secure. Consequently you have every expectation that, whatever form of financing is deemed appropriate, the investment banking arm of MSG (MSGIB) will win the mandate for the new instructions. 

In due course, you learn that the Changs have discussed the various options available with MSGIB who have suggested that they should consider raising the funds through a ‘bought deal’ with 60% of the shares being issued to outside professional investors.  This would reduce the Chang family’s holding to 40% of the capital of the company, with Mrs Chang’s interest being diluted to 20% overall. She is not happy with this proposal as she sees herself as the guardian of her late husband’s legacy, over which she will then have little influence. 

As Mrs Chang’s agreement is essential to the new financing proceeding in the manner envisaged, MSGIB have asked that PB try to convince her to accept the proposal, explaining, among other things, that she will receive a large amount of money from the sale and will still own a substantial piece of the business. MSGIB analysts believe the business is likely to become even more successful following the external capital injection. 

JM accordingly arranges a meeting with Mrs Chang to try to resolve the issue and afterwards advises you that Mrs Chang considers that the deal is designed solely to enrich her sons and the bank at the expense of the business. She has nevertheless suggested to JM that in light of the Changs’ long friendship with JM’s wife, the only way in which she would agree is if JM is made a non-executive director of the business to manage Mrs Chang’s interests. JM informs you that he considers that this is a good idea, as he would then be able to look after Mrs Chang’s interests at the same time as those of all of PB’s other clients. 

JM further believes from conversations with MSGIB, that they will expect PB’s client managers to recommend investment in the issue to their clients or, where they are 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

discretionary managers, to buy on their clients’ behalf. JM also believes that the issue will be substantially sold to MSG Fund Management’s Far East Fund. You are concerned about the potential conflicts of interest that may arise. While some of them may be resolved, you are not clear whether the full implications of these potential conflicts have been fully considered and the potential damage that this may cause to the bank’s reputation especially in the private client area. 

a) Explain the various conflicts of interest that may arise with regard to Mrs Chang, JM, MSGIB, PB or elsewhere. _(16 marks)_ 

- b) How should Private Bank and MSG deal with the proposal? _(4 marks)_ 

## **B27.** 

You are the Compliance Officer in London for a brokerage firm which is 60% owned by a large US investment house. While inter-bank markets have remained relatively stable, rumours have spread around the City of London and New York about the stability of your US parent company. The brokerage firm has been able to borrow sufficient short-term funds until recently although only at increasingly higher rates over the last few weeks. Three of the non-executive directors on the Board of the UK firm approach you in connection with possibly disposing of some of their shares in the London company which is listed on the London Stock Exchange and the US parent company which is listed on NASDAQ. 

The three directors had attended a full meeting of the Board two days previously where the funding difficulties of the UK operation had been discussed. The Chief Executive Officer (CEO) and Chairman both confirmed that they were satisfied with regard to solvency and continued viability of the US parent, although the UK firm may experience some credit difficulties going forward which may last a few months. 

The non-executive directors had also attended a separate meeting of the Audit Committee during which the recent sharp decline in the UK firm’s share price had been discussed. A copy of a separate external research report had been referred to which stated that the share price was expected to continue to fall. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

The three non-executive directors had separately heard over coffee in the executive sitting room that a number of the executive members of the Board had already disposed of some of their shares. The non-execs wished to dispose of all of their current holdings and asked you whether there were any legal or regulatory restrictions that may apply. 

- a) Explain the general legal and regulatory issues that arise. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Identify the principal offences, penalties and defences that may apply with regard to market abuse. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Advise whether any separate criminal law provisions may apply. _(4 marks)_ d) Advise on any separate reporting obligations that should have been complied with. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Discuss whether any additional conflict of interest issues may arise. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B28.** 

You learn that one of your investment firm’s former star traders in global equity instruments has recently been sentenced by the courts for market distortion related offences. The trader had been removed from his position over a year ago although the firm had decided not to take any further action until the court case had been settled. You work for the senior compliance officer and are asked to prepare a short memorandum advising the board on the regulatory implications of the losses suffered by the trader and the trader’s subsequent conviction for market related offences. 

- a) Outline to the board the principal regulatory restrictions that may have been breached by the trader. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Explain the general regulatory provisions that apply with regard to maintaining adequate systems and controls within the firm. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Outline possible oversight and monitoring systems that may be set up above traders to limit their activities and potential losses to the firm in extreme cases. _(4 marks)_
