**Section B: Answers** 

- d) Advise the board on any interim or longer term corrective action you recommend should be taken. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Explain the regulatory action that the FCA may take against the firm in the event that it fails to monitor traders and manage the exposures created adequately. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B29.** 

The investment management division of a major European investment bank is anxious to off-load a substantial amount of residual ‘super-senior’ debt attached to a series of US sub-prime related asset-back securitisations. A team within the bank’s structured finance division had set up the programme although all of the former members of staff have since left or otherwise lost their positions. The price of the debt has recovered substantially since the crisis and the investment team is anxious to try to move it into the portfolios of certain wealthy individuals for whom the firm acts on a discretionary basis. The debt had been downgraded from AAA to BBB- with the investment team portfolio guidelines allowing up to 10% of the portfolio to be invested in BBB- securities provided they have longer term capital growth potential. You work in the compliance department and are asked to sign-off on the proposed asset transfer. 

- a) Comment briefly on any conflicts of interest issues that may arise in the circumstances. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Explain the documentation requirements that would apply before any work could have been carried out on behalf of clients. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Outline the main regulatory restrictions that would apply with regard to the management of the investment portfolios on behalf of individual clients. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Advise on any rights of recovery that may be available in the event that the super-senior debt substantially decreases rather than increases in value. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Comment on any separate rights the individual clients may have under the Financial Ombudsman Scheme or Financial Services Compensation Scheme. _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B30.** 

You work for a small boutique bank in the West End of London which has a number of clients in the Middle East and Central and East Asia. You receive instructions from an overseas government agency to transfer a large amount of funds from the government account with your bank to the account of another association held with a separate bank in London. You regularly handle transactions on behalf of the government although not the specific agency concerned. You are advised by a colleague that you should check whether the association is listed on any of the restricted lists of terrorist groups. After some further research, you also uncover that the association is based in a compound in a major city in a Central Asian country which does not appear to carry on any separate business activities or send or receive funds on a regular basis. 

- a) Explain any immediate concerns that may arise with regard to the requested funds transfer and what action you should take within the bank. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Outline the principal offences and penalties that may apply and any possible defences. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Outline any separate anti-terrorist considerations that may apply either within the UK or internationally. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Explain the action that you should take to prevent any firm or personal liability from arising. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) How should you respond to any requests for further information from your client with regard to progress or completion of the transaction and why this is necessary? 

_(4 marks)_ 

## **B31.** 

The fixed interest desk of a large Asian investment house in London has over committed itself in southern European euro government debt. It has become increasingly concerned about possible rescheduling talks following the announcement of additional financial support for another large European country. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

The head of the team contacts your group who work in the area of discretionary portfolio management and asks if you can pass on some of their holdings on to your institutional investor clients. They only wish to transfer around 20% of their euro bond portfolio to stay within internal risk management limits and there are no limits on the amount of government debt that you can hold within your discretionary portfolios. There has also been no confirmed announcement of rescheduling or default on the bonds held yet although clearly this may occur in the future. You are asked on the following. 

- a) Explain the initial procedures and documentation arrangements that you would already have put in place to carry on business for your clients. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Outline briefly any potential conflicts of interest that may arise in this case. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Explain what action you, as an approved person, propose to take to resolve the conflicts, to avoid any potential liability for the firm or you personally. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) One of the members of the fixed interest desk asks you separately whether there was any action that could be taken to support the price of the debt held until it could be disposed of separately. Explain the regulatory implications of taking any such action. _(4 marks)_ 

- _e)_ Outline the remedies available to your institutional clients in the event that they suffer loss following a forced rescheduling of the government debt positions transferred into their portfolios. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B32.** 

Glisten is a recently privatised mining company in the central Asian republic of Manowar but the Life President of the republic is believed to retain a substantial stake via the government’s retained 40% stake, together with undisclosed private interest arising from the IPO. 

The company dominates industrial activity in its domestic market and exports its products around the world. With the strength of mineral prices this year was a bumper year for 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Glisten, which generated substantial profits leading to the expectation of significant dividends. 

Public disquiet both domestically and internationally about the huge profits of the company, its opaque governance structure and the large current account deficit in Manowar mean that Glisten is frequently criticised in the media. 

You work as a senior manager for Chimera Investment Bank who advised on the IPO of Glisten some years ago and your firm continues to provide a broad range of services to the company as well as to the Manowar government, which plans further privatisations of state assets. As a result of the commentary about Glisten, Manowar has asked Chimera for a proposal to capitalise on the value of various state assets without the unwelcome publicity resulting from public ownership. The Structured Finance Department of Chimera has originated a proposal that will, as its principal attribute, generate significant cash to the government via a sophisticated leasing arrangement undertaken through a British Virgin Islands incorporated entity. 

Initial discussion with Manowar representatives suggests that they are interested, but they have no previous experience of such a structure and their Finance ministry representative, who is a nephew of the President has told Chimera that because of the sensitivity of the President it is imperative that no hint of this transaction should become public knowledge. 

The transaction will use a special purpose vehicle and will earn Chimera a substantial fee, which would be particularly valuable to the company, which has seen little activity this year and would be a key factor in enabling you to meet your personal income target for the year. 

The proposed structure has not been subject to comment or scrutiny by any tax, accounting or regulatory agency but, as part of your approval process, you have received two legal opinions, one from a global and one from a local Manowar practice. Both state, unequivocally, that the proposed transaction does not contravene any existing Manowar laws. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) State, with reasons, whether or not you would support this transaction. _(10 marks)_ 

- b) What further information might you seek? 

   - _(5 marks)_ 

- c) Are there ways in which you might mitigate some of the risks? 

- _(5 marks)_ 

## **B33.** 

You are the newly appointed compliance officer for a boutique hedge fund in the West End of London. You notice that the head partner has been very successful in recent years in trading shares in public limited companies in the UK and US. He has always able to ‘beat’ the markets and any comparable market indexes and appears able to predict key company decisions and price movements. You understand that he spends a considerable amount of office time with chief executive officers (CEOs) or chief financial officers (CFOs) from a number of the companies in which the fund trades. You understand that he had been attempting to obtain confidential information on the rumoured purchase of a major company by a large US private equity fund. In reviewing the recent transactions, you notice that the fund appears to have been pursuing similar trading strategies with other UK and US hedge funds, which have resulted in certain price movements. 

- a) Explain the principal concerns regarding the head partner's conduct in light of his anticipating major company decisions and price movements. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Identify the principal regulatory offences, defences and penalties that may apply under relevant legislation. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Outline any separate criminal law provisions that may apply with regard to the attempt to obtain confidential information on the rumoured private equity fund acquisition. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Explain any separate concerns that you have regard to the parallel strategies apparently adopted by your fund and other hedge funds in the UK and US.

## **B34.** 

One of the lead managers in a major asset management firm is concerned with the poor performance of his fund over the past year. He has just placed a deposit for the purchase of an expensive new apartment in a central new development within the City of London. He is purchasing the apartment ‘off plan’ and will move in next year after his ‘Christmas bonus’. The manager concludes that the Eurozone will not be able to withstand a possible default by a large southern European country and decides that he will ‘short sell’ the euro using all of the freely available funds in his investment portfolios which is managed on a discretionary basis. You work in the compliance, division of the firm and are informed about this by another colleague over a coffee break. 

- a) Explain the initial procedures and documentation requirements that would have been complied with in setting up the portfolios under management. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Outline briefly any potential conflicts of interest issues that may arise in this case. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Explain any relevant restrictions that may apply with regard to the proposed short selling. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Identify any other more general regulatory breaches that may be involved and the action you would take to prevent any firm liability arising. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Outline any remedies available to the firm’s institutional clients in the event that they suffer loss following that short selling on the portfolio. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B35.** 

Problems in demonstrating client suitability are a continuing source of comment and criticism of the industry by the FCA as financial regulator. Your director has told you, as the compliance officer in a small firm of financial advisers with a wide spread of clients, that he wants you to design and initiate a programme to ensure that your advisers adhere to all of the relevant regulations. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

He adds that because of your firm’s public commitment to the highest standards of integrity, he wants the programme to focus initially on ethical principles and considerations in the advisory process and then to ensure that it meets other regulatory requirements. Explain how you would design such a programme. Focus on the issues above, identifying key risk areas and demonstrate how you would deal with them to meet the task set by your director, using examples. _(20 marks)_ 

## **B36.** 

You work within the compliance department of a major UK banking group. Concerns have been raised at board level with regard to the conduct of a small group of sales staff within the private client department who formerly worked in the structured finance unit within the investment banking division of the bank before the crisis. The group had developed a new form of ‘Structured Mortgage Product’ which was sold on the basis that it would allow wealthy private clients to increase their loan to value (LTV) borrowings to up to 10 times LTV and to reduce the nominal interest rates paid to only 0.25 basis points above Bank Rate in the UK. The product had nevertheless been designed using interest rate swaps and credit default swaps (CDSs), which had been modelled on the assumption that interest rates would rise substantially towards the end of 2011 and early 2012. The purchasers of the product now find that they are locked into paying substantially in excess of UK Bank Rate for the foreseeable future and have no rights of review or cancellation under the product documentation. 

a) Outline the general provisions that would apply with regard to the promotion of the Structured Mortgage Product to the wealthy clients approached and any potential exemptions. _(4 marks)_ 

b) Explain any specific restrictions that would have applied with regard to coldcalling and any rights of a client having been cold-called. _(4 marks)_ 

c) Outline any separate requirements that should have been complied with in designing the new product and having it approved within the firm before launch. 

_(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

d) Outline the general enforcement powers that might be available to the FCA against the firm or individuals involved. _(4 marks)_ 

e) Explain the advice that you would provide to the board to attempt to mitigate any damage suffered or penalties imposed. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B37.** 

You are the compliance officer for a small boutique broking firm in Canary Wharf in London. The head of the UK Trading Desk approaches you concerning a transaction executed by one of his star brokers the previous day. The firm had a large position on an outstanding Structured Global Depositary Product entered into some years ago that had matured the day before. The product was tied to the share price of a South African mining company listed on the London Stock Exchange, with your firm losing £3 million if the closing share price was below £125 and realising a profit of £10 million if the share price was £125 or above. The star broker had entered the market 10 seconds before it closed and placed substantial buy orders, the effect of which was to move the price from £122.5 to £125.3 forcing the counterparty on the structured product to pay out the £10 million. The broker had also informed another broker within his team about the proposed strategy with the other broker being able to make a separate profit on the rise in the share price on the mining stock when the market opened on the following morning. 

a) Explain the general legal and regulatory issues that arise with regard to the conduct of the star broker. _(4 marks)_ 

b) Identify the principal offences, penalties and defences that apply. 

. 

_(4 marks)_ 

c) Outline any separate criminal law provisions that may apply with regard to the conduct of the second broker. _(4 marks)_ 

d) Explain the action that you would take on behalf of the firm to limit any enforcement or reputational damage. _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

e) Explain any separate action that you would recommend to the firm’s board to avoid any similar breaches in future. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B38.** 

Bob is an experienced senior manager who has spent most of his working life in the securities industry, during which time he has been an approved person and held Board level positions in FCA regulated firms for many years. He has always enjoyed good relationships with other industry practitioners, as well as serving on an FCA advisory panel regarding the RDR. 

Bob is now semi-retired and is a non-executive director with Optimist, a small wealth manager, where he has been asked to undertake an informal review of how they meet customer due diligence obligations, such as ‘know your customer’, when taking on board a new client and subsequently monitoring their transactions. 

Bob’s initial findings are that Optimist’s processes are not of the standard suggested by the industry guidance and he reports his findings to the non-executive Chairman of the Audit Committee and Board Chairman, Kevin. 

Kevin is not surprised and explains that this confirms his beliefs, which is why he asked for the review. Kevin mentions to Bob that he has discussed this matter informally at an industry dinner with a mutual colleague of theirs, Guy, who now works in the supervisory division of the FCA. 

Bob is asked to present his findings to Adam, the Chief Executive, who angrily refutes the findings saying that Optimist’s processes are in line with all the firms that he has ever worked for and states he does not intend to waste resources on this or worry his clients over trifling administrative matters. 

Bob accepts that no customers appear to have been disadvantaged, and he has no evidence of the firm being associated with financial crime, but says there is not sufficient management information to confirm the issue either way. Bob’s report is then discussed at the Audit Committee where, in view of Adam’s apparent hostility to Bob’s report, Kevin thanks Bob for his “excellent wok, which has provided much food for thought” adding that, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

in due course, it may be felt appropriate to look at some of the individual parts of the report. 

As a result of this decision Bob tells Kevin that he feels that since the main focus of his position on the board was the production of the report, he does not feel that it is in the interest of either party for him to remain on the board and he tenders his resignation as a non-executive director of Optimism. 

Shortly after that, Bob receives an email from Guy saying that he understands that Bob has left Optimist and asks him if “he is aware of anything that he would like to disclose”. Bob is somewhat surprised by this and wonders whether it would be unethical for him to respond at all, now that he has left Optimist. Nevertheless, he considers various possibilities, and writes them down so that he can mull over how best to proceed. The four courses of action which he considers are: 

- Ignore the email and do nothing; 

- Respond saying that he would be happy to meet Guy to discuss anything specific that Guy may have in mind; 

- Respond saying that he left Optimist as a result of a disagreement over policy; 

- Respond saying that he had nothing specific to raise, but then to phone the whistleblowing line to report Optimist’s poor standards regarding basic compliance issues. 

Explain your considerations in each case and the conclusion you reach as to why the course of action is, or is not, your preferred response. 

_(20 marks)_ 

## **B39.** 

You have just joined the compliance team within a major international bank with its headquarters in the City of London. Your boss has become very concerned with regard to investigations being conducted by US regulatory authorities into the activities of one of the bank’s overseas branches (rather than subsidiaries) in South America. The press has already reported possible money laundering allegations with the branch allegedly having been involved in channelling funds from major drug groups from various South American countries into the US and into the UK. Separate reports have been made of accounts

having been open for major terrorist groups in South America. ## 

You have been asked to review the conduct of the Head of Corporate Banking within a large European bank operating in London. The manager had pursued an aggressive lending policy immediately before the UK financial crisis which culminated in the government’s recapitalisation of a number of large banks in October 2008. The manager had constructed a property portfolio principally made up of high risk but previously high returning commercial property mortgages. While the manager had been advised by the bank’s risk committee to reduce some of the exposure in the portfolio, he had expanded the portfolio to attempt to generate further returns in the six month periods before and after October 2008. Some of the funds used to build up the portfolio had been taken from separate client investment accounts. Enlarging the portfolio would also have increased his bonus entitlement if the higher returns had continued rather than collapsed following the crisis. 

You are asked to consider and explain the following:

- a) The potential breaches of High-Level Standards that may have been committed by both the bank and the manager in this case. _(4 marks)_

## **B41.** 

You work in the compliance department of a major UK commercial banking group. Senior management has become concerned with the Group’s possible involvement with the London Interbank Offered Rate (LIBOR) and European Interbank Offered Rate (EURIBOR) scandal. You were asked to undertake some research into the activities of the Group staff responsible for LIBOR and EURIBOR submissions and, in particular, to confirm the content of e-mail exchanges within the Group and between the Group and other banks. You discover that traders within the Group had asked the rate setters to lower rate submissions both before and during the global financial crisis. You also find out that some traders have contacted rate setters at other banks to attempt to adjust the rates. You are asked to advise on the following:

## **B42.** 

Bert and Ernie both work at boutique banking firm, Golden Fleece. Bert is a high-tech equity analyst while Ernie works in M&A advisory, focusing on small tech companies. One Sunday as they play golf together, Ernie mentions a young tech company, Whizzo, he thinks has great growth prospects (Ernie owns shares in the company), but its share price has languished. Bert covers the stock, rating it a hold. 

Unbeknown to Bert, Ernie has also had prior informal conversations with Whizzo’s CEO at an industry conference. Ernie is enthused about Whizzo, but laments its lack of visibility among investors, and suggests that Bert take a closer look at it. A few days later, Bert reads in the press that Whizzo is acquiring a small, privately-held technology company, and is being advised by Golden Fleece. The firm is now obliged to provide an updated view but Bert fears that if he writes a glowing report on Whizzo, based on what Ernie has intimated to him, he could be accused of acting on inside information. 

- a) Explain the concerns that you have about the situation from a compliance perspective. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Outline the principal offences and penalties that may apply and any potential defences. _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c) Apart from statutory and regulatory considerations, what breaches of ethical behaviour do you see? _(4 marks)_ 

- d) What would you advise Bert to do and why? _(4 marks)_ e) What would you advise Ernie to do and why? _(4 marks)_

## **B45.** 

Your bank has agreed to offer safeguard facilities to a newly established Electronic Money Institution (EMI). Under this arrangement, your bank will hold funds for the EMI’s customers. The EMI is responsible for performing due diligence, ongoing monitoring, including transaction monitoring, and screening. As part of the agreement, your firm has requested that the EMI conduct an annual Anti-Money Laundering (AML) audit and provide the audit reports to your bank. _(6 marks)_

These rates are collected from other firms on the market and averaged to generate the market’s ‘standard offered rate’. One of the oil traders in the firm, on a separate floor of the building from the submitter, emails the submitter and asks him to submit a high figure to allow him to close out on a very large deal that will earn a substantial amount of money for the firm and a large bonus for him at the end of the year. ## **B50.** 

You are the newly appointed compliance officer at Trapezium Capital Ltd, which deals in asset management for high-net-worth clients. As compliance officer, you are part of the senior management team and work closely with other stakeholders in the firm. The chief executive officer (CEO) of Trapezium is also the head of finance and manages the firm’s risk. Since the departure of the last chief operating officer (COO), the position remains vacant. There is only one non-executive director and only two executive directors have been attending the board meetings in the last few months. 

Whilst reviewing the financial regulatory returns to be submitted to the FCA for the previous year, you become aware that the highest paid director of the firm is the CEO, who gave himself a 30% pay rise last year. The average pay rise of other employees was around 8-10%. The firm does not have a formal remuneration policy committee and only has a brief guideline policy on firm remuneration that was last updated in 2011. Trapezium is a subsidiary of the parent company Zane Holdings Ltd, which has another regulated subsidiary firm called New World Broking Ltd. New World deals in stock-broking and contracts for difference (CFDs) as principal with permission to hold client money. ## **B51.** 

The investment bank in which you work has just taken a short lease on a small singleroom office in premises immediately beside the London Stock Exchange (LSE). The server will only engage in high-frequency trading (HFT) carrying out trades within fractions of seconds. The bank's IT department has advised that the server has to be as physically close to the LSE servers as possible, in order to obtain maximum market advantage, in terms of speed of message delivery and receipt (referred to as ‘colocation’). The bank’s Irish Government Bond desk asks you whether it can use the new HFT facilities to unload a backlog of two year government debt that it cannot otherwise sell. ## **B52.** 

William and Alana work in the sales team at Wheelwright brokers, where the team is known for its supportive culture and positive working environment. William, who is particularly popular, is five years older than Alana, whom he was instructed to mentor when she joined the firm, as a result of which they have become good friends in the office and occasionally socialise outside work. 

William invites Alana to a dinner which he is hosting at a restaurant for friends and acquaintances and Alana is slightly surprised to find out that she is the only other person from Wheelwright’s at the dinner. Later during the evening as the men are bragging about their sporting achievements and contacts, William throws in that he has been invited on an 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

all-expenses paid trip to the next US Masters Golf tournament in Atlanta. Alana, not being at all interested in golf, excuses herself from the table for a few minutes and hears no more. 

A few weeks later as Alana passes William’s desk she hears him discussing commission payments with a major new client and is struck by the fact that William seems to be indicating a scale of payments far outside the firms published rates. She also hears William mentioning Atlanta, saying that his visit to the golf tournament had been “tacked on” to his much talked about “boys group” holiday to New York so it was “under the radar.” Alana is surprised at what she has overheard and wonders whether she should do anything about what she thinks was said. 

- a) Discuss the risks posed by William’s behaviour. 

_(4 marks)_ 

- b) What policies and procedures might firms have in place to deal with situations of this nature? _(3 marks)_ 

- c) Discuss the various courses of action that could be open to Alana. 

_(4 marks)_

## **B53.** 

Go2FX is a large foreign exchange trading firm headquartered in Greek Cyprus and you work as compliance officer for its UK subsidiary and are the SMF for this role. You receive a query from a sales manager who has introduced a new client to the business based in Pakistan. The client holds funds with other FCA regulated brokers in the City of London and wants to transfer all his funds to Go2FX in a B2B (business to business) medium, meaning that funds will be coming from these regulated brokers on behalf of the client. An amount of over $300,000 is expected to be funded by money coming in from 8 different City brokers. The UK firm’s Know Your Customer (KYC) function is outsourced to your

**Section B: Answers** 

headquarters and Pakistan is listed as a high risk jurisdiction in your firm’s annual AntiMoney Laundering (AML) policy.

- b) Identify any potential offences that may be involved and relevant penalties and defences. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Explain the three staged process of money laundering and where the above transactions could possibly fit. _(4 marks)_

   - _(4 marks)_

## **B54.** 

YouWin CFDs is an FCA regulated market maker based in London with branches in other financial centres in the UK offering contracts for differences and spread betting. YouWin CFDs recently launched a promotional offer whereby a client’s initial deposit would be matched 100% by them and any trading losses would be covered up to 50% by the firm. A loophole was nevertheless discovered by some clients in the promotion, whereby they could hedge their trades with competitors and lock in profits if they got “stopped out” with YouWin CFDs, since 50% of their losses were covered. This was highlighted to the firm by certain sales staff, but since the number of abusers was small and the dealing desk was still making money, the firm did not conduct any further investigation. However, the problem escalated over 3 months and about 40% of the firm’s new clients turned out to be using such trading strategies which the firm considered to be abusive. On discovering this, the firm blocked all withdrawals by these clients and marked the accounts as market 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

abusers. No further action was taken by the firm and withdrawals were overdue by six weeks. 

The head of sales issued the following statement, “We are not here to donate free money to these abusive traders. We will wait and watch. If they go to court, then we will fight them there. All sales managers are to avoid client calls whose withdrawals are pending and marked as abusive on our systems.” 

The firm’s compliance team believe that their terms and conditions cover them adequately for this kind of action and are not concerned. The client money belonging to these so called “abusive clients” is also not currently being segregated by the firm as it believes that it is not owed to them. The regulatory authority has not been informed of any of the above developments. You work for a compliance consultancy firm who is appointed to advice YouWin CFDs on possible further action and potential regulatory action by the FCA. 

- a) Which specific section of the FSMA has been breached and which of the Principles for Businesses (PRIN)? Provide explanations. _(4 marks)_ 

b) What other sections of the FCA Handbook may have been breached by the firm? _(4 marks)_ c) Has Youwin CFDs acted correctly from a Consumer Duty perspective? Justify your answer. _(4 marks)_ d) What action can the FCA take against the senior management and compliance team of YouWin CFDs? _(4 marks)_ e) Outline an appropriate remedial programme and the next steps that could be recommended by the consultancy firm to be taken by YouWin. _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B55.** 

You work in the UK Compliance and Ethics department of a major U.S. bank with a separate large subsidiary in Canary Wharf, London. The bank is one of the largest suppliers of prime brokerage services to many of the biggest hedge funds. This involves providing a range of services including cash account management, securities settlement, stock lending, foreign currency exchange and global custody. The board of the bank has become increasingly concerned about the large number of recent fines imposed on major financial institutions especially by the US and UK authorities and a number of mediumsized hedge funds which are clients of the bank have also been subject to recent investigation in the US. You are asked to advise on the ethical and conduct risk issues that may arise in the UK. 

You are specifically asked to prepare a memorandum covering each of the following: 

- a) Outline some of the types of hedge funds activities that may cause specific concerns from a regulatory perspective. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Explain the general nature of the UK authorities' expectations and requirements to promote an appropriate culture within firms. _(5 marks)_ 

- c) Outline the FCA approach to conduct risk management. _(5 marks)_ 

- d) Explain the various courses of action that your bank may take to meet regulatory expectations in identifying and mitigating culture and conduct risk. 

   - _(6 marks)_ 

## **B56.** 

You have just been transferred from the fixed income desk within a major European securities firm to the restructured new products division which was formerly referred to as the structured finance division and colloquially as the junk CDO group. The former head of the division, who likes to refer to himself as the Paris Dolphin, reputedly received the largest bonuses in the firm immediately before the European financial crisis. You are called to a board meeting where the Dolphin announces that he has just designed a new ‘Synthetic Portfolio Strategy’ (SPS) for the division which could generate enormous 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

income for the firm and potential bonuses for all the individuals within the division involved. You look at the summary figures provided in the presentation documents circulated at the meeting and become very concerned with the size of leverage involved and potential losses to the firm. No-one questions the data during the short meeting. Just before the meeting closes the Dolphin also mentions that, in the event of a sharp collapse in the value of the portfolio, he has a back-up ‘Product Support Plan’ (PSP) based on the co-ordinated disposal of other assets by another division within the firm, although he cannot explain the detail for regulatory reasons. The Dolphin has become aware of your uneasiness during the course of the meeting and asks you afterwards not to cause any trouble and ‘to look after’ the team. 

- a) Outline the initial concerns that may arise from a regulatory perspective. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Identify the specific general principles that may have been breached within the FCA Principles for Businesses (PRIN). _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Outline the potential heads of liability that may attach to the firm. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Outline the potential sanctions that may be imposed on the individuals concerned. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Explain the possible action that you might take to protect your own position within the firm. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B57.** 

The local branch of a major UK banking chain in a small provincial town has been approached by new clients to open a bank account for cash deposits and international money transmission services. The clients explain that they have opened a van and truck wash facility in a brown-field site on the edge of the town. They explain that this can be very profitable because of the large number of small, medium and large commercial vans and trucks used by businesses in the area. The clients then produce a rucksack full of cash of various denominations and request that this is deposited immediately. They also wish to set up a standing order to enable all net balances at the end of each month to be 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

transferred to a bank account in a different name in an Eastern European capital city. They explain that they expect to receive thousands of pounds in cash each week and that they will be making substantial cash deposits either on a weekly or monthly basis. The new young manager in charge of the branch is anxious to open the account, in order to be seen to bring in new clients, although he asks for your opinion as the compliance officer first. 

- a) Explain any concerns that may arise from a criminal or ethical perspective in opening the account. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Identify any criminal offences that may be involved and relevant defences and penalties. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Explain any identification, documentation or other record requirements that may apply. _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Outline any institutional regulatory liabilities that may arise. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Explain any separate provisions that may apply if it is subsequently confirmed that the account into which the monies are to be paid is linked to a terrorist groups. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

## **B58.** 

Brian has worked in the compliance department of Shangri La stockbrokers for many years and he has recently become the head of compliance and registered with the FCA as holding the firm’s SMF16 compliance oversight function. 

The firm has a good reputation and has not had any regulatory problems over the years. Brian is not unduly worried when he receives notification of a forthcoming FCA visit. He does, however, recall an incident four years ago when, as a result of changing the firm’s banking arrangements, the client money accounts had not been properly separated from the firm’s own funds and this was not noticed for nearly a year. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Brian remembers that there had been board level discussions at the time on whether to submit a report on the matter to the FCA. While he was too junior to have been involved in the decision taken at the time, he understood that it was decided not to report. This was on the basis that the auditors had fully tested and ‘signed-off’ on the figures, no client had been affected and the situation had been quite quickly resolved. 

With the impending regulatory visit, Brian becomes concerned that the firm’s unreported actions may now come to light which may have serious regulatory consequences and therefore considers whether it might be advisable to report the matter to the FCA immediately. 

a) Discuss whether you think that the firm’s actions four years ago were reasonable. 

_(6 marks)_ 

b) Although Brian was not involved in the decision taking at the time, should he have taken action before now and, if so, what and when? _(4 marks)_ 

- c) What course of action would you recommend that Brian takes at this time? Give reasons. 

_(6 marks)_ 

- d) What are the potential regulatory sanctions that may apply now? _(4 marks)_ 

## **B59.**

The app will provide access to a new digital platform which invites customers to open ‘Digital Trading Accounts’ with the firm, with the funds being invested through a specialist algorithm that the team has developed. Customers will receive all of the profits generated (less a fixed ‘digital trading fee’) and bear any losses (with the fee still being paid in all cases). The platform will also offer a ‘RoboAdvice’ service using the team’s new audio IT speaking programme and trading algorithm. A third full ‘Digital Discretionary Trading Service’ will be made available for wealthy clients. The 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

platform will also operate a full set of payment services and may wish to offer separate ‘Digital Deposit Accounts’ later with customers having automatic credit facilities on these accounts. Before the firm decides on whether to approach the FCA or not, you are asked to advise on the following: 

- a) Outline the general regulatory requirements that may apply. 

_(5 marks)_ 

- b) Explain the separate permissions that may have to be applied for, depending upon whether the firm wishes to provide some or all of its proposed services. 

   - _(5 marks)_ 

- c) Explain the principal regulatory agencies that may be involved in the UK and their general functions. _(5 marks)_ 

- d) Outline the application procedure that would have to be complied with. 

   - _(5 marks)_ 

## **B60.** 

A major City investment bank established a business relationship with ultra-high net worth (UHNW) clients and politically exposed persons (PEPs) involving a structured finance transaction comprising investments in notes backed by underlying warrants and thirdparty bonds. A number of companies were used to make the investments and the proceeds of the investments were held in an offshore trust of which the clients were beneficiaries. The transaction was the largest of its kind that the bank had executed for natural persons and was nicknamed the ‘Monster Deal’ internally, consisting of a £1.88 billion transaction. The bank did not follow its standard procedures that it would normally follow for establishing relationships with sensitive PEPs and high net worth (HNW) clients due to time constraints, and decided to adopt a very basic alternative procedure, with one senior manager expressing a desire to ‘race this through’ and calling it the ‘deal of the century’. Few people knew of the existence and location of the firm's due diligence records for this deal, which were kept in hard copy and not on the bank’s systems. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) Explain any concerns that may arise from a financial crime or ethical perspective in going through with the deal. _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Explain any enhanced due diligence requirements that may apply. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) How would the Senior Managers of the bank be held responsible under the Senior Managers Regime (SMR)? _(4 marks)_ 

- d) Outline any institutional regulatory liabilities that may arise. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Outline any action that you would suggest the bank take in order to resolve the above situation effectively. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B61.** 

George Dixon has worked in the City for many years, the last few of which had been compliance oversight and money laundering reporting officer for the UK subsidiary of a bank based in a part of the world regarded as a higher risk jurisdiction for anti-money laundering (AML) risks. 

The bank is involved in international remittances to its home country for both customers and non-customers and has been the subject of a regulatory visit to inspect its systems and controls for ensuring that its AML policies, procedures and systems are adequate for dealing with the business that it conducts. 

George, whose attitude to his job is that he is overworked, underpaid and always teetering on the brink of disaster, is at first pleased that attention is now being focussed on his area of responsibility as he feels that it will ensure that he is provided with adequate support. However, despite a skilled persons report being commissioned recommending such support, little is forthcoming in terms of additional full time staff and George carries on with “business as usual”. 

As part of the ongoing monitoring by the regulator of the bank’s remedial actions resulting from the regulatory visit, George was asked by the Chief Executive to write a paper setting out what needed to be done to address the deficiencies identified in the skilled persons report and George was pleased to do this on the assumption that it would produce the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

extra resources needed. However, he was less pleased when he learned a few days later that the CEO had written to the regulator assuring them that all the steps outlined in George’s paper had already been taken or were about to be implemented. 

George moaned to colleagues about the position that he was in, saying that he was “caught between the proverbial rock and a hard place” since the regulator wanted one thing, the chief executive was reassuring them that action was being taken but the reality was that there was very little actual change taking place. He only has 12 months until retirement so he will just keep his head down, hoping that if things go wrong it won’t all come back to him. 

|a)|Outline the major risks to the bank from its principal lines of business.|
|---|---|
||_(4 marks)_|
|b)|What should George be doing to mitigate these?<br>_(4 marks)_|
|c)|What is George’s liability bearing in mind his senior manager function|
||categorisation?<br>_(4 marks)_|
|d)|George appears to be complicit in the actions of the CEO. What might he do|
||differently?<br>_(4 marks)_|
|e)|What should the bank be doing to support him?<br>_(4 marks)_|



## **B62.**

## **B63.** 

A new Financial Technology (FinTech) digital platform wishes to apply for a banking licence through the FCA’s dedicated Project Innovate regulatory unit. It understands that this will allow it to be placed in the FCA’s regulatory sandbox which will allow it to avoid initial penalties and sanctions. The platform staff are already discussing the licensing conditions with the FCA although someone has noted that separate money laundering requirements will also be applicable. The platform hopes to be able to source funds from all around the world through its new site and to make loans available to all new customers including principally teenagers, students and other young people (born in the 1980s,1990s and early 2000’s) as well as any other users looking to be able to pay lower interest rates. You are asked to advise on the money laundering obligations that may apply.

## **B64.** 

A large UK commercial bank is a regular operator in the London inter-bank markets. You work in the compliance division and have been asked to review some old files that the Head of the bank’s Treasury Department has become concerned about. These involve the bank’s borrowing activities during the UK financial crisis in 2007-2009 and, in particular, with regard to a series of liquidity auctions that the bank participated in to raise funds during this period. The borrowing was used to support the bank’s general deposit taking and lending as well as other investment activities. You learn separately that concerns have been raised that some banks may have provided incorrect information to the central bank with regard to their liquidity positions and the value of the collateral offered to support their borrowings at the time. The Head of the Treasury Department has asked you to keep this information confidential and for you only to report back to him on any issues that may arise following your review of the files. 

- a) Outline the principal statutory offence that may apply with regard to the incorrect information provided to the central bank and any relevant defences and penalties. 

- _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Explain any separate regulatory concerns that may arise. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Outline any action that you may take in the event that the Head of the Treasury Department does not report any concerns that you raise and asks you not to discuss this with anyone else within the bank. _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- d) Explain any further remedies available to the regulatory authorities and any enforcement penalties that they may impose once they become aware of the issues involved. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Outline any action that would you propose that the bank takes in response to the issues involved. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B65.** 

You are the recently recruited Compliance Officer and MLRO (Senior Manager Functions SMF16 and SMF17) of a small UK private client brokerage house that is 80% owned by a well-known UK financial services group and 20% by the Chief Executive Officer. The Portfolio Control Function monitors the trading performance of advisory and executiononly clients, and provides regular exception reports to you, the Head of Risk (also recently appointed) and the Chief Executive Officer. You have noted that one EU resident execution-only client appears to have been particularly successful in timing five purchases in certain NASDAQ listed securities over a two-year period. Your enquiries have revealed that most, but not all, of these US firms share the same corporate broker. 

It is known that the client had for some years worked in the US office of an unrelated New York brokerage house before retiring three years ago, but you have not been able to establish any connection between the client and the US corporate broker. 

You have raised the issue with the Head of Risk, your day to day reporting line, who does not share your concerns. He points out that, although the client is most successful in the US and Latin America, the client trades frequently in a significant number of overseas securities in other markets with mixed results. The client is very wealthy and important to the firm. The client relationship is managed by the Chief Executive Officer. 

- a) What reporting obligation(s) do you have as a compliance officer? _(5 marks)_ 

- b) What opportunities are there within your firm and/or group to discuss your concerns and what problems do you foresee? _(8 marks)_ 

- c) What other actions would you propose to take and why? _(7 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B66.** 

A new start-up company based in Old Street in London has been developing a computer programme to provide ‘Robo advice’ services on portfolio management to online clients. It has been working on the software for a number of years and is now ready to go to market. It has applied to the FCA to participate in its Project Innovate and regulatory sandbox to ensure that it develops the necessary internal staffing and control systems and ensures full compliance with all relevant regulatory obligations. The company is waiting to hear whether it has been accepted into the latest group of companies which will participate in the most recent Project Innovate round. The company has approached you to provide outline initial advice as to its expected compliance obligations. 

a) Explain the principal issues that arise from a regulatory perspective in determining whether or not the company will require authorisation and permission under the FSMA. 

( _4 marks_ ) 

- b) Outline the core obligations that will have to be complied with under section/s 19, 20 and 21 FSMA. ( _4 marks_ ) 

- c) Explain the penalties that may be imposed for breach of these requirements. 

( _4 marks_ ) 

- d) Outline the specific issues that arise with regard to whether the Robo advisory programme will itself constitute a regulated activity. ( _4 marks_ ) 

- e) Explain the additional action that the company would have to take if is wishes to expand the range of financial activities that it wishes to carry on through its new online facilities. ( _4 marks_ ) 

## **B67.** 

Henry is a junior compliance officer at Winter Bank. He reports into Charlotte, although she is only two years older than Henry and has only recently been promoted to a manager role. Henry was pleased when Charlotte was promoted, as he saw the amount of hard work and effort she put into getting the promotion, including long hours in the office as well as often answering her phone and emails from home. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Grace, the Compliance Director, who is responsible for overseeing the whole Compliance function within Winter Bank, has assigned Henry and Charlotte to work together on an important project, reviewing Winter Bank’s Anti-Money Laundering processes. They are also instructed to report their findings and progress to the firm’s Money Laundering Reporting Officer (MLRO), Gavin, for the duration of this project. Gavin has a bad reputation within Winter Bank, known as being someone who is surly and unapproachable. Part of this stems from the fact that Gavin is very protective of his personal time - he does not answer calls or emails after he has left the office for the day, and gets angry if anyone tries to contact him after 6:00pm. 

Henry and Charlotte work hard together on the project. Henry is assigned the task of researching the current Anti-Money Laundering laws and regulations, and mapping these next to the bank’s current policies and processes, whilst Charlotte takes responsibility for working with Gavin on gathering the necessary statistics, such as how many reports have been made, the time they took to resolve etc. They have worked well together, but Henry notices that Charlotte is becoming increasingly ‘burned-out’. The amount of effort she put into getting her promotion, her new management responsibilities, and the pressure working with Gavin on this project are clearly getting to her. It gets so bad that Charlotte tells Gavin that she has booked a few days of annual leave next week (which has been approved by Grace), and is going away for a few days. Her flight home is due to land on Thursday night, meaning she will be back in the office on Friday in time for last-minute finalisation of the report before they need to submit it to Grace on Monday. In the project brief, Grace had stated that she needed the report on the Monday so that she could prepare for the Board meeting later in the week, so Henry and Charlotte have plenty of time, and besides, Charlotte assures Henry that her part of the report will be done before she goes on leave. 

However, last thing on Thursday (around 05:00pm) Grace calls Henry and tells him to get the report to her first thing on Friday morning. She sounds very forceful on the phone and says, “I don’t care what you need to do to get this done, but I just need it by 09:00am tomorrow”. Henry is panicked, as Charlotte’s half of the presentation is not in the shared team folder, but in her personal area of Winter Bank’s network. Henry tries to call Charlotte several times, but her phone is turned off and he realises that she must be on 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

the plane. By this time, it is past 06:00pm and Henry knows that if he calls Gavin he will be extremely angry. After receiving an email from Grace saying “I’m leaving for the day. Make sure I have the AML report by 08:30am tomorrow LATEST!”, Henry takes the only option which he thinks is available to him, and convinces a colleague, Jess, to call IT support pretending to be Charlotte to say that she forgot her password, and can they please reset it? Unbelievably, Sam from IT support believes he is talking to Charlotte, resets the password and tells Jess what he has reset it to! Henry thanks Jess profusely, finds the information he needs for the report in Charlotte’s folder, adds it to the report and emails the whole thing to Grace. Before he leaves the office, he resets Charlotte’s password again so that Jess cannot login to her folders – thinking to himself that he will tell Charlotte what he has done in the morning. 

|a)|Who is responsible for Henry gaining access to Charlotte’s personal folder?|
|---|---|
||List all the individuals responsible and discuss to what extent you believe|
||one or more characters are ‘more’ responsible than the rest.<br>(_8 marks_)|
|b)|Outline the risks involved in this situation being able to occur.<br>(_4 marks_)|
|c)|What could have been done to stop this situation occurring?<br>(_4 marks_)|
|d)|Are Grace and Gavin more liable than the other characters in the story?|
||(_4 marks_)|



## **B68.** 

An established bank from the Middle East is considering expand its activities in London and Europe and specifically in the areas of Islamic finance. There has clearly been growing interest in the provision of Islamic finance within the UK and Europe with a large potential customer base being interested in receiving these services. The bank has been advised that it could either establish a representative office and then either a branch or are subsidiary operation in London. It also requires advice as to how it may be able to expand onto the European Continent given how Brexit has proceeded. You are asked to advise on the possible options available and regulatory implications of each. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) Explain the regulatory differences between a representative office and fully authorised institution and the difference between establishing a branch or a subsidiary in London. ( _4 marks_ ) 

- b) Outline the principal statutory restrictions that would apply if the bank wishes to carry out general banking or Islamic banking activities within the UK. ( _4 marks_ ) 

- c) Explain the application procedure that the bank would have to comply with before it could provide any financial services in the UK? ( _4 marks_ ) 

- d) Explain the different functions of the various authorities involved in the oversight of its activities. ( _4 marks_ ) 

- e) To what extent would the new operation in London able to provide financial services in other EU Member States following Brexit? ( _4 marks_ ) 

- **B69.** 

Nina is employed on a graduate training scheme at a boutique wealth management firm, Secure Wealth. As part of her graduate training, Nina has been enrolled as a CISI student, and is working towards achieving her Chartered Wealth Manager Qualification. 

Before joining Secure Wealth, Nina had worked for several summers at a call centre for a large UK bank, which had very clear processes and policies in place with regard to the handling of customer data. This experience was part of the reason why she was selected for the graduate scheme, as Secure Wealth wanted someone to help implement its new General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) compliant data management processes. Over the course of a few months, Nina settles into her new role, which involves working with different teams within Secure Wealth for short periods. She is expected to report directly to the Head of HR (who oversees the graduate scheme), Hilary, but finds herself working the most with the Head of Operations, Ganesh, as he is responsible for implementing the data protection process. Ganesh asks Nina to assist him by ensuring that all the teams she works within are following the process carefully and diligently. Ganesh provides Nina with a checklist, and tasks her with meeting each of the senior 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

people she works with to go through the checklist, which asks some simple questions about how they ensure adherence to the GDPR policy within their teams. 

After a while, Nina is given the opportunity to work with Bob, one of Secure Wealth’s star fund managers. She is really excited to accompany Bob to some of his client meetings, and knows that she is going to learn a lot from him. As soon as she starts working with him, she lets him know that she needs to set up a meeting to run through Ganesh’s GDPR checklist with him. Bob simply replies “Oh, I shouldn’t think he meant for you to do that with me… he knows that I’m reliable.” When Nina insists, Bob replies “Don’t worry about it. I’ll take this up with Ganesh directly. We can just focus on all the interesting things you could do as a wealth manager – don’t need to worry about boring data stuff!” 

As the weeks go on, Nina observes Bob doing some things that are against Secure Wealth’s GDPR policies. He often takes documents with his clients’ personal information on them outside of the office to work on while commuting, and he uses his personal email address as well as his work address to communicate with clients. He also once accepted a call from a client’s husband, and gave him an update about the status of a particular fund in which his wife had invested money. When Nina asked him if this was OK, Bob said “I would never normally do something like that, but she is an important client with a busy life, and I know her husband well. She’s told me before that it’s fine for me to give her husband updates on their investments from time to time.” 

At a regular one-to-one to discuss her progress, Nina brings up her concerns about Bob’s disregard for the data protection processes with Hilary. Hilary tells Nina that she should be more insistent about the meeting to go over Ganesh’s checklist, and lets her know that if she is seriously concerned that there has been a breach of data protection, she should report this to Ganesh, saying “I’m not really responsible for our data processes… that doesn’t sit within my team.” When Nina explains that Bob told her that he would discuss this with Ganesh directly, Hilary seems relieved and says “Problem solved! I’m sure that they’ve already talked about this. Bob is reliable, and probably just preferred to deal directly with Ganesh to take the pressure off you and let you focus on everything you could learn from working with him.” 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

|a)|Which staff member(s) are ultimately responsible for data protection within Secure|Which staff member(s) are ultimately responsible for data protection within Secure|
|---|---|---|
|Wealth?||(_4 marks_)|
|b)|What are the risks associated with this scenario?|(_4 marks_)|
|c)|Has there been a breach of data protection?|(_4 marks_)|
|d)|Could Nina be considered to be a whistleblower?|(_4 marks_)|
|e)|What should Nina do next?|(_4 marks_)|



## **B70.** 

A UK securities firm is considering the implications of the continuing difficulties that have arisen in trying to secure some new trading relationships between the UK and the EU following the UK Referendum vote to leave the EU on 23 June 2016. The firm exercises passport rights under the EU Market in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID) and the EU Capital Requirements Directive (CRD 4) measures. The firm has a representative office in Dublin and is considering transferring some of its operations and staff to Dublin to allow it to continue to service EU clients or engage in EU related trading and other activities which may be restricted to EU authorised firms in future. You are asked to advise on the possible options available. 

- a) Explain the nature of the rights currently available to the bank to carry on regulated activities on either a services or establishment basis in the UK and across the EU. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Explain the application procedure that the bank must comply with before it can carry on any regulated activities in the EU and outside the UK at the present time. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Outline the principal statutory restrictions that may be imposed on the bank if the UK withdraws from the EU Single Market in financial services. _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- d) Explain the options that may be available to the bank to allow it to continue to carry on banking and financial services in the UK and across the EU. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) Advise the bank on the extent to which it would be able to provide financial services in other non-EU countries depending upon the options exercised following the UK’s possible withdrawal from the EU single market in banking and financial services. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

## **B71.** 

As an external consultant, you have been asked to advise the Board of a fund management firm about the threats and opportunities posed by financial technology to their business. The firm sells its products solely to retail investors in the UK via telephone dealing, its website and platforms. All back-office functions are performed in-house.

- b) Assess the potential of these to disrupt the firm’s current business model. 

   - ( _5 marks_ )

- ( _5 marks_ )

( _5 marks_ ) 

## **B72.** 

The compliance department of a large commercial bank operating in London has become concerned with the operations of one of its branches in the North of England. The bank had taken on a group of jewellery businesses as a customer which had been making large cash deposits, although they had stated that they generally only conducted business using cheques and bank and credit cards. Plastic sacks were often used to deposit the cash using wheelbarrows. The regional compliance officer at the bank, who was also the MLRO, was aware of this, although appeared to take little action. One of the junior 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

members staff at the bank noticed that the cash deposits were often made after weekends during which the police had notified the public that large illegal raids had been held in the areas where the jewellery businesses were based. There has also been a series of house burglaries in richer residential areas with the businesses having clients in politically volatile overseas territories. The compliance department had been advised that the member of staff was considering whistleblowing to local newspapers if no action was taken. Money laundering had already been highlighted as an area of concern by the FCA and the bank was under a direction to improve its systems and controls in this area. 

- a) Explain the principal issues or concerns that arise from a money laundering and ethical perspective. ( _4 marks_ ) 

- b) Outline the key statutory offences, defences and penalties, and other key regulatory provisions that may apply. 

   - ( _4 marks_ ) 

- c) Explain any enhanced identification conditions that would apply with regard to new clients being taken on by the bank. 

   - ( _4 marks_ ) 

- d) Outline any institutional regulatory liabilities that may arise. 

_(4 marks)_ 

- e) Explain whether any criminal action may be taken, and the relevance of the bank having failed to act on an earlier direction to improve its systems and controls, and outline any action that the bank may take to limit the severity of any sanctions imposed. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

## **B73.** 

Holly is a junior staff member at a small firm of wealth managers (Health & Wealth), where she has been employed for about a year. Health & Wealth are an FCA regulated firm, but are not a deposit taker or insurance firm. Holly’s job involves supporting all the senior wealth managers in servicing the needs of Health & Wealth’s clients. She reports into Lucy who, as well as being Head of Wealth Management, is the firm’s appointed Money Laundering Reporting Officer. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Holly has been assigned to work with Steven, one of the senior wealth managers, on the account of one of the firm’s important clients, the Harris family. Steven has been assisting the family arrange their assets to reduce the impact of inheritance tax, including setting up various forms of trust arrangements. Regrettably, earlier this year, Mr Harris was diagnosed with cancer and consequently, Steven was asked by the family to review all of Mr Harris’ tax arrangements, which involved requiring Mr Harris’ signature on several documents. 

Steven has worked with the Harris family for years, and the family are his key client account. He therefore instructed Holly to ensure that the required paperwork (which required not only the signature of all the trustees, but also witnessing of all the signatures) was achieved with as little disturbance of the family as possible. 

Unfortunately, after working on the Harris account for a couple of weeks, Holly learnt that her grandmother had sadly passed away, and Lucy granted her request to take a few days off as allowed by Heath & Wealth’s grievance procedure. 

On returning to work, Holly reviewed the Harris family documents and discovered that Mr Harris’s signature was missing in one place. She returned the documents to the family so his signature could be added, and when the document was returned  it was accompanied by a note from Mr Harris’s son suggesting that, Mr Harris was now very unwell, and the family did not think he had long left to live. Holly, being reminded of the recent loss of her grandmother, put the document to one side. A further day passed before Holly once again reviewed the documentation, when she realised to her dismay that although Mr Harris’s signatures were all complete, one of the other trustees –Mr Harris’ son - had not had their signature witnessed as required. 

Imagining the situation in the Harris family household, Holly feels uncomfortable at the prospect of sending the document back but, on the other hand, Mr Harris might die before the document was fully complete and effective. Lucy is on leave and not due to return to the office for another week, so Holly cannot discuss the situation with her. So, Holly arranges a meeting to discuss the situation with Steven, who is furious about the oversight. He tells Holly “this is all your fault! I can’t believe you could have cost me my most important client! Just fix it… I don’t care how!” 

Holly, shaken, returns to her desk and reviews the paperwork again. She can see that the witness – Mrs Green - completed the rest of the form correctly and inserted her signature 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

at all other necessary points. She therefore believes that Mr Harris’ son’s signature was witnessed properly, and the omission of Mrs Green’s signature on this page was an ‘honest mistake’. As all of the parties to the documents are known to her, at least to the extent that their signatures appear frequently on Harris family documents, she decides to sign her own name, Holly Smith, in the witness signature space and, in doing so, effectively act as a witness to the signature. After all, it is only the confirmation of the trustee’s signature and she does not see an alternative solution. She cannot delay matters further, and was told by Steven to do whatever it takes to fix her mistake. 

On returning to the office, Lucy and Holly meet for an update meeting. Holly tells Lucy about the situation with Mr Harris, the difficulties that she encountered in obtaining the necessary signatures and reassures Lucy that she resolved them all, just in time, as sadly Mr Harris had passed away the previous day. Holly also explains to Lucy that there had been a missing witness signature, and she had inserted her own signature to confirm the authenticity of the original. 

- a) Who is at fault for this situation arising? Assess the roles of each of the characters – Holly, Lucy, Steven, Mr Harris’s son and Mrs Green – and establish who (if anyone) you believe to be most at fault. ( _8 marks_ ) 

- b) Which character is ultimately responsible (in a regulatory sense) for this problem? Explain your reasoning. ( _4 marks_ ) 

- c) What does this scenario suggest about the company culture at Health & Wealth? Could changes to the culture prevent a similar issue from arising in the future? 

( _4 marks_ ) 

- d) What, if anything, should Lucy do next regarding Holly, Steven, and/or the Harris family? What are the options available to her, and what are the pros and cons associated with each one? ( _4 marks_ ) 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B74.** 

A group of computer programmers have developed a new form of algorithmic trading as part of a new online trading platform that they have designed. The program can manage proprietary trading and undertake discretionary account management. A medium sized UK securities firm is considering purchasing the platform either to run it on its own website or a separate website. You are asked to advise on the regulatory issues concerned. 

- a) Explain the principal issues that arise from a regulatory perspective in determining whether or not the platform will require authorisation and permission under the FSMA. 

   - ( _4 marks_ ) 

- b) Outline the specific issues that arise with regard to whether the trading programme will itself constitute a regulated activity 

   - ( _4 marks_ ) 

- c) Outline the core obligations that will have to be complied with under ss 19, 20 and 21 FSMA. ( _4 marks_ ) 

- d) Explain the penalties that may be imposed for breach of these requirements. 

( _4 marks_ ) 

- e) Explain the additional action that the company would have to take if it wishes to expand the range of financial activities undertaken through the algorithm and provide additional services through the new online facility. 

( _4 marks_ ) 

## **B75.** 

You are the Compliance Officer for a UK-based fintech firm that has grown rapidly in recent years. The firm’s founder and current CEO, who has been instrumental in developing a strong, customer-focused culture, is stepping down due to health reasons. The Board has shortlisted two candidates for the CEO position: 

**Candidate A:** An internal candidate who has been with the company for five years. They are known for their commitment to the firm's compliance culture and have a strong 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

relationship with both the regulators and the board. However, some stakeholders believe they lack the bold vision needed to keep the company growing at its current pace. 

**Candidate B:** An external candidate from a tech-driven financial services firm abroad, with significant experience in scaling companies quickly. While they bring fresh ideas and strategies for growth, there are concerns that their aggressive approach might overlook regulatory and compliance concerns, potentially putting the firm at risk. 

The Board has requested your regulatory advice, focusing on how each candidate would align with the Senior Managers & Certification Regime (SM&CR), and how the new CEO's responsibilities would impact the firm's future. 

- a) Explain the general purpose and key elements of the Senior Managers & Certification Regime (SM&CR). 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Identify and explain the specific responsibilities that would fall under the new CEO’s Senior Management Function (SMF) within the UK subsidiary. How should these responsibilities influence the Board's decision when selecting the candidate? 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- c) How should the appointment of a new CEO under the SM&CR be handled to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements? What steps must the UK subsidiary take to assess the suitability of each candidate for the role? 

   - _(6 marks)_ 

- d) Considering the principles of the SM&CR, what are the potential risks to the firm’s culture of compliance and ethical behaviour with each candidate? How can the Board mitigate these risks in the selection and onboarding process? 

   - _(6 marks)_

## **B76.** 

A major Chinese telecommunications company wishes to set up a subsidiary operation in the UK to apply for a banking licence to provide a wide range of banking services over mobile telephones. The new business will be named ‘SmartBank’. The company is concerned with potential money laundering and anti-terrorist financing restrictions on its activities. The new operation will initially make available a range of savings, lending and payment facilities using simple keypad instructions with other services, including investment and more complex portfolio management functions, being added subsequently. It will principally market its operations to UK customers initially although customers will be allowed to receive funds from anywhere abroad and to transfer amounts overseas without restriction. It will then expand its customer base to Europe, Africa, Asia, the Americas and elsewhere. You are asked to advise on any criminal offences or penalties that may apply. 

- a) Explain the immediate concerns that may arise with regard to the activities of SmartBank and its initial UK and wider subsequent customer base. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Outline the principal offences and penalties that may apply, either in terms of potential criminal or any terrorist related activity involved, and any possible defences. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Explain the general procedures that you would advise SmartBank to set up to deal with new customers to prevent any offences being committed. 

_(4 marks)_ 

- d) Outline any separate regulatory issues or requirements that may have to be considered. _(4 marks)_ 

- e) In the event that any particular customer or transaction raises specific concerns, explain the action that you would recommend is taken to avoid any criminal or regulatory penalties being imposed. _(4 marks)_

**Section B: Answers**
