## **B78.** 

Footes & co. is a privately-run boutique asset management firm, founded by John Footes in the 19th century. Ownership of the firm has been passed down through the Footes family ever since. 

The firm has a set of core values, established by John Footes, which are: Respect; Integrity; Customer Care; and Honesty (forming the acronym RICH). All staff members, including the family members who sit on the board and within the management team, are expected to abide by these values. 

Mike Footes, the son of John Footes III (current chairman of Footes & co), pursued a career outside the firm after graduating from university. Determined to forge his own path, he joined a rival asset management firm, causing a rift in the family. During his 30s, the tension became so bad that he ended up not speaking with his father for a number of years, until the rift was healed when Mike married and had two children. Step by step, the relationship improved, and John was impressed when Mike told him about his professional successes, rising through the ranks at the firm in which he worked, finally being promoted to managing director. 

In 2016, the chief executive, Sheryl Brogues (not a member of the Footes family) decided to stand down from her post. To John’s delight, Mike informed him that he would be interested in the role and John used his influence as chairman of the board to fast-track Mike’s application for the role. While there were other applicants, it was seen as a foregone conclusion that Mike would step into the role. Richard Footes, head of HR, did his best to remain impartial during the recruitment process (which was difficult, as Mike is his cousin), but in the end, he determined that Mike’s impressive resume and excellent interview marked him apart from the other candidates, and he was appointed as the new chief executive of Footes & co. 

The family were delighted to have Mike join the firm, and a couple of years passed without incident. Mike was known for his strong leadership style, and he would often dominate meetings, yet this was just seen as his way of achieving results. However, in 2018, a complaint was made against Mike by a fellow member of the management team, saying that Mike had been particularly aggressive in a meeting, and that he had shouted at a junior staff member who had disagreed with him. The complaint centred around the fact 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

that this behaviour was not in keeping with the firm’s RICH values (particularly the value of Respect). An investigation was launched, and Mike extended an apology, which was accepted, and the matter was considered closed. However, over the next few months, staff morale at the firm sharply declined, and a couple of key staff members (not part of the Footes family) decided to leave the firm. 

In her exit interview, Lisa, formerly head of compliance at the firm, mentioned to Richard Footes, head of HR, that Mike was a bully. She said he was known for his coarse language and aggressive behaviour, and that these traits were not consistent with the firm’s RICH values. However, she also mentioned that staff were scared to raise concerns, especially if they were not members of the Footes family. Lisa asked Richard to keep their conversation confidential, saying: “I only told you this because I trust you. I’m sorry for putting you in a difficult position, as I would not want to be in your shoes.” 

Richard recognises that he has been put in a difficult position. He is part of the Footes family, and doesn’t want to upset the relationship between Mike and his father that was only restored a few years ago. However, if Mike’s behaviour is really that bad, it has the potential to impact the business, and with it, the family. 

- _a)_ Outline some options (minimum of three) available to Richard in this situation, and set out the merits and disadvantages associated with each. _(6 marks)_ 

- b) The Footes & co. core values are Respect, Integrity, Care and Honesty. What are the CISI’s four key values, set out in the Code of Conduct and represented by the acronym ‘HOTF’? What is the meaning behind each value? _(2 marks)_ 

- c) Draft a brief speak up/whistleblowing policy that you feel would be appropriate for this business. _(8 marks)_ 

- d) Identify the factors that led to this situation being able to occur, and the risks that could have been identified at an earlier stage. _(4 marks)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers**

## **B80.** 

Will is a recent graduate, who has just started work at a small wealth management firm. Will is gay and lives with his boyfriend. They are both out to their family and friends. When Will started at the firm, however, he was intimidated about starting a new job and meeting new people, so hasn’t officially come out at work yet – preferring to talk about his ‘partner’, rather than his boyfriend, and not correcting people when they assume his partner is female. 

The CEO, Seth, is the biggest culprit of making this assumption. Will has overheard Seth make comments about LGBTQ+ people which he appeared to think were funny, but actually boarded on derogatory and rude, if not actually offensive. As a result, Will feels intimidated about coming out at work. This is very stressful for him, as he had no desire to go ‘back in the closet’, he simply wanted to feel comfortable around his new colleagues before divulging personal information. Now, however, he worries that he may never be able to be his true self at work, which he finds upsetting and demotivating. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Will is shadowing Seth on some of his client meetings. One day, they have a scheduled appointment with some new clients – a couple – Dr Chris Glad and Mrs Heather Glad. Will notices that Seth is taken aback when the clients arrive and are both women, although they seem to take it in their stride, explaining that some people often expect Christine to be male due to the fact she prefers to go by Chris. The rest of the meeting is pleasant, although Will feels that Seth is a little tense and stand-offish, and the Glads seem happy with the recommendations given to them and the overview of service available. Their overall assets are on the lower side compared to the kind of clients the form usually accepts, but Dr Glad’s potential future earnings are significant, which would normally mean that the couple are a good candidate for becoming a long-standing client. 

After the meeting, Seth turns to Will and exclaims “Well, that was awkward! Can you believe they were a couple of lezzers?! I’ll have to think hard before deciding whether to take them on as clients, especially since their assets are far below what we would normally expect of our clients.” Will, stunned at Seth’s attitude and his use of a homophobic term, mumbles something incoherent and explains he needs to go and start writing up the notes of the meeting. 

A couple of weeks pass, and Will notices that Seth has sent a letter to Dr and Mrs Glad explaining that, unfortunately, he will not be able to take them on as clients, as their assets do not meet the required threshold. It is a polite letter, which wishes them all the best for the future. However, Will is aware that a heterosexual client couple has recently been taken on who have assets lower than the Glads, and with lower future earning potential. Will wonders whether Seth has deliberately discriminated against Dr and Mrs Glad due to their sexuality. Even if this is not the case, he remains unhappy about the language Seth has used in relation to the LGBTQ+ community and is now deciding what to do. 

- a) Outline four options available to Will in this situation and set out the merits and disadvantages associated with each of the option you have identified, and which option would you take. _(8 marks)_ 

- b) HOTF is an acronym used in the CISI Code of Conduct and Principles – what does HOTF stand for? _(1 mark)_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c) Please provide all five FCA individual COCON rules. _(5 marks)_ 

- d) The FCA has recently been commenting on transforming culture in financial services and in particular the promotion of a safe environment. “We mean that for a culture to be healthy, leaders at all levels need to foster an environment in which employees feel comfortable to express their opinions and crucially, are listened to when they do.” 

Please give three reasons why it is important to promote a speak up environment. 

_(6 marks)_ 

## **B81.** 

A medium sized European bank has a locally authorised subsidiary operation in London. The bank specialises in providing savings and lending facilities as well as international money transfer services across the EU. The capital of the bank is low following a number of years of low interest rates. The banks deposit base is also relatively small with the firm dependent on wholesale and interbank markets to fund its London and European operations. The bank’s ability to raise wholesale funding has been further restricted due to the difficulties experienced in interbank markets following the onslaught of the Coronavirus (Covid 19) crisis at the beginning of 2020 despite the support programmes set up by different central banks and authorities across the EU. You work in the compliance department of the bank and have been asked to advise on the following: 

- a) Explain the extent to which the bank may have breached its threshold conditions applicable to its London operations with the legal effects of this and whether the bank should discuss this with the regulatory authorities. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Identify the other principal regulatory provisions within the FCA Handbook that may be relevant and have been breached. _(4 marks)_ 

- c) Identify the remedial action that the authorities may require the bank to take in connection with any breach of its threshold conditions. _(4 marks)_

**Section B: Answers** 

d) The bank is specifically concerned with how it should manage customers in difficulty during the crisis and asked for specific guidance on this. 

_(4 marks)_ 

e) Explain the further disciplinary action that the authorities may take in this case. _(4 marks)_

## **B83.** 

You are the Head of Compliance in a small firm of private client stockbrokers which, because of its limited resources, contracts out its ‘back office’ functions to a third party, Scarlet, although it does employ a small administration team to manage matters at your end. 

Colin, one of the partners, asks you urgently into his office to explain that he has just received a telephone call from a firm of solicitors, Helium, who are executors for the estate of Godfrey, one of your firm’s long standing clients, who had been unwell in a residential home for a number of years and had died a few weeks earlier. 

The substance of the telephone call was that the value of the securities held by your firm was significantly lower than Helium had been led to believe and that there were a number of unexplained transactions in the statements that had been sent to Godfrey, who appeared not to have opened them. 

While Colin appears concerned, he says that he is sure that there is an innocent explanation, suggests that it is probably Scarlet’s fault (again) and asks you to look into the matter and report back to him with your initial findings the following morning. 

You return to your office to consider what steps to take, what compliance issues may arise and the impact that these may have. 

- a) Outline the principal issues that might be of concern to you and briefly explain why. 

   - _(4 marks)_ 

- b) Explain the processes that your firm should have in place for responding to client complaints. _(6 marks)_ 

After you have spoken to Scarlet about what has transpired and asked them to look into the transactions in Godfrey’s name, they telephone you and say that all of the transactions were initiated by your firm and signed off by Nicola, the former head of your administrative team, who left the firm three months ago to move to Australia. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c) Prepare a briefing to Colin setting out your concerns, explain the reason for these concerns and suggest the action that needs to be taken to deal with the situation. 

_(10 marks)_

## **B85.** 

A young Financial Technology (FinTech) platform has been developing its business plan and model operations from a shared WeWork office space in Old Street in London. It has developed a new set of integrated computer algorithms with exceptional speed and low latency, which can replicate the functions of a major investment bank including carrying out dealing, arranging deals and investment management (on a discretionary or nondiscretionary basis). This may also be extended to provide investment advisory services (and bots) later. The platform has been operating within a FinTech incubator maintained by a larger UK commercial bank, although it now wishes to make an application to enter the FCA Regulatory Sandbox. ## **B86.** 

Green Finance Investments Ltd (GFI) has recently launched its EcoSustain Fund, which is marketed as being "100% sustainable" and "carbon neutral". The fund has attracted a significant number of investors, and the company anticipates strong performance, with the potential for substantial bonuses for all staff if the fund does well. 

During a routine internal review conducted by the compliance department, and specifically against the Financial Conduct Authority’s (FCA’s) Anti-Greenwashing Rule, discrepancies were found between the sustainability claims made in the marketing materials and the actual investments in the fund. Despite these findings, David, the Head of Sustainable Investment and a member of a professional body, decides not to update the marketing materials immediately. He argues that the fund's overall positive impact justifies the current approach and believes that addressing these discrepancies could jeopardise the fund’s success and the expected bonuses. 

Sarah, a Senior Compliance Officer and member of the Chartered Institute for Securities & Investment (CISI), raises concerns about the ethical implications of continuing to market the fund with potentially misleading information. She is torn between her responsibility to 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

uphold ethical standards and the pressure to support the firm’s success and the financial benefits that could follow. 

a) Outline the FCA’s Anti-Greenwashing Rule and explain how it would apply to the EcoSustain Fund in this case. 

_(6 marks)_ 

b) Identify the ethical issues arising from David’s behaviour in this situation and outline the potential professional implications for him. 

_(4 marks)_ 

c) Analyse the broader potential risks to GFI Investments Ltd. and its leadership if the ethical issues related to the EcoSustain Fund are not addressed. Consider specifically both reputational and regulatory consequences. 

_(6 marks)_ 

d) Discuss the actions Sarah should take in response to David’s decision, referencing the CISI Code of Conduct. What ethical and professional obligations does she have in this situation? 

_(4 marks)_

## **B89.** 

Satish is an ambitious junior manager in the certifications team of a regulated wealth management firm. The firm experienced a difficult period some years ago, when it struggled to keep up with a greatly increased flow of regulatory reporting, the consequences of which led to adverse comments and a warning of potential sanction by the regulator. 

As a result of these regulatory findings, the firm had recruited more staff but, given the COVID-19 pandemic, a number of people have been put on furlough, the majority of staff are working from home and there seems to be a growing trend within the company to make decisions in isolation. There is more work to do with fewer people and some managers are taking an ‘out of sight, out of mind’ attitude by reducing interactions with their teams on a day-to-day basis. 

Satish, however, is conscientious and knows that the new certifications reporting process is very important. One of the biggest changes is the introduction of an internal reporting system, which is seen as increasingly onerous and somewhat irrelevant by those not involved in the firm’s earlier problems. As a result, the requirement for an e-signature by nominated position holders is not always followed to the letter. 

Approaching the new reporting deadline, Satish is asked by Eamonn, his manager, to ensure that the new departmental management information (MI), which Satish coordinates and analyses, is up to date for discussion at the heads of division meeting, which is taking place on Zoom. Bearing in mind the previous and well-documented difficulties of his team, Eamonn will likely be the focus of attention as he will need to give an update on the new regulatory reporting process to the board. 

Satish is feeling under some pressure, especially with the reduced headcount, and is working longer hours at home, including time at the weekends. He is determined to meet all his deadlines, conscious that his chances of promotion will be influenced by how well 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

he performs at times like this, and fearful that he may be let go by the company if he doesn’t, given the economic environment. 

The end of the reporting period approaches and Eamonn begins to press Satish for the MI in their online daily meetings. The firm’s regulatory reporting unit also reminds Satish that his department’s report is due and that it needs to be signed off by Eamonn. 

As his deadlines loom, Satish calls Eamonn and shares the MI report on screen, taking the opportunity to remind him to officially sign off the department’s regulatory report, which will be ready the next day. Sounding exasperated, Eamonn says that he is too busy preparing for his meeting to be concerned about “that sort of stuff” and suggests to Satish that, if he is confident that it is correct, he should sign it on Eamonn’s behalf. The firm’s strict guidelines on the sign-off process stipulate that this can only be done by Eamonn, but Satish feels unable to say anything. 

The following day, his department’s regulatory report is completed and sent to Satish to obtain Eamonn’s signature. Satish tells his colleague that he will see whether he can get Eamonn to e-sign it, but he is extremely busy, and the meeting is soon. Satish calls Eamonn to ask him to sign the report. “I told you yesterday that you could sign it” says Eamonn. “It’s only a report and you know what it is all about. You have my complete confidence – so take some personal responsibility”. 

a) Outline four options available to Satish. 

_(4 marks)_ 

b) Explain the merits and disadvantages associated with two of these options. 

_(4 marks)_ 

c) List two principles from the Code of Conduct that would be relevant for Satish to consider and explain why. _(4 marks)_ 

d) What are the CISI four key values, set out in the Code of Conduct and represented by the acronym ‘CISI’? What is the meaning behind each value? _(2 marks)_ 

e) Identify three risks from this scenario and explain how they could have been mitigated at an earlier stage. _(6 marks)_

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B90.** 

A boutique asset management firm in Hong Kong is considering relocating part of its operations back to the UK and expanding into the EU, European Economic Area (EEA) and Switzerland, where it sees specific growth opportunities. The firm has to review its physical and remote office and operational arrangements and relevant legal and regulatory compliance requirements. The board have decided that the firm will need to have a core operation in London which can provide a full range of discretionary investment management, advisory and dealing operations either from its physical offices or online. It will also wish to expand the same services on the European continent and into lucrative Switzerland. It would separately wish to have a presence in the US and provide marketing and promotional services there initially and then later replicate its London operations in New York, Boston and San Francisco. You are asked to advise on the various regulatory options available. 

a) Explain the principal options that may be available in terms of either operating a branch, subsidiary or representative office and outline the regulatory advantages and disadvantages of each. _(4 marks)_ 

b) Outline the principal statutory restrictions that will apply with regard to the proposed London operations. _(4 marks)_ 

c) Outline the principal EU directives or regulations that would apply if the firm decides to establish an operation in the EU or EEA. _(4 marks)_ 

d) Explain the nature of the rights available to the firm if it establishes in London to carry on activities within the EU, the EEA or Switzerland, on either a services or establishment basis. _(4 marks)_ 

e) Explain the extent to which the firm would be able to provide financial services in other non-EU or non-EEA countries depending upon the options exercised. _(4 marks)_

## **B91.** 

A newly established digital bank in London is considering the most effective manner in which to carry out its various compliance functions. It has been advised that one option would be to outsource certain functions either to larger legacy banking and financial groups or to specialist third party service providers that support similar smaller platforms and firms. It is specifically interested in outsourcing its Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and terrorist financing customer identification and other compliance obligations due to the significant administrative burden and costs that these generate. 

You are asked to advise the managing team of the bank on the following: 

a) Explain the principal issues or concerns that arise from a money laundering and ethical perspective. _(4 marks)_ b) Outline the key provisions that apply with regard to the outsourcing of relevant compliance and regulatory obligations. _(4 marks)_ c) Outline the key statutory offences, defences and penalties and other key regulatory provisions that may apply. _(4 marks)_ d) Explain the identification conditions that would apply with regard to new clients joining the site. _(4 marks)_ e) Outline the institutional liability issues that may arise. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B92.** 

The auditors of a large investment bank operating in London have asked for a review of the firm’s procedures for managing its benchmark rate and liquidity operations. The bank is involved with benchmark rate setting in London and had misreported some of its submissions during 2021 through an internal reporting error which has since been corrected. An increased liquidity requirement had also been imposed on its operations by the PRA which it breached at the same time through other systems failures which have again been resolved. The directors had nevertheless decided not to notify these events to 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

the PRA for a four-month period until the issue could be resolved internally. The board of the bank is now anxious to ensure that any potential liabilities that may arise in this regard are reduced in so far as possible. 

You are asked to advise on the following: 

a) Outline the principal statutory offences that may apply with regard benchmark rate manipulation and any defences and penalties. _(4 marks)_ 

b) Outline any separate statutory civil prohibitions that may apply with any defences and relevant penalties. _(4 marks)_ 

c) Explain the significance of the decision to delay reporting the systems failures to the PRA and any wider regulatory conduct or systems breaches that have to be considered. 

_(4 marks)_ 

d) Explain the range of penalties that the regulatory authorities in the UK and elsewhere may consider imposing on the bank if any breaches arise. _(4 marks)_ 

e) Explain any action that might be taken to reduce the penalty or avoid any regulatory action in future. _(4 marks)_ 

## **B93.** 

Jane has worked as a customer services administrator for Identity Finance, a mediumsized wealth management firm, for five years. The job is a stepdown for Jane, who had previously been head of customer relations at a prestigious firm, but, at 64 years old, she had wanted to take on a less taxing role before she retired. Nevertheless, because of her experience and reputation for excellent customer service, Identity pay her a higher than average salary. 

The CEO, Seth, is the biggest culprit of making this assumption. Will has overheard Jane’s job involves liaising with clients via email and phone – everything from booking appointments to managing complaints. Over the years, clients have come to trust and rely on Jane, and she takes her responsibility as a brand ambassador seriously. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Knowing that Jane wishes to retire before turning 70, senior management at Identity begin making contingency plans for when she eventually leaves, tasking the head of operations, Sammy, with finding a solution. They cannot afford to hire another person of Jane’s calibre and sterling reputation, so Sammy looks for alternatives, one of which is a new chat software run by AI Made Simple. AI Made Simple’s director of development assures Sammy that the service their ‘chatbot’ function offers is as good as the real thing and that it can ‘learn’ Jane’s tone and preferred language, thereby providing a seamless transition for when Jane retires. 

Sammy presents the idea of a chatbot to the Board, saying that it will not only replace Jane, but is also cheaper than hiring a person, and will streamline all client communications, including messaging via a chatbot on the company’s website. The software can chat to more than one client at a time and will record all client satisfaction data, plus information about the types of queries received, response time, and how the problems are resolved. The chair of the Board is impressed, but some members express reservations about moving to AI. They cite the trust the clients have in Jane and wonder whether they will be happy speaking to a robot instead. The Board advises Sammy to ensure a smooth transition to the new program and comes up with the idea of calling the bot ‘Jane’, so clients do not notice any difference in the service. 

The software is added to the firm’s system and starts to ‘learn’ from Jane (the person). AI Made Simple calls this a digital handover, whereby the system can learn to replicate her turns of phrase and tone. Jane’s picture, with a friendly greeting message, is added to the chat function. Jane agrees to this, as she is eager to ensure a smooth transition for the clients after she retires. 

Jane’s last day arrives, and she is bid a heartfelt farewell. The next day, Jane the bot takes over. The launch seems to go well, with Jane proving to be efficient and helpful for clients. Nobody seems to notice they are talking to a robot! 

A couple of months later, however, several complaints are received by Alfie, the head of client services, about Jane. Clients say that Jane is unreliable, does not properly respond to their queries, and seems rude and dismissive. Unaware that they have been speaking to a chatbot, clients ruefully ask Alfie to take disciplinary action against Jane. Alfie 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

apologises to the clients, informs them that the matter will be dealt with, and contacts AI Made Simple, who adjust the software to fix the problems. 

At its next meeting, the Board reviews the usual papers, which include information about the number of complaints received. Usually, complaints about staff members are reported to the FCA, but on this occasion it appears that no client services complaints have been received or escalated to the regulator, which is unusual. Alfie explains that company policy states that complaints about staff members must be escalated, and therefore complaints about Jane (a computer program) do not fall under this category. 

- a) What are the CISI’s four key values, set out in the Code of Conduct and represented by the acronym ‘CISI’? What is the meaning behind each value? 

## _**(4 marks)**_ 

- b) Identify three factors that led to the situation being able to occur, and the risks that could have been identified at an earlier stage. 

## _**(6 marks)**_ 

- c) List four of the five FCA individual Code of Conduct (COCON) rules. 

## _**(4 marks)**_ 

- d) Outline some options of the Board (minimum of three) to deal with the problem, and set out the merits and disadvantages associated with each. 

## _**(6 marks)**_

## **B97.** 

The complaints handling division of a major financial services firm receives a disturbing phone call. In the phone call, a customer states that they have purchased an investment product from your firm through an agent. The customer elaborates that on the maturity of the product they contacted the agent as to the payout. The customer then claims they were told they were not entitled to a payout from the product due to an unexplained internal process. The customer is very annoyed, claims the agent has acted deceitfully and has suggested contacting the Financial Ombudsman Service unless this matter is resolved promptly. Following up on the case, you contact the agent who confirms the customer was sold an investment product. Further the agent argues the customer is not entitled to a payout due to the agent’s internal discussions and policies. a) How should the firm respond to this concern raised by the employee?

## **Answers** 

## **B1.** 

a) The MiFID system was developed and redeveloped to ensure that advice on the provision of investments has been focused on providing appropriate investments for clients and that any advice provided is appropriate for the needs and the circumstances of the client. This might include many issues, for instance understanding the client’s income, commitments, assets and ability to make decisions. Under the MiFID rules the firm needs to undertake a suitability assessment of the client’s needs to inform any advice provided as to whether the client should buy, sell or hold an investment or other financial instrument. After the advice has been provided a copy of the suitability assessment and why the decisions as to the type/content of advice provided were made should be provided to the client. In some cases, repeated suitability assessment reports should be forwarded to the client. It is essential the overall advice leads to an outcome which is in the best interests of the client. 

This information would include the following: 

- the types of service, transaction, and financial instrument with which the client is familiar. 

- the nature, volume, and frequency of the client’s transactions in financial instruments and the period over which they have been carried out. 

- the level of education and profession/relevant former profession of the client/potential client. 

This information is used to then inform any advice provided. Such advice should adhere to the following: 

- meets the client’s investment objectives, including risk tolerance. 

- the client has the financial ability to bear any related investment risks consistent with their investment objectives. 

- the client has the necessary experience and knowledge to understand the risks involved in the transaction or in the management of their portfolio. 

For non-MiFID related investment advice, the broad expectations are similar. Any advice should account for the client’s knowledge and experience of making investment decisions, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

the circumstances of the client such as their financial situation and risk tolerance. Again, a suitability report should be provided under many circumstances. There are outcomes where such advice is not required. 

b) Assessing the suitability of clients is a core activity in the provision of investment advice. Central to this requirement is ensuring that the risks a client is prepared to take are considered and incorporated in the advice and guidance provided to clients. Specifically, and as stated in the workbook, a firm should ensure it has a robust process for assessing the risks a client is willing and able to take, including: 

- assessing a customer’s capacity for loss. 

- identifying customers that are best suited to placing their money in cash deposits because they are unwilling or unable to accept the risk of loss of capital. 

- appropriately interpreting customer responses to questions and not attributing inappropriate weight to certain answers. 

- tools, where used, are fit for purpose and any limitations recognised and mitigated. 

- any questions and answers that are used to establish the risk a customer is willing and able to take, and descriptions used to check this, are fair, clear and not misleading. 

- it has a robust and flexible process for ensuring investment selections are suitable, given a customer’s investment objectives and financial situation, and it understands the nature and risks of products or assets selected for customers, and 

- it engages customers in a suitability assessment process (including risk-profiling) which acts in the best interests of those customers. 

c) There are many actions required when discovering a range of practices which raise concerns as to the form and content of advice provided to clients. It should be priority to undertake a thorough review of these practices. This would involve a past business review and a root cause analysis of the forms of advice which raise concerns. At the heart of such a review is an examination as to whether customers have been in any way disadvantaged. These reviews need to be undertaken in a highly structured and transparent manner as there is a high possibility the financial regulator will need to be informed as to this review being undertaken with outside scrutiny of this examination. If concerns are seen to have disadvantaged customers, the potential for future actions such as remediation is present. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

d) The Financial Advice Market Review (FAMR) was released in 2016 and provides several recommendations. These include: 

1. Affordability – can clients afford financial advice, developing robo-advice etc. delivering tailored advice and enhancing the ability of the public to make financial decisions? 

2. Accessibility – building trust in the market for financial advice and increasing the ability for people to access these services at reasonable cost. 

3. Liabilities and consumer redress – enhancing the funding of the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. 

In the evaluation of these recommendations in 2020 the FCA indicated that progress had been made in improving the market, yet this progress had been very slow. Most financial advice still is provided to the relatively wealthy and most advice is holistic considering the scope of a client’s financial commitments and circumstances. Further robo-advice has been quite slow to develop and a significant proportion of the population of the UK has funds to effectively invest yet are still reluctant to undertake this decision due to continued uncertainties with seeking and using financial advice. 

## **B2.** 

This question asked candidates the FCA’s Consumer Duty within the context of a fictional case study involving the sale of insurance and critical illness policies to consumers. 

a) The first part of the question asked candidates how the situation relates to the FCA’s Consumer Duty, and what key principles one of the characters of the case study may potentially have breached. Responses missed marks by predominantly focusing on the individual’s behaviour without recognising the firm’s wider failings. 

Several FCA Principles for Businesses appear to have been breached in this scenario, both by the individual sales representative and the firm as a whole. The conduct raises serious concerns under Principle 1 (Integrity), as there was a failure to act honestly by downplaying the costs and features of the policy. There is also a breach of Principle 2 (Skill, Care and Diligence), as the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

individual did not act with appropriate care when recommending a complex and costly product to a vulnerable customer with a limited budget. Principle 6 (Customers’ Interests) is likely breached, as neither the individual nor the firm acted in the customer’s best interests. The recommendation appears to have been motivated by commission incentives, and the firm’s culture may have enabled or encouraged this behaviour. Principle 7 (Communications with Clients) is also relevant, as the individual failed to provide clear, fair, and not misleading information, resulting in a financial decision the customer did not fully understand. 

The introduction of Principle 12 (Consumer Duty) further underlines the seriousness of these breaches. Both the individual and the firm likely failed to support customer understanding, did not properly assess vulnerability, and disregarded affordability constraints. These failures could have been avoided with a proper suitability assessment, but the lack of due diligence and care led to a poor customer outcome. The firm may also be in breach of its obligations to ensure that remuneration and incentive structures do not conflict with the customer’s best interests. The behaviour was clearly influenced by a poorly designed bonus scheme, and the firm failed to monitor or mitigate the risks associated with sales-driven conduct. 

b) The second question of the case study asked candidate what steps the firm should take to ensure compliance with Consumer Duty in situations like this. Responses missed marks by providing broad explanations of the Consumer Duty without applying it to the specific details of the scenario. They referred to fairness or positive customer outcomes in general terms but failed to link their answers to the cross-cutting rules or the four required outcomes, resulting in responses that lacked precision and regulatory alignment. 

To ensure compliance with the Consumer Duty, the firm must adopt a structured approach that addresses both the cross-cutting rules, and the four outcomes set out by the FCA. Acting in good faith means putting the customer’s needs ahead of commercial targets. This requires designing sales processes and incentive structures that reflect the customer’s best interests rather than focusing solely on financial gain. Firms must also take active steps to support and enable customers to make informed decisions. In this case, the customer did not receive adequate information or support to properly understand the product being purchased. All product communications must be clear, fair, and not misleading, with advisers checking the customer’s understanding throughout the sales process. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Firms also have an obligation to avoid causing foreseeable harm. Indicators such as a limited budget or financial vulnerability should be identified early in the advice process. A thorough affordability and vulnerability assessment should be conducted, and any recommendation should reflect the customer’s actual needs and financial capacity. When considering the four outcomes, the firm must ensure its products and services are designed with vulnerable customers in mind, using available data and insight to assess whether product features and risks are suitable for the intended market. 

The price and value outcome are particularly relevant in this scenario. A high-cost product was sold that offered features unnecessary for the customer’s circumstances and which were not well understood. To improve consumer understanding, the firm should strengthen its communication strategy by simplifying documentation, providing explanatory tools for complex terms, and assessing comprehension at key stages in the advice process. Communications must be accessible and appropriate for varying levels of financial literacy. 

Finally, firms must clearly define and adhere to their target markets. Sales practices should be reviewed regularly to ensure that products are being sold appropriately, and that customers at greater risk of harm are not being mis-sold unsuitable solutions. Ongoing sales monitoring, frequent staff training, and root cause analysis of complaints are essential to building a customerfocused culture that aligns with the expectations of the Consumer Duty. 

c) The third section of this question asked candidates what the potential regulatory consequences for the firm and its sales representative are if they are found to have failed in their duty to the customer. Responses missed marks by failing to explain both the regulatory and personal consequences of breaching Consumer Duty, often focusing only on firm-level penalties without addressing potential disciplinary action for individuals. Some overlooked key powers available to the FCA, such as imposing enhanced reporting obligations, ordering remediation programmes, or requiring changes to sales incentives. 

If the FCA determines that there has been a breach of Consumer Duty obligations, both the individual involved, and the firm could face serious consequences. The firm may be required to compensate affected customers through a formal remediation process. Financial penalties could be imposed, particularly if the regulator identifies systemic failings in sales practices. The firm might also be subjected to heightened regulatory scrutiny, including mandatory audits and more stringent oversight of its insurance sales activities. Where widespread issues are identified, the firm could be instructed to revise its incentive structures or eliminate certain high-risk sales 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

behaviours. Additional reporting obligations may be introduced, requiring the firm to provide regular updates demonstrating progress in addressing compliance weaknesses. 

On an individual level, disciplinary action may include suspension, financial penalties, or prohibition from working in regulated financial services. If there is evidence of deliberate customer deception, this could trigger enforcement action with long-term consequences for career and reputation. In serious cases, personal liability may arise under Section 138D of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000, allowing affected customers to seek damages for breaches of regulatory requirements. 

Beyond legal and regulatory consequences, reputational damage can be substantial. Findings of mis-selling can erode consumer trust, attract negative media attention, and lead to the loss of clients and partners. In cases where misconduct is proven to be systemic or cultural, the firm may even face restrictions on its permissions or, in extreme circumstances, a revocation of its licence to operate in the insurance sector. 

## **B3.** 

Again a typical client categorisation question from the examiner. This needed a good explanation from you of the requirements of the rules regarding categorisation criteria, documentation and record keeping. 

- a) Every client must be categorised under COBS before a firm commences investment business with or for the client. This requires the firm to take steps to establish whether the client is a retail client, a professional client or an eligible counterparty. 

Since the client in this case is an individual, only retail client or professional client status will be relevant. All persons who do not fall into any other category must be categorised as retail clients (who receive the greatest protection under COBS). 

The client here does not meet the criteria to be categorised as a per se professional client. He could be either a retail client or an elective professional client. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Firms are required to retain an appropriate record of the categorisation of each client, including sufficient information to support the categorisation. Then records must be retained for an appropriate period of time – usually three years (five years in the case of MiFID business and indefinitely in the case of pension transfers and opt-outs). 

- b) If the client is initially categorised as a retail client, he may be re-categorised as an elective professional client if the form complies with the qualitative test and the procedures. The qualitative test requires the firm to assess the client as capable of making his own investment decisions and understanding the risks involved. The procedures require the client to state in writing that he wishes to be treated as a professional client; he must be given a clear written warning by the firm of the protections and compensation rights he may lose; and he must state in writing that he is aware of the consequences of this. (There is also a quantitative test in the case of MiFID business which relates to the frequency of the client’s transactions, the value of his portfolio and his professional financial sector working experience.) 

If the client is initially categorised as an elective professional client, he may be recategorised as a retail client either at the client’s own request or when the firm becomes aware that the client no longer fulfils the relevant requirements for elective professional client status. 

- c) There are three main items of documentation that the firm must provide to a client before conducting regulated activities: 

- i.  Client agreement. This sets out the essential rights and obligations of the firm and the client and it is required when a firm carries on designated investment business for a retail client and (in relation to MiFID business) for a professional client. 

- ii.   Information about the firm, its services and remuneration. This must be provided in a durable medium either before any service is provided or (if this is impossible) as soon as possible thereafter. 

- iii.   Order execution policy. In order to fulfil its obligations under the best execution rule, the firm must provide its clients with its order execution policy and obtain the client’s consent to this policy before transacting any business. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

You should discuss the suitability COBS (for discretionary) and appropriateness COBS (for execution-only trades) in derivatives. 

- d) If the client is categorised as an elective professional client, the firm would need to keep this status under review in order to ensure that it continues to be appropriate. In addition, the firm would need to keep under review its basic documentation (and notify the client of changes, as necessary): 

   - Information about the firm (including contact details, conflicts of interest policy, client assets arrangements, costs and charges, etc); 

   - Client agreement (changes to terms of business); 

   - Order execution policy (changes to policy, execution venues, etc). 

Other documentation could include: 

- Initial disclosure documents and menus (packaged products); 

- Occasional and periodic reporting; 

- Compliance with record keeping rules. 

## **B4.** 

This question looks at the application of the general prohibition under s19 and the need for permission with regard to collective investment schemes under FSMA. 

- a) Reference should be made to the general prohibition under s19 and the need for permission. The concepts of authorisation and exemption should have been explained and the relationship between authorisation and permission explained. As the company is non-EEA then the provisions for passporting under MiFID or UCITS or other treaties do not arise, and so permission under Part 4A of FSMA will be required. The application procedures including the threshold conditions should be set out in your answer. 

- b) The definition of a collective investment scheme set out in FSMA should have been referred to. In addition you could also set out the different types of CIS and the general requirements under FSMA. 

- c) In addition to the activities of establishing operating, and winding up a collective investment scheme the other relevant activities could include the following: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Arranging deals, dealing, safe custody, advising, investment management and offering to perform regulated activities. 

- d) Reference should have been made to the general restriction on financial promotion (s21) even though the question focuses upon the difference between regulated and unregulated schemes. A regulated scheme (which would include authorised unit trusts, OEICs and other recognised schemes) may be freely marketed. All other schemes are generally unregulated and subject to marketing restrictions. Such unregulated schemes may be sold to those such as eligible counterparties, professional clients and certain other exempt persons under the Financial Promotions Order. Otherwise the firm would have to establish the suitability of such schemes which would be possible in the case of persons who are or who have been a participant in such schemes within the previous 30 months, or existing or new clients for whom the firm has established that such investments are suitable. 

You may also have expanded your answer with consideration of financial promotions such as the need to be fair, clear and not misleading, and the differences between real and non-real time promotions and direct offer financial promotions. 

- e) Here the possibility of authorisation being available under the relevant EU directives (i.e. passporting) should have been mentioned, whether for the firm performing investment services and activities under MiFID (and these should have been explained and contrasted with ancillary services) or for the scheme under UCITS. 

## **B5.** 

This question provided a case study on how a firm’s rapid growth can expose weaknesses in its compliance framework, highlighting the need for strategic oversight, effective risk management, and enhanced regulatory controls to address operational gaps and prepare for increased scrutiny. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

a) The first question under this scenario asked candidates what the key compliance risks faced by the firm are, and why effective governance is important in managing these risks. 

The compliance weaknesses identified in the scenario, such as failures in transaction monitoring, staff training, and accountability, could have serious regulatory and operational consequences. These weaknesses could lead to regulatory breaches, increased financial crime risk, and harm to clients or market integrity. The FCA’s Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls (SYSC) rules require firms to have effective systems and controls in place to manage their regulatory obligations. Inadequate compliance infrastructure would breach SYSC requirements, particularly SYSC 6, which outlines obligations regarding internal controls. Under the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SMCR), specific individuals are accountable for ensuring appropriate systems are in place within their areas of responsibility. If the failings identified result in consumer harm or regulatory breaches, senior managers may be subject to regulatory enforcement. The firm may also be seen as failing to meet key threshold conditions, which require that authorised firms are properly resourced and effectively managed. In this context, poor compliance oversight could lead to FCA intervention, enforcement action, or reputational harm. 

b) The second question under this case study asked candidates what steps the Head of Compliance should take to enhance compliance oversight and ensure regulatory obligations are met. 

To enhance compliance oversight, the Head of Compliance should first ensure that the firm has adequate governance and control frameworks in place. This includes improving internal systems, clearly defining roles and responsibilities, and ensuring that governance structures meet SYSC expectations. She should also work closely with senior management to ensure compliance with SMCR requirements, including the appointment of appropriately accountable senior managers (e.g. SMF16 and SMF17) and regular review of their responsibilities. Improving resourcing within the compliance function is also critical. The Head of Compliance should make a case for increased budget to recruit additional compliance staff or invest in compliance technology, particularly for transaction monitoring and training systems. She should lead a 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

compliance risk assessment, strengthen internal reporting procedures, and ensure that senior management receives timely and accurate information about key risks and breaches. To reinforce effectiveness, the Head of Compliance should also consider periodic independent reviews of the compliance function and recommend structured, ongoing training programmes for all staff. Together, these measures will ensure that the firm’s compliance function is equipped to meet regulatory expectations and support sustainable growth. 

c) The third question asked candidates to discuss how senior management can support the compliance function and why a strong compliance culture is essential for the firm. 

Senior management plays a pivotal role in supporting the compliance function by setting the tone from the top. They must allocate sufficient resources and funding to compliance, ensuring the function is adequately staffed and equipped with the right tools. Management should actively engage with compliance reports, regularly reviewing risk assessments and supporting initiatives to enhance regulatory oversight. A strong compliance culture ensures that employees prioritise ethical conduct and regulatory adherence rather than seeing compliance as an afterthought. This can be reinforced by embedding compliance-related performance metrics into employee evaluations and offering regular compliance training at all levels of the organisation. By fostering open communication and encouraging employees to escalate concerns without fear of retaliation, senior management can reinforce a culture where compliance is fully integrated into daily operations. 

Responses to part c) of the question missed marks by giving generic statements about the importance of compliance without explaining how senior management can actively support it in practice. Some also missed the importance of open communication and the need for staff to feel safe escalating concerns, which is a core part of building a strong compliance culture. 

d) The fourth question under this scenario asked candidates to consider what potential regulatory and reputational consequences the firm could face if it fails to improve its compliance framework. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

If the firm fails to improve its compliance framework, it could face significant regulatory and reputational consequences. The FCA may impose fines or sanctions for compliance failures, particularly if breaches result in client harm or financial misconduct. The firm could also face increased scrutiny and mandatory remediation programs, requiring it to overhaul its compliance structure under regulatory supervision. If non-compliance persists, the FCA may impose restrictions on the firm’s business activities, affecting its ability to operate in certain markets. From a reputational perspective, non-compliance could lead to a loss of investor confidence and damage relationships with clients. Firms with weak compliance frameworks may struggle to attract institutional investors or partners due to concerns about regulatory risks. Reputational damage can also impact employee morale, leading to higher turnover rates and difficulties in recruitment, as skilled professionals prefer to work in firms with strong compliance cultures. Internally, poor compliance oversight could result in staff misconduct, increased operational errors, and internal disputes, further undermining the firm’s stability. In extreme cases, compliance failures could lead to direct legal action against senior executives, particularly if they are found to have neglected their regulatory responsibilities. 

Responses to part d) of the question were generally strong in identifying that regulatory breaches can lead to fines and reputational damage. However, marks were missed by not exploring the full range of regulatory consequences, such as increased FCA scrutiny, mandatory remediation programmes, or business restrictions. 

## **B6.** 

The objective of this question is for you to identify potential problems and discuss particular issues. These would cover areas arising under the principles for business such as integrity, skill, care and diligence, market conduct, customers’ interests, conflicts of interest, relationship of trust and client assets, as well as market abuse, insider dealing and misleading statements. 

- a) It is not appropriate for research analysts to be taken to new client meetings to pitch for business. The availability of research teams may be used to strengthen the profile and ability of the firm more generally, but it seems as though the independence of the research is not sacrosanct. Reference should therefore have been made to the need for 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Chinese walls, also to issues of receipt of unpublished price sensitive information and relevant information not generally available. 

The need for a reality check for staff suggests that there are issues that the firm needs to address internally and in relation to market abuse and Part 7 of FSA 2012 if not in relation to fair, clear and not misleading in its research publications. 

- b) The issues with regard to conflicts of interest and acting in the client’s best interest as well as best execution and suitability should have been raised. Reference should also have been made to the obligations on systemic internalisers under MiFID. 

- c) Again this creates potential problems in managing inside information, and the offences of misusing or passing on such information other than in the proper performance of their duties. You should describe the offences and sanctions in such cases, and reference to the managing of conflicts of interest should also be made. 

- d) Issues arise again of conflicts of interest as s793 notices are often used to ascertain whether a takeover bid is imminent. The information may fall to be regarded as unpublished price-sensitive information. The importance of Chinese walls in maintaining barriers to the flow of information should again have been emphasised. 

## **B7.** 

a) 

- (i) It would appear that the following rule breaches have, or may have, occurred: 

- It is unclear if the original sale of shares was on an execution-only basis. If not, was suitability relevant, and was it considered? If the order was as a result of advice given by the firm, then Bell Jones is obliged to consider suitability. 

- The contract note (confirmation) should have been despatched no later than the day following the transaction rather than two days later. On the contract note the fact that part of the transaction was as principal must be stated. 

- The funds should have been despatched to Ruth without her having to request them. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

   - When Ruth was told that the funds ‘were available’ they do not appear to have been put into a client account, which they should have been following settlement. 

   - The provision of the investment advice might involve a number of rule breaches, although not all are clear from the limited information: 

      1. Is there a written client agreement in place? 

      2. Was the employee qualified to provide investment advice (approved person status)? 

      3. What evidence is there that suitability was considered? Advising a widow to have her money working on the market is contrary to the advice that is usually given in these circumstances. (However, you must bear in mind that a widow is not necessarily either poor or lacking in investment expertise.) 

      4. Are options suitable? 

      5. The repeated emphasis by the employee may constitute undue pressure being placed on the customer. 

   - The request was for £7,000 to be paid into Ruth’s bank account. This implies that an arrangement was in force that would enable a direct transfer to be made. If this is so, Bell Jones should have organised the transfer rather than sending a cheque. 

   - The information is unclear as to whether or not the firm was given discretion as to when to invest the £21,000 or was given an immediate dealing instruction. If the former was the case, then the firm acted properly in (belatedly) putting the money into a client bank account; whereas, if the latter was true, the firm has broken the timely execution rule. 

   - The gilts should either have been registered in the name of Ruth Kendall or in the firm’s nominee, in which case the certificate must be kept separate from any gilts owned by the firm in its own name. We have no information as to what happened. 

   - The firm is not permitted to use the gilts as collateral (and if they were correctly registered in Ruth Kendall’s name, the bank should not have accepted them as such). 

- (ii) As regards recovering her money, it is unlikely that Ruth will be able to recover anything from the firm if it is in receivership. Depending upon the precise circumstances, she might have a claim against the firm’s directors and/or the bank. As a last resort, she should be able to make a claim on the Financial Services Compensation Scheme, which was set up to assist eligible claimants who have lost money in the event of a firm’s insolvency. 

- b) The following are some of the regulatory issues and rule breaches that appear to be relevant: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- A firm must take reasonable steps to ensure that a personal recommendation, or a decision to trade, is suitable for its client. This involves obtaining appropriate information about the client’s investment knowledge and experience, financial situation and investment objectives. 

- The firm must have a reasonable basis for believing that a proposed transaction meets the client’s investment objectives and is one in relation to which the client has the necessary experience and knowledge to understand the risks and is able financially to bear those risks. 

- In the light of Brown’s background, it is doubtful whether an investment in equity options (or, to a lesser extent, smaller AIM shares) would meet the required criteria for suitability. 

- The rules on client order handling provide for the prompt, fair and expeditious execution of client orders. Concerns arise as a result of the delay in execution (going out to lunch first), which suggests that the orders were not handled promptly, fairly and expeditiously. 

- In addition, the rule on best execution requires a firm to take all sufficient steps to obtain the best possible result for its client, taking into account the execution factors. The statement that buying orders were executed “hurriedly” suggests that the firm’s obligations under the best execution rule may not have been fulfilled. 

- There is no satisfactory explanation given for transacting a traded option deal on the following morning. 

- Smith is not performing a certified function. As a trader, he would not need to have approved person status, and he would not be permitted to make a personal recommendation to a client. 

## **B8.** 

## a) **Analyst** 

The analyst does have inside information because: 

- the information relates to a particular issuer (Imperial Computers); 

- it is specific (the hospitalisation of the company’s chief executive); 

- it has not been made public; and 

- if it were made public, it would be likely to have a significant effect on the price of the securities (because of Mr Merchant’s status within the business). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **The secretary** 

On the face of it the secretary has communicated inside information to the analyst. 

It might be argued in her defence that: 

- she did not expect dealing (but should she have done?); and 

- she disclosed the information in the proper performance of her duties, as an 

explanation of Mr Merchant’s inability to attend a meeting (but was she indiscreetly 

over-informative?); 

and that therefore she has not acted unlawfully. 

An informed observer might take the view that the secretary to the chief executive of a quoted company should have acted with more restraint and discretion. Her disclosure to the analyst, on a strict view, comes close to a breach of CJA 1993 (insider dealing) and the EU’s Market Abuse Regulation. 

## b) **Insider dealing** 

After the press release from Imperial Computers the information that the analyst has is no longer “inside” information (since the facts have been made public); so it does not appear that any offence has been committed here. 

## **Market abuse** 

Probably no problem here either. The trader is selling. That is what a user of the market would expect in the light of the bad news from Imperial Computers. So the trader’s action would not give, or be intended to give, a false or misleading impression. 

## **Front running** 

Under the front running rule, where a firm intends to publish to clients a written recommendation, it must not knowingly effect an own account transaction in the investment concerned. 

The trader’s action in selling stock is clearly a breach of the rule, since the publication by a “star” analyst is likely to cause the price of the investment to move (downwards). 

The only available exemptions to the ban on front running are where: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- The firm is a market maker acting as a market maker and in good faith 

- The firm is dealing in order to fulfil an unsolicited customer’s order. 

## c) **Fitness and propriety** 

## **Suitability** 

A firm must take reasonable steps to ensure that it does not effect a discretionary 

transaction for a retail client unless the transaction is suitable for him. 

Dealing in high tech companies may not be a suitable strategy for a customer requiring both income and growth. In addition, selling short is always a risky strategy. 

The fact that the customer makes a profit is not relevant, and the fund manager may well be in breach of the suitability rule. 

## **Churning** 

Until 2007 there was a specific prohibition in the COB rules preventing a firm from dealing in the exercise of discretion for any client if the dealing would be regarded as too frequent in the circumstances. Given that the fund manager appears from the scenario to be motivated by the generation of additional trading commission, and given the number of trades on the account in the last month, the fund manager is potentially engaged in churning. The COBS provision on churning now has the status of guidance (rather than a rule), but it would clearly still represent best practice. 

## **Conclusion** 

In view of these potential breaches it is likely that the fund manager is acting in a way which the FCA would regard as not being “fit and proper”. 

## **B9.** 

## a) **Legislation** 

Under FSA 2012, s.90, a person commits an offence (punishable by a fine and/or up to ten years in jail) if he engages in any course of conduct which creates a false or misleading impression of the market for a particular security. In attempting to disguise the size of the ‘rump’, Agrobank would clearly be committing such an offence. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

[Tutor’s Note: The penalties have increased to ten years under FSA 2021.] 

Under the EU’s Market Abuse Regulation, a person will commit market abuse if his behaviour is likely to give a false or misleading impression as to the demand for a particular security. The burden of proof is civil, and the penalties include public censure, restitution orders and unlimited fines. 

## b) **Disclosure** 

The disclosure obligations under the FCA’s Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules (DTR) are in respect of issued shares of the company. As proposed allocations do not constitute share issues, no obligation arises at this stage. 

## c) **Criminal offences** 

In communicating information selectively to Sharp and to the institutional investors, the finance director may be committing the criminal offence of insider dealing, since the information is clearly disclosed with a view to encouraging dealing. 

Any subsequent investment by Agrobank or the institutional shareholders would constitute insider dealing, since the parties would then be dealing in price affected securities on the basis of inside information. A potential defence might be raised on the grounds that the information had been disclosed widely enough to ensure that no market participant would be disadvantaged by the lack of such information. 

In addition, market abuse (with a civil burden of proof) is deemed to occur if individuals take advantage of information not generally available to the market. This is clearly the case here. 

## d) **Disclosure** 

Disclosure, where relevant below under the FCA’s DTR, should be made to the company within two business days of the relevant transaction: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- Market maker – holdings below 10% are exempt from disclosure so long as the shares are held for the purpose of its market making business 

- Agro Fund Management – this 9% holding would require disclosure as investment managers’ holdings are disclosable at thresholds of 5% and 10% (and 5% changes above 10%) 

- Principal funds – this 2.9% holding would not normally require disclosure, as being below the standard DTR disclosure threshold of 3%.  However, it would need to be aggregated with the fund manager’s holding of 9% (and therefore disclosed) if the parent company (Agrobank) controls or influences the voting rights of the fund manager. 

## **B10.** 

- a)  Your answer should commence with s21 and that to the extent that the website will be a financial promotion that its content must be approved and comply with the requirements of COBS 4. Your answer should distinguish between real and non-real time promotions and should also have made reference to the E-commerce Directive. 

b) 

   - (i) The information should be given to the NCA officer. There might be concerns that passing this information on is a breach of the duty of confidentiality owed to customers; however, the obligation to co-operate with NCA supersedes this. There are specific exemptions from breach of confidentiality lawsuits for such cooperation. 

   - (ii) Mr Corleone should not be informed. Whilst the FCA Principles for Businesses do require authorised firms to communicate effectively with their customers, the act of informing Mr Corleone would constitute “tipping off”. This is a criminal offence under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, punishable by an unlimited fine and/or imprisonment for up to two years. 

- c) In order to accept Mr Staksovcashsky (S) as a customer, Osprey will need to undertake identification procedures and will not be able to rely on the personal introduction of Mr Corleone. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

S should be asked to provide either his passport or a valid ID document. Additionally, a verifiable postal address must be given. 

If S is based outside the UK, then separately verifiable details of a permanent address must be given - if based in the UK, normal evidence (voters roll, utilities bill, etc.) would suffice. 

## **B11.** 

- a) The question raises a number of issues with regard to breach of the insider dealing requirements imposed under the Criminal Justice Act as well as market abuse under MAR and market manipulation offences under Part 7 of FSA 2012. 

A share sale in knowledge of possible imminent publication may constitute both insider dealing and market abuse. The relevant components and conditions of each offence should have been referred to and factual relevance discussed. Available defences should have been listed and sanctions noted. 

Reference may also have been made to relevant UK Listing Authority rules and the rules in MAR on managers’ transactions. Reference could also have been made to restrictions on internal personal investments and Price Sensitive Information Guidance (PSI). 

Reference may also be made to the enforcement measures imposed under the UN sanctions requirements referred to as well as breach of relevant internal controls procedures within the firm (although it is not a regulated entity as such). 

- b) Pre-release of profit warnings raises issues of both insider dealing and market abuse. Relevant conditions, defences and penalties should have been noted. 

The position of the journalist should have been referred to. While there appears to have been no direct insider dealing, the information may still have been passed on or used for the purposes of making favourable reports. While the journalist is exempt from the financial promotions regime, complaints may nevertheless be made to the Press Complaints Authority. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Possible liability by the firm and journalist under Part 7 of FSA 2012 could have been noted. Reference should also have been made to general listing requirements for information to be made available to the markets in a timely and proper manner. 

- c) The earlier corporate finance advisers may have been guilty of market abuse or market manipulation under MAR and Part 7 FSA 2012. Relevant conditions, defences and penalties should have been referred to. 

As an authorised firm, there would have been consequential breaches of a number of provisions of the Handbook including PRIN, SYSC and COCON. Relevant sanctions should have been noted. 

Despite the earlier fault, the firm remains responsible for ensuring that all significant information is made available to the market. The need for corrective disclosure should have been noted. 

- d)  No difficulties arise with regard to the content of the earlier disclosures which were accurate at the time of release. Supplementary disclosure is nevertheless required to ensure that accurate current information is provided. A market abuse or market manipulation offence may otherwise be committed under MAR or Part 7 FSA 2012. 

The potential liabilities involved must be drawn to the attention of the firm as you are acting as its investment adviser. Failure may amount to breach of specific Handbook provisions (including PRIN) or general common law and fiduciary duties. 

- e) The provision of analysts’ briefings may continue provided that these are conducted in a proper manner. Revised provisions have been included within COBS. 

There must be no pre-release of price sensitive information, attendant staff must be properly trained and advised, no inducements must be provided, non-invitees must be allowed to attend and proper records must be kept. 

The separate financial analysts’ requirements under COBS 12.2 and inducements under SYSC 10 may also have been referred to. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B12.** 

- a) The purpose of this question was to test the ability of candidates to understand the general ethical background to the FSMA and FCA Handbook and to be able to identify and deal with specific types of difficulties that may arise in practice. 

The general environment includes the importance attached to management function and integrity as developed in the Principles for Businesses (PRIN), Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls (SYSC) and Conduct Rules (COCON). Reference may also have been made to the FCA’s statutory objectives and supervisory principles, as well as to the threshold conditions (Schedule 6 and COND) and fit and proper requirements (FIT). 

Some of the more important specific requirements dealing with misconduct could have been referred including market abuse (MAR) and misleading statements and impressions (Part 7 of FSA 2012). Remedies may also have been noted. Reference should also have been made to more general common law duties of obedience, care and skill, good faith and confidentiality and the uncertain relationship that still exists between these potential heads of liability and the new statutory scheme of regulation set up under the FSMA. 

- b) The specific requirements in COBS and SYSC concerning conflicts of interest and independence policies should have been explained. Firms should seek to avoid conflicts, in particular, under PRIN 8 and cannot advise customers or act in a discretionary capacity where a material interest or conflict arises. The general obligations imposed concerning independence, the maintenance and notification of independence policies to staff and customers as well as specific disclosure obligations imposed should have been explained. 

- c) The particular conditions imposed under SYSC with regard to the operation of Chinese walls should have been outlined. The nature of Chinese walls should have been explained as wall as relevant operational conditions and use of stop or restricted lists and watch lists referred to. 

- d) Possible options including no action, declining to act or ‘crossing the wall’. The situation may initially be dealt with by asking the PE operation to confirm its valuation and to ensure that proper due diligence has been carried out. It is essential to respect the Chinese wall and to protect the client’s interest. In the event that the over valuation is not identified, the PE team may have to be directed to withdraw although this should be confirmed first. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Possible insider dealing and market abuse breaches should nevertheless be considered in participating in the auction (and then withdrawing) as well as in subsequently dealing (or not dealing) on behalf of other clients. More information may be required. Difficult issues must be discussed with compliance and the FCA. 

- e) The discovery of accounting irregularities must initially be brought to the client’s attention by the M&A team. Breach of relevant company law and market abuse requirements as well as the criminal law may be involved. This would have to be disclosed to the FCA and possibly the relevant accountancy bodies. Any potential money laundering concerns would have to be separately reported.
