Additional disclosure obligations may also apply under the UK Listings Rules although it is not clear whether ABC is listed or not. Possible breach of insider dealing and market abuse restrictions within your firm should also be considered to avoid any liability issues. No breach of any common law confidentiality obligations should arise in this case due to the irregularities and possible rules breaches involved. 

## **B13.** 

- a) The question was concerned with the action that the FCA can take in connection with misleading financial promotions. GRAB is an authorised person as the question refers to it being supervised by a member of the FCA staff. The relevant restrictions on financial promotion would then apply under s.21 FSMA subject to the Financial Promotion Order (FPO) and COBS. Advertisements must generally be fair, clear and not misleading. 

The nature of the advertisement as a specific non-real time financial promotion should have been noticed. Relevant obligations with regard to the description of the investment, commitment and risks should have been noted with the firm’s interest and accurate past performance reporting with a warning that performance may not be repeated. Other information should have included the firm’s name and address or contact point. Relevant record keeping requirements could also have been referred to. 

Relevant issues that arise from the advertisement include the fact that the shares are unlisted, the period over which the performance comparison was made and nature and definition of relevant industry, the relevance of the relationship between total asset and 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

sales without further information, any conditions applicable to commission free dealing and any other charges imposed and the unsubstantiated nature of 25% projection return period. 

- b) The relevant supervisory and enforcement powers of the FCA should have been explained. This included the use of supervisory, warning and decision notices and the full range of enforcement powers set out in the FSMA and DEPP. The most likely initial action taken would be to request that the advertisement is initially withdrawn and substantiated. In the event that the company fails to do so, an injunction could be obtained. In the event that the promotion was considered to be misleading, appropriate disciplinary action would be taken including a fine or public and private censure. 

- c) The additional information provided confirms that appropriate records have not been kept contrary to COBS. The firm’s interest has not been properly disclosed and it did not have necessary permission to deal as principal. The asset figure is also completely inaccurate with liabilities of £350 million not having been taken into account. The interest of the directors should also have been declared. Unlisted securities would generally be unsuitable for discretionary portfolios. Conflicts of interest arise under PRIN (1, 6 and 8), COCON (1 and 2) and SYSC10. There has also been a general failure of PRIN (1, 2, 3 and 7) and COCON senior manager rules as well as SYSC. 

- d) GRAB has since clearly breached a number of principles set out in PRIN as well as COCON and SYSC. Appropriate fines and public or private censure would be considered. The advertisement would also have to be immediately withdrawn through injunction if this has not already been achieved. Individual action would also be considered against the Chief Executive for a breach of PRIN and COCON as well as SYSC. Fines, censure and prohibition would all be considered. 

## **B14.** 

- a) A number of separate conflicts of interest were raised in this question.  These were principally concerned with client and proprietary trading, use of research and front running, personal account dealing and board representation.  The other main issues 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

were concerned with remuneration and inducements and failure to report (whistleblowing). 

References should have been made to all available PRIN, COCON, SYSC and COBS provisions.  The CISI Principles could also have been noted.  As much detail as possible should have been provided with discussion of each relevant factual situation. 

Separate reference may also have been included to possible market abuse (MAR), insider dealing (CJA) and misleading statements and impressions offences (ss 89 and 90 FSA 2012).  Breach of relevant duties of a fiduciary under the common law could also have been noted. 

Relevant tools include general systems and controls obligations as well as specific conflicts of interest measures.  This, in particular, includes disclosure, declining to act, Chinese walls or maintenance of an independence policy. 

- b) Justification for the imposition of controls on conflicts of interest could have been explained principally in terms of market inefficiency and treating customers fairly (an FCA priority).  Some reference could have been included to the FCA’s operational objectives including specifically consumer protection and integrity. Reference may also have been made to possible sources of risk (including financial risk, operational, legal and reputational risk) as well as to breach of specific regulatory provisions including FSMA and the FCA Handbook (PRIN, COCON and SYSC as well as COBS).  Relevant ethical issues could have been highlighted with specific reference to the CISI Principles. Some candidates did refer to the nature of the penalties and sanctions imposed although this is more concerned with the consequences rather than justification for the imposition of such controls. 

- c) George’s failure to report should have been criticised in terms of relevant reporting and whistle-blowing obligations.  Specific reference should have been made to COCON and SYSC.  The prohibition on the imposition of penalties on whistle-blowers under the Public Interest Disclosure Act should have been noted.  Additional reference may also have been made to the CISI Principles. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B15.** 

- a) The main issue raised is concerned with scope of permission and authorisation to conduct an investment and management activity.  SCFL’s permission is restricted to arranging deals in investments and providing advice.  SCFL has contravened s 20(1) FSMA.  This does not make SCFL guilty of an offence, but SCFL would be subject to disciplinary action by the FCA. 

Reference could also have been made to relevant systems and controls and personnel requirements in the event of SCFL undertaking investment management or custody functions.  Relevant SYSC and COCON requirements should have been referred to. 

- b) Robbins and SCFL would breach various PRIN as well as SYSC and COCON requirements by continuing with the business, failing to take proper compliance advice and failing to notify the FCA. 

- c) All relevant requirements for extending SCFL’s scope of permission and related management, personnel and systems and controls obligations should have been referred to in taking on this new business.  All relevant provisions within the FSMA, SYSC and COCON should have been referred to.  Additional obligations concerning investment management and discretionary investment management set out in COBS and CASS could also have been referred to. 

## **B16.** 

a) Financial regulation on promoting investments is used to ensure the promotion is fair, clear and not misleading. This arises from repeated concerns that many customers have been mis-sold financial services and through this process have received financial services which are inappropriate for their needs. This mis-selling and the subsequent regulatory responses have been a substantial cost of the financial services industry in the last two decades. It is important to acknowledge that many customers can be vulnerable to the actions of financial services firms if promotions are delivered in a manner which may mislead and lead to sub-optimal decisions. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

This can result in substantial customer harm. Reducing such customer harm is one of the key motivating elements of the regulation on financial promotions. The promotion of investments is influenced by Principles 6 and 7 of the Principles for Businesses where: • **Principle 6** – a firm must pay due regard to the interests of its customers and treat them fairly. 

• **Principle 7** – a firm must pay due regard to the information needs of its clients and communicate information to them in a way which is clear, fair and not misleading. 

Section 21 of FSMA also imposes a restriction on the communication of financial promotions by unauthorised persons. In all such advertising it is imperative that the information provided is accurate, balanced, does not obscure important information and includes the risks of the investment and does not just outline benefits of the investment. Such marketing communications should also include the firm’s name and be presented in a manner which is easy to comprehend by the target of the promotion activity. If comparisons are used as part of the marketing materials, the data sources should also be indicated among other requirements. It is also important that the promotional material is cognisant of the guidance on prominence whereby risk warnings and the benefits of the investment would be displayed with similar prominence, such as using the same font size. 

There are multiple outcomes for inappropriate and misleading financial promotions. It is important to consider issues of consumer harm in such an answer. In cases where consumer harm seems possible, the Financial Services Act 2012 can ban and immediately remove misleading financial promotions from the market, without going through the enforcement process. Perhaps the most headline catching outcome for firms has been the requirement that many firms which have mis-sold financial services have had to provide refunds and compensation to their clients/customers. These costs can be staggering, for example, up to nearly £40bn in the case of payment protection insurance. There are other consequences we should observe in an answer. These include an awareness that only authorised persons should communicate financial services. The penalty for a breach of Section 21 of FSMA considering this concern is two years in jail and an unlimited fine. Other offences introduced by the Financial Services Act 2012 relating to the making of misleading statements and practices include: 

- making misleading statements (Section 89) 

- creating false or misleading impressions (Section 90), and 

- making misleading statements in relation to benchmarks (Section 91). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

b) There are a number of troublesome elements to the advertisement. Time share investments have been an area in which a range of regulatory and mis-selling concerns have arisen previously and should have raised wider concerns. Other issues with the advertisement include the suggestion there is a guaranteed return. The investment is a high risk and offshore unregulated investment scheme. The promotion is subject to UK regulation (COBS4) as the promotion has occurred in the UK, rather than involving an investment in a non-EU nation. There is a range of misleading and inaccurate information provided. This would include the suggestion of risk solid or guaranteed returns, and the provision of reliable and regular income when clearly this is not provided in the investment product as it is sold. Suggesting the investment has no risk and the returns would be 10% is also misleading. The statement that the scheme is approved under Section 21 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 is particularly worrisome in that it suggests this is a protected or safe investment when clearly this is not the case. In the answer we would hope to see a discussion of a range of these issues and any further pertinent points as to the advertising of financial services. 

c) In responding to this outcome, there are several actions to take. Initially it is essential to be transparent to the regulator and report that these failings have occurred. This will involve an examination of the firm and its processes. The clients of this investment need to be contacted immediately and refunded for their investment and compensated for any losses they might have incurred for this outcome. Working with the CEO and the Head of Marketing is more challenging. The CEO clearly has not involved you in all discussions of new products and the Head of Marketing has not acted in an appropriate manner. Discussion of this issue is a grey area, and the marking should reflect considered responses to the situation. 

d) When considering the ethical dimensions of this case it is imperative that the answer consider behaviours needed to focus on prevention of future concerns, following the spirit of the law rather than considering whether the firm may get ‘caught' for wrongdoing. For instance, has the marketing of the investment product been clear and honest, impartial, transparent, or fair? In this case all these values are breached. In relation to the code of conduct we would hope that the answer would include some consideration of CISI conduct principles of Personal Accountability, Client Focus, Respect for Market Participants, Professional Development, Aware of Capabilities, Respect Others and the Environment, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and Speak Up and Listen Up. In this case the Marketing Director clearly has failed in terms of many of these, including Personal Accountability, Client Focus and Respect for Market Participants. The compliance officer clearly has chosen to speak up and listen when needed and has displayed an awareness of these principles. 

More generally, how marketing has been considered under financial regulation has been an ongoing issue where a diversity of opinion has been raised. Additionally, the suggestion that marketing should be a regulated function was raised repeatedly during the PPI scandal but has since receded in its profile. This area does sometimes involve a balance between protecting customers and ensuring that market competition can occur, and firms effectively communicating the features of their services. Section 21 of FSMA also imposes a restriction on the communication of financial promotions by unauthorised persons. 

In such an answer we would like to see a range of personal characteristics, expected for working in financial services, to be raised such as being competent, solvent, honest and with an intact reputation. For the ‘profits come first’ concern, it is essential to indicate that the responsibilities of the compliance officer go beyond those of the firm alone and do include responsibilities to act in accordance with statutory and also fiduciary expectations. Of course, it is important to indicate that all appointments should be made formally and with regards to such requirements rather than through friendship networks. 

## **B17.** 

The question is concerned with ‘wall-crossing’ and supporting restrictions in connection with short selling of internationally traded stock and potential breach of PRIN and COCON as well as the commission of insider trading and market abuse (CJA and MAR). This parallels the decision issued by the Financial Services and Markets Tribunal (as it was then called) in the case of Philippe Jabre and the FSA on 27 July 2006. The FSMT rejected the appeal by Philippe Jabre and held, in particular, that the FSA could ask that the issue of suitability was reconsidered at the appeal stage and that short selling of equivalent stock listed in London constituted market abuse under s 118 FSMA (now superseded by MAR). 

- a) Rapier has ‘wall-crossed’ (or become restricted) with the pre-marketing (pre-sounding) discussions conducted with the salesman from the investment bank. The relevant issue is accordingly coded. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (i) The consequences of wall-crossing and restriction in prohibiting subsequent dealing; 

- (ii) The passing of inside information (specific and precise, price sensitive, unpublished and relevant); 

- (iii) Whether the stock was listed on a prescribed market in London; 

- (iv) Whether the stock was listed on a prescribed watch or restricted list; 

- (v) Whether Rapier was already on an insider list; 

- (vi) Whether and when the forthcoming issue would be announced to the public; 

- (vii) Market abuse and relevant conditions (as defined in MAR); 

- (viii) Whether Rapier has already disclosed or discussed any of this with Compliance; 

- (ix) Whether existing reporting procedures were adequate; 

- (x) Whether the firm’s existing conflicts of interest, policy and, in particular, Chinese walls procedures were operating effectively; 

- (xi) Whether there had been any breach of PRIN by the firm or COCON by Rapier. 

As much details as possible should have been provided within the time available. The more general issue that arises is whether the firm can be held liable for any offences committed by a senior manager. Vicarious liability was confirmed in the case of Jabre where the seniority and authority of the individual concerned was sufficient. This could either be raised under sub-section a) or d) below. 

- b) The completion of pre-existing orders would not constitute insider trading or market abuse under relevant defences. The entering into any new transactions to borrow or short sell would nevertheless be prohibited. No new contracts or orders should be entered into following the initial contact and discussion. All relevant offence conditions and defences could have been discussed in as much detail as possible. 

Reference may also have been made to the due diligence and reasonable precaution defence in s123 FSMA. 

Reference may also have been made to breach of PRIN and COCON. 

- c) The issue relates to the territorial scope of application of the insider trading and market abuse offences. If Rapier is not trading in the particular securities to which the information relates, there may be no offence of insider trading under the CJA 93. The scope of 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

application of MAR is nevertheless wider as this applies to any dealing in shares dealt with on a regulated market, MTF or OTF. If the shares in the Brazilian bank are already dealt with on any UK market, any dealing in shares in the bank in any other market will constitute market abuse as confirmed by the FSMT in the Jabre decision. It was stated that it would otherwise have been absurd for traders to misuse information by trading on any non-UK market where this would otherwise constitute market abuse under MAR. 

Reference may also have been made to breach of PRIN and COCON. 

- d) As Rapier has already traded and booked a substantial profit for the firm, Compliance will now be concerned with the effects of this on the operation of its internal systems and controls, the need to report the breach to the FCA (under PRIN (11)) and the internal regulatory action and external (FCA) penalties that will be imposed. 

Relevant issues include: 

- (i) The nature of any internal sanction to be imposed on Rapier; 

- (ii) The need to review reporting and conflicts of interest policy management practice within the firm; 

- (iii) Any more general corrective action to be taken to prevent substantial FCA sanction; 

- (iv) The facts should be reported immediately to the FCA under PRIN (11); 

- (v) Applicable sanctions for breach of MAR against Rapier (unlimited fine); 

- (vi) Sanction against firm for vicarious liability of Rapier under MAR (as confirmed in Jabre); 

- (vii) Possible penalties for breach of CJA 93 in the event that insider dealing may have been established (although unlikely); 

- (viii) Penalties for breach by Rapier of COCON 2 (due skill, care and diligence) and 3 (market conduct); 

- (ix) Possible breach by Rapier of COCON 1 (integrity) (not confirmed in Jabre); 

- (x) Possible breach by the firm of PRIN 5 (market conduct); 

- (xi) Possible related breaches of PRIN or SYSC as well as implication on COND and FIT; 

- (xii) Possibility of public or private censure and consequent reputational damage. Reference may also have been made to difficulty for Rapier in subsequently reapplying for approved person’s status under FIT in the event of previous restriction or cancellation. Even if his earlier approval was not formally withdrawn, Rapier may have significant 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

difficulties in retaining this status subsequently. If approval had been cancelled, Rapier would have the responsibility of establishing fit and properness again. The issue was left open in the Jabre case. 

Reference could also have been made to breach of the CISI Principles as well as the more general need to maintain ethical standards in financial markets. 

## **B18.** 

This question posed a case study involving a situation related to ethical and regulatory conflict between sales pressure and client suitability, highlighting the responsibility of financial advisers to act in the client's best interests despite internal incentives. 

- a)   There are several ethical issues present in this scenario. Emma is being encouraged to sell a product that is not aligned with the client’s risk profile or investment needs, which raises concerns around mis-selling and a potential breach of duty to the client. 

Pressuring an adviser to recommend an unsuitable product represents a serious ethical failure. There is also a clear conflict of interest. Emma’s incentive to meet sales targets appears to be compromising her ability to act in the best interest of her client. The scenario suggests that the firm’s culture is placing commercial objectives ahead of client outcomes, which undermines trust in the advice process and could cause financial harm to the client. A badly designed bonus scheme is likely contributing to the issue, as it encourages behaviour that prioritises product sales over suitability. This kind of scheme can create systemic ethical risks, especially when combined with pressure from line managers. Emma may feel unable to challenge the firm’s expectations, which further exacerbates the risk of poor client outcomes. The ethical environment of the firm is central to this problem. Firms have a responsibility to promote a culture where employees feel empowered to prioritise the client’s interests and uphold professional standards. In this case, the firm is failing to support ethical conduct and may be placing its staff in situations where doing the right thing is made more difficult. From a 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

professional ethics perspective, Emma should reflect on the CISI’s principles, including integrity, fairness, and acting in the client’s best interests. While she may be under pressure, her obligation is to provide suitable, objective advice to her client and to escalate any concerns if she feels her professional judgement is being compromised. 

- b) The adviser should apply the CISI Code of Conduct by acting with integrity and ensuring the client is given clear and accurate information about the product, including the risks and likely returns. Transparency is essential to avoid misleading the client and to maintain trust. The adviser must also demonstrate client focus by placing the client’s interests above the firm’s sales targets, ensuring that any recommendation is based on what is suitable for the client’s financial needs and risk appetite. Professional competence is key, and the adviser should only proceed after fully assessing the client’s situation, goals, and ability to absorb losses. Accountability is also important. The compliance officer must document their concerns and escalate them to senior management to help prevent regulatory breaches and reinforce the firm’s commitment to ethical conduct. If the product is sold and later deemed unsuitable, serious consequences could follow. The adviser may face disciplinary action, loss of FCA approval, or a prohibition from working in financial services. If it is shown that the sale was driven by internal pressure rather than client need, this could be treated as a breach of professional standards. The manager who encouraged the sale may also face individual sanctions under the Senior Managers and Certification Regime, including personal fines or regulatory action. The compliance officer could come under scrutiny if it emerges that concerns were not properly followed up. The firm itself could be penalised financially, required to compensate affected clients, and forced to undertake a mis-selling review or root cause analysis. The FCA may also impose additional controls or even commission an independent skilled person reviews to assess the firm’s systems and controls. 

- c) If the product is sold and is later deemed unsuitable, there could be serious consequences for the individuals involved and for the firm. The adviser may face professional and regulatory sanctions, such as disciplinary action, withdrawal of FCA authorisation, or even a prohibition from working in regulated financial services. Selling an unsuitable product under sales pressure may amount to a serious breach of professional standards and could significantly affect future career prospects. A line manager who encouraged the sale may face similar consequences. If it is 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

established that pressure was knowingly applied to act against the client’s interests, the manager could be held personally accountable under the Senior Managers and Certification Regime, which may result in personal fines, prohibition, or reputational damage. The compliance officer, although not directly responsible for ensuring regulatory compliance, has a duty to support the firm’s framework. If aware of misconduct and failing to escalate or address it, that inaction could prompt regulatory scrutiny and questions about the strength of the compliance function. The firm itself could also face serious outcomes. If the sale causes financial loss or harm to the client, the FCA may impose a financial penalty and require the firm to carry out a mis-selling review, root cause analysis, and client redress. The firm may also be instructed to improve its internal controls, retrain staff, and strengthen product governance. In more severe cases, the FCA could suspend or restrict permissions, or appoint a skilled person under section 166 to conduct an independent review. 

## **B19.** 

The question was generally concerned with the identification of a series of breaches in the brokerage, research and corporate finance operations within a securities firm. Each part carried equal marks. 

- a) Practices which should have generated concerns with regard to the quality of the control infrastructure maintained by the firm would include: 

- (i) Head of Research reporting directly to the Head of Brokerage; 

- (ii) Absence of any formal organisational chart which suggests ineffective demarcation of functions and responsibilities within the firm more generally; 

- (iii) Broking team attending weekly research team meetings; 

- (iv) Research payments tied to brokerage use; 

- (v) Provision of privileged information to separate sets of client; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (vi) Failure to maintain effective separation between brokerage and research personnel; 

- (vii) Failure to complete training of staff in corporate finance before being allowed to conduct certified functions; 

(viii) Absence of effective oversight over trainee staff; 

(ix) Conflicts of interest in acting on both sides of corporate acquisitions; 

- (x) Failure to maintain effective demarcation between corporate finance and research and broking teams (including conduct of allegedly social discussions within office situation). 

- b) The following revisions should be considered to the control systems maintained within the firm: 

- (i) Research should report to a senior manager outside brokerage; 

- (ii) The organisational structure of the firm should be reviewed to ensure that all relevant operations function effectively and independently where necessary with a revised organisational chart being provided to all personnel; 

- (iii) The broking team should not be required to attend research meetings although they may be entitled to see the final reports produced the following week; 

- (iv) Remuneration packages should be reviewed within research and across the firm generally; 

- (v) The extent and nature of any preferential treatment given to clients should be reviewed. This is not inappropriate as such provided that relevant dealing and managing rules are complied with including ensuring customer order priority and best execution; 

- (vi) Effective separation (Chinese walls) should be maintained between brokerage and research at all times; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (vii) The training of junior members of staff should be reviewed with personnel not being entitled to carry out any specific certified function until they are appropriately qualified and a certificate issued by the firm; 

- (viii) Effective oversight over all staff should be reviewed and maintained at all times; 

- (ix) Corporate finance should be prohibited from acting on both sides of any particular acquisition in light of the clear conflicts of interest that arise; 

- (x) The effective separation (Chinese walls) should be maintained between corporate finance and the other core sections within the firm with all personnel being made aware of the firm’s policy with regard to the management of conflicts of interest. 

- c) Failure to maintain appropriate management and control systems could lead to a breach of the following provisions: 

- (i) Various principles within PRIN may have been breached including 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 3 (management and control), 5 (market conduct), 6 (customers’ interests), 8 (conflicts of interest) and 9 (trust). 

- (ii) Many of the core provisions within SYSC may have been breached (including on apportionment, organisation, compliance function, employees and remuneration). 

- (iii) Many of the core provisions within COCON may also have been breached (including 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence) and 5 (market conduct). 

- (iv) Various provisions within COBS may have been breached (including Chinese walls, ‘spinning’, conflicts of interest, investment research and research recommendations). 

- (v) Consequential statutory provisions including ss 59, 71 and 138D FSMA. 

- (vi) A number of core provisions with the CISI Code of Conduct may also have been breached (including Principles 1 (acting honestly and fairly), 2 (integrity), 4 (market conduct), 5 (conflicts of interest), 6 (professional competence) and 7 (personal standards)). 

- d) The FCA may exercise any of its enforcement powers against the firm including: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (i) Varying or withdrawing permission (s 31 FSMA); 

- (ii) Unlimited fines (s 206 FSMA); 

- (iii) Issue public of private censure; 

- (iv) Prohibit individuals from carrying on specified functions (s 56 FSMA). 

Restitution may also be available (ss 382-386 FSMA) with individuals suffering loss of being able to proceed under a separate action for damages for breach of an FCA rule (s 138D FSMA). 

The FCA may also consider applying for an injunction (s 380 FSA) or ultimately for the firm’s insolvency (Part XXIV FSMA). 

## **B20.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to the management of complaints and redress available to private clients in dealing with UK authorised institutions. Candidates may have dealt with the question solely in terms of rights of redress and remedy although the best answers also referred to specific instances of breach of legal or regulatory obligation. Candidates should have read the question in full initially and dealt with each subpart in turn. 

- a) The structure and operation of the internal complaints procedures that are required to be maintained within all financial firms should initially have been explained under DISP. Firms must maintain written procedures that are notified to and made available to all customers on request. Eligible complaints must be dealt with under relevant policies with a senior independent member of staff considering the complaint fully and promptly and with appropriate redress being provided as necessary including compensation by the firm. Records of the decision and response must be kept for three years (five years in the case of MiFID business). The procedure must be carried out in accordance with the specified timetable with all of the relevant responses being carried out within eight weeks as specified. Complainants must also be notified of their right to apply to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) separately with firms also reported to the FCA on a 6 monthly basis of all complaints received or that no complaints had to be dealt with. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- b) The client’s further rights of redress are to the FOS in the event that the Firm’s internal procedures fail to deliver a satisfactory response. As much information as possible should have been provided on the nature and operation of the FOS procedure. This includes the definition of ‘eligible complainants’ (essentially private individuals and small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), charities and trusts). Reference may also have been made to FOS investigation, hearings, representations and reporting as well as the power of the FOS to make an award of up to £350,000 and reasonable costs. 

- c) The office manager may or may not have been an approved person carrying out a controlled function within the firm. If the manager was not properly approved, there may be a right under s 71 FSMA or a separate more general right of action against the firm under s 138D FSMA for breach of relevant rules under the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance. There may be a separate breach of the CASS rules. Other common law rights of action may also be available including damages for breach of contract, negligence or possibly conspiracy. 

- d) While the FCA will generally not become involved in individual client redress matters, it has power to require authorised firms to provide compensation or restitution without a court order or to apply for a court order for restitution requiring any person who has breached a rule or other requirement of the FSMA 2000 to provide compensation or restitution (ss 382-386 FSMA). The FCA would probably be unlikely to act in the event that adequate redress was already available under internal procedures or through the FOS ((a) and (b) above). It may nevertheless assist in the event that both of these options failed in the particular case. Action may then be available against the firm as an authorised person or against the manager individually assuming that he was still in possession of the funds misappropriated. Reference may also again be made to common law rights of action. Injunctions may also be available under s 380 FSMA if this may assist. 

- e) In the event of the firm’s collapse, compensation would be available from the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) under COMP. Relevant entitlements (including the definition of ‘relevant firm’ and ‘eligible claimant’) should also have been explained and the relevant compensation limits. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B21.** 

The question was concerned with possible breach of the certifed persons regime and relevant ethical issues. Candidates should have noted the uneven assignment of marks and provided appropriate detail under each section. 

- a)  A general review of the certifed persons regime should have been provided for the full eight marks. This includes all relevant core statutory provisions and principal provisions contained in FIT and COCON. 

It would appear that Herbert would have been required to be approved to conduct a significant harm function. Herbert must also satisfy the requirements set out in FIT including honesty, integrity and reputation, competence and capability and financial soundness. The firm would have to have certified Herbert, and also informed the FCA so as to ensure Herbert is entered into the FCA Directory. 

The FCA may take disciplinary action specifically under s 66 FSMA against persons guilty of misconduct under the approved persons requirements. This is subject to appeal to the Upper Tribunal (Tax and Chancery Chamber). 

- b) Relevant factors may have been listed with the answer structured with reference to the separate parts of the Handbook including FIT and COCON as well as PRIN, COND and TC. The ‘whistle-blowing’ provisions in the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (PIDA) should also have been referred to as well as the FCA’s other enforcement powers. Additional ethical considerations may also have been noted including the FCA’s general approach to ethical issues or possibly the CISI Code of Conduct. 

Relevant factors could have included: 

- i. Breach of s59 FSMA; 

- ii. Possible liability under s71 FSMA; 

- iii. Possible breach of FIT and COND; 

- iv. Breach of SYSC 27 (certification regime); 

- v. Breach of PRIN 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 3 (management and control) and 11 (regulatory relations); 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- vi. Breach by the firm of SYSC 2 (senior management arrangements), 4 (organisational requirements), 5 (procedures), 6  (compliance), 7 (risk controls) and 10 (conflicts of interest); 

- vii. Breach of COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 5 (market conduct), and 3 (regulatory relations); and 

- viii. Breach of CISI 1 (honesty and fairness), 2 (integrity), 3 (compliance), 4 (good conduct), 5 (conflicts), 6 (professional competence) and 7 (personal standards). 

## Relevant action includes: 

- i. Holding immediate meetings with Herbert and relevant staff including Divisional Managing Director; 

- ii. Conducting internal review; 

- iii. Producing early compliance report for senior management; 

- iv. Possible suspension of Herbert and prevent contact between Herbert and his team as well as possible suspension of Managing Director; 

- v. Refer matter to audit committee or possibly non-executive board committee; 

- vi. Report to FCA; 

- vii. Confirm with FCA whether any transactions had to be reopened; 

- viii. Confirm relevant internal information and resources; 

- ix. Confirm Divisional Managing Director knowledge and complicity; 

- x. Consider possible disciplinary action against Herbert and Divisional Managing Director; 

- xi. Confirm suitability of Herbert and whether Herbert may have sufficient experience from internal perspective including possible conduct of internal examination to allow to act in advance of taking separate external qualification examinations; and 

- xii. Confirm whether firm breached SYSC in absence of adequate supervisions. 

Relevant corrective action may include: 

- i. Reviewing internal procedures concerning assessment of experience and expertise of staff and need to undertake external examination; 

- ii. Confirm adequacy of internal TC arrangements; 

- iii. Confirm internal review procedures; 

- iv. Confirm internal disciplinary procedures; and 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- v. Confirm ‘whistle-blowing’ procedures in the event that senior management do not follow advice of Compliance Officer. 

The Compliance Officer may also wish to confirm possible available exemptions under the approved persons and TC rules and possibly take advice from FCA or CISI. 

The factors which would be taken into account may also include relevant enforcement action that the FCA may take for breach of the provisions referred to. The FCA’s enforcement powers may be summarised. This would be in addition to possible liability under s71 FSMA. 

- c) The policy would generally not differ, although additional reference may be made to preparing a specific report for the FCA on the breach and proposed corrective actions to be adopted. Reference may also have been made to the FCA’s early settlement procedure. 

## **B22.** 

The question raised the issue of potential insider trading and market abuse in connection with the activities of a UK based hedge fund management company. This could generally be dealt with by applying insider trading and market abuse principles to the particular conversation that took place between the fund manager and an investment bank. 

- a) Six marks were available for explaining the general nature of the market abuse regime including specific offences having regard to the particular circumstances. As much detail as possible should have been provided on relevant provisions. This includes the definition of market abuse in MAR. 

- b) The FCA should be notified with the FCA expected to undertake an investigation that would include issuing requests for documentation and further information. Corrective action would include launching an immediate internal review, possibly reconstituting a note of the telephone recording (making it expressly clear that this was a reconstruction of the earlier conversation), reviewing the manager’s notes on the refinancing deal, reviewing relevant internal policies including with regard to market abuse, conflicts of interest and the firm’s open-desk policy. The firm may also offer to reopen the contract 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and repay the profit generated from the transaction. The FCA’s power to conduct investigations and obtain information, documents or interview style could be referred to as well as the enforcement powers available to the FCA. The FCA may wish to discuss the matter with staff from the Listing department or from the Luxembourg Stock Exchange or regulatory authorities in Germany. 

Reference may be made to the Tribunal decision on Paul Davidson (The Plumber) under Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights and the heightened standard of proof required by the FCA. 

- c) In conducting the review, reference may be made to all types of relevant risk including market risk (equity risk, interest rate risk, currency risk and commodity risk including price, volatility, liquidity and basis risk) and operational risk (including regulatory, liquidity, delivery, political, human error, systemic, legal, IT, transaction and reputational risks).  Specific reference could also be made to the relationship between reputational and ethical risk. 

General requirements with the need to maintain adequate systems and controls specifically under SYSC should have been noted as well as the specific constituent elements of the market abuse and insider trading offences again. Relevant control systems should have been noted. Specific policies in connection with resolving conflicts of interest may also have be referred to including declining to act, disclosure, Chinese walls and independence policy, although this was not strictly necessary. While the question was focused on market abuse, additional reference may have been made to relevant insider trading provisions, although this was not strictly necessary. A relatively substantial answer was expected for the full six marks. 

- d) The relevant penalties and offences with regard to market abuse should have been noted as well as additional potential failings in connection with PRIN, COCON and SYSC as well as the CISI Code of Conduct. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B23.** 

a) The guidance for providing a prospectus is outlined within the FCA Prospectus Regulation Rules sourcebook. This process has also been influenced by the HM Treasury Prospectuses Regime Review. The regulation surrounding the issue of a prospectus requires that a prospectus is provided when any transferable securities are issued to the public or are to be admitted to a regulated exchange. This applies to offerings of €1m or more in the UK or €8m under EU directives. There are exceptions, for example for nontransferable shares, for cases where a private placement of shares is made on certain exchanges such as AIM, for bonuses or payments to directors etc., for dividends paid out to existing shareholders in the form of shares, for shares issued in replacement of other equity removed, for financial instruments with a maturity of less than one year, for EU member states that may not require a prospectus for an offering up to €8m, if the offering is not public and made to qualified investors, if the offering involves non-equity securities offered in a continuous and repeated manner, and for a unit trust or an open ended investment company. There are also other premium listing requirements over prospectuses that can be usefully raised and discussed. Consideration of these points or other valid points is important. 

b) It is essential a prospectus allows a potential investor to make an informed decision as to whether to invest or not invest in the financial offering. This would require any prospectus to include details as to the risks, profitability and potential future cash flows arising from the financial offering. In the case of company equity, for example, this would include assets and liabilities, profits and debts and prospects of the firm. 

To ensure an investor can make an informed decision this information needs to be provided in clear English and a comprehensible manner suitable for who may be investing in the financial offering. Throughout, the document needs to be accurate and not misleading. Key elements of any prospectus are the summary of the offering, containing key information required and the nature of the risks of the offering and information as to the issuer and the securities being offered. The prospectus also needs to contain a registration document including information as to the securities issued and the issuer. Lastly a securities note needs to be issued including detailed information about the securities offered. In addition, there is other associated documentation which needs to be developed and provided as part of the prospectus development process. This would include the application 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

form of potential investors in the offering, documents referenced in the prospectus and checklists cross-referencing the prospectus to its requirements and contact details for questions an investor would pose. The documentation needs to be submitted to the FCA. These documents can take 10 to 20 days to be approved by the FCA. There are also other premium listing requirements regarding prospectuses that can be usefully raised and discussed. 

c) The UK Listings Review is a review for possible future changes to the regulation surrounding the perceived competitiveness of UK financial markets and addressing the issue of raising of corporate finance in the City of London and for UK businesses. Key areas of development listed in the review include the following (The Listing Review, p.13): 

• changing prospectus requirements so that in future, admission to a regulated market and offers to the public are treated separately. 

• changing how the prospectus exemption thresholds function so that documentation is only required where it is appropriate for the type of transaction being undertaken and suits the circumstances of capital issuance. 

• use of alternative listing documentation where appropriate and possible, e.g., in the event of further issuance by an existing listed issuer on a regulated market. 

There are also other amendments as to the provision of past information and what projections of estimated future cash flow might be and how prospectuses for dual listings are considered. 

d)There are many perspectives which may be considered when discussing this major change to the prospectus rules. As part of this wide-ranging discussion are a number of changes to the rules for prospectuses. It is proposed that the current regulations for retail investments are too onerous and need to be altered and simplified considerably. Part of this discussion incorporates the view that a downside of the prospectus approach is that it draws together differing regulatory objectives. That of the issue of securities to markets and the public issue and trading of securities and that these regulatory properties may not always be consistent. This is a viewpoint arising from a particular opinion as to how we view regulation i.e., where less regulation is preferred to more state control and supervision over the private sector. These neoliberal views have been disputed in many other nations but 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

persist in the UK. Overall, the question probes an opinionated area in which serious commentators are split. Any coherent discussion really following whatever perspective the learner considers right is fine: what we would like to see is an informed and considered discussion. 

## **B24.** 

The question was generally concerned with the marketing and sale of unregulated collective investment schemes. 

- a) Financial products are covered by the FSMA to the extent that they fall within the 

   - definition of an ‘investment’. The general category of investments covered by the FSMA are listed in Schedule 2 although investment means of a ‘specified kind’ (s.22(1) a)) with specified meaning by order of the Treasury (s.22(5)). The categories and investments covered are accordingly as set out in the Regulated Activities Order (as amended). This includes unregulated collective investment schemes. 

Reference may also have been made to the treatment of collective investment schemes under Part XVII of FSMA. A ‘collective investment scheme’ is, in particular, defined in s.235. 

- b) FSMA s.238(1) and (4) prohibits an authorised person from communicating an invitation or inducement to participate in any collective investment scheme (other than an authorised unit trust scheme, an authorised open-ended investment company or a recognised scheme) unless permitted under the FCA rules (s.238(1) and (5)) or by order of the Treasury under s.238(6). Relevant exemptions are set out in the Promotion of Collective Investment Schemes Order (FSMA 2000 (Promotion of Collective Investment Schemes)(Exemptions) Order 2001). 

The promotion is accordingly only permitted to the extent that it falls within the exemptions listed in the Treasury Order or the FCA rules. 

Additional reference may have been made to the specific conditions set out in Article 21 of the Exemptions Order 2001 although not necessary to obtain the main marks. Relevant conditions include the exemption only applying to specified investments, the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

communication being non-real-time or solicited real-time and the customer having signed an exemption statement. 

- c) The certified high net worth exemption requires that the recipient has a current certificate in writing or other legible form signed by their accountant or employer or be self certified. 

The certificate must be signed and dated within 12 months ending on the date of the communication. The certificate must confirm that the person has had an annual income of not less that £100,000 during the financial year immediately preceding the date on which the certificate is signed or has held net assets to the value of not less that £250,000. 

Net assets exclude primary residence or loan secured on the residence, rights under a qualifying contract of insurance or pension or other benefits payable on termination of service or death or retirement. 

- d) Other exempt persons include investment professionals, existing participants in unregulated schemes, high net worth companies and unincorporated associations, sophisticated investors or persons in the business of disseminating information. 

- e) The promotion should generally only be directed at relevant permitted persons. Communications should accordingly be accompanied by a restriction on directed persons, a warning against non-professional persons participating in unregulated schemes and confirmation that proper systems and procedures are maintained to prevent recipients other than investment professionals from acquiring units in such schemes. 

The alternative would be to adopt some form of password protection with supporting systems and procedures to ensure that the identities of relevant persons are verified before passwords are provided. 

## **B25.** 

- a) Certainly the discovery of losses of client money demonstrates that the firm is in serious breach of FCA rules. It is not stated how ‘infrequent’ the reconciliations of client money 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

have been but it seems likely that the firm has failed to comply with the requirement to reconcile client money accounts as regularly as is necessary. 

It is also true that the FCA, having written the rulebook, do have the power to investigate breaches of rules and to impose penalties on firms for serious breaches. 

That said, the firm’s own internal controls appear to have identified the problem, corrective action is being taken and the firm appears to be able to make good client losses. 

The firm has an obligation under Principle 11 to be open and co-operative with the 

regulator and this principle extends to notifying the regulator if there have been serious breaches. 

It is likely that the firm will escape with a much lesser penalty (possibly even only a private reprimand) for disclosing the discovery and corrective action now than would be the case were it discovered later by the FCA having gone unreported. 

- b) When applying for Part 4A permission an applicant must request permission for each category of investment business it wishes to conduct. The FCA will then grant permission for such specified activities as it sees fit. 

Whilst a firm is not in breach of the general prohibition and so is not in breach of FSMA it is in breach of FCA rules and could again find itself open to the FCA’s enforcement powers. 

The extent of any penalty by the FCA will depend on a variety of issues – the number of clients concerned, the length of time the firm has been in breach and the circumstances under which the firm came to be trading outside its permissions. 

The FCA has the powers to impose unlimited fines and to remove a firm’s permission to conduct investment business; however, it is also under the statutory duty to use its enforcement powers in a manner that is proportionate. 

- c) In view of the fact that the FCA should be informed of the problems identified in a) and b) above, the proposal to give the outgoing Finance Director a ‘C’ certificate is a far from good idea. It would constitute actively misleading the regulator and would almost certainly 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

result in the person signing the ‘C’ certificate and/or those requiring him/her to sign it being considered by the FCA not to be fit and proper persons. 

It is for the FCA to decide whether the outgoing FD is a fit and proper person in relation to his future employment and not a matter of whether he has ‘suffered enough’. 

- d) There are a number of regulatory implications of this. Firstly not being able to identify clients’ physical addresses and not having kept records of client identification puts the firm in breach of the Money Laundering Regulations. This could open up the possibility of the firm’s directors becoming liable to imprisonment for up to 2 years. 

The failure to record client data does also mean that we are unable to determine whether or not they are rightly categorised as professional clients or whether they should be categorised as retail clients. 

There should be an immediate check to see whether any of these clients has been party to any of the futures transactions in part (b). Under the rules on suitability and appropriateness, the firm is responsible for assessing whether the client has the necessary experience and knowledge to understand the risks involved in relation to a product or service. While some experience and knowledge may be assumed in the case of professional clients, no such assumption may be made in the case of retail clients. 

When taken with the weaknesses in Systems and Controls stated in parts (a) and (b) as well as the suggestion of active dishonesty in part (c) it appears that the firm is in danger of failing the fitness and properness test: as Compliance Officer I would consider both directly notifying these issues to the FCA and resigning from the firm. 

## **B26.** 

The fact pattern raised difficult issues of conflicts of interest and ethical and reputational risk. As PB is based in the Far East, it is unclear whether the FCA rules will strictly apply. MSG and MSGIB may nevertheless be based in London with PB either being a branch or subsidiary operation of a London regulated institution. PB, MSG and MSGIB may also adhere to the CISI Code of Conduct or equivalent provisions in the Far East. The question can accordingly be answered by applying equivalent relevant principles with or without specific reference to the FCA or CISI provisions. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

a) The questions asks for the relevant conflicts of interest in connection with each party to be considered separately. The most comprehensive answers would have dealt with each set of relations individually although this was not strictly necessary. 

## Mrs Chang 

As Mrs Chang is not a regulated person, conflicts of interest and ethical standards do not strictly apply. JM, PB, MSGIB and MSG more generally must nevertheless consider relevant conflicts and ethical issues in their relations with Mrs Chang. Mrs Chang’s relations with her three sons again do not raise any regulatory conflicts issue although their differences of opinion mean that they should at minimum be advised separately to take independent legal advice concerning their own interests in the company and proposed restructuring. 

The other relevant issue to Mrs Chang personally is her status as a client and the degree of protection required. Mrs Chang would be classified as a retail client with an appropriate full client agreement having been entered into and with all other relevant provisions within COBS applying. 

In terms of JM’s relationship with Mrs Chang. As the client agreement only covers advice on non-commercial matters, JM cannot act in connection with the proposed restructuring without amendment which will, in turn, depend upon JM’s relevant experience and expertise. JM should have considered referring this to a separate department or relationship manager at an early stage. JM’s wife’s close relation with Mrs Chang is not strictly relevant from a regulatory perspective although JM should have referred this to compliance and ensured that this does not interfere with his professional ability or judgement. JM must, of course, also be careful not to use his wife’s relationship with Mrs Chang directly or to pass information back to Mrs Chang through his wife. In light of the close nature of these relations and size of the transaction, JM may again have elected to disqualify himself from acting at an early stage either on grounds of conflict or ethics and have the matter referred to a separate relationship manager. 

MSG Group 

Strict Chinese walls must be maintained between each of the divisions and separate entities within the MSG group. The restructuring more generally and Mrs Chang’s interest in the restructuring may be passed on to a separate division within PB and/or 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

MSGIB provided that full separation is respected. Necessary security arrangements must be put in place to ensure that any sensitive information remains confidential. Appropriate security arrangements must also be maintained between PB and MSGIB This may include documenting any contact between employees and information transfers. The compliance departments within each operation should be notified appropriately. 

## MSGIB 

The introduction by PB of Mrs Chang (and possibly her sons) to MSGIB should also have been properly documented with written consent taken to pass on names, identities and other file information. 

MSGIB must properly identify and document its relationship with its client which will be the company in this case. The sons should already have been advised to take separate advice on their personal interests to the extent relevant. MSGIB must properly identify its client as a professional party and carry out all necessary pre-service actions including agreeing appropriate terms of business and disclosing all relevant costs, charges and other payments. 

The recommendation of the ‘bought deal’ does not raise any direct regulatory issues provided that the MSGIB staff consider that this is the most appropriate recommendation in the particular circumstances. This advice would, of course, normally include other options with comment on their relevant merits and demerits. MSGIB will be required to act in the interests of the company as a whole as its client with each of the other parties concerned taking their own independent legal and financial advice on the merits of the transaction. 

MSGIB must not attempt to place any pressure in any way on PB or JM to ‘convince’ Mrs Chang or any other party to support the restructuring proposed. This would be contrary to PRIN, SYSC, COCON, FIT and CISI. The severity of the breach would require immediate notification to the FCA to the extent relevant or other local authority (below). 

Investment Analysts 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Investment analysts working within MSGIB corporate finance will provide documentation in support of their recommendation. Any other analyst within MSGIB or MSG issuing independent advice must be separated by appropriate Chinese walls and comply with relevant regulatory provisions including, in particular, COBS 12 or the local equivalent standards. 

## Client Managers 

Chinese walls must also be maintained between MSGIB and PB client managers and discretionary portfolio managers. MSGIB must not attempt to influence their decisions with investment decisions being taken on an independent and client basis. This also applies with regard to MSG Fund Management. 

Mrs Chang has requested that JM act as a non-executive director on the restructured company board to represent her interests. This would be wholly inappropriate. It would also be inappropriate for JM to attempt to act on behalf of other PB clients as suggested. As a nonexecutive director, JM would be required to act solely in the interests of the company under relevant company law provisions. 

Information must not be passed between MSG companies or divisions in connection with the proposed restructuring and share offer which may otherwise constitute insider trading, market abuse or be contrary to Part 7 FSA 2012. 

Insider lists may also be considered under the requirements in MAR. 

- b) PB and MSG would have to deal with both the significant breaches that have already occurred as well as avoid any further infraction. No further action should be taken on the restructuring until all relevant matters have been dealt with. These would include the following: 

- i. The issue must be referred to compliance within PB, MSGIB and MSG to the extent relevant; 

- ii. JM should consider withdrawing immediately and passing responsibility on to dealing with Mrs Chang to another relationship manager within PB; 

- iii. The terms of service for Mrs Chang should be confirmed and extended if necessary; 

- iv. A separate relationship manager may be appointed to Mrs Chang to advise on commercial matters either within PB or independently; 

- v. Mrs Chang’s sons should be advised to take separate advice on their own positions; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- vi. JM must not be allowed to be appointed a non-executive director to the board of the restructured company; 

- vii. Any independent research advice must comply with all relevant provisions; 

- viii. PB client managers and discretionary managers must act independently; 

- ix. MSG Fund Management must also act independently; 

- x. As MSGIB is already conflicted, it may consider withdrawing its initial tender for the restructuring mandate; 

- xi. Following discussion with the company and full disclosure, MSGIB may be given permission to re-tender and resubmit; 

- xii. Strict Chinese walls must be maintained between all parties; 

- xiii. Any transfers of information between the various divisions must be strictly controlled; 

- xiv. In light of the inappropriateness of the action already taken by MSGIB staff (in trying to convince PB to persuade Mrs Chang and in expecting PB client managers and discretionary managers to purchase the new company stock), compliance may consider referring the matter to the FCA, CISI or any other appropriate regulatory authority with full details of the remedial action and proposed arrangements set up to ensure that no future breach of the conflict provisions arise; 

- xv. To the extent that any pressure may be placed on you not to disclose any matters, relevant ‘whistle-blowing’ provisions may apply; 

- xvi. Senior management within PB and MSG may consider writing to Mrs Chang and her sons explaining the regulatory issues concerned and the action taken in the interests of good client relations and to avoid any reputational damage; 

- xvii. The board of PB, MSGIB and MSG may also consider withdrawing from the tender to avoid any reputational damage and to be seen to promote high ethical standards; 

- xviii. Internal disciplinary action may be considered against the MSGIB staff involved with approaching PB; 

- xix. Separate issues may arise with regard to the disclosure of short positions during a rights issue under FINMAR. 

[Tutor’s note: These provisions in FINMAR have since been superseded by the European Short Selling Regulation.] 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B27.** 

The question raised issues of share disposal following the internal notification of possible trading difficulties with specific reference to the responsibilities and possible liability of nonexecutive directors within a larger financial group. The fact pattern was loosely based on the circumstances surrounding the collapse of Lehman Brothers in September 2008. 

- a) As candidates were to assume that they were the compliance officer, a short reference may have been made to the role and function of compliance within a regulated entity. The question then asked for relevant legal and regulatory issues to be identified. These include: 

- i. Possible market abuse under MAR; 

- ii. Possible insider trading under CJA 1993; 

- iii. Possible market manipulation under Part 7 FSA 2012 (although less likely but still a relevant issue); 

- iv. Role and function of non-executive directors (NEDs) and NED-COs; 

- v. Possible liability of NEDs; 

- vi. Possible liability of executive members for previous disposal; 

- vii. Breach of PRIN, SYSC and COCON; 

- viii. Breach of exchange rules on LSE and NASDAQ; 

- ix. Need to report to FCA and possible sanctions 

- x. Need to maintain insider list and watch list. 

b) The principal non-criminal penalty related to market abuse under MAR and possibly market manipulation under Part 7 FSA 2012. Candidates should have noticed that only 4 marks were available with subsection (c) referring to separate criminal provisions which would include insider trading under CJA 1993. Relevant market abuse offences, penalties and defences should have been noted. These should have been linked to the relevant facts insofar as possible. This included internal discussion of firm’s financial condition, extent to which this may or may not have constituted relevant inside information, distinction between information and speculation, the fact that share price information was already public although reasons for possible future continued fall uncertain, relevance of executive directors prior disposal and possible defences, safe harbour or consent. 

- c) Equivalent discussion with regard to insider dealing under CJA in as b). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- d) Candidates could have referred to possible reporting obligations under Listing Rules (LR), MAR provisions on managers’ transactions, Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules (DTR) and any separate requirements under the LSE and NASDAQ admission rules. 

Reference may also have been made to personal account dealing under COBS 11.7. 

- e) Conflicts of interest may arise in connection with the personal account nature of the disposal by the NEDs (COBS 11.7 above) as well as the executive directors’ disposals and the interests of the London subsidiary and US parent. Reference should have been made to the need to avoid conflicts under PRIN 8 and their management under SYSC 10 including maintaining an appropriate conflicts policy (SYSC 10.1) and Chinese walls (SYSC 10.2). Enforcement powers of the FCA may have been referred to and possible penalties. 

## **B28.** 

The question raised the types of compliance review issues that arise, for example, following the conviction of Jerome Kerviel with the losses suffered at Societe Generale in Paris. A number of important compliance considerations apply apart from in connection with the conviction of the trader concerned directly. 

- a) The board is to be informed about relevant possible offences. No further information is provided in the question on this. The trader may have been guilty of carrying out market abuse under MAR as well as possible market manipulation (Part 7 FSA 2012) and insider trading (CJA 1993). Relevant offences should have been outlined in a summary form. 

Reference may also have been made to breach of relevant provisions in PRIN, SYSC, COCON and FIT and COND as well as specific obligations in COBS and CASS. Reference may have been made to the Listing Rules (LR), Prospectus Rules (PR) and Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules (DTR) as well as the obligations imposed by the specific exchange on which the trader was dealing. 

- b) Relevant provisions include PRIN 2 (skill, care and diligence) and 3 (management and control) as well as SYSC and COCON. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c) Relevant oversight and monitoring procedures are generally maintained under SYSC and COCON (above). Particular obligations may be imposed on staff dealing or arranging deals in investments including: 

   - (i) Trading price and volume limits; 

   - (ii) Stop loss and limit orders; 

   - (iii) An order execution compliance policy; 

   - (iv) Risk appetite and trading policy limits including maintaining a trading parameters policy; 

   - (v) Supporting management information systems (MIS) including trade capture and trade execution systems; 

   - (vi) Regular reporting of all open positions and risks; 

   - (vii) Daily mark-to-marking of net positions; 

   - (viii) Daily value-at-risk (VAR) calculations on the firm’s trading risk for Basel II and Basel III capital compliance purposes; 

   - (ix) Senior management approval for breach of trading parameters; 

   - (x) Maintenance of a Personal Account Dealing (PAD) system; 

   - (xi) Insider lists; 

   - (xii) Front running and ‘churning’ limits; 

   - (xiii) Appropriate remuneration and incentives policy; 

   - (xiv) Appropriate conflicts of interest management policy; 

   - (xv) Full compliance with relevant provisions in COBS and exchange rules; and 

   - (xvi) Full separation of front and back office functions. 

- d) Interim action would include confirming that no clients of the firm suffered loss as a result of the trader’s activities. All appropriate corrective action should be taken to ensure that no individual loss is suffered ad otherwise the firm may be liable for damages (s138D FSMA), restitution (ss382 and 384 FSMA) and injunction (s380 FSMA) as well as other regulatory penalty (above). 

- e) The firm should consider a review of all relevant systems longer term including: 

   - (i) PRIN, SYSC, COCON and FIT and COND compliance; 

   - (ii) Trading and risk strategy (above); 

   - (iii) Relevant trading limits (above) 

   - (iv) Risk management policy; 

   - (v) Remuneration policy; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (vi) Reporting procedures; 

- (vii) Conflicts of interest policy; 

- (viii) Training and competence policy and practices; 

- (ix) Transaction reporting policies and practices; 

- (x) Capital adequacy compliance policy and practice 

## **B29.** 

The question dealt with the conflicts of interest that may arise where an investment bank attempts to off-load distressed (toxic) structured finance debt through its discretionary investment management division. Difficult issues can arise where large investment banks act on behalf of large numbers of clients in different capacities as well as deal and trade on their own account. The question also raises a number of other issues with regard to the provision of investment management services and client redress and recovery. 

- a) The specific conflict of interest that arises is concerned with the attempt by the firm to dispose of the residual tranche of debt in a structured re-securitisation of US sub-prime debt. This relates to the un-covered portion of the debt that is usually held by the originating firm and not sold on to typical institutional investors (super-senior debt). This either has to be insured or sold on separately. Super-senior debt may not generally attract a high credit rating. 

As this is not specifically held on behalf of another client, the conflict is between the firm and its portfolio management clients rather than between the sub-prime debt holders and investment clients. 

Conflicts of interest are now dealt with under SYSC 10. Specific types of conflicts are referred to in SYSC 10.1.4R with firms being required to establish, implement and maintain an effective conflicts of interests policy (SYSC 10.1.10R) which deals with specific matters (SYSC 10.1.11R). Appropriate records of conflicts must be maintained (SYSC 10.1.6R). Disclosure of conflicts is dealt with under SYSC 10.1.8R and Chinese walls under SYSC 10.2. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Candidates should also have referred to the implications of breach of the conflicts of interest requirements including with regard to possible recovery by clients under s138D FSMA and regulatory action by the FCA (above). 

- b) Documentation requirements generally depend on client classification although a client agreement will be required in this case as investment management or discretionary investment management services are provided. Managing investments constitutes designated investment business under the FSMA and FCA Handbook with client agreements being dealt with under COBS 8.1. Other documentation requirements relate to firm, service and fee information, conflicts of interest policy, complaints policy and designated investments holdings. 

- c) Apart from client agreements and suitability reports ((b) above), relevant provisions with regard to dealing and management are imposed under COBS 11. These include best execution (COBS 11.2), client order handling (COBS 11.3), client limit orders (COBS 11.4), record keeping (COBS 11.5), dealing commission (COBS 11.6), personal account dealing (COBS 11.7) and recording (COBS 11.8). Reference may have been made to the investment research requirements (COBS 12) and client reporting (COBS 14). Additional protections also apply with regard to client money, securities and collateral under CASS. 

- d) Where the question refers to recovery, clients would generally initially consider proceeding under the firm’s complaints procedures and then Ombudsman. Damages may be available for breach of Handbook rules under s138D FSMA with restitution also being available by the court (s382 FSMA) or by the FCA directly (s384 FSMA), having served relevant warning and decision notice (ss385 and 386 FSMA). 

- e) Candidates should have reviewed the separate payment entitlements under the Ombudsman (£350,000) and Financial Services Compensation Scheme. Relevant procedures may also have been referred to. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B30.** 

The fact pattern is loosely based on the location of Osama Bin Laden in Pakistan by US Special Forces. This raised a number of issues with regard to potential money laundering and the funding of terrorist activity. 

- a) Immediate concerns arise with regard to potential money laundering and the funding of terrorist activity. Relevant factors include: 

   - The large sum of money involved; 

   - The unknown source of the money; 

   - The unknown destination of the transfer; 

   - The fact that the agency is not registered; 

   - The location of the compound; 

   - The absence of any separate business activities; 

   - The absence of any other funds movement; and 

   - The geographic location of the agency. 

The possible listing of the agency on the ‘Prescribed Terror Groups or Organisations’ maintained by the UK Home Office should be confirmed against their website. A _Consolidated List of Financial Sanctions Targets in the UK_ is also maintained on the HM Treasury website. A separate ‘Current List of Designated Foreign Terrorist Organisations’ is maintained on the US Department of State website with the FATF also maintaining a further list of ‘Non-Co-operative Countries and Territories’. 

The matter should be reported immediately to the firm’s Money Laundering Reporting Officer (MLRO) who should submit a Suspicious Transaction Report (STR) to the National Crime Agency (NCA) as appropriate. 

- b) All of the relevant money laundering offences, penalties and defences should be outlined for the full four marks. 

- c) Equivalent provisions under the Terrorism Act 2000, Anti-Terrorism Crime and Security Act 2001 and Counter-Terrorism Act 2008, including relevant regulations issued under Schedule 7 CTA, should have been referred to. Reference may also have made to equivalent provisions under the US Patriot Act. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers**
