- d) All of the relevant provisions with regard to institutional compliance with the UK money laundering statutes and regulations should have been referred to. This includes ‘Know your Customer’ (KYC) and Customer Due Diligence (CDD) and Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) with Simplified Due Diligence (SDD) not being appropriate in this case. Standard identification checks should have been carried out. Special rules apply on introductions although it is not clear to what extent these apply in this case. Other relevant provisions under the Money Laundering Regulations 2017 as well as the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance Notes as well as FCA policy statement requirements should have been referred to. 

Staff training obligations and the role and function of the MLRO could have been noted. Individual liability arises under the principal statutes referred under (b) above with necessary action being outlined in a) above. 

- e) Care has to be taken not to commit the separate offence of ‘tipping off’ under s333a of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002. Individuals must not disclose that information has been passed to the police, HMRC or the firm’s MLRO or NCA or that an investigation into money laundering allegations is being contemplated or carried out. This is subject to the relevant exceptions and defences provided. The offence is punishable with up to two years’ imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine (or three months’ imprisonment and a £5,000 fine in the Magistrates’ Court). The client should simply be advised that the instructions are being carried out and that the transaction is proceeding. 

## **B31.** 

The question raises a number of issues with regard to possible sovereign debt restructuring within the Eurozone area and possible implications on portfolio management within firms. 

- a) General client categorisation and documentation requirements would apply, although with additional provisions on portfolio management. Reference may also have been made to relevant reporting and record requirements (COBS 16) and client asset rules (CASS).  Separate reference may also have been made to specific COBS obligations including Dealing and Managing (under COBS 11) and, in particular, best execution. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- b) It is arguable that no strict conflicts of interest arise insofar as the purchase of a small amount of government debt is within the portfolio investment limit. The value of the bonds may also remain high if additional financial support is provided. There is nevertheless a potential risk that the bonds may default with a conflict of interest arising between the fixed interest desk and the management team and the clients’ interests. Relevant provisions under PRIN, SYSC and COCON should have been referred to. Separate issues with regard to investment research under COBS 12 may have been relevant although insufficient information is provided. Even if it is arguable that no strict conflicts arise, ethical issues arise under relevant FCA and CISI provisions. 

- c) Firms must be able to identify all relevant conflicts of interest, maintain an effective written conflicts of interest policy for their resolution and keep separate up-to-date records of all past and present conflict situations. Relevant requirements and procedures under SYSC 10 should have been reviewed, including through disclosure and consent, Chinese walls, or declining to act. An independence policy would be of no use once a conflict has arisen. 

- d) Price support is only permitted under the Price Stabilisation rules within MAR. Any other attempt to interfere with market prices would constitute market manipulation under Part 7 FSA 2012 or possibly market abuse under MAR. Relevant conditions, defences and penalties should have been reviewed. 

- e) Clients would be able to make complaints to the firm and to the Financial Ombudsman Service under DISP or claim for compensation in the event of firm failure under COMP (with relevant investment limits being specified). Injunctions and restitution may be available (Part XXV FSMA). Damages may also be available under ss71 and 138D FSMA. 

## **B32.** 

The fact pattern raises a number of sensitive issues with regard to balancing fee income within an investment bank and being engaged in ethically questionable transactions for overseas governments and private entities. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) All candidates concluded that they would not support the transaction with various grounds being identified. The following sets of issues could have been identified: 

## i. **Regulatory Perimeter** 

Some candidates initially focused on the relevance of UK financial law and the scope and competence of the FCA and relevance of the Handbook provisions in this case. It is assumed that Chimera is based in London and holds relevant permissions from the FCA. While the point is unclear, much of the proposed work may be carried out of London with the full application of the FCA Handbook and other civil and criminal provisions. Short reference may have been made to the general prohibition (s19 FSMA), permission requirement (s20 FSMA), authorised persons (s31 FSMA), Part 4A permission (s40 FSMA), approved persons (s59 FSMA) and FIT and COCON. General reference may also have been made to PRIN (and COND) although this may also have been dealt with, with regard to specific points of concern (below). 

## ii. **Client Classification** 

The nature of the client classification of the Republic as well as the President and his nephew could have been commented on. 

## iii. **Asset Ownership** 

A number of immediate concerns arise with regard to the source of assets and 

beneficial share ownership, including: 

- a) The President’s interest in the IPO; 

- b) The President’s control of the government’s 40% stake; 

- c) The President’s other undisclosed private interests; 

- d) The ownership of proposed future state assets to be disposed of; and 

- e) The nephew’s beneficial interest in any of the assets. 

The clear implication is that the President may have misappropriated state assets as part of the IPO or proposed future arrangements with the involvement of his nephew. 

## iv. **Transaction Structure** 

Further concerns arise with regard to the proposed future transaction structure: 

- a) The complex nature involving sophisticated leasing arrangements; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- b) Prepared by the Structured Finance Department within the investment bank; 

- c) The use of a special purpose vehicle (SPV) in the British Virgin Islands; 

- d) The lack of information with regard to potential investor groups; and 

- e) The lack of information with regard to payment arrangements. 

Specific product approval issues may arise under the (then) FSA Discussion Paper 11/1 on _Product Intervention_ (January 2011) and Feedback Statement 11/03 (June 2011). 

- v. **Governance and Transparency** 

Further concerns arise with regard to governance and transparency arrangements: 

- a) Lack of local experience in proposed transaction arrangements; 

- b) Opaque nature of the transactions proposed; 

- c) Specific request for non-disclosed involvement of President; 

- d) Presumed exclusion from Parliamentary and other public oversight; 

- e) Local and international criticism of IPO activities; 

- f) Lack of proper governance within IPO; and 

- g) Arguable misappropriation of assets with large profits but with current account deficit. 

## vi. **Conflicts of Interest** 

A number of potential conflicts of interest may arise: 

- a) The President’s involvement with the IPO, including undisclosed private interests and his state position; 

- b) The involvement of the nephew in the proposed future arrangements; 

- c) The bank’s acting for the President and government; 

- d) The bank’s potential involvement in future IPOs and separate structured finance arrangements; 

- e) The manager’s and the bank’s interests with possible compliance procedures and ultimate ‘whistle-blowing’ under the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (PIDA); and 

- f) The manager’s compliance concerns and project bonus. 

## vii. **Money Laundering** 

A number of separate issues arise with regard to potential money laundering: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) The President and/or nephew may be involved in having used the IPO or future IPOs and leasing arrangements for money laundering purposes; 

- b) Enhanced due diligence (EDD) would be required with the President being a ‘politically exposed person’ (PEP); 

- c) General ‘know your customer’ (KYC) compliance; 

- d) Suspicious Transaction Reporting (SAR); 

- e) Money Laundering Reporting Officer (MLRO) functions; 

- f) Money laundering penalties under the Proceeds of Crime Act; 

- g) Offences under the Money Laundering Regulations (MLR); and 

- h) Recordkeeping and other regulatory breaches. 

## viii. **Bribery** 

Potential concerns arise under the Bribery Act 2010 in connection with possible corrupt payments made by a commercial organisation, as a Glisten, or under equivalent laws in the Republic. 

## ix. **Legal Opinions and Tax, Accounting or Regulatory Approval** 

The legal opinions obtained as part of the approval process are only concerned with the local law. Confirmation of no separate breach under UK law or regulatory requirements should be considered in addition to general ethical considerations. 

No tax, accounting or regulatory approval has been obtained presumably in the Republic and to the extent relevant in the UK. This would include breach of any local or UK company law requirements on IPOs and privatisations. 

## x. **Regulatory and Ethical Compliance** 

All of the above concerns raise significant further dangers of reputational risk and damage to the bank. The bank must also comply with the CISI Code of Conduct as well as breach of other more specific FCA provisions including: 

- a) Breach of PRIN 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 3 (management and control), 6 (customers’ interests), 7 (communications with clients), 8 (conflicts of interest), 8 (customers’ relationship of trust) and possibly10 (client assets) and 11 (regulatory relations); 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

      - b) Breach by the firm of SYSC 2 (senior management arrangements), 3 (systems and controls), 4 (organisational requirements), 5 (procedures), 6 (compliance), 7 (risk controls) and 10 (conflicts of interest); 

      - c) Breach of COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 5 (market conduct), and 3 (regulatory relations); and 

      - d) Possible breach of COND and FIT; 

      - e) Possible breach Suitability (COBS 9); 

      - f) Possible breach Appropriateness (COBS 10); 

      - g) Possible breach of s59 FSMA; 

      - h) Possible liability under s71 FSMA; and 

      - i) Possible liability under s138D FSMA. 

- b) Further information could be requested on any of the matters of concern referred to above, including: 

   - i. All beneficial shareholder interests in the IPO; 

   - ii. All beneficial interests in future privatisations; 

   - iii. Proposed acquisitions by the President or nephew under future privatisations; 

   - iv. Accounts for IPO; 

   - v. Governance arrangements for IPO; 

   - vi. Proposed income streams for future privatisations; 

   - vii. Any potential conflicts of interest; 

   - viii. All relevant money laundering identity and source and application of funds checks; 

   - ix. Confirmation of no corrupt payments; 

   - x. Relevant tax approvals; 

   - xi. Relevant accounting approvals; 

   - xii. Relevant regulatory approvals from authorities in the Republic and possible legal and regulatory opinion on UK compliance. 

- c) A number of possible risk mitigation steps may be taken: 

   - i.  Complete full approval process for future work, including obtaining new mandate letter from the Republic; 

   - ii. Separate work for the Republic and President or nephew on a personal basis; 

   - iii. Consider separate product approval procedure for proposed structured finance arrangements; 

   - iv. Ask for possible Executive or Parliamentary confirmation for proposed privatisations to proceed; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- v. Confirm beneficial ownership interests in privatised assets with the Finance Ministry and future projected payment streams following IPOs; 

- vi. Consult with financial and company regulatory authorities in the Republic; 

- vii. Obtain relevant tax, accounting and regulatory approvals referred to above; 

- viii. Obtain UK legal opinion; 

- ix. Discuss any residual concerns with the FCA; 

- x. Discuss any residual concerns with the CISI. 

## **B33.** 

The question was loosely based on the circumstances surrounding the conviction of former hedge fund manager Raj Rajaratnam in New York in October 2011. Rajaratnam was fined $92.8m and sentenced to 11 years in prison. 

- a) The principal concerns relate to possible market abuse under MAR in the form of insider dealing, misuse of information and manipulating transactions as well as possible separate offences of market distortion under Part 7 FSA 2012 and insider dealing under the Criminal Justice Act. Specific concerns arise with regard to: 

- i. Being able ‘beat’ the markets and comparable indexes; 

- ii. Public limited companies; 

- iii. Time spent with CEOs and CFOs; 

- iv. Specific transaction information; 

- v. Recurring pattern of activity. 

- b) The relevant offences, defences and penalties applicable under MAR and Part 7 FSA 2012 should have been provided. 

- c) Equivalent provisions under the Criminal Justice Act should have been specified with possible cross-reference to Part 7 FSA 2012 (as sub-question 12(b) was concerned with legislation more generally and sub-question 12(c) with criminal law provisions alone). 

- d) The parallel strategies raised the issue of collusion. This may specifically include the various types of market abuse under MAR. All of the offences are generally also committed where a person acts or requires or encourages another person to engage in market abuse. Part 7 FSA 2012 is specifically extended to include inducing other 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

persons to act, with insider dealing under the Criminal Justice Act also expressly 

including encouraging another person to act or disclosing inside information. 

- e) Relevant internal action may include: 

- i. Conducting full review of head partner’s actions; 

- ii. Reviewing any possible other staff involvement; 

- iii. Reviewing any possible client loss; 

- iv. Reviewing any possible relevant systems and controls; 

- v. Reviewing risk management; 

- vi. Reviewing trading procedures; 

- vii. Reviewing compliance function and other relevant reporting lines within firm; 

- viii. Reviewing approved person arrangements; 

- ix. Reviewing training and competence procedures; 

- x. Discussing concerns with relevant executive and non-executive committee and possible whistleblowing if necessary under PIDA. 

Relevant external action may include: 

- i. Notifying FCA of conduct and corrective action taken; 

- ii. Contacting any possible clients affected; 

- iii. Liaising with any other relevant regulatory or criminal authorities involved in the UK or elsewhere; 

## **B34.** 

The question raised possible compliance issues with regard to short selling activities in anticipation of a possible collapse in the euro. 

- a) Relevant issues arise with regard to client classification (COBS 3), communications 

   - (COBS 4 and 5), client agreements and disclosures (COBS 6, 8 and 14) as well as possibly suitability (COBS 9) and appropriateness (COBS 10). 

- b) The manager has an immediate conflict of interest between his own personal desire to receive his bonus and the clients for whom he acts. This also creates a conflict between the manager and the firm. There is also a conflict between the colleague who becomes 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

aware of the activity and advises you and the manager involved. Conflicts are dealt with SYSC 10 and PRIN 6 and 8. 

- c) Short selling rules were introduced following the global financial crisis with the (then) FSA’s short selling ban continuing until January 2009. Relevant provisions are now set out in FINMAR 2 concerning disclosable short positions of 0.25% or more in the issued capital of a company with ongoing disclosures having to be made through a Regulated Information Service (RIS) by 3.30 on the following business day where the position moves above or below 0.25%, 0.35%, 0.45% and 0.55% and other 0.1% movements. The short selling rules strictly only apply where the manager was taking positions in securities rather than currencies directly. 

[Tutor’s note: These provisions in FINMAR have subsequently been superseded by the European Short Selling Regulation.] 

- d) A number of other more general breaches may have occurred of PRIN, SYSC and COCON in addition to the more specific provisions referred in subsection a) above. Reference may also have been made to the reporting requirements (COBS 16) and Client Assets Rules (CASS). The manager may also be liable for market abuse under MAR or Part 7 FSA 2012. 

- e) Client remedies would depend upon classification. Institutional clients would have limited protections including, in particular, in respect of compensation under COMP or complaints and financial awards under the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). Private persons may have an action in damages for breach of any rule under s138D FSMA although this would generally exclude institutional clients. Restitution orders may nevertheless be available under s382 FSMA with specific orders in relation to market abuse available under s383 FSMA. 

Other more general remedies would also be available in a court of law. They could only otherwise rely on the FCA’s exercise of its other enforcement powers such as through fines or public or private censure although this would not benefit them directly. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B35.** 

The question was concerned with the design of a compliance programme for client suitability incorporating appropriate ethical elements. This could have been dealt with in a number of ways. 

Candidates could have referred to the importance of the compliance and audit functions within a firm and the general functions and responsibilities involved. This could have included the general risks managed although this was not strictly necessary. Specific provision should then have been included with regard to relevant ethical standards and then suitability requirements. 

Candidates could have referred to the FCA’s general approach to ethics as set out in the draft Code of Ethics in CP09/18, Competence and Ethics document (CP10/12) and the Conduct Rules (COCON). The (then) FSA had issued a Discussion Paper on Ethics in October 2002. Reference may also have been made to Hector Sants’ speech on ‘Regulatory Culture’ in October 2010 and FSA discussion paper on ethics in October 2002. Appropriate principles could also have been incorporated such as using those of the CISI Code. 

Specific reference could have been made to particular FCA Handbook provisions concerning client relations and suitability including within PRIN, SYSC and COCON and COBS. Specific reference could also have been made to the FCA’s Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC), Retail Distribution Review (RDR), Consumer Duty (PRIN 12), and similar initiatives. 

Each of the key provisions within PRIN, SYSC and COCON concerning client relations should have been outlined and then the detailed requirements imposed under COBS 9 on suitability. These include general provisions (COBS 9.1), suitability assessments (COBS 9.2), guidance (COBS 9.3), suitability reports (COBS 9.4), record keeping and retention (COBS 9.5) as well as special provisions concerning stakeholder products (COBS 9.6), initial disclosure of information (Annex 1) and sales processes (Annex 2). Reference could also have been made to client classification in COBS 3. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B36.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to the promotion of Structured Mortgage Products. 

(a) The general provisions applicable with regard to financial promotion should have been outlined. All financial promotions to be approved by an authorised person (s21 FSMA) and financial promotions must be ‘fair, clear and not misleading’ (s21 FSMA and COBS 4.2). 

Relevant client classification provisions may have been referred to determine the nature of the client as either a retail or professional client (COBS 3.4 and 3.5). The following additional Handbook provisions may also have been referred to: retail client communications (COBS 4.5); Past and future information (COBS 4.5); Promotions with an overseas element (COBS 4.9); Approving financial promotions (COBS 4.10). 

(b) The cold calling provisions provided for under COBS 4.8 should have been reviewed. These include the application rules (COBS 4.8.1), restrictions on cold calling (COBS 4.8.2) and non-written promotions requirements (COBS 4.8.3). Clients have a right to damages for loss suffered as a result of a breach of rule under s 138D FSMA. 

(c) The relevant provisions with regard to product design and approval should have been outlined, such as with regard to New Product Due Diligence (NPDD). New product regulation was dealt with under Discussion Paper 11/1 on Product Intervention in January 2011 and Feedback Statement in 11/3 in June 2012. Products are specifically to be assessed across the whole product cycle, including with a Retail Conduct Risk Outlook Analysis and Business Model and Strategy Analysis (BMSA). 

- (d) The full range of the FCA’s enforcement powers include: 

(i) Fines; 

- (ii) Private or public censure; 

(iii) Injunction; 

(iv) Varying or withdrawing approval; 

- (v) Varying or cancelling permission; 

(vi) Information gathering; 

(vii) Investigations; 

- (viii) Reports by skilled persons; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## (ix) Restitution; 

- (x) Bankruptcy or insolvency. 

(e) Possible remedial action may include: 

- (i) Carry out a full internal review of the promotions used; 

- (ii) Confirm all relevant breaches of COBS 4; 

(iii) Confirm the number of possible clients affected and losses incurred; 

(iv) Contact relevant clients with offers of possible assistance; 

(v) Confirm possible applicability of Financial Services Compensation Scheme payments; 

(vi) Review any possible relevant Systems and Controls; 

(viii) Review Approved Persons arrangements; 

(ix) Review Training and Competence procedures; 

- (x) Notify FCA immediately. 

## **B37.** 

This question raised a number of issues with regard to the conduct of a broker in a boutique firm in London who attempted to move the price of mining shares immediately before the market closed. 

(a) The principal legal and regulatory issues that arose were with regard to possible market abuse in manipulating transactions (MAR) and market manipulation (Part 7 FSA 2012). The clear objective of the conduct was to move the price of the shares to avoid a loss and realise a profit on the Structured Global Depositary Product. 

There may have been a separate issue of insider trading either under MAR or the Criminal Justice Act (CJA) in passing information on to the other broker. 

A number of separate potential issues arise with breach of PRIN, SYSC and COCON. 

(b) All of the relevant offences, defences and penalties applicable under MAR and Part 7 FSA 2012 should have been reviewed with regard to the initial broker. 

(c) All of the relevant offences, defences and penalties should have been reviewed with regard to possible insider dealing with regard to the second broker. The first broker may 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

also have been guilty of a range of behaviours that amount to market abuse. There may also have been a separate element of collusion. 

- (d) Relevant remedial action may include: 

   - (i) Conducting a full internal review of the conduct of both brokers; 

   - (ii) Reviewing the involvement of any other members of staff; 

   - (iii) Commencing internal disciplinary procedures as necessary; 

   - (iv) Reviewing any possible client loss; 

   - (v) Contacting any other clients affected with offers of possible compensation; 

(vi) Liaising with any other relevant regulatory or criminal authorities involved in the UK or elsewhere; 

   - (vii) Notifying the FCA. 

- (e) Possible corrective action may include: 

   - (i) Reviewing trading practices; 

   - (ii) Reviewing trading limits; 

   - (iii) Reviewing trading teams’ oversight; 

   - (iv) Reviewing relevant Systems and Controls; 

   - (v) Reviewing risk management in the trading unit; 

   - (vi) Reviewing disciplinary procedures; 

(vii) Reviewing the compliance function and other relevant reporting lines within firm; 

(viii) Reviewing the Approved Person arrangements; 

- (ix) Reviewing training and competence procedures. 

## **B38.** 

This question raised a number of difficulties with regard to the conduct of a retiring nonexecutive director in dealing with senior staff within a regulated firm and with the FCA. The question could have been dealt with in a number of different ways. Candidates should generally have identified the principal regulatory and ethical issues that arose and then have commented on each of the four possible response options referred to. 

Bob had been specifically engaged to prepare an internal report on Customer Due Diligence (CDD) that identified possible failings, although without any confirmation of 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

disadvantage or financial crime. Initial reference could have been made to the need to verify customer identity (ID) and collect relevant ‘know your customer’ (KYC) information under the Money Laundering Regulations 2007. This may either be carried out on a standard CDD, Simplified Due Diligence (SDD) or Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) basis with additional requirements applying in regard to continuing monitoring. 

The failure by the Board Chairman (and Chairman of the Audit Committee) as well as by the Chief Executive to consider the findings contained in the report seriously raised a number of potential breaches of PRIN (1,2,3,6,7,8,9,10 and 11), SYSC (6,7,10 and 18) and COCON (1,2,3, 5, and senior manager rules) as well as potentially COND and FIT. Reference could also have been made to certain COBS including COBS 3 (client classification) and COBS 9 (suitability). 

In terms of relevant ethical provisions, reference could have been made to the FSA’s / FCA’s general approach to ethics (Discussion Paper October 2002) and draft Code of Ethics (CP09/18), Competence and Ethics document (CP10/12) and the Conduct Rules (COCON). Reference may also have been made to the earlier speeches by Howard Davies and Hector Sants’ speech on ‘Regulatory Culture’ in October 2010. Members of the CISI are also subject to the eight principles set out in its Code of Conduct. 

Candidates should then have commented on each of the four possible conduct options listed. 

## a) No Response 

Making no response as a former non-executive director, approved person and member of the CISI cannot be considered a credible option. Former approved persons can be held responsible for their conduct for up to three years. Notification of Bob’s resignation would presumably have been given to the FCA on Form C. 

## b) Informal Meeting 

While agreeing to attend an informal meeting with Guy may appear to be a credible option, especially in cooperating with the regulator authority under PRIN 11 and COCON 3, Bob is no longer a member of staff, Guy has not approached him formally and potential conflicts of interest arise with Guy appearing to have been a former member of staff of the regulated firm concerned. Potential 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

difficulties also arise with regard to continuing contractual confidentiality obligations even after Bob has left the firm. 

Bob could agree to meet Guy although this would have to be on a formal basis with any information being provided in response to formal requests under relevant statutory powers and preferably with, at least, one other member of the FCA staff present including from the firm’s line management team within the FCA. 

## c) Departure Over Policy Disagreement 

Informing Guy that Bob had left over a policy disagreement is a possible option as this is accurate in fact and invites Guy subsequently to approach the firm formally and ask for additional disclosure and information on the nature of the disagreement which would have revealed the difficulties over the lack of proper treatment of the findings of the CDD report. 

The issue does arise as to whether Bob should have taken more action before resigning. He had nevertheless discussed the matter with the Chief Executive directly (and the Chair of the Audit Committee) and the Board although he could strictly have referred the matter separately to compliance. As there was no actual evidence of customer disadvantage or financial crime, there did not appear to have been any offence committed in connection with money laundering failure to disclose. 

Comment could have been made on the fact that Kevin was Chair of the Board and of the Audit Committee which may be considered to amount to a conflict and undermine his ability to carry out both roles adequately. 

- d) Whistleblowing under the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (PIDA) and SYSC18 Whistleblowing may also appear to have been an attractive option, although the question states that Bob would initially have had to state that no issues had arisen which would have been inaccurate, with whistleblowing generally  being used by current rather than former members of the staff. This may have been relevant if Bob was looking for reinstatement, although this is not referred to in the facts provided. If Bob was considering whistleblowing, his could have been affected while he was still at the firm. There is also an issues as to whether there was sufficient evidence of wrongdoing and impropriety, although Bob is principally 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

concerned with the manner in which his report was considered by the Chief Executive, Board and Audit Committee. 

The most attractive option then may appear to have been c) on the basis of the limited information provided, subject to further formal action being taken by the FCA. Bob could discuss the matter with the CISI or with a solicitor if he wished to obtain independent advice. 

## **B39.** 

The question generally reflected the facts surrounding the $1.9b (£1.17b) fine of HSBC by US authorities in relation to activities in Mexico involving criminal groups in Colombia and Russia. This raises a number of issues with regard to potential money laundering and group controls. Other banks, such as Credit Suisse, ING and Standard Chartered have also been fined for alleged breach of US sanctions against Iran, Libya and other countries. 

- a) Immediate concerns arise with regard to potential money laundering and the funding of terrorist activity. Relevant factors include: 

   - (i) the press reports of alleged money laundering activities; 

   - (ii) possible earlier investigations; 

   - (iii) unidentified account holders; 

   - (iv) the possibly large sums of money involved; 

   - (v) the recurrent nature of the transactions; 

   - (vi) the unknown source of the monies concerned; 

   - (vii) the unknown destinations of the transfers; 

   - (viii) the geographic location of the branch. 

- b) All of the relevant money laundering offences, penalties and defences should have been outlined for the full four marks. The principal UK money laundering offences under Part VII of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (PCA) include Concealing (s 327), Arranging (s 328) and Acquisition, Use and Possession (s 329) as well as attempting, conspiring or inciting to commit any such offence (s 340(11)(a) and (b)). Money laundering includes aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring the commission of any of these offences (s 340(11)(c) and (d)). Other relevant 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

provisions under the Money Laundering Regulations 2017 as well as the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance Notes as well as FCA policy statement requirements could have been referred to. 

- c) The separate anti-terrorist breaches may have occurred under the Terrorism Act 2000, Anti-Terrorism Crime and Security Act 2001 and Counter-Terrorism Act 2008, including relevant regulations issued under Schedule 7 CTA, should have been referred to. Reference may also have been made to US sanctions including equivalent provisions under the US Patriot Act. Staff should be directed to confirm all relevant outstanding US laws and regulations to avoid substantial penalty. Private rights of action for damages may also be available under s138D FSMA. 

- d) A number of potential breaches arise under SYSC (including SYSC 6) and PRIN (1,2,3,6,9, AND 11) with the FCA being able to impose a full range of sanctions including public or private censure, financial penalty, restriction of permission and ultimate withdrawal of authorisation. Private rights of action for losses suffered as a result of breach of a rule may also be available under s138D FSMA. The FCA may separately prosecute firms for breach of criminal laws such as money laundering under ss401-422 FSMA. Specific requirements with regard to group systems and controls are imposed under SYSC 12 although these would not strictly apply in the present case as this is a branch and not a subsidiary operation. 

- e) Action could be taken within the firm with regard to each of the following: 

   - (i)   Commission a full review of the regional manager’s actions; 

   - (ii)   Review possible involvement of other staff in the local office or elsewhere; 

   - (iii)   Review the effectiveness of local money laundering control systems; 

   - (iv)   Confirm the identity and effectiveness of the MLRO functions with regard to branch and group levels; 

   - (v)   Review all other local and group systems and controls; 

   - (vi)   Review risk management; 

   - (vii)  Review compliance function and other relevant reporting lines at front and group level; 

   - (viii) Review approved person arrangements; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (ix)   Review training and competence procedures; 

- (x)   Discuss concerns with relevant executive and non-executive and audit committees. 

Relevant external action would also include. 

- (i)   Notifying FCA of conduct and corrective action taken; 

- (ii)   Contacting any possible clients affected; 

- (iii)   Liaising with any other relevant regulatory or criminal authorities including in the UK or elsewhere and attempting to agree early settlement where possible to avoid higher potential fines. 

## **B40.** 

The fact pattern raises a number of issues similar to those involved with the management of the corporate portfolio at HBOS immediately before the financial crisis and the responsibility of the senior personnel involved. Although this is now quite an old question, it is still valuable as an example as to how the examiner likes to set questions around ‘hot topics’ from the real world. You are also advised to read through the commentary provided as useful ‘background knowledge’ on these topics. 

- a) A number of potential breaches of PRIN (1,2,3,4,6,7,8,9,10 and 11) and SYSC (4,5,6,7,9,10,11 and 12) could have been committed by the bank and of COCON by the manager. Relevant provisions of COND (and especially 2.4 and 2.5) and FIT (2.1, 2.2 and 2.3) could also have been breached as well as in relation to training and competence and TC. 

- b) Conflicts of interest may specifically have arisen between the bank and the holders of client investment accounts, some of which funds were used to support the portfolio. A more general conflict may also be considered to have arisen between the property portfolio division and the banks’ clients more generally in light of the potential instability and risk of closure created. It may also be argued that a conflict arises between the manager’s remuneration entitlements and client interests. Conflicts of interest must be managed under SYSC 10 with the firm maintaining an effective conflict of interest policy (under SYSC 10.1) including declining to act, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Chinese walls, independence and disclosure. The specific issues that have arisen may also be dealt with through strengthening the firm’s CASS controls and remuneration policy (paragraph c) below) and improving its compliance culture more generally. 

- c) Relevant provisions with regard to remuneration are now set out in the FCA’s Remuneration Code which is included within SYSC 19A. Firms are generally required to implement and maintain remuneration procedures and practices that are consistent with and promote sound and effective risk management (SYSC 19A.2.1) with a number of more specific remuneration principles being imposed on banks, building societies an investment firms (SYSC 19A.3 principles 1-12 and 12(a)-(h)). As much relevant detail as possible should have been provided within the time available. Candidates may have noted that the Code generally only came into effect from January 2011 with earlier provisions being governed by the general provisions imposed under the High Level Standards. 

- d) The full range of sanctions may be available against the firm including public or private censure, financial penalty, restriction of permission and ultimate withdrawal of authorisation. Interim remedies include injunctions (s380 FSMA) with the FCA also having power to apply for restitution orders (ss382-386 FSMA). Private rights of action for losses suffered as a result of breach of a rule may also be available (s138D FSMA). Relevant factors for consideration include the nature and seriousness of the breach, the conduct of the firm and previous regulatory infractions. Reference may also have been made to the FCA’s credible deterrence policy on enforcement adopted since 2007. 

- e) Individuals are subject to a fine or public or private censure or possible restitution orders. Approval may also be varied or withdrawn where a person is no longer fit and proper to conduct a controlled function. Withdrawal of approval must be referred to the Regulatory Decisions Committee (RDC) which will serve a warning notice and then a decision notice, with the person concerned being able to refer the matter to the Tax and Chancery Chamber of the Upper Tribunal. A number of factors should be taken into account in determining whether withdrawal is appropriate and the size of any fine imposed. Specific issues relevant in the particular case concern the extent to which the Head of Corporate Banking acted with the full knowledge and possible support of other senior managers and the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Board despite the concerns expressed by the Risk Committee. Individuals should also be treated in a consistent manner with individual, selective and disproportionate penalties being avoided. 

## **B41.** 

The fact pattern reflects the issues that have arisen with regard to a number of major international firms concerning the possible criminal and regulatory enforcement action in connection with manipulation of benchmark rates including specifically LIBOR and EURIBOR. 

- a) The principal regulatory offence that arises is with regard to possible breach of Part 7 FSA 2012 concerning market manipulation. The relevant offences should have been referred to including dishonestly or recklessly making any statement that is false or misleading, or dishonestly concealing any material facts. It also applies where any person commits any act, or engages in any course of conduct which creates a false or misleading impression as to the market in, or the price or value of, any investment. Relevant defences and penalties should also have been referred to. These provisions would nevertheless generally not apply in the present case as they are only concerned with ‘relevant investments’ which would not include benchmark rates as such. Reference may also have been made to other possible offences in connection with common law or statutory fraud conspiracy. 

[Tutor’s note: Manipulation of benchmarks is now covered by new provisions in s.91 FSA 2012.] 

- b) The most relevant civil provision would be market abuse and possibly either manipulating transactions or manipulating devices or dissemination. Relevant defences and penalties could have been referred to including relevant due diligence defences. This would nevertheless again not apply to the extent that it only covers ‘qualifying investments’ traded on prescribed markets. 

- c) FCA information gathering and investigatory powers are dealt with under ss165176 FSMA. The FCA has power to require any authorised person, appointed representatives and certain other persons to provide it with relevant information, reports or other documents. The FCA may appoint an investigator possibly in 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

collaboration with other agencies or overseas authorities. The FCA can require persons under investigation (and connected persons) to attend for questioning. The FCA can enter premises and obtain a court warrant where necessary. It is a criminal offence to falsify, conceal, destroy or dispose of any relevant document (s177 FSMA) subject to two years imprisonment or an unlimited fine. The FCA may also apply for an injunction to restrain or prohibit any person from breaking a rule or other requirement (s380 FSMA). 

- d) A number of relevant provisions of PRIN and SYSC may have been breached. Specific requirements also apply with regard to group systems and controls under SYSC 12. Firms must, in particular, ensure that they have adequate, sound and appropriate risk management processes and internal control mechanisms for the purpose of assessing and managing its own exposure to group risk, including sound administrative and accounting procedures, and that ensure that the group has adequate, sound and appropriate risk management processes and internal control mechanisms at the group level (SYSC 12.1.8R). Specific provisions apply with regard to financial conglomerates and banks and investment firms under BIPRU with additional risk management requirements (SYSC 12.1.11-12, 13-15 and 18-22). 

- e) The firm must make full disclosure to and co-operate fully with the FCA and all other relevant UK and overseas authorities. It should conduct its own internal reviews and investigations into possible breaches and internal systems and controls, governance, reporting and record keeping failures. It should implement any necessary internal changes with immediate effect. It should consider the recommendations of any external review reports, such as the Wheatley Review in September 2012, and make any relevant internal adjustments as necessary. It should suspend any staff involved and commence appropriate disciplinary action conditional upon any separate external action being taken. It should investigate the extent to which any specific customers may have suffered loss or injury and consider contacting them and making appropriate compensation offers, although this may again be dependent upon continuing external investigatory action. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B42.** 

This question raised a number of difficult issues with regard to the conduct of staff members working in separate analysis and advisory departments within an investment bank. Bert is a high-tech analyst while Ernie works in Mergers & Acquisitions advisory with the question focusing on the firm's treatment of a young tech company Whizzo, in which Ernie owns shares and is potentially assisting acquire a separate technological company. 

- a) Candidates were asked to explain the relevant concerns from a compliance perspective. One candidate initially explained the function of compliance and the responsibilities of the compliance officer. The principal issues that arose were then concerned with PRIN, SYSC and COCON  generally, conflicts of interest (SYSC 10.1), Chinese walls (SYSC 10.2), personal account dealing (COBS 11.7), investment research (COBS 12) as well as potential insider dealing, market abuse and misleading statements and impressions (ss 89 and 90 of FSA 2012). Relevant factual factors included: 

   - (i) Bert and Ernie were from separate departments and discussing client business outside the office; 

   - (ii) They should have been aware of and respected the firm's conflicts of interest policy (SYSC 10.10 R); 

   - (iii) The situation appeared to fall outside the ‘effective organisational and administrative arrangements’ that the firm is required to maintain to manage conflicts of interest (SYSC 10.1.7 R); 

   - (iv) Ernie encouraged Bert to ‘take a closer look’ at Whizzo without disclosing his share interest; 

   - (v) It is initially unclear whether Ernie knows that Bert covers the stock although this is implied with the reference to suggesting that Bert reconsiders the stock; 

   - (vi) Ernie also fails to disclose his conversation with the company's CEO; 

   - (vii) It is unclear whether Ernie has acquired any inside information although this still raises ethical rather than strictly regulatory or legal issues; 

   - (viii) Bert considered that he may be conflicted having learnt that the bank is to advise Whizzo on the acquisition; 

   - (ix) Bert only learns about this from the press rather than internally; 

   - (x) Bert has to reconsider whether he can provide an independent research opinion; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

(xi) The firm’s potential failure to comply with PRIN; 

(xii) The firm's potential failure to comply with SYSC; 

(xiii) Bert and Ernie's potential failure to comply with COCON; 

(xiv) Potential failure of the firm's reporting lines; 

- (xv) Potential failure of board responsibility; 

- (xvi) Potential liability of the firm and of Bert and Ernie. 

b) No clear information is provided as to whether any civil or criminal offences have been breached with the question only referring to offences that may apply. These are specifically concerned with insider dealing (CJA 1993), market abuse (MAR) and misleading statements and impressions (ss 89-90 FSA 2012). The question asked for relevant offences and defences. Reference may also have been made to breach of PRA or FCA Handbook rules under s 138D (1) and (2) FSMA as amended (formerly s 150) which create actions for damages. 

- c) Sub-section (c) refers to other ethical behaviour apart from statutory and regulatory considerations. Regulatory issues (including PRIN, SYSC and COCON) should strictly have been dealt with under (a) although a number of candidates discussed these in further detail in (c). Specific difficulties arise with regard the firm under PRIN 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 3 (management and control), 5 (market conduct), 6 (customers' interest), 7 (client communication), 8 (conflicts of interest), 8 (relationship of trust), and regulatory relationship (11) as well as SYSC 2 (senior management arrangements) and 3 (systems and controls) as well as 4 (organisation requirements), 5 (skills, knowledge and expertise), 6 (compliance), 7 (risk control) and 10 (conflicts of interest). 

Bert and Ernie may also be approved persons with Ernie falling within CF 30 (customer function) with a number of possible breaches of COCON. Reference has already been made to personal account dealings (COBS 11.7) and investment analysis (COBS 12). 

Specific facts raising issues of concern from an ethical perspective are: 

- (i) Discussion client’s position in a social situation; 

- (ii) Ernie's lack of disclosure of interest; 

- (iii) Ernie's unapproved discussions with the client CEOs; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (iv) Ernie's personal dealing and lack of compliance with the PAD (personal account dealing) rules; 

- (v) Ernie's attempt to influence Bert to his advantage; 

- (vi) Ernie's attempt to benefit himself even if there has been no strict breach of insider dealing, market abuse or misleading statements or impressions rules; 

- (vii) Possible breakdown of the firm's management systems and controls in such a case. 

Reference could also have been made to the Chartered Institute for Securities & Investment's (CISI) Code of Conduct including Principle 1 (acting honestly and fairly at all times), 2 (integrity), 3(observance of applicable law, regulation and professional conduct standards), 4 (market integrity in dealings), 5 (conflicts of interest), 6 (professional confidence) and 7 (upholding the highest personal standards). 

Candidates could have referred to the FSA's earlier general approach to ethics and to restatements by the PRA and FCA that they will attempt to enforce high ethical standards in their approach documents. Reference could also have been made to the Parliamentary Commission on Banking Standards' report on ‘Changing banking for good’ (12 June 2013). 

- d) Bert has not committed any regulatory breach or offence as such. He would nevertheless be advised to: 

   - (i) Contact his line manager to disclose all of the relevant facts immediately; 

   - (ii) Discuss any outstanding issues with compliance; 

   - (iii) Ask compliance to take any necessary legal advice if considered appropriate; 

   - (iv) Ask to be recused from preparing the revised report on Whizzo; 

   - (v) If Bert is asked to prepare the report, carry this out in an independent and objective manner; 

   - (vi) Attempt to ensure compliance with all of the relevant requirements in COBS 12.2 on investment research although this is strictly a firm obligation; 

   - (vii) Request to be given additional training; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

   - (viii) If Bert remains dissatisfied with the advice given by his line manager or compliance, contact Human Resources and obtain independent advice within the firm such as from one of the Non-Executive Directors; 

   - (ix) If Bert is still dissatisfied with the advice given, contact the FCA or CISI including using the Public Interest Disclosure Act (whistle blowing) procedures provided for under SYSC 18; 

   - (x) If Bert is still dissatisfied, seek independent legal advice. 

- e) It is not clear whether Ernie has breached any specific regulatory or statutory obligations although he has clearly acted in his own self-interest and in an unethical manner. Ernie would be advised to: 

   - (i) Disclose everything to his line manager immediately; 

   - (ii) Make full separate disclosure to compliance; 

   - (iii) Ask compliance to take legal advice to the extent necessary to confirm whether or not he had breached any statutory requirements, in particular, on insider dealing, market abuse or misleading statements; 

   - (iv) Ask to be removed from acting on the acquisition by Whizzo; 

   - (v) Ensure compliance the firm's PAD policy including disposal of the shares in Whizzo to the extent necessary; 

   - (vi) Disgorge any profit considered to have been earned unethically; 

   - (vii) Ensure compliance with the firm's conflicts of interest policy in future; 

   - (viii) Request to be given additional training. 

## **B43.** 

The question raised the issue of making an application to act as a bank under the new regulatory regime that came into effect on 1 April 2013. This raises specific issues with regard to the respective responsibilities of the PRA and FCA and their operational relations. As a US parent bank is involved, the facts could have raised EU passporting issues although this was not referred to in any of the specific sub-questions asked. 

- a) Authorisation is dealt with under s 31 FSMA. Authorisation is made available to persons holding a Part 4A permission, EEA qualifying firms under Schedule 3, Treaty firms qualifying under Schedule 4 or any person who is otherwise authorised under the FSMA (s 31 (1) (a) - (d) FSMA). Applications for Part 4A 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

permission are now made under s 55A FSMA to the appropriate regulator (s 55A (2)). Firms subject to PRA regulation are set out in the FSMA 2000 (PRA-regulated Activities) Order 2013 (SI 2013/556) and include banks, insurance companies, designated investment firms, credit unions and building societies. Applications are made to the PRA directly with firms having to satisfy the threshold conditions set out in Schedule 6 FSMA. New firm authorisations are dealt with on the PRA website. The PRA is required to consult with the FCA with applications being determined within six months of receipt of the complete application or 12 months of receipt of an incomplete application. 

- b) The FCA is responsible for the prudential regulation of all firms not subject to PRA regulation and the for conduct regulation of all firms of whatever type. A bank will become a dual regulated firm subject to PRA and FCA oversight. The FCA includes a separate section on banking applications on its website with separate PDFs on ‘The Banking Authorisations Process’ (including Options A and B which assist firms raise investment or capital or set up infrastructure) and ‘Banking Authorisations Applicant Journey’. 

- c) The FSA Handbook has been split between the PRA and FCA with a separate Combined Handbook also being available (on http://fsahandbook.info/ ). The PRA and FCA both have rule making and the FCA guidance powers (ss 137A-T and 139A-B FSMA as amended). The PRA Handbook consists of the High Level Standards (PRIN, SYSC, COCON, FIT, FINMAR, GEN and FEES), Prudential Standards (GENPRU, BIPRU, INSPRU, MIPRU, IPRU-FSOC, IPRU-INS and IPRU-INV), Business Standards (COBS), Regulatory Processes (SUP), Redress (COMP), Specialist sourcebooks (BSOCS and CREDS) and Regulatory Guides (BSOG). All of the other sections within the FSA Handbook are now included within the FCA Handbook as revised. The Treasury is to specify by order the regulated activities falling within the responsibility of the PRA under s 22A FSMA as amended which was given effect under The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (PRA-regulated Activities) Order SI 556/2013. 

[Tutor’s note: This part of the answer has now been superseded by the development of a separate PRA Rulebook.] 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

d) The separate objectives and functions of the FCA and PRA are set out in ss 1A-O and 2A-M FSMA as amended with joint provisions being set out in ss 3A-O FSMA as amended. The regulators are to ensure the coordinated exercise of their functions (s 3D) and to enter into a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) setting out the terms on which they will operate together (s 3E). The MOU covers their respective roles and responsibilities (arts 3-5), information sharing (9-12), confidentiality (13-15), policy and rule-making (16-20), firm authorisation and individual approval (21-25), supervision of firm subject to dual regulation (26-31), overseas firms (37-38), UKLA coordination (39- 42), regulatory processes and enforcement (43-53), unauthorised regulated activities (54-55), financial crime (5657), agency coordination (58-60), fees (61-63), cross-board positions (64) and maintenance (65-67). 

The Treasury may by order specify the boundaries between the FCA and PRA responsibilities (s 3G FSMA). The PRA may require the FCA to restrain from certain action (s 3H FSMA) and may revoke an FCA direction (s 3I FSMA) with the PRA being required to consult with the FCA on the giving of directions (s 3J FSMA). Separate provisions apply on the consolidated supervision of groups (ss 3KN). The FCA and PRA are required to cooperate with the Bank of England. 

- e) Complaints are to be considered by a Complaints Commissioner. Complaints cover any act or omission including mistakes or lack of care, unreasonable delay, unprofessional behaviour, bias or lack of integrity. Complaints should be made within 12 months of the date on which the complainant first became aware of the relevant circumstances. Complaints must be made in a durable medium. Regulators must acknowledge receipt of complaints within five business days of receipt and complete their investigation within four weeks or write to the complainant setting out a reasonable alternative timetable. 

## **B44.** 

The question was based on the facts surrounding the Galleon Group hedge fund which formerly managed $7 billion in assets before its closure in October 2009. Its lead manager, Raj Rajaratnam, was found guilty on 14 charges of fraud and insider trading in May 2011 and received 11 years in prison. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) The facts raised potential concerns with regard to insider trading and market abuse under the CJA and MAR. It is less likely that there were any issues of misleading impressions or statements under ss 89-90 FSA 2012. The facts do not clearly indicate that any offences have been carried although the trader has referred the matter to compliance to consider. Relevant facts include: 

   - (i) Large trade volumes although this has to be expected as part of the trading and prime brokerage services provided for a hedge fund; 

   - (ii) The rumours of merger talks and potential imminent merger; 

   - (iii) The contact between the manager and CEO and CFO of the firms concerned; 

   - (iv) The profits that the hedge fund would earn; 

   - (v) The banking group's involvement as provider of trading and brokerage services for the fund and its potential involvement in any offence; 

   - (vi) The group's potential financial liability; 

   - (vii) The firm's potential reputational damage. 

- b) The relevant offences involved insider dealing under CJA 1993 and market abuse under MAR. The conditions for each of the offences and relevant penalties and defences should have been reviewed. Specific points to highlight are that insider dealing covers direct dealing and encouraging another to do so (either the manager or CEO or CFO at either firm), disclosing inside information (by the CEO and CFO), the securities covered (which requires a regulated market with the nature of the trading instructed unclear), nature of the information involved, inside source and territorial scope. The CEO and CFO may also have been guilty of improper disclosure. 

Relevant market abuse issues include the need for the activity to be with regard to financial instruments traded on a regulated market, MTF or OTF. UK 

establishments are also required to disclose suspicions of abusive trades to the FCA under MAR and avoid tipping-off as with money laundering. 

Separate concerns may arise under the Bribery Act 2010 (BA 2010) which came into effect on 1 July 2011. This covers active bribery, passive bribery, bribing a foreign official and failure to prevent bribery. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c) Potential criminal provisions apply in respect of insider dealing under CJA 1993. Explain relevant offences defences and penalties. 

- d) A number of provisions of PRIN, SYSC and COND may have been breached by the firm and COCON and FIT by the individuals involved. Detail relevant provisions. 

- e) The following action could have been considered by the trader: 

   - (i) Act on any instructions given by compliance following full disclosure; 

   - (ii) Possibly ask to be removed from acting if there are continuing concerns; 

   - (iii) If the trader remains dissatisfied with the advice given by compliance, obtain independent advice within the firm such as from one of the NonIndependent Directors; 

   - (iv) If the trader is still dissatisfied, contact the FCA or CISI including under the Public Interest Disclosure Act (whistle blowing) procedures provided for under SYSC 18; 

Firm relevant remedial action may include: 

- (i) Conduct an internal review of the previous or related transactions; 

- (ii) Notify the FCA and criminal investigation authorities; 

- (iii) Liaising with any other relevant regulatory or criminal authorities involved in the UK or elsewhere; 

- (iv) Consider withdrawing from acting if necessary to avoid potential involvement and liability; 

- (v) Take legal separate advice if necessary. 

## **B45.** 

This question provided a case study related to anti-money laundering systems and controls in relation to safeguard facilities. 

- a) While not a regulatory requirement, conducting an annual AML audit serves as a proactive measure to ensure compliance with AML regulations and to mitigate the risks associated with financial crime. The FCA’s Financial Crime Guide outlines the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

need for firms to establish and maintain effective systems and controls to counter the risk of financial crime, including money laundering and terrorist financing. An annual AML audit can provide the bank with assurance that the EMI is adhering to these regulatory requirements by systematically reviewing its policies, procedures, and operational practices. The audit provides an independent assessment of the effectiveness of the EMI’s AML framework, ensuring that it complies with the FCA’s standards. The financial sector is constantly evolving, with new risks and threats emerging regularly. An annual AML audit allows the EMI to identify and address vulnerabilities in its systems and controls. By regularly auditing its AML practices, the EMI can adapt to changes in the regulatory environment and respond to new typologies of financial crime. This continuous improvement process is essential for managing risks effectively and ensuring that the EMI is not exploited by criminals. An annual AML audit also benefits the EMI, by enhancing the oversight function within the EMI and keeping senior management appraised of the firm’s AML practices. It ensures that the senior management and board are fully aware of the institution’s AML performance and are taking appropriate actions to address any deficiencies. This level of oversight is critical for maintaining a strong culture of compliance and for demonstrating to regulators that the EMI is committed to preventing financial crime. 

For the bank providing safeguarding services to the EMI, the annual AML audit serves as a crucial line of defence. It ensures that the EMI is not engaging in practices that could expose the bank to financial crime risks. By requiring the EMI to conduct an annual AML audit and reviewing the audit reports, the bank can gain assurance that its partner is compliant with AML regulations and that the funds it holds are not linked to illicit activities. This practice helps to protect the bank from regulatory penalties, reputational damage, and potential legal liabilities. The audit fosters transparency and accountability within the EMI by ensuring that all aspects of its AML framework are scrutinised and reported on. This transparency helps to build trust with stakeholders, including customers, regulators, and business partners, who rely on the EMI to conduct its operations ethically and in compliance with the law. Finally, the audit process is helpful in driving continuous improvement within the EMI. By identifying gaps and areas for enhancement, the audit provides a roadmap for strengthening the firm’s AML framework. This ongoing refinement 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

helps the EMI stay ahead of potential threats and maintain a robust defence against financial crime. 

b) Failing to enforce robust AML controls can expose a bank to severe legal and reputational risks, which could have long-lasting consequences. The FCA requires banks to implement and maintain adequate systems and controls to prevent money laundering and terrorist financing. By not having sight of the EMI’s AML arrangements, the bank may be in breach of its obligations under SYSC 6.1.1R and SYSC 3.2.6R, which could result in significant regulatory sanctions. These could include financial penalties, restrictions on the bank’s ability to operate, or even the revocation of licences for severe breaches. The FCA has the authority to impose substantial fines, which not only affect the bank’s financial stability but also its operational capabilities. A failure to enforce AML controls could trigger heightened scrutiny from regulators. The bank may face more frequent inspections, audits, and demands for detailed reports on its AML practices. This increased scrutiny can strain the bank’s resources, both financially and operationally, as it attempts to demonstrate compliance in the face of regulatory concerns. Additionally, a history of non-compliance can lead to a loss of the regulator’s trust, making it more challenging for the bank to gain approvals for new products or services in the future. If the EMI, under the bank’s safeguard facilities, engages in money laundering activities due to inadequate AML controls, the bank could be held legally liable. Investors, customers, or third parties who suffer losses due to the bank’s failure to prevent money laundering might pursue legal action, seeking damages for negligence. This could lead to costly litigation, further financial losses, and additional strain on the bank’s resources. In some cases, senior executives could also face personal liability if it is found that they were negligent in their duties to enforce AML controls. 

A bank’s reputation is one of its most valuable assets. If the public or clients perceive that the bank is not taking its AML responsibilities seriously, it could lead to a significant loss of trust. Clients may withdraw their funds and move their business to more reputable institutions, leading to a decline in the bank’s customer base and revenues. Additionally, potential clients may choose to avoid the bank, fearing that it may be involved in or susceptible to financial crimes. The media plays a critical role in shaping public perception. If the bank’s failure to enforce 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

AML controls leads to a money laundering scandal, it could attract widespread negative media coverage. This not only damages the bank’s reputation but can also influence investor sentiment, potentially leading to a decline in stock prices and market value. The negative publicity could also have a long-term impact, making it difficult for the bank to recover its reputation even after corrective actions are taken. The bank’s relationships with key stakeholders, including regulators, investors, and business partners, could be severely damaged if it is found to be lax in enforcing AML controls. Regulators may become more cautious and impose stricter requirements on the bank, while investors might lose confidence in the bank’s ability to manage risks effectively. Business partners, such as other financial institutions, may reconsider their associations with the bank to protect their own reputations, leading to a potential loss of strategic alliances and partnerships. The consequences of failing to enforce AML controls can also extend to the bank’s day-to-day operations. The need to address regulatory concerns, manage legal actions, and restore its reputation can divert significant resources away from the bank’s core business activities. This diversion can hinder the bank’s ability to innovate, expand, and compete effectively in the market. Moreover, the internal culture of the bank may suffer, with employees becoming disengaged or demoralised if they perceive that ethical standards are being compromised. Waiving the requirement for an annual AML audit could therefore have farreaching legal and reputational consequences for a bank. 

c) Senior management plays an important role in the effectiveness of a firm’s AML compliance programme. Their involvement is crucial for setting the tone at the top, ensuring that AML policies and practices are prioritised, adequately resourced, and effectively implemented across the organisation. The FCA’s Financial Crime Guide emphasises the importance of senior management in actively overseeing financial crime risks, which includes AML compliance. Senior management is responsible for establishing a robust governance framework that supports AML compliance. This includes setting clear expectations for compliance, creating accountability structures, and ensuring that there is a dedicated team or department responsible for AML activities. Senior leaders must also ensure that there is effective communication between the board, management, and operational teams regarding AML issues. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

When senior management is actively engaged, it signals to the entire organisation that AML compliance is a critical priority, which fosters a culture of vigilance and responsibility. One of the key responsibilities of senior management is to ensure that sufficient resources – both financial and human – are allocated to the AML programme. This includes investing in advanced technology for transaction monitoring, employing skilled personnel for compliance roles, and providing ongoing training for all employees. Without adequate resources, even the most well-designed AML programme can fail. Senior management’s commitment to providing these resources reflects the institution’s dedication to preventing money laundering and complying with regulatory standards. Senior management must be actively involved in the ongoing assessment and management of AML risks. This includes reviewing and approving the institution’s AML risk assessments, ensuring that the institution’s risk appetite aligns with its AML policies, and regularly monitoring the effectiveness of the AML controls. Their oversight helps to identify emerging risks and ensures that the AML programme adapts to changing regulatory requirements and threat landscapes. 

When senior management is disengaged, the institution may fail to recognise or respond to new risks, leading to potential breaches in compliance. The presence of senior management in AML oversight is essential for fostering a culture of compliance within the organisation. They are accountable not only to regulators but also to shareholders and customers for the institution’s conduct. By leading by example, senior management can instil a strong ethical culture where compliance is embedded in the daily operations of the institution. Conversely, a lack of engagement from senior management can lead to a culture of complacency, where AML compliance is seen as a box-ticking exercise rather than a fundamental component of the institution’s operations. Senior management plays a critical role in maintaining positive relationships with regulators. They are often the primary point of contact in regulatory discussions and are responsible for ensuring that the institution meets all regulatory expectations. Active engagement with regulators helps to build trust 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and can lead to more constructive regulatory interactions. If senior management is not engaged, the institution may struggle to meet regulatory expectations, leading to more frequent inspections, stricter oversight, and potential sanctions. When senior management is not actively involved in overseeing AML compliance, the effectiveness of the programme can be severely compromised. A lack of leadership can result in insufficient resources, poor risk management, and a weak compliance culture. This disengagement can increase the institution’s vulnerability to money laundering, resulting in regulatory penalties, reputational damage, and financial losses. Furthermore, disengaged senior management may fail to respond appropriately to regulatory changes or emerging threats, leaving the institution exposed to significant risks. 

   - d) In addition to due diligence, ongoing monitoring, transaction monitoring, and screening, the audit should focus on several other critical elements to ensure the EMI has a robust financial crime framework in place, including: 

- **Business-Wide Risk Assessment –** The audit should evaluate whether the EMI has conducted a comprehensive business-wide risk assessment that identifies and assesses the risks of financial crime across all areas of its operations. This includes understanding the nature, scale, and complexity of the EMI’s business and the risks posed by different types of customers, products, and services. The risk assessment should be regularly updated to reflect changes in the business environment and emerging threats. 

- **Management Information (MI) –** The quality and effectiveness of the management information used by the EMI to monitor and manage financial crime risks should be assessed. MI should provide senior management with timely, accurate, and relevant data on the institution’s exposure to financial crime risks and the effectiveness of its AML controls. This includes information on suspicious activity reports, transaction monitoring alerts, and the outcomes of due diligence checks. 

- **Suspicious Activity Reporting (SAR) –** The EMI’s procedures for identifying, reporting, and escalating suspicious activities should be reviewed to ensure the EMI has clear, documented processes for filing SARs with the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

appropriate authorities in a timely manner and that staff are adequately trained to recognise and report suspicious transactions. The audit should also verify that SARs are appropriately logged and that management is informed of significant reports. 

- **Training and Awareness –** The effectiveness of the EMI’s AML training programmes should be evaluated. This includes ensuring that all employees, from frontline staff to senior management, receive regular training on AML policies, procedures, and the latest typologies of financial crime. The audit should verify that the training is tailored to the specific risks faced by the EMI and that it equips staff to effectively carry out their AML responsibilities. 

- **Governance and Oversight –** effectiveness of the EMI’s governance and oversight structures in managing financial crime risks should be assessed. This includes evaluating the role of the board and senior management in setting the tone at the top, providing oversight, and ensuring that AML controls are adequately resourced and enforced. The audit should also verify that there are clear lines of accountability, and that senior management is actively engaged in AML risk management. 

- **Incident Reporting and Record-Keeping –** The EMI’s processes for reporting financial crime incidents and maintaining records should be reviewed. This includes ensuring that incidents are promptly reported to the relevant authorities, that records are kept in accordance with regulatory requirements, and that there is a clear audit trail for all AML-related activities. 

- **Technology and Systems –** The adequacy of the EMI’s technology and systems used for AML compliance should be evaluated. This includes assessing the effectiveness of transaction monitoring systems, the accuracy of screening tools, and the ability of the EMI’s systems to detect and prevent financial crime. The audit should also ensure that the EMI’s technology is regularly updated to address new threats and that any system deficiencies are promptly addressed. 

By focusing on these additional elements the audit can provide a comprehensive 

evaluation of the EMI’s financial crime framework, ensuring that it is capable 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

of effectively managing the risks associated with money laundering and other financial crimes. 

## **B46.** 

The question relates to the money laundering and anti-terrorist financing as well as possible bribery concerns that may arise with regard to the establishment of a new Digital Internet Bank (DIB) by a famous branded travel company. 

## a) Immediate concerns arise with regard to: 

   - (i) The large new potential client base; 

   - (ii) The global coverage of the travel group's client base which may be transferred to DIB and involve potential money laundering, terrorist financing or bribery activities; 

   - (iii) The expected absence of any previous money laundering checks; 

   - (iv) Expected lack of any FATF list checks; 

   - (v) Absence of any branch structure as the DIB is an internet bank; 

   - (vi) Absence of any personal or face to face contact with customers; 

   - (vii) New licence and absence of previous experience or expertise in operating as a bank; 

   - (viii) New licence and absence of any previous expertise in managing money laundering, antiterrorist financing and bribery compliance; 

   - (ix) Potential systems and controls challenges; 

   - (x) Potential language difficulties; 

   - (xi) Potential breach of other overseas regulatory and criminal provisions including in the US; 

   - (xii) Ability to secure compliance with COND, PRIN, SYSC and COCON and FIT in the UK. 

- b) The principal offences with regard to money laundering, anti-terrorist financing and bribery should have been noted including relevant penalties and defences. 

- c) The company will have to set up specific anti-money laundering, anti-terrorist financing and anti-bribery systems and controls. Specific money laundering 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

obligations would apply with regard to CDD, KYC and EDD. As much detail as possible should have been provided. 

- d) The DIB must also ensure full compliance with COND, PRIN, SYSC, COCON and FIT as noted. Specific systems and controls requirements on identifying, assisting, monitoring and managing money laundering are imposed under SYSC 6.3.1R. 

- e) The firm is required to report any suspicious transactions to the authorities although it must not alert (tip-off) the customers concerned. The matter will be dealt with the MLRO. The transactions involved should be frozen during the period of notification. 

## **B47.**
