The question was based on the recent LIBOR offences uncovered in the UK, US and elsewhere. The question is concerned with oil contract rates although the same principles would apply. 

- a) Concerns arise with regard to the following: 

   - (i) Inappropriate requests made manipulate reported rates; 

   - (ii) Commission of offence of making false or misleading statements by the submitter with regard to a relevant benchmark under s91 FSA 2012; 

   - (iii) Commission of offence of creating a false or misleading impression by the oil trader as to the price or value of any investment contrary to s91 (2) FSA 2012; 

   - (iv) Possible market abuse contrary to MAR; 

   - (v) Possible separate misleading statements or impressions contrary to ss8990 FSA 2012; 

   - (vi) Conflict of interest between the trader and submitter; 

   - (vii) Conflict of interest between the firm and clients or other market counterparties affected by the manipulation of the benchmark rate; 

   - (viii) Defective supervision; 

   - (ix) Defective systems and controls; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

   - (x) Breach of the Remuneration Code in SYSC 19A; 

   - (xi) Possible breach of specific COBS; 

   - (xii) Defective Chinese Walls between separate departments; 

   - (xiii) Breach of a number of PRIN by the firm; 

   - (xiv) Breach of a number of provisions of SYSC by the firm; 

   - (xv) Breach of COCON by the trader, submitter and two other dealers; 

   - (xvi) Weak compliance. 

- b) The principal statutory prohibition would be on market abuse under MAR. This applies with regard to insider dealing, improper disclosure, breach of reasonable market behaviour, false or misleading impression, use of fictitious devices, dissemination of false information or other similar behaviour. The FCA may impose appropriate penalties. The courts may impose a penalty on the request of the FCA (s129 FSMA). Various statutory defences and safe harbours are available. 

- c) New offences relating to the issuance of misleading statements in relation to benchmarks were created under s91 and Part 7 FSA 2012. Two offences are introduced in respect of a person making a statement in relation to the setting of a relevant benchmark knowing that the statement is false or misleading and a person who acts or engages in a course of conduct creating a false or misleading impression as to the price or value of an investment or the interest rate appropriate to the transaction which is relevant to the setting of the benchmark (s91 (1) and (2) FSA 2012). 

Defences are available in relation to stabilisation rules, control of information rules and EU buy-back programmes and financial instrument stabilisation (s91 (4)). A person is liable on summary conviction to up to 12 months imprisonment or ten years on indictment or a fine or both. 

[Tutor’s Note: The penalties have been increased to ten years under FSA 2021.] 

- d) Separate regulatory breaches may apply with regard to: 

   - (i) Breach of PRIN 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9 and 11; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

   - (ii) Breach of SYSC 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 10, 18 and 19A; 

   - (iii) Breach of COND 2, 4 and 5; 

   - (iv) Breach of COCON 1, 2, 3 and 5 as well as possibly COCON senior 

      - manager rules with regard to the controlled function of setting benchmarks; 

   - (v) Breach of FIT 1, 2 and 3. 

- e) Relevant penalties include: 

   - (i) Variation of Part 4A permission; 

   - (ii) Withdrawal of authorisation; 

   - (iii) Withdrawal of approval in respect of the trader, submitter and other dealers; 

   - (iv) Public or private censure; 

   - (v) Financial penalty; 

   - (vi) Prohibition order; 

   - (vii) Restitution order; 

   - (viii) Investigation and information requests; 

   - (ix) Approved persons reports. 

- f) The firm should consider: 

   - (i) Immediately suspend the trader and submitter; 

   - (ii) Conduct an internal review of the immediate conduct; 

   - (iii) Review any previous transactions carried out by the trader and submitter and other traders and submitters; 

   - (iv) Review benchmark setting procedures; 

   - (v) Review systems and controls; 

   - (vi) Review Chinese Walls; 

   - (vii) Review conflicts of interest, policy and controls 

   - (viii) Review escalation procedures; 

   - (ix) Review record-keeping procedures; 

   - (x) Review wider compliance functions; 

   - (xi) Confirm whether any clients or other counterparties suffered loss and offer appropriate compensation arrangements; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (xii) Notify and cooperate fully with the FCA and other regulatory and police authorities. 

- (xiii) Review general firm compliance with PRIN, SYSC, COND, COCON and FIT. 

## **B48.** 

- a) As of 29 July 2024, significant changes have been made to the listing rules for the London Stock Exchange (LSE) aimed at enhancing the flexibility and attractiveness of the UK’s capital markets. Key changes include: 

   - The previous distinction between the Premium and Standard listing segments has been removed. There is now a single listing segment that streamlines the requirements and focuses on high corporate governance standards without the need for dual requirements. 

   - The minimum free float requirement has been reduced from 25% to 10%. This change is designed to make it easier for companies to list by allowing them to retain a larger portion of their equity. 

   - The eligibility criteria for listing have been simplified, with a focus on ensuring companies have sufficient working capital and governance structures in place rather than meeting complex financial thresholds. This includes the removal of the requirement for a three-year track record for eligibility. 

   - The new rules allow for dual-class share structures (DCSS) for a period of up to 10 years, which can enable founders or major shareholders to retain control of the company even after it is publicly listed. 

• There are increased disclosure requirements around environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors, reflecting the growing importance of sustainability in investment decisions. Companies are now expected to provide more detailed information on their ESG practices and impacts. 

These changes aim to attract more companies to the LSE by reducing the complexity of the listing process while maintaining high standards of transparency and corporate governance. 

- b) A prospectus is the document prepared in connection with an application for admission of securities to trading on a UK regulated market or an offer of securities 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

to the public in the UK pursuant to the Prospectus Rules. The prospectus serves as the primary source of information for investors, enabling them to make informed decisions regarding their investments. A prospectus is mandatory when a company proposes to make a public offer of securities. A public offer is defined as any communication to the public, in any form, that provides sufficient information on the terms of the offer and the securities to be offered, allowing an investor to decide to purchase or subscribe to those securities. This applies whether the offer is made to retail or institutional investors, ensuring that all potential investors receive adequate information. A prospectus is also required when securities are to be admitted to trading on a regulated market like the LSE. The prospectus must be approved by the FCA before the securities can be admitted to trading. The purpose of the prospectus in this context is to ensure that investors are fully informed about the company’s business, financial position, and the risks associated with the securities being listed. 

There are specific exemptions where a prospectus may not be required. For instance, offers made exclusively to qualified investors, such as financial institutions or large corporations, are exempt from the prospectus requirement. This is because these investors are deemed capable of assessing the risks associated with the investment without the need for the detailed information typically provided in a prospectus. Offers with a total consideration of less than €8 million over a 12-month period are also exempt from the prospectus requirement. This threshold is intended to reduce the regulatory burden on smaller companies seeking to raise capital without compromising investor protection. Offers made to fewer than 150 persons per EEA state (excluding qualified investors) also qualify for exemption. This allowance facilitates more streamlined fundraising processes for private companies or limited-scale offerings. The prospectus must comply with the content and format requirements set out in the UK Prospectus Regulation. This includes presenting information in a structured and consistent manner, covering key areas such as the company’s business model, financial statements, risk factors, and details of the offering. The prospectus should be written in clear, concise language, avoiding jargon and ensuring that it is accessible to a wide range of investors. The FCA expects the prospectus to be comprehensive yet digestible, providing investors with all the necessary information to make an informed decision. Before the prospectus can be published, it must be submitted to 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and approved by the FCA. The approval process involves a thorough review to ensure that the document meets all regulatory requirements and provides a true and fair view of the company and the securities being offered. Companies and their directors are legally accountable for the accuracy and completeness of the information contained in the prospectus. Any material misstatements or omissions can lead to significant legal consequences, including fines, sanctions, and potential civil liability from investors who suffer losses as a result of relying on the misleading information. 

c) The contents of the prospectus are governed by the UK Prospectus Regulation and must be clear, comprehensive, and presented in a manner that is easily understandable by investors. The prospectus should begin with a thorough description of the company’s business, including its history, core activities, principal products or services, and markets served. This section should provide an overview of the company’s operational structure, including any subsidiaries, and its position within the industry. Information about the competitive environment in which the company operates, including key competitors and the company’s market share, should be included. This helps investors understand the company’s strategic positioning and the external factors that may impact its business. The risk factors section must outline all the significant risks associated with the company’s business, industry, and the securities being offered. This includes operational risks, financial risks, market risks, legal and regulatory risks, and any other factors that could materially affect the company’s performance or the value of the securities. 

The FCA requires that risk factors are presented prominently and in a way that reflects their importance. Risks should be described clearly and concisely, without downplaying their potential impact. Investors should be able to easily understand the nature and magnitude of the risks involved. The prospectus must include audited financial statements covering at least the last three financial years, or since the company’s incorporation if it has been operating for less than three years. These statements should provide a comprehensive view of the company’s financial health, including its balance sheet, income statement, and cash flow statement. The prospectus should provide 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

management’s analysis of the company’s financial performance over the reporting period. It should include a discussion of significant trends, events, or uncertainties that have affected or are expected to affect the company’s financial condition and results of operations. If the company has undergone significant transactions, such as mergers or acquisitions, or if it is planning to do so, pro forma financial information may be required. This information shows how the company’s financial statements would have looked if the transaction had occurred at an earlier date. The prospectus must provide detailed information about the company’s directors and senior management, including their qualifications, experience, and roles within the company. This section should also disclose any potential conflicts of interest and the mechanisms in place to manage them. The prospectus should include information on the company’s governance framework, including its board committees, internal controls, and policies on executive remuneration, risk management, and ethical conduct. The prospectus must clearly outline the terms and conditions of the securities offering, including the type and number of securities being offered, the offer price, and the offer timetable. The company must explain how it intends to use the funds raised from the offering. This could include specific projects, debt repayment, capital expenditures, or general corporate purposes. The prospectus should include information about any regulatory approvals that the company requires or has obtained in connection with the offering or its business operations. This might involve licensing, environmental permits, or compliance with industryspecific regulations. Any ongoing or pending legal proceedings that could have a material impact on the company’s financial position or operations must be disclosed. 

Given the increasing importance of ESG factors in investment decisions, the prospectus should include information on the company’s environmental, social, and governance practices. This might cover areas such as sustainability initiatives, workforce diversity, community engagement, and corporate responsibility. The prospectus should also address how ESG factors impact the company’s business and what strategies it has in place to manage ESG-related risks and capitalise on opportunities. This information is increasingly critical for investors who are focused on sustainable and responsible investing. The prospectus should explain the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

company’s dividend policy, including its historical dividend payments (if applicable) and future dividend plans. Investors need to understand how the company intends to return value to shareholders. Information on the company’s capital structure, including its current indebtedness and any plans to raise additional capital, should be provided. This helps investors assess the company’s financial leverage and its ability to service debt. For companies listing securities, the prospectus should include information about the market where the securities will be traded, including details on the trading currency, market practices, and any special considerations for investors. 

## **B49.** 

The fact pattern was concerned with the compliance issues that may arise for firms involved with foreign exchange markets and exchange rate setting. This follows the LIBOR scandal and separate concerns with regards to possible gold market fixing. Revised provisions were introduced on misleading statements and impressions with regards to benchmark setting under the FSA 2012. 

- a) The principal statutory provision on benchmarks is now set out in s91 FSA 2012. This creates two specific offences of making a false or misleading statement to another person in connection with benchmark setting arrangements or acting or engaging in a manner that creates a false or misleading impression as to the price or value of an investment or the most appropriate interest rate (s91(1) and (2) FSA 2012). Defences apply with regard to price stabilisation and control of information rules and buy-back programmes. A person found guilty of the offence is liable for up to ten years imprisonment or a fine on indictment. Reference may also have been made to the more general offences of making a misleading statement or impression under ss89 and 90 FSA 2012. 

   - [Tutor’s Note: The penalties have increased to ten years under FSA 2021.] 

- b) Separate offences may have been committed under MAR on market abuse depending upon the particular circumstances. This may, in particular, have included such behaviour as improper disclosure, misuse of information, manipulating transactions, dissemination or misleading behaviour or distortion. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- c) A number of provisions of FCA PRIN (1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 9 and 11), PRA FR, SYSC (2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 10, 18 and 19A), COCON (1, 2, 3 and 5 as well as possibly the senior manager rules), COND (2, 4 and 5) and FIT (1, 2 and 3) may have been breached with the full range of regulatory penalties applying. These would principally relate to integrity, systems and controls, organisation, compliance and well as the Remuneration Code in SYSC 19a. 

- d) The head of foreign exchange and firm could consider: 

   - (i) conduct of internal review into activities of the bank's foreign exchange traders; 

   - (ii)   review relations with traders and other firms under investigation to ensure no firm involvement; 

   - (iii)   consider possible disciplinary action with regard to any traders involved in any of the activities referred in (i) or (ii) above; 

   - (iv)   investigate any potential client losses with regard to any affected transaction; 

   - (v)   review benchmark setting procedures; 

   - (vi)   review systems and controls; 

   - (vii)  review conflicts of interest policy and control; 

   - (viii) review recordkeeping procedures; 

   - (ix)   review wider compliance functions; 

   - (x)   review any relevant escalation procedure to the extent relevant; 

   - (xi)   review reporting lines and governors' functions. 

- e) The firm is required to cooperate with the authorities under PRIN 11, PRA FR 7 and COCON 3. The firm may, in particular, consider: 

   - (i)   discussing any concerns with the regulators following the internal reviews conducted; 

   - (ii)   explaining all of the internal action carried out to the firm's principal line supervisor; 

   - (iii)   review board oversight of foreign exchange operations; 

   - (iv)   ensure that specific director has operational responsibility for the firm's foreign exchange operations: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (v)   ensure that foreign exchange operations monitored by non-executive directors; 

- (vi)   confirm internal training with regard to compliance with internal benchmark setting arrangements; 

- (vii)  review internal whistle blowing procedures with regards to internal staff reporting; 

- (viii) consider reporting any instances of criminal activity or conduct contrary to regulatory provisions involving other firms to the regulators. 

- (ix)   review remuneration policy in light of findings; 

- (x)   review firm's ethics policy with regard to findings. 

## **B50.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regards to the governance structure of an asset management firm within a larger corporate group. This involves a combination of governance, conflicts, regulatory and other ethical considerations. 

a) The governance arrangements raised a number of immediate concerns: 

- (i)   The chief executive officer (CEO) acts as CEO and head of finance and risk management; 

- (ii)   The position of the chief operating officer (COO) has remained vacant for an uncertain period; 

- (iii)   While the point is not confirmed, finance and risk management may have reported separately to the chief operating officer (COO) rather than CEO; 

- (iv)   Attendance by the executive directors at asset management company meetings is irregular with only two directors having been appointed; 

- (v)   The asset management company only has one non-executive director; 

- (vi)   There is no remuneration policy committee and presumably no separate statement of remuneration policy. 

- (vii)  The CEO was apparently able to secure the awarding of a 30% pay increase for himself without any apparent performance review or discussion; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (viii) The asset management company holds a client account with another subsidiary company with regular client order flow being placed through the account; 

- (ix)   The CEO is a majority shareholder in the parent company of both the asset management company and the separate stock broking firm. 

The governance structure of the asset management company and group can accordingly only be described as being weak. Specific regulatory concerns then arise with regard to: 

- (i)   FCA PRIN 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 3 (management and control), 4 (financial prudence), 6 (customers' interest), 7 (client communication), 8 (conflicts of interest), 10 (client asset protection) and 11 (regulatory relationships); 

- (ii)   SYSC 2 (senior management arrangements), 3 (systems and controls), 4 (general organisational requirements), 6 (compliance and internal audit), 7 (risk control), 10 (conflicts of interest), 12 (groups risk systems and controls), and 19A (remuneration code); 

- (iii)   COND 2.7 (business model); 

- (iv)   COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence); 

- (v)   FIT 2.1 (honesty, integrity and reputation), 2.2 (competence and capability) and 2.3 (financial soundness); 

- (vi)   Possibly TC 2 (competence) and 3 (recordkeeping); 

- (vii)  CASS 1.A (oversight responsibility), possibly 6 (custody rules) and 7 (client money rules). 

Separate issues may arise with regard to money laundering controls and the apparent absence of any MLRO as well as market abuse and insider dealing controls. 

- b) The question refers to separate ethical breaches apart from the statutory and regulatory matters already referred to in (a) above. No specific answers were expected. Relevant issues may have included: 

   - (i) The general attitude of the CEO to regulatory compliance; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (ii)   The general culture of the firm in terms of weak governance and compliance; 

- (iii)   The potential dominance of the CEO in managing the firm’s affairs; 

- (iv)   The potential conflicts of interest between the firm and clients in using the separate stock broking subsidiary; 

- (v)   The potential losses suffered by clients through intra-group order flow; 

- (vi)   The potential client loss with client accounts being held by a separate subsidiary within the group; 

- (vii)  The apparent lack of disclosure of the order flow activity and separate client account with the group subsidiary; 

- (viii) The lack of disclosure of the CEO's majority shareholding in the group parent company; 

- (ix)   The absence of a remuneration code; 

- (x)   The absence of any effective non-executive committee or non-executive director oversight; 

- (xi)   The lack of involvement of executive directors in regular board matters; 

- (xii)  The more general perception of a lack of care for client interests and assets; 

- (xiii) Apparent lack of concern with conduct risk; 

- (xiv) Apparent lack of concern with potential regulatory damage and risk. 

Reference may also have been made to additional provisions within, for example, the CISI Code including with regard to acting honestly and fairly at all times (CISI 1), avoiding reputational damage (CISI 2), observance and compliance (CISI 3), conflicts (CISI 5), professional competence (CISI 6) and possibly failing to act with the highest personal and professional standards (CISI 8). 

- c) A number of potential conflicts of interest may have been identified including between: 

   - (i)   The CEO and other members of the firm in terms of pay aggravated by the absence of a separate remuneration policy committee and remuneration policy; 

   - (ii)   The asset management company and clients in connection with order flow through separate subsidiary company without apparent disclosure; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

      - (iii)   The asset management company and separate subsidiary with clients with the holding of client accounts with the separate subsidiary without apparent disclosure; 

      - (iv)   The possible lack of independence between the asset management company and separate subsidiary; 

      - (v)   Possible control exercised by the CEO over activities of the group and of the separate subsidiary companies to client detriment. 

- d) Reports by skilled persons may be commissioned (s166 FSMA) in connection with any matter in relation to which information or the production of documents could be requested (s165 FSMA). 

   - Information or documents may be requested in connection with the exercise by an authority of any functions conferred by or under the Act (s165 (4) FSMA). The report may then cover any of the matters referred to in (a) or (b) above including specifically: 

      - (i) Potential breach of PRIN 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 10 and 11 (from sub-section (a) above); 

      - (ii)   Potential breach of SYSC 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 10, 12 and 19A; 

      - (iii)   Potential breach of COND and COND 2.7; 

      - (iv)   Potential breach of COCON 1, 2, 3 and FIT 2.1, 2.2 and 2.3 as well as TC 2 and 3; 

      - (v)   Potential breach of CASS and CASS 1.A and 6 and 7; 

      - (vi)   Potential breach of specific COBS; 

      - (vii)  Other potential governance failures; 

      - (viii) Absence of a remuneration committee and remuneration policy; 

      - (ix)   Apparent absence of any conflicts of interest policy; 

      - (x)   Other potential conduct risk and compliance failures and consequent client damage to clients. 

- e) Firms are required to deal with the authorities in an open and cooperative manner and to provide all reasonably expected information (FCA PRIN 11 and PRA FR 7). Appropriate remedial action should be taken and fully reported to the authorities. This may include: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (i) Reviewing the firm's governance arrangements especially with regard to CEO role and function; 

- (ii)   Appointing a COO and separating the finance and risk management from CEO function; 

- (iii)   Reviewing the firm's systems and controls; 

- (iv)   Reviewing the firm's remuneration policy; 

- (v)   Reviewing the conduct of executive functions within the firm; 

- (vi)   Reviewing the conduct of non-executive functions within the firm; 

- (vii)  Reviewing risk management policy and function; 

- (viii) Reviewing firm's internal audit policy and function; 

- (ix)   Reviewing compliance policy and function; 

- (x)   Reviewing compliance with PRIN, SYSC, COND, COCON and FIT; 

- (xi)   Reviewing compliance with CASS and client money arrangements; 

- (xii)  Reviewing the firm's training and policy function under TC; 

- (xiii) Possibly ensuring appropriate disclosure of CEO's shareholding under relevant Listing and Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules (DTR) as necessary; 

- (xiv) Reviewing the firm's conduct risk policy and environment; 

- (xv)  Reviewing the firm's ethics policy and environment; 

- (xvi) Reviewing recruitment policy and HR; 

- (xvii) Reviewing conflicts of interest policy and disclosure arrangements; 

- (xviii) Reviewing wider group arrangements especially with regard to client account management; 

- (xix) Reviewing compliance with the Skilled Persons Report; 

- (xx)  Cooperating closely with the regulatory authorities under PRIN 11 and COCON 3. 

## **B51.** 

The question raised issues with regard to the use of a high frequency trading (HFT) facility to dispose of otherwise unsellable government debt. New provisions were introduced under MiFID II on HFT and algorithmic trading. This specifically requires authorisation of proprietary trading through HFT facilities, market making arrangements, order to trade 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

(message) ratios, tick (volume) sizes and venue pricing to ensure transparent, fair and nondiscriminatory fees. Rather than raise regulatory issues directly, the question was concerned with different forms of market manipulation. 

- a) The principal potential offences are concerned with misleading statements and impressions (ss89-90 Part 7 FSA 2012). These apply with regard to the making of false or misleading statements or creating a false or misleading impression as to the market, price or value of relevant investments. Penalties are provided under s92 with up to ten years imprisonment or a fine or both with specific defences included under ss89 and 90. As much detail as possible should have been provided in the time available. 

- [Tutor’s Note: The penalties have increased to ten years under FSA 2021 

- b) The FCA may impose penalties on market abuse under MAR including with regard to manipulating transactions, manipulating devices, dissemination and misleading behaviour and market distortion. 

- c) Acting on the information received may amount to dealing on inside information contrary to MAR or insider dealing under the Criminal Justice Act 1993. Relevant defences and penalties could have been provided although not specifically referred to. 

- d) Disposing of the debt through the HFT facilities which another client has paid for separate access to may constitute a conflict of interest contrary to PRIN 8 and SYSC 10. Firms are required to identify all conflicts of interest between the firm and its clients or between clients under SYSC 10.1.3R with guidance on the types of conflicts covered provided in SYSC 10.1.4R. Firms have to keep a record of conflicts (SYSC 10.1.6R) and maintain and operate effective organisational and administrative arrangements to manage conflicts (SYSC 10.1.7R) or disclose the conflicts to the client (SYSC 10.1.8R) with a separate conflicts of interest policy also being produced (SYSC 10.1.10R). Chinese walls are provided for although these are not relevant in this case (SYSC 10.2). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

e) Regulated firms are required to deal with regulators in an open and cooperative manner and disclose anything that the regulator would reasonably expect notice of under PRIN 11 and PRA FR7. The investment bank could specifically consider: 

- (i)   Undertaking an internal review of the request made by the bond desk; 

- (ii)   Considering any appropriate disciplinary action; 

- (iii)   Reviewing compliance arrangements, in particular, to ensure that the firm maintains appropriate systems to prevent market manipulation, market abuse and insider trading or other benchmark offences; 

- (iv)   Reviewing reporting arrangements; 

- (v)   Reviewing whistleblowing procedures; 

- (vi)     Reviewing training under TC; 

- (vii)  Reviewing whether the client suffered any loss and make appropriate disclosure and compensation as necessary; 

- (viii)  Providing a full report on all action taken to the FCA. 

## **B52.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to the conduct of a sales broker and the responsibility of a co-broker. Candidates were required to identify relevant issues, regulatory and ethical requirements, implementing firm policies and procedures and comment on the options available to the co-broker. 

Candidates had to identify the risks generated by William's behaviour. This was principally concerned with identifying the facts that raised issues from a compliance and ethical perspective. Candidates could also have referred to the FCA's conduct risk framework or to background papers on financial ethics. They could also have referred to the nature of risk and compliance more generally. 

Relevant procedures should have been identified. Alana essentially had four choices in terms of taking no action, discussing the matter with William, raising concerns within all of the relevant office procedures or taking the matter outside the firm. Candidates were invited to discuss the relative advantages and disadvantages of each. Candidates then had to decide on the most appropriate action. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) Candidates were asked to identify the relevant risks. This could have been dealt with in a number of ways with no specific answer being required. Candidates could have identified relevant factual concerns that may have presented risks to the individuals involved and the firm, the specific regulatory and ethical provisions that may have been breached or the more general financial risks involved as discussed in the CISI Regulation & Compliance workbook. 

Relevant issues from the facts provided that may be of concern include: 

- (i)   No specific difficulties may arise with regard to the dinner as this was for friends and acquaintances rather than clients; 

- (ii)   Reference to the ‘all-expenses paid trip’ to a golf tournament appears unusual for a sales broker; 

- (iii)   Commission payments outside the grade scales for the new client; 

- (iv)   The possible connection between the golf trip and new client commission rates; 

- (v)   The reference to ‘under the radar’ suggests that whatever is involved is outside approved firm policies and procedures; 

- (vi)   The possible validity in terms of firm policy of the separate ‘boys group’ holiday to New York and its connection with the other matters raised; 

- (vii)  William's apparent failure to disclose the gift or benefit; 

- (viii) The possibility of previous misconduct by William including failure to disclose earlier gifts or benefits; 

- (ix)   Possible damage to the new client relationship; 

- (x)   Potential regulatory breaches of PRIN, SYSC, COCON, FIT, COND, COBS and TFC; 

- (xi)   Possible misappropriation of funds from the new client by William to fund the golf trip; 

- (xii)  Possible breach of conduct by Alana through failure to disclose; 

- (xiii) Possible regulatory sanctions against the firm; 

- (xiv) Possible reputational damage to the firm. 

The specific regulatory and ethical provisions that may have been breached include: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (i) PRIN (integrity), PRIN 2 (skill, care and diligence), PRIN 3 (management and control), PRIN 6 (customers' interests), PRIN 7 (client communications), PRIN 8 (conflicts of interest), PRIN 9 (customers' trust) and PRIN 11 (regulatory relationship); 

- (ii)   SYSC 2 (senior management arrangements), SYSC 3 (systems and controls), SYSC 4 (general organisation requirements), SYSC 5 (employees), SYSC 6 (compliance), SYSC 7 (risk control), SYSC 10 (conflicts of interest) and SYSC 19a (Remuneration Code); 

- (iii)  Threshold conditions s41(2) and (Schedule 6 FSMA) and COND (Suitability); 

- (iv)   COCON 1 (integrity), COCON 2 (due skill, care and diligence), COCON 5 (market conduct), COCON 3 (regulatory relationship); 

- (v)   FIT (honesty, integrity and reputation and competence and capability); 

- (vi)   Possible breach of COBS 11 (use of dealing commission); 

- (vii)   Possible breach of Treating Customers Fairly outcomes; 

- (viii)   Possible breach of CASS through misappropriation of client funds; 

- (ix)   Breach of FCA conduct risk policy; 

- (x)   Breach of FCA ethical policy; 

- (xi)   Breach of CISI Code of Conduct including CISI 1 (act honestly and fairly), CISI 2 (integrity), CISI 3 (regulatory observance), CISI 4 (market integrity), CISI 5 (conflict of interest), CISI 6 (professional competence), CISI 7 (decline to act) and CISI 8 (highest personal and professional standards). 

Candidates may also have referred to the specific risks that William's activities created. These include credit, market and liquidity risk, investment risk, operational risk, enterprise risk, business risk and corporate governance and risk oversight. Reference may have been made to the ten principles imposed under the BIS paper on Compliance and the compliance function in banks (April 2005) and the MiFID new compliance function requirements and the MiFID Implementing Directive and relevant European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) guidelines in 2012 although this was not necessary. 

Candidates may also have referred to possible enforcement action by the FCA outlining all possible investigatory and other penalties that may have been imposed. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- b) The three most important firm policies and procedures were possibility on Gifts and Inducements, Remuneration and Conflicts of Interest (with the question having three marks). The full range of relevant policies and procedures may have included: 

   - (i) Gifts and Inducements Policy (including limits and reporting requirements); 

   - (ii)   Remuneration Code; 

   - (iii)  Conflicts of Interest Code; 

   - (iv)  General Compliance Manual; 

   - (v) Client Commission Rate policy; 

   - (vi)  Treating Customers Fairly policy; 

   - (vii)  Conduct Risk policy; 

   - (viii)  Bribery and Corruption Act policy; 

   - (ix)  Systems and Controls (SYSC) policy; 

   - (x) Governance policy (if separate); 

   - (xi)  Suitability and Appropriateness policy (if separate); 

   - (xii)  Training policy; 

   - (xiii)  Mentoring policy (if separate from Training); 

   - (xiv)  Disciplinary policy; 

   - (xv)  Whistleblowing policy (under the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 

      - (PIDA)); 

   - (xvi)  Reporting policy; 

   - (xvii)  Commission Rate policy (if separate); 

   - (xviii) Compliance policy; 

   - (xix)  CISI Reporting or Speak Up policy. 

- c) Alana may consider taking, at least, four principal courses of action (with the question having four marks) although these may include various sub-alternatives: 

   - (i) Take no action; 

   - (ii) Discuss the matter with William; 

   - (iii)  Refer the matter to her Line Manager, failing which escalate this through the Compliance Department and then Non-Executive Directors (NEDs) or Audit Committee; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (iv)  Whistleblowing under the PIDA including referring the matter to the FCA or CISI. 

Alana may also want to take independent legal advice. No fixed answer was necessary. 

- d) Candidates may have discussed the advantages and disadvantages of the options they identified in different ways. The principal issues that arise include: 

   - (i) Take no action; 

      - Alana is an approved person (presumably CF30 (Customer-dealing function)) and cannot responsibly take no action. She does not appear to carry out a significant management or governing function although she cannot support or condone misconduct. Her team is specifically known for its supportive culture and positive working environment. She should support the firm's overall conduct risk management and compliance function. She may also be considered to be involved if no separate action is taken. Former approved persons can also be held responsible for conduct for up to three years. She should, at minimum, try to obtain further information as to whether any misconduct has been carried out by William. 

   - (ii) Discuss the matter with William; 

      - Alana may wish to discuss the matter with William although it has to be expected that he may deny any wrongdoing. This would nevertheless give William the opportunity to explain his conduct and possibly take any remedial action such as by reporting the gift or correcting the commission rate. Alana may consider this appropriate as William is popular within the team. He was also her mentor which may include some personnel responsibility within the firm. It may also be the case that no misconduct was involved. 

   - (iii)  Refer the matter to a line manager or compliance; 

      - Alana may always report any matter to her Line Manager although as the team is supporting and as William is popular, it is possible that Alana 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and William have the same Line Manager who may not wish to take any action. Alana should be aware of this and if she reports the matter to her Line Manager, follow-up to ensure that some action is taken, at minimum, to confirm that no misconduct has been involved. Alana can discuss the matter with her Line Manager in confidence if she does not wish William to know due to their friendship and close working relationship. 

Alana can consider escalating the matter if no action is taken by the Line Manager by reporting it to Compliance and if no action is taken by Compliance to one of the NEDs or the NED Co or Audit Committee. This will ensure that the appropriate personnel within the firm have been properly and fully advised and that appropriate action should be taken. Escalating the matter immediately would nevertheless generally be inappropriate as the firm's proper procedures should be followed first. While formal investigations and possible disciplinary action may damage William's reputation within the firm and possibly result in his eventual dismissal, this may also confirm that no misconduct has been involved. Alana should be entitled to ask for any enquiries and investigations to be conducted in a confidential manner with William not knowing that she reported the matter and to avoid any recrimination within the team or within the wider firm. 

- (iv)  Refer the matter to the CISI or FCA; 

Alana can always consider external reporting under the PIDA with the FCA being a ‘prescribed Person’ under the Act. The FCA attempts to ensure that the anonymity of whistleblowers is protected unless released by the whistleblower or required by court. The FCA policy with regard to whistleblowers is set out in its statement on ‘How we handle disclosures from whistleblowers’ (February 2015) with Whistleblowing being dealt with on the FCA website at http://www.fca.org.uk/siteinfo/contact/whistleblowing. 

Alana may also consider referring the matter to the CISI. The CISI has specifically promoted its own ‘Speak Up’ policy which was launched on 22 September 2014. The five checks referred to consist of ‘See, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Prepare, Evaluate, Act and Keep trying’. CISI also refers to ‘four steps to Speak Up’ consisting of informal disclosure, formal disclosure, regulatory disclosure and public disclosure. These are set out in CISI, Speak Up (2015) and http://www.cisi.org/speakup. 

e) Alana would be advised initially to try to obtain some further information to confirm whether her suspicions have any basis or not. If this indicates that there may have been some misconduct, she may refer this to her Line Manager (HR) on a confidential basis. She should ensure that some further enquiries and investigations are conducted to confirm whether any misconduct has been involved. If no action is then taken, she should escalate through Compliance and the NED Co or Audit Committee failing which she should refer the matter to the CISI and FCA. Separate public disclosure may be considered although in practice this would be unlikely especially if the CISI and FCA were already involved. 

## **B53.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to potential money laundering concerning an overseas foreign exchange trading firm operating through a UK subsidiary. A new client wishes to use business to business (B2B) funds transfer procedures with eight different source brokers within the City of London. The country of origin of the client is listed as a High Risk jurisdiction and the money laundering function has been outsourced to the overseas headquarters. Earlier guidance on banks' responsibility with regard to money laundering was, for example, set out in FSA, Banks' management of high money-laundering risk situations (June 2011). 

Candidates should have identified the factual points that raised concerns from a money laundering and compliance perspective. Relevant offences, defences and penalties should have been listed. The nature of the FATF three stages of layering, placement and integration should have been explained. Additional money laundering enquiry and oversight requirements should have been noted and the most appropriate action to be taken by the firm explained. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

a) Initial concerns arise with regard to the following: 

   - (i) Satisfaction with core know your customer (KYC) and other Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing requirements; 

   - (ii) The original source of the funds involved; 

   - (iii)  The need for the funds to be diverted through eight different brokers; 

   - (iv)  The use of B2B business funds transfer (possibly to avoid anti-money laundering detection); 

   - (v) The adequacy of the AML controls carried out by the other brokers involved; 

   - (vi)  The High Risk jurisdictional status of the new client; 

   - (vii)  The size of the initial transfer and possible future transfers; 

   - (viii)  The adequacy of the firm's AML compliance procedures in its headquarters; 

   - (ix)  Possible AML breaches within the UK; 

   - (x) Possible breach of other FSMA regulatory requirements in the UK; 

   - (xi)  Possible breach of other Terrorist Financing controls in the UK and abroad. 

- b) The full range of relevant offences, defences and penalties under the Proceeds of Crime Act should have been noted including assistance (ss327-329 POCA 2002), failure to report (ss330-332), tipping off (s33A POCA 2002 as amended) and prejudicing an investigation (s342 as amended). The principal offence was possibly concerned with concealment. Revisions to the POCA were made under the Serious Organised Crime and Police Act 2005 with separate reference possibly being made to reporting under s21A Terrorism Act 2000 and to the Bribery Act 2010. A Consolidated List of persons and countries against whom financial sanctions are in place is maintained by HM Treasury and the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) with separate lists of uncooperative jurisdictions being managed by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF) which should be consulted. 

- c) The FATF refers to the three stages of money laundering as consisting of placement, layering and integration. Each of these should have been explained. Placement involves the introduction of illegal profits into the financial system. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Layering consists of the conversion or movement of funds to distance or separate them from the source. Integration consists of the re-entering or re-use of funds within the legitimate economy. These are, for example, referred to on the FATF website under ‘What is Money Laundering?’ (available http://www.fatfgafi.org/pages/faq/moneylaundering/. 

- d) Further investigations and monitoring should have been considered with regard to each of the following: 

   - (i) Confirm whether the client is a Politically Exposed Person (PEP); 

   - (ii) Confirm identity of clients on the PEP register and Sanctions Lists; 

   - (iii)  Carry out Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) if the client is a PEP and if the client is not physically present in addition to simplified due diligence (SDD); 

   - (iv)  Ask for additional identification (including proof of residence); 

   - (v) Confirm source of funds; 

   - (vi)  Track funds and withdrawal patterns; 

   - (vii)  Confirm adequacy of know your customer (KYC) and other procedures outsourced to the head office; 

   - (viii)  Confirm checks carried out by City brokers if required. 

- e) The following further action may be considered appropriate: 

   - (i) Review adequacy of Anti-Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing policies and procedures; 

   - (ii) Ensure that the firm maintains an appropriate policy for higher risk clients (including PEPs and EDDs); 

   - (iii)  Confirm reporting procedures between head office and London parent; 

   - (iv)  Confirm any separate outsourcing arrangements and policies; 

   - (v) Confirm reporting lines and general compliance policy; 

   - (vi)  Ensure no separate breach of relevant regulatory requirements (including PRIN, SYSC, COND, COCON and FIT as well as TC, COBS and CASS); 

   - (vii)  Ensure that no other clients have suffered any connected losses and offer compensation as necessary; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

(viii)  Report any residual concerns to the FCA under PRIN 11 and COCON 3. 

## **B54.** 

An FCA regulated firm offering contracts for differences and spread betting has launched a promotional product with the firm covering part of the clients' losses. Some clients realised that they could lock in profits by hedging the trades with other firms. While the firm initially thought that only a few clients would attempt to take advantage of this, forty per cent of new clients adopted the strategy within three months. The firm decided to treat these clients as abusive and block withdrawals from the accounts. 

Financial promotions are generally dealt with under s21 FSMA and the Financial Promotion rules as amended. New powers were brought in under the s137S Financial Services Act 2012 to ban misleading financial promotions through directions. Candidates should have referred to s21 FSMA and also the new direction powers set out in s137S FSMA as amended. 

Relevant PRIN should have been listed and other Handbook sections noted including SYSC, COCON, COBS, CASS, TC and PERG. The nature of the Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) initiative could have been explained and relevant outcomes identified. FCA penalties should have been reviewed and the most appropriate remedial action to be taken by the firm outlined. 

_[Tutor note: Part c) of the original exam referred to FTOC in terms of YouWin CFD’s behaviour. I have edited and re-written both the requirement, and the answer, to Part c) so as to reflect the Consumer Duty, and how this could be tested and answered. Part c) provided here, therefore, no longer follows the original Examiner’s Report.]_ 

- a) The principal statutory section breached is s21 FSMA on financial promotion restrictions which specifies that a financial promotion may only be communicated in the course of business by an authorised person or if the content has been approved by an authorised person. Exemptions are set out in the Financial Promotions Order (FPO) as amended. The Financial Promotion rules are provided for under s137R FSMA as amended. Financial promotions must be fair, clear and not misleading (COBS 4.2.1R). Candidates may also have referred to the directions contained in s137S FSMA as amended by the FSA 2012 through the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

serving of a notice on the persons concerned. The firm may also have breached PRIN 1 (integrity), 3 (management and control), 6 (customers' interest), 9 (customers' relationship of trust), 10 (clients' assets) and 11 (regulatory relationship). 

- b) The firm may have breached COBS 2.1 in failing to act honestly, fairly and professionally (COBS 2.1.1R) and COBS 2.2 in failing to provide appropriate information in a comprehensive form (COBS 2.2.1R). The firm may have breached COBS 4, in particular, in failing to ensure that communications are not fair, clear and not misleading (COBS 4.2.1R), COBS 4.10 in failing to maintain proper systems and controls for approving and communicating financial promotions (COBS 4.10.1 and 2) and COBS 4.11 adequate record keeping (COBS 4.11.1R). The firm may have breached SYSC 3 systems and controls and 6 on compliance. 

The client may also have breached CASS 7 on client money rules including treatment of client money (CASS 7.11.1R) and client segregation (CASS 7.13.1, 2 and 3). The firm will also have breached DISP 1 in failing to treat complainants fairly including awareness (DISP 1.2), complaints handling rules (DISP 1.3), resolution rules (DISP 1.4), the next business day rule (DISP 1.5) and record keeping (DISP 1.9). Reference may also have been made to PERG 8 financial promotion and related activities and COBS 11 on dealing and managing. 

The firm may also have breached COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (due skill, care and diligence), 5 (market conduct standards), and 3 (regulatory cooperation). 

- c) Whether YouWin CFDs has acted correctly from a Consumer Duty perspective would depend, first of all, upon whether or not YouWin CFDs is within scope of the Consumer Duty. Assuming that consumers can take part in the services offered by YouWin CFDs, and there is nothing in the scenario to suggest that consumers are prevented from opening accounts with YouWin CFDs, then YouWin CFDs is in scope of the Consumer Duty. As such, it is clear that YouWin CFDs has not ‘acted to deliver good outcomes to retail customers’, as PRIN 12 requires. 

_[Tutor Note: There are two methods we can employ so as to justify our belief that YouWin CFDs has failed in the Consumer Duty expectations. One would be to look_ 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

_at the FCA’s guidance on ‘good outcomes’; the other would be to examine YouWin CFDs’s conduct in terms of the Cross-cutting Rules. Either would do well in terms of scoring good marks in the exam; remember to be careful with your time management here.]_ 

## _[First method]_ 

The FCA’s Consumer Duty guidance refers to ‘good outcomes’ as meeting four criteria. These are: 

- The Products and Services Outcome: products are fit for purpose. 

- The Price and Value Outcome: products represent fair value 

- The Customer Services Outcome: customer service from firms meets customers’ needs. 

- The Customer Understanding Outcome: customer understanding is maintained at each step of the customer’s journey. 

YouWin CFDs has not ensured that its service has been correctly designed before launch; if it had, it seems clear that the product would not allow the hedging with competitors activities of the so-called ‘abusers’ to take place. Additionally, YouWin CFDs would have made it clear that such activities are disallowed in both its terms and conditions, and also in its promotional material. 

Furthermore, YouWin CFDs has taken a decision to stop withdrawals by all ‘abusive’ clients without checking to see that each of these clients is actually abusing YouWin CFDs’s service – this is unfair to those customers that are being accused by YouWin CFDs without evidence or justification. Finally, YouWin CFDs’s decision to stop all communications with these customers, and instead wait to see if those customers take their case to court, is a breach of the requirement to provide customer service meeting those customer’s needs. 

## _[Second method]_ 

PRIN 12 requires in-scope firms to comply with three ‘cross-cutting rules’: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

**Acting in Good Faith Towards Retail Customers** – Firms must act with honesty, fairness, and openness in all dealings with retail customers. Actions should align with what customers reasonably expect, and this principle should be embedded at the design stage of products and services to ensure fairness from the beginning. 

It is clear that YouWin CFDs is breaking this rule – it is not fair to target a large proportion of customers (over 40%) and claim they are ‘abusers’ without evidence and without a proper investigation. Nor is it ‘open’ to withdraw from communication with these customers, unless they take YouWin CFDs to court. 

**Avoiding Foreseeable Harm** – Firms are responsible for ensuring that retail customers understand and accept the inherent risks in products and services. They must take proactive steps to prevent foreseeable harm within their control and must not exploit customers' vulnerabilities, behavioural biases, or lack of financial literacy. 

YouWin CFDs should have carried out proper testing of its products before launch, and identified the so-called ‘abuse’ of hedging exposures with other firms, so as to be able to warn customers that this action would be perceived as abusive by YouWin CFDs, triggering freezing of their account. 

## **Enabling and Supporting Retail Customers to Pursue Their Financial** 

**Objectives** – Firms should provide relevant information and understanding to support customers in making their own financial decisions. This approach empowers customers to take responsibility for their choices, fostering an environment of trust and confidence in the firm's products and services. 

Spreadbetting accounts are necessarily much more complex than many retail customers are used to, and so they would find these accounts difficult to understand. YouWin CFDs should have provided adequate factsheets and risk warnings to customers so that such customers would have had a clear idea of how the product worked before investing. 

- d) The FCA may proceed against the firm by issuing a direction under s137S FSMA as amended. A direction may be issued where the FCA considers that the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

communication or approval breaches the financial promotion rules with the direction requiring withdrawal, refraining from making the communication, publishing details of the direction or taking any other action (s137S (1) and (2)). Directions generally take effect immediately although the person may make representations within the period specified (s137S (4) and (6)). The FCA may revoke the direction following representations (137S (8)). 

A person breaching s21 is guilty of an offence and liable for up to six months imprisonment (on summary conviction) or two years (on indictment) or a fine or both (s25 (1) FSMA). Agreements entered into in consequence of an unlawful communication are nevertheless unenforceable against the other party who is entitled to recover any money or property paid and compensation for loss sustained (s30 (2) (a) and (b) FSMA as amended). The FSA may impose a public or private censure (s205) or general financial penalty (s206 (1)). An action for damages may be available under s138D FSMA as amended. Injunctions are available under s380 and restitution orders under s382 FSMA. The firm's permission may be varied and authorisation withdrawn if necessary. 

- e) The firm should consider the following remedial action: 

   - (i) Suspend or cancel the promotion until a full investigation and review have been carried out; 

   - (ii) Conduct an investigation to determine whether any statutory or regulatory provisions have been breached; 

   - (iii)  Determine whether any clients have suffered loss and offer compensation as appropriate; 

   - (iv)  Advise the FCA accordingly and cooperate fully under PRIN 11 and COCON 4; 

   - (v) Review the firm's systems and controls under SYSC; 

   - (vi)  Review the firm's governance arrangements and decision-taking and compliance procedures; 

   - (vii)  Review the firm's FTOC/Consumer Duty procedures; 

   - (viii)  Review the firm's complaints handling procedures; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (ix)  Review the firm's training and policy function under TC; 

- (x) Review the firm's remuneration policy to the extent relevant; 

- (xi)  Review the firm's ethics policy to the extent relevant; 

- (xii)  Review the firm's conflicts of interest policy to the extent relevant. 

## **B55.** 

The question raised a number of issues with of concern with regard to the activities of a US investment bank in Canary Wharf. This reflected the circumstances surrounding the closure of Lehman Bros in the UK (Re Lehman Bros International (Europe) [2012] UK SC 6). The purpose of the question was to separate the UK regulatory treatment of culture from financial ethics and conduct risk. Some candidates mixed the two points together in the separate subsections (b) and (c). 

- a) Candidates were asked to identify initial relevant regulatory concerns. The first hedge fund was a $100,000 equity fund set up by Alfred Winslow Jones in 1949 using short sales and leverage. Hedge funds are established with limited membership to benefit from exemptions available under the US Securities Act 1933, Securities Exchange Act 1934, Investment Company Act 1940 and Investment Advisers Act 1940. 

The question could either have been dealt with by listing specific facts or activities that may raise regulatory issues or by referring to specific sets of regulatory provisions that may be breached. Referring to specific regulatory provisions nevertheless overlapped with the treatment of culture and conduct in subsections (b) and (c) and was therefore not necessary for subsection (a) although not penalised. 

Relevant activities or aspects of hedge fund operations include: 

- (i) adopting aggressive trading practices including short selling and excessive stock lending 

- (ii) using aggressive trading strategies (including possibly global macro, event driven, directional, market neutral, arbitrage, short/long, emerging markets, managed futures and funds of funds strategies) 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (iii)  high leverage 

- (iv)  trading in high-risk instruments including structured products and natural derivatives 

- (v) trading on the over-the-counter (OTC) market 

- (vi)  establishment in off-shore financial centres 

- (vii)  lack of transparency and limited reporting 

- (viii)  lack of direct regulatory oversight and control 

- (ix)  high fee and remuneration structures 

- (x) potential market abuse of insider trading 

- (xi)  potential money laundering 

- (xii)  more general possibly weak cultural and ethical frameworks. 

These may create a full range of higher financial risks (including credit, market, interest rate, falling currency, commodity and derivative exposures), operational risks (including delivery and settlement risks), legal risks, management risks and wider environmental risks. 

Against these disadvantages, candidates could have referred to hedge funds advantages although this was not necessary. These include higher investment return, improved portfolio management, greater portfolio diversification and increased market correction, market volatility, market liquidity, market efficiency, financial engineering, financial vision, market return to profitability and overall market stability. 

- b) Candidates were asked to explain the general nature of the UK authorities’ expectations and requirements with regard to culture within financial firms. The authorities have increasingly discussed ethics in terms of culture although this remains a difficult issue. 

A number of general documents and speeches have been issued on ethics over time. The FSA's initial general approach to ethics was set out in FSA, An ethical framework for financial services (October 2002) with competence being examined in FSA, Competence and ethics (June 2010) CP 10/12. Ethics was considered as part of the Retail Distribution Review (RDR) with the FSA recommending the creation of an enforceable Code of Ethics for investment advisors in FSA, Retail Distribution Review (November 2008) Feedback Statement 08/06. This was 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

referred to again in FSA, Distribution of retail investments: Delivering the RDR (June 2009) CP 09/18 with eight general principles proposed. These can be summarised in terms of acting honestly and fairly at all times, integrity, legal and regulatory compliance, market integrity standards, conflict, professional competence, declining to act and upholding the highest personal and professional standards. FSA Chairman, Howard Davies, discussed ethics in ‘Ethics in regulation’ in 2001. 

FSA CEO Hector Sants switched the focus from ethics to culture in ‘Do regulators have a role to play in judging culture and ethics?’ in June 2010 and ‘Can culture be regulated?’ in October 2012. Hector Sants noted in the first speech that when he joined the FSA, he was told by senior management that ‘the FSA does not do ethics’. Howard Davies had earlier stated that the FSA could not ‘seek to act as the conscience of the square mile.’ Rather than focus on ethics, the ‘better focus for regulators would be culture, which no doubt reflects the ethical framework of the organisation’. Sants considered that the work ethics was ‘not precise enough’ and carried ‘too much baggage for regulators’. There was nevertheless ‘already a consensus of what constitutes the right behaviour’ which included through the codes produced by the CISI and Worshipful Company of International Bankers which had ‘broad acceptance in the community’. Firms should have ‘a culture which encourages individuals to make judgements and deliver the outcomes we want’ and that ‘at all times we want an undertaking to act with integrity’. The regulators had therefore to focus on ‘what an unacceptable culture looks like and what outcomes that drives’. 

A number of speeches were subsequently made by FCA Chief Executive, Martin Wheatley (such as ‘Competing on integrity’ (November 2013); ‘Ethics and economics’ (March 2014); ‘The fairness challenge’ (October 2013); and ‘Good conduct and market integrity’ (June 2014)) and other FCA staff (including Clive Adamson, FCA Director of Supervision, ‘The importance of culture in driving behaviours of firms and how the FCA will assess this’ (April 2013); and ‘Trust and confidence – ensuring firms' ethics are built around their customers’ (November 2013); and later Mark Steward, ‘Culture and governance’ (November 2015)). 

Culture was referred to in the FCA Business Plan 2015/16 as a key driver in behaviour, with an effective culture being one that supports a business model and 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

business practices that have the fair treatment of customers and clients at their core and support market integrity. Culture began at the top with the FCA ensuring that firms and boards dealt with conduct issues as a priority. Boards had to engage in conduct issues by specifically investigating products or business lines, understanding cross selling product strategies, possible loss and suitable product sales. Customer focus had also to be translated into how business decisions were made, the treatment of consumer responses, individual behaviour, issue escalation and remuneration and incentive awards. 

The emerging UK ethical framework is also reflected in the more specific provisions contained within the High Level Standards and other more specific laws and regulations designed to ensure ethical outcomes. No separate ethical sourcebook has been prepared at this time although the issue of banking standards is to be considered by the new Banking Standards Board (BSB) set up following the Banking Standards Review (May 2014) by Sir Richard Lambert after the earlier recommendations for the establishment of a professional body by the Parliamentary Commission on Banking Standards (PCBS) final report on Changing banking for good (12 June 2013) Vols I and II. 

Candidates could also have referred to the specific requirements imposed under FCA PRIN, SYSC, COCON (and PRA FR) as well as COND for firms and FIT for individuals. While a number of PRIN are more prudential based including 3 (management and control) and 4 (financial prudence), others are more ethical and conduct based including 1 (integrity); 6 (customers' interests); 7 (communication); 8 (conflicts of interest); 9 (trust); and asset protection (10) as well as skill, care and diligence within PRIN 2. This is also reflected in COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), and 5 (proper standards of market conduct). Conflicts of interest are dealt with under SYSC 10 and remuneration under SYSC 19A. Elements of this are also reflected under more specific COBS including 3 (categorisation), 4 (communication and financial promotions), 6 (disclosure), 8 (agreements), 9 (suitability), 10 (appropriateness), 11 (dealing and managing), 12 (investment research), 13 and 14 (product information), 15 (cancellation) and 16 (reporting). 

Reference may also have been made to the business standards sourcebooks including Conduct of Business (COBS), Insurance: Conduct of Business (ICOBS), 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Mortgages and Home Finance: Conduct of Business (MCOB) and Banking: Conduct of Business (BCOBS). 

A number of other statutory provisions can also be considered to include an ethical element such as the prohibition on market abuse in MAR and misleading statements and impressions in ss89-90 FSA 2012 as well as separate insider dealing offences in the CJA. 

c) Conduct risk also remains a difficult issue. The FCA has generally been made responsible for conduct regulation with the PRA managing prudential regulation under the revised institutional structure established under the FSA 2012. The FCA is specifically responsible for ‘conduct risk’ although this is a difficult issue to define precisely. The term conduct risk is not referred to in the FSMA as amended. Conduct risk is not separately defined in the FCA Glossary nor is the term ‘risk’ separately defined. The objectives of the FCA are referred to under the FSMA in terms of acting as a financial function authority with the specific operational objectives of promoting consumer protection, system integrity and competition (s1B (2) and (3) FSMA). Conduct risk is not separately defined and it would generally appear that this refers to anything that is not of a prudential regulation nature in practice. 

The FCA has been developing its approach to conduct risk under its 2013 and 2014 Risk Outlook documents with a series of priority actions being set out in its Business Plans for 2013 and 2014. While the 2013 Risk Outlook expressly referred to ‘conduct risk’, this is simply expressed in terms of risk in the 2014 Risk Outlook and Risk Outlook included within the 2015 Business Plan. The Risk Outlook was incorporated into the FCA Business Plan 2015/16 released on 24 March 2015. The FCA explains its prudential responsibility and prudential risk in its 2014 Risk Outlook at 65-67. 

The new policy risk model is generally made up of two parts with ‘Drivers of Risk’ (Part A) consisting of Underlying drivers (inherent factors and Structures and business conduct features) and Environmental developments) and ‘Evolving risk landscape’ (Part B) consisting of Cross-market pressures and related risks and Forward-looking areas of focus (2014 Risk Outlook P7). The key drivers are 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

expanded in the circular diagram provided by the FCA in the 2013 and 2014 Risk Outlooks. This refers to ‘Inherent risks’ (Information asymmetries, Biases (rules of thumb and mental shortcuts) and the growing importance of financial capability), ‘Structures and business conduct’ (market structures, culture and incentives and conflicts of interest) and ‘Environmental drivers’ (economic and market, technological and policy and regulatory) (FCA, Risk Outlook (2013) 10; and FCA, Risk Outlook (2014) 8). 

The principal types of conduct risk may have accordingly been identified with reference to the key drivers of risk set out in the FCA Risk Outlook (including inherent, structures and business conduct and environmental factors and subfactors referred to above). Candidates could also have referred to the forwardlooking areas of focus referred to in the Risk Outlook and Business Plan (including technological developments overtaking consumer capability and regulatory response, poor culture and controls undermining market integrity, large backbooks leading firms to act against customers' best interest, retirement income products and distribution delivering poor consumer outcomes, the growth of consumer credit creating unaffordable debt levels, terms and conditions being excessively complex and house price growth creating conduct issues). As the FCA refers to a number of other more specific areas of current concern under each of these headings, candidates may refer to any of these. No strict response was expected. 

The FCA published a separate new Our Strategy document in December 2014. Seven strategic themes were identified in Our Strategy and in the Business Plan 2015/16. These consisted of maintaining a sustainable model of regulation, adopting a common view of markets and sectors, new priority setting, developing a more strategic approach to risk, engagement and proactive influence, increasing the FCA’s internal ability to respond flexibly and making the most use of resources. 

Later acting FCA CEO, Tracey McDermott, referred to conduct risk in a speech on ‘Wholesale Conduct Risk’ at the BBA conference on ‘Wholesale Markets and Risk: FEMR and beyond’ on 24 July 2015. Tracey McDermott confirmed that conduct risk was treated like any other risk and referred to five key conduct questions that firms had to ask in managing conduct risks. Firms had to identify relevant conduct 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

risks inherent in the business, individuals responsible, support mechanisms to improve business conduct, board and executive committee oversight of conduct and whether there were any ‘perverse incentives’ or other activities that could undermine conduct strategies. Tracey McDermott also highlighted the importance of individual accountability and the operation of the new Senior Managers Regime. Candidates may also have referred to relevant high level regulatory provisions covering conduct risk including PRIN 1 (integrity), 5 (market conduct), 6 (customers' interest), 7 (client communication), 8 (conflicts of interest), 9 (trust) and 10 (asset protection) as well as COCON 1 (integrity) and 5 (market conduct), SYSC 10 (conflicts of interest) and 19 (remuneration) as well as COND and FIT more generally. Training requirements may also have been referred to under TC as well as Consumer Duty/Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) and the Retail Distribution Review (RDR). 

This is a difficult area and no specific answer was expected. 

- d) Relevant firm responses might have included the following: 

   - (i) Review the firm's ethics and conduct risk environment. 

   - (ii) Prepare an internal culture and ethics code. 

   - (iii)  Establish a separate ethics or conduct risk committee or subcommittee of the board or, at minimum, assign these functions to a specific internal body. 

   - (iv)  Review internal governance arrangements. 

   - (v) Review the firm's compliance policy and function. 

   - (vi)  Review the firm's risk management policy and function. 

   - (vii)  Review the firm's internal audit policy and function. 

   - (viii)  Review High Level Standards compliance (including PRIN, SYSC, COND, COCON and FIT). 

   - (ix)  Review the firm's training policy and function under the TC sourcebook. 

   - (x) Review internal systems and controls. 

   - (xi)  Review remuneration policy under SYSC 19C. 

   - (xii)  Review whistle blowing policy. 

   - (xiii)  Prepare a more specific firm Conduct Risk Framework (including 

Conduct Risk Appetite and Conduct Risk Strategy). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (xiv)  Integrating the conduct risk policy within the firm's Enterprise Risk Management Framework (ERFM) or equivalent. 

- (xv)  Review recruitment policy. 

- (xvi)  Commission independent reports on ethics compliance or conduct risk management to the extent necessary. 

- (xvii)  Review money laundering policy and MLRO function. (xviii) Review reputation or risk management. 

- (xix)  Review client cash and securities segregation functions. 

- (xx)  Cooperate closely with the regulators under PRIN 11 and COCON 3. 

## **B56.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to the conduct of the ‘Paris Dolphin’ in a major European securities firm (following the activities of the ‘London Whale’ which incurred $2 billion trading losses through a credit default swap (CDS) ‘hedging strategy’). Concerns arise with regard to product design, financial promotion, incentives and remuneration, possible market abuse or market manipulation and whistleblowing. 

Financial promotions are generally dealt with under s 21 FSMA and the Financial Promotion rules as amended. New powers were brought in under s 137S Financial Services Act 2012 to ban misleading financial promotions through directions. Candidates should have referred to s 21 FSMA and also the new direction powers set out in s 137S FSMA as amended. 

Relevant PRIN should have been listed and other Handbook sections noted including SYSC, COCON, COBS, CASS, TC and PERG. The nature of the Consumer Duty/Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) initiative could have been explained and relevant outcomes identified. FCA penalties should have been reviewed and the most appropriate remedial action to be taken by the firm outlined. 

- a) Financial promotions are principally dealt with under s 21 FSMA which specifies that a financial promotion may only be communicated in the course of business by an authorised person or if the content has been approved by an authorised person. Exemptions are set out in the Financial Promotions Order (FPO) as amended. The Financial Promotion rules are provided for under s 137R FSMA as amended. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Financial promotions must be fair, clear and not misleading (COBS 4.2.1R). Candidates should also have referred to the power to issue directions contained in s 137S FSMA as amended by the FSA 2012 through the serving of a notice on the persons concerned. The firm may also have breached PRIN 1 (integrity), 3 (management and control), 6 (customers' interest), 9 (customers' relationship of trust), 10 (clients' assets) and 11 (regulatory relationship). 

The firm may also have breached the FSA's policy on product regulation which was set out in Discussion Paper 11/1 on Product Intervention in January 2011 and Feedback Statement in 11/3 in June 2012. Product approval is sometimes referred to as ‘New Product Due Diligence’ (NPDD) which has become of more importance under the FSA and FCA’s new product intervention policy. 

Relevant product considerations included the following: 

- (i) There appeared to have been no written material provided explaining the product. 

- (ii) None of the material to be provided to investors appeared to have been made available. 

- (iii)  None of the marketing material to be used was provided. 

- (iv)  The product team representative only explained the product in outline terms. 

- (v) The nature of the product and any new features were not explained. 

- (vi)  The reasons for the withdrawal of any earlier products were not explained.
