- (vii)  The action to correct the earlier defects were not explained. 

- (viii)  The features which might still cause ‘alarm’ were not explained. 

- (ix)  The target customer group was not identified. 

- (x) No full report from Risk Management was considered. 

- (xi)  No critical comment was made by the Line Manager. 

- (xii)  No critical comment was made by Marketing. 

Factors relevant to the overall assessment process may also have included the following: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (i) There was no meaningful examination of the earlier product. 

- (ii) There was no meaningful examination of the replacement product. 

- (iii)  There was no meaningful contribution from Risk Management to the process. 

- (iv)  There was no balanced nor constructive contribution by Product Development. 

- (v) There was no balanced nor constructive contribution by Marketing. 

- (vi)  The role of compliance may have been marginalised. 

- (vii)  The interests of prospective investors were not properly respected. 

Separate offences may have been committed under MAR on market abuse. This may have included behaviour such as improper disclosure, misuse of information, manipulating transactions, dissemination or misleading behaviour or distortion. 

- b) The firm may have breached all of the FCA PRIN. Each of the relevant provisions should have been referred to briefly explaining their relevance. 

- c) Other policy initiatives that could have been referred to in further detail include: Product Regulation under DP 11/1 FS 11/3 with products to be assessed across all product cycle, including with a Retail Conduct Risk Outlook Analysis and Business Model and Strategy Analysis (BMSA); Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) (under DP 6/04 and other papers); and Retail Distribution Review (RDR) (under DP 7/1 and other papers). 

The firm may also have breached COBS 2.1 in failing to act honestly, fairly and professionally (COBS 2.1.1R) and COBS 2.2 in failing to provide appropriate information in a comprehensive form (COBS 2.2.1R). The firm may have breached COBS 4, in particular, in failing to ensure that communications are not fair, clear and not misleading (COBS 4.2.1R), COBS 4.10 in failing to maintain proper systems and controls for approving and communicating financial promotions (COBS 4.10.1 and 2) and COBS 4.11 adequate record keeping (COBS 4.11.1R). The firm may have breached SYSC 3 systems and controls and 6 on compliance. 

The firm may have separately breached CASS 7 on client money rules including treatment of client money (CASS 7.11.1R) and client segregation (CASS 7.13.1, 2 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and 3). The firm will also have breached DISP 1 in failing to treat complainants fairly including awareness (DISP 1.2), complaints handling rules (DISP 1.3), resolution rules (DISP 1.4), the next business day rule (DISP 1.5) and record keeping (DISP 1.9). Reference may also have been made to PERG 8 financial promotion and related activities and COBS 11 on dealing and managing. 

The firm may also have breached COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (due skill, care and diligence), 5 (market conduct standards), 3 (regulatory cooperation), and the senior manager rules. 

The firm may also have breached the FCA Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) requirements as originally set out in FSA, Treating customers fairly – towards fair outcomes for consumers (July 2006) with relevant management information (MI) being set out in FSA, Treating customers fairly – guide to management information (July 2007). FTOC is based on PRIN 6 (firms must pay due regard to the interest of customers and treat them fairly) and PRIN 7 (information needs and 

communicate in a clear, fair and not misleading way). The firm would specifically have breached FTOC Outcome 1 (customer confidence that they are dealing with firms with fair treatment being central to corporate culture), 2 (products and services marketed and sold are designed to meet the needs of customer groups), 3 (clear information and appropriately inform before, during and after sale), 5 (products perform as expected) and 6 (no unreasonable post sale barriers). 

- d) A person breaching s 21 is guilty of an offence and liable for up to six months’ imprisonment (on summary conviction) or two years (on indictment) or a fine or both (s 25 (1) FSMA). Agreements entered into in consequence of an unlawful communication are nevertheless unenforceable against the other party who is entitled to recover any money or property paid and compensation for loss sustained (s 30 (2) (a) and (b) FSMA as amended). The FCA may impose a public or private censure (s 205) or general financial penalty (s 206 (1)). An action for damages may be available under s 138D FSMA as amended. Injunctions are available under s 380 and restitution orders under s 382 FSMA. The firm's permission may be varied and authorisation withdrawn if necessary. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

The FCA may also proceed against the firm by issuing a direction under s 137S FSMA as amended. A direction may be issued where the FCA considers that the communication or approval breaches the financial promotion rules with the direction requiring withdrawal, refraining from making the communication, publishing details of the direction or taking any other action (s 137S (1) and (2)). Directions generally take effect immediately although the person may make representations within the period specified (s 137S (4) and (6)). The FCA may revoke the direction following representations (137S (8)). 

The FCA may impose penalties on market abuse under MAR including with regard to manipulating transactions, manipulating devices, dissemination and misleading behaviour and market distortion. 

Misleading statements and impressions are now dealt with under s 89-90 Part 7 FSA 2012. These apply with regard to the making of false or misleading statements or creating a false or misleading impression as to the market, price or value of relevant investments. Penalties are provided under s 92 with up to ten years imprisonment or a fine or both with specific defences included under s 89 and 90. 

[Tutor’s Note: The penalties have increased to ten years under FSA 2021.] 

- e) The firm could consider the following remedial action: 

   - (i) Suspend or cancel the promotion until a full investigation and review have been carried out. 

   - (ii) Conduct an investigation to determine whether any statutory or regulatory provisions have been breached. 

   - (iii)  Determine whether any clients have suffered loss and offer compensation as appropriate. 

   - (iv)  Advise the FCA accordingly and cooperate fully under PRIN 11 and COCON 3. 

   - (v) Review the firm's systems and controls under SYSC. 

   - (vi)  Review the firm's governance arrangements and decision-making and compliance procedures. 

   - (vii)  Review the firm's Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) procedures. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (viii)  Review the firm's complaints handling procedures; 

- (ix)  Review the firm's training and policy function under TC. 

- (x) Review the firm's remuneration policy to the extent relevant. 

- (xi)  Review the firm's ethics policy to the extent relevant. 

- (xii)  Review the firm's conflicts of interest policy to the extent relevant. 

## **B57.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to potential money laundering concerning the activities of potential new clients at the local branch of a major UK banking chain. A new clients wishes to start a new cash-intensive business with the account being used to transfer large volumes of funds to a number of accounts in different Eastern European countries. Earlier guidance on banks' responsibility with regard to money laundering was, for example, set out in FSA, Banks' management of high money-laundering risk situations (June 2011). 

Candidates should have identified the factual points that raised concerns from a money laundering and compliance perspective. Relevant offences, defences and penalties should have been listed. The nature of the FATF three stages of layering, placement and integration should have been explained. Additional money laundering enquiry and oversight requirements should have been noted and the most appropriate action to be taken by the firm explained. 

## a) Specific concerns include the following: 

- (i) The cash-intensive nature of the business. 

- (ii) The need for international money transmission as well as basic deposit services. 

- (iii)  The questionable size of the business being on a brownfield site in a small provincial town. 

- (iv)  The attempt to use a local branch in a small provincial town to avoid more central detection. 

- (v) The high profit expectations with the business having only been recently opened. 

- (vi)  The delivery of cash in a rucksack rather than use of cheques, bank or credit cards or other forms of funds transfer. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

      - (vii)  The different denominations involved. 

      - (viii)  The absence of additional information on the fees charged and questionable profitability. 

      - (ix)  The use of standing orders to transfer all net balances into different bank accounts. 

      - (x) The transfer of funds abroad on a monthly basis. 

      - (xi)  The lack of information on the start-up funds used to open the business. 

   - Additional issues may also have arisen from an ethical perspective: 

      - (i) Potential breach of PRIN 1, FR 1 and COCON 1 (integrity). 

      - (ii) The need to report suspicions under the Proceeds of Crime Act. 

      - (iii)  The need to cooperate with the regulators under PRIN 11, FR 8 and COCON 3. 

      - (iv)  The need to comply with other Codes of Conduct including the CISI Code. 

- b) The full range of relevant offences, defences and penalties under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (PCA) should have been noted. The principal UK money laundering offences are now restated under Part VII of the PCA. The PCA creates three main offences relating to Concealing (s 327), Arranging (s 328) and Acquisition, Use and Possession (s 329) as well as attempting, conspiring or inciting to commit any such offence (s 340(11)(a) and (b)). Money laundering includes aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring the commission of any of these offences (s 340(11)(c) and (d)). Two separate offences are also created of Failing to disclose (ss 330-332) and Tipping off (s 333). 

The principal offence was possibly concerned with concealment. Revisions to the PCA were made under the Serious Organised Crime and Police Act 2005 with separate reference possibly being made to reporting under s 21A Terrorism Act 2000 and to the Bribery Act 2010. 

- c) Relevant, identification and record requirements are imposed under the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance and Money Laundering Regulations 2007 including with regard to Customer Due Diligence (CDD), Know Your Customer (KYC) and identification requirements as well as Simplified Due 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Diligence (SDD) and Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) for higher risk entities as well as potential Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs). 

A Consolidated List of persons or countries against whom financial sanctions are in place is maintained by the HM Treasury and Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG). Separate lists of uncooperative jurisdictions are also maintained by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). 

- d) Institutional liabilities can be considered under the MLR 2007 provisions or general regulatory requirements. The MLR imposes internal systems and control requirements on firms including with regard to customer due diligence (CDD) and monitoring, internal reporting, five-year record-keeping, internal control, risk assessment and management, compliance monitoring, recognition and reporting of suspicious transactions and staff training. Specific FCA Handbook provisions include SYSC 3.2.6 (systems and controls with regard to compliance, financial crime and money laundering), SYSC 3.2.6I (MLRO) with FCA guidance on financial crime being provided in the FC guide. Breach of the MLR requirements is punishable by up to two years imprisonment and an unlimited fine or six months imprisonment and a £5,000 fine before a Magistrates Court. The full range of enforcement actions would be available for breach of relevant Handbook provisions. 

- e) Separate provisions with regard to anti-terrorist requirements are contained in the Terrorism Act 2000, Terrorism Act 2006 and Counter-Terrorism Act 2008. The 2000 Act defines terrorism and lists prescribed terrorist organisations with the police being given power to detain suspects and conduct stop and search interventions. The offence of glorifying terrorism was introduced under the 2006 Act after the July 2005 London bombings. The 2008 Act inter alia permits the Treasury to issue directions to financial firms in relation to CDD, ongoing monitoring, systematic reporting and limiting or ceasing business. Such directions are only used with regard to non-EEA, countries and on FATF advice to the Treasury. Civil penalties and criminal terms of up to two years imprisonment and fines are imposed. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Other relevant legislation includes the Anti-terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001 introduced after 11 September 2001, the Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005 which introduced control orders and the Terrorism (United Nations Measures) Order 2009 and Terrorist Asset-Freezing (Temporary Provisions) Act 2010 which allowed freezing orders despite a Supreme Court ruling that a 2006 United Nations Order was void under English law. 

## **B58.** 

This question raised a number of issues with regard to the ethical decisions taken by the firm, the board, the new head of compliance and the firm’s auditors. The issue of the more general treatment and promotion of ethical standards by FSA and FCA was also raised. The question was specifically concerned with segregation failures and breach of the Client Assets sourcebook (CASS). 

Candidates should have carefully read each sub-question and noted the relevant mark allocation before attempting to answer the question. They should then have moved through each sub-part in turn. 

a) Candidates should initially have explained the relevant breaches that the firm committed four years before. These principally related to CASS and segregation of client money under CASS 7.4. Candidates could have outlined the principal requirements of CASS (including 1, 1a, 3, 5, 6, 7, 7a and 8) with specific reference to the need to maintain client bank accounts (CASS 7.4.11) and to make payments into client bank accounts (CASS 7.4.14 and 15). 

Breach of CASS involves corresponding breach of PRIN 10 (clients’ assets), 6 (customers’ interest), 7 (communications with clients), 8 (conflicts of interest), 9 (trust) as well as 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence) and 3 (management and control). The firm also breached PRIN 11 (relations with regulators). Candidates could have referred to corresponding breaches of SYSC and COCON as well as COND and FIT. 

Candidates could have referred to approved persons breaches although these are more specifically raised in (b). As the question specifically asked whether the firm’s conduct had been reasonable, reference should have been made to relevant ethical rather than purely regulatory standards including specifically Discussion Paper 18, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

recent FSA and FCA statements, the CISI Code and the need to manage reputational risk effectively more generally. 

The best answers included a list of all specific regulatory and ethical breaches having regard to the facts raised. Particular points identified included the board decision not to report, reliance of the auditor report, apparent failure to consult compliance, length of breach and subsequent failure to report and take appropriate corrective compliance or regulatory advice. 

Reference could also have been made to the nature and importance of compliance and the role and function of the compliance officer within a regulated firm more generally. 

- b) Brian’s initial status is unclear and it can be assumed that he was not an approved person at the time of the initial infractions. If this was the case, it can be argued that he had no regulatory responsibility to take any action. If there had been any clear breach by senior compliance officers at the time, Brian could have considered relying on ‘whistle blowing’ protection under the Public Interest Disclosure Act. 

Brian should have taken action immediately on becoming head of compliance and an approved person under SMF16 and on becoming aware of all of the facts. This would have involved discussing the issue with relevant parties involved. 

- c) Brian should discuss the issue with relevant parties immediately (above) and try to obtain agreement to report to the FCA, failing which he should report the breach and corrective action taken directly. This could be done on either a full report basis or provisional notification and then subsequent discussion basis. Brian should also consider carrying out a number of separate internal reviews especially with regard to: 

   - i. Client bank account and segregation compliance; 

   - ii. General CASS compliance; 

   - iii. SYSC compliance; 

   - iv. COCON and FIT compliance; 

   - v. Training and Competence (TC) compliance; 

   - vi. Threshold conditions (Schedule 6) and COND compliance; 

   - vii. PRIN compliance; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

viii. Compliance with all other relevant ethical standards including the CISI Code. 

Reference may also have been made to: 

- i. Considering a review of the firm’s board functions and decision-taking procedures; 

- ii. Review of the composition of the board; 

- iii. Confirming original auditor’s finding that no clients suffered loss; 

- iv. Reviewing the conduct of any specific approved persons involved in the original decision including directors (SMF1 and SMF3), as well as former head of compliance (SMF16) and finance (SMF2), risk assessment (SMF4) and internal audit (SMF5) functions; 

- v. Consider possible action against relevant staff members including fines, suspension or dismissal (although unlikely in light of the length of time that has passed); 

- vi. Review of firm’s general ethical compliance policy. 

The firm may also want to consider providing a report to clients on the earlier breach of the segregation rules and corrective action taken following discussion with the FCA. 

Candidates should have distinguished the possible action that the FCA may have taken against the firm, Brian as an individual, other staff members and also possibly the auditors. 

## **B59.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to the establishment of a new financial technology (FinTech) firm that would presumably operate through a mobile platform out of London. The company intends to undertake a number of specific activities and may consider subsequent expansion across Europe and into the US or Asia. The FCA has created a dedicated section on Fintech and innovative businesses on its new website (https://www.the-fca.org.uk/firms/fintech-and-innovative-businesses). This specifically explains the nature and operation of Project Innovate and the new Innovation Hub and Regulatory Sandbox which are intended to support the development of innovative financial products and services in the UK. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) All of the principal statutory provisions contained the FSMA as amended would apply. This includes the general prohibition (s19 FSMA), permission requirement (s20 FSMA) and restrictions on promotion (s21 FSMA). The firm would have to apply for Part 4A permission (s40 FSMA) to become authorised (s31(1)(a) FSMA) and comply with the threshold conditions imposed (s41 and Schedule 6 FSMA as amended and COND). Relevant personnel would have to be approved persons (s59 FSMA) and comply with the suitability requirements imposed under FIT and conduct requirements in COCON. The firm would be subject to the rules and guidance imposed by the FCA and PRA (ss137A, 137G and 139A FSMA including the FCA PRIN, PRA Fundamental Rules and all other relevant provisions). 

- b) The firm would have to apply for permission in each of its proposed principal areas of activity. Regulated activities are defined in s22 FSMA and the Regulated Activities Order 2001 (as amended) and the FSMA 2000 (PRA Regulated Activities) Order 2013. The 2013 Order divides activities between the FCA and PRA by specifying that anything not specifically assigned to the PRA under the Order is reserved to the FCA. Permissible activities overseen by the FCA will include dealing in investments, arranging deals in investments, managing investments and providing investment advice. Deposits and deposit taking would fall within the PRA RAO and consumer credit and mortgage services (arranging home finance transactions) within the RAO (as amended by the FSMA 2000 (Regulated Activities) (Amendment) (No 2) Order 2013) which transferred consumer credit regulation from the Office of Fair Trading to the FCA. 

Payment activities would be regulated under the new payment systems regime set up under Payment Services Regulations (PSRs), and Part 5 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013. The FSA produced an approach document in April 2009 which was updated in June 2013 (FCA, the FCA’s role under the Payment Services Regulations 2009 - Our Approach). 

Any dealing in foreign exchange is not a regulated activity under the FSMA as such although foreign exchange dealing as principal is governed by the Bank of England's Non-Investment Products (NIP) Code (April 2009). 

The bank would be dual regulated by the PRA for regulatory purposes and the FCA in respect of conduct. The bank would apply for Part 4A permission (s40 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

FSMA) to become an authorised person (s31(1)(a) FSMA). Compliance with the revised threshold conditions would be confirmed (s41 and Schedule 6 FSMA as amended by the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Threshold Conditions) Order 2013 (SI 2013/555) and COND). The threshold conditions generally apply with regard to office location, effective supervision, appropriate resources and suitability as well as business model condition (COND 2.7 and para 2F Schedule 6 FSMA.) Conditions may be imposed under s 137O FSMA with guidance provided under the Perimeter Guidance Manual (PERG). 

Reference may also have been made to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), Financial Service Compensation Scheme (FSCS), the Complaints Scheme and Complaints Commissioner as well as the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Competition Commission and the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) within the Bank of England although this was not strictly necessary. 

- c) Relevant application forms are available on the PRA and FCA websites with full and restricted options and applications considered within six months if complete and 12 months if incomplete. Separate PDF documents are available on Banking Authorisation Process (January 2014) and Banking Authorisation Process – Applicant Journey (June 2013). The application forms generally cover core details, business plan, scope or permission, financial resources, recovery and resolution, human resources, compliance, internal audit and policy, infrastructure, regulatory reporting, fees and levies, and fee, checklist and declaration. The PRA and FCA cooperate in the application process. 

- d) Passport rights only apply in relation to the provision of financial services on a services or establishment basis within the EU. The firm would not be able to carry on financial services in any EU country unless it applies for local (EU) authorisation. For business in the US or Asia, the extent to which the firm would have to make a full new original application would partly depend upon the relationship between the separate third country and either the UK or the EU depending upon whether the new services were to be carried on from London or the EU country (assuming the firm gains the EU licence) 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B60.** 

The question raises a number of issues with regard to money laundering, systems and controls and financial ethics in connection with ultra high net worth (UHNW) clients and politically exposed persons (PEPs) involved with a complex structured finance arrangement. This is similar to the ‘Elephant’ deal that Barclays bank entered into worth £1.9 billion and was fined £72 million by the FCA failing to maintain and comply with appropriate money laundering and anti-terrorist financing controls. (Elephant deals are worth more than £20 million.) The identities of the parties had only been kept in a hardcopy in a safe that few people had access to with other systems and controls requirements not having been properly adhered to. Barclays was expected to have me around £50 million profit although the FCA accepts that no financial crime was actually involved. 

- a) Specific concerns arise with regard to: 

   - (i) The involvement of UHNW clients; 

   - (ii) The involvement of PEPs; 

   - (iii)  The complex nature of the structured finance transaction involved; 

   - (iv) The use of a large number of companies to place investments; 

   - (v) The size of the transaction; 

   - (vi)  The failure to comply with standard procedures and adoption of a simplified procedure; 

   - (vii)  The speed with which the transaction was entered into; (viii) The limited number of personnel involved; 

   - (ix)  The highly secretive nature of the transaction; 

   - (x) The use of hard copy rather than electronic filing systems; 

   - (xi)  The high profit generated; 

   - (xii)  The apparent lack of involvement of other senior personnel; 

   - (xiii)  The apparent failure to have this approved at board level; 

   - (xiv)  The failure to contact compliance. 

Reference could also be made to breach of relevant statutory provisions on money laundering and anti-terrorist financing in the UK and the money laundering regulations. The principal UK money laundering offences are now restated under Part VII of the PCA. The PCA creates three main offences relating to Concealing (s 327), Arranging (s 328) and Acquisition, Use and Possession (s 329) as well as 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

attempting, conspiring or inciting to commit any such offence (s 340(11)(a) and (b)). Money laundering includes aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring the commission of any of these offences (s 340(11)(c) and (d)). Two separate offences are also created of Failing to disclose (ss 330-332) and Tipping-off (s 333). Revisions to the PCA were made under the Serious Organised Crime and Police Act 2005 with separate reference possibly being made to reporting under s21A Terrorism Act 2000 and to the Bribery Act 2010. Reference should also have been made to the Money Laundering Regulations 2007 as amended. 

Additional issues may also have arisen from an ethical perspective: 

- (i) Potential breach of PRIN 1, FR 1 and COCON 1 (integrity); 

- (ii) The need to report suspicions under the Proceeds of Crime Act; 

- (iii)  The need to cooperate with the regulators under PRIN 11, FR 8 and COCON 3; 

- (iv)  The need to comply with other Codes of Conduct including the CISI Code. 

Separate provisions with regard to anti-terrorist requirements are contained in the Terrorism Act 2000, Terrorism Act 2006 and Counter-Terrorism Act 2008. Other relevant legislation includes the Anti-terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001 introduced after 11 September 2001, the Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005 which introduced control orders and the Terrorism (United Nations Measures) Order 2009 and Terrorist Asset-Freezing (Temporary Provisions) Act 2010. 

b) Identification and record requirements are imposed under under the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance and Money Laundering Regulations 2017 including with regard to Customer Due Diligence (CDD), Know Your Customer (KYC) and identification requirements as well as Simplified Due Diligence (SDD) and Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) for higher risk entities as well as potential Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs). 

Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) is provided for under Regulation 14 (1) and Section 5.5 of the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance. EDD is specifically to be used in higher risk situations including the customer is not physically present when identities are verified, correspondent banking or with 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

PEPs. Additional documents, data or information have to be collected. 

EDD is to be applied on a risk sensitive basis where insufficient information has been obtained under standard due diligence (JMLSG Section 5.5.1). Firms should obtain sufficient information concerning the circumstances and business of customers and beneficial owners to support the risk assessment process and monitor customer activity and transactions (JMLSG Section 5.5.2). The relevant additional information will depend on the nature of that money laundering or terrorist financing risk JMLSG Section 5.5.3). 

PEPs consists of high ranking non-UK public officials, Members of Parliament and immediate families and close associates. 

Reference could also have been made to the Consolidated List of persons or countries against whom financial sanctions are in place as maintained by the HM Treasury and Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG). Separate lists of uncooperative jurisdictions are also maintained by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). 

c) The Senior Managers Regime (SMR) and Certification Regime (CR) was created for banks under the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 Part 4 which came into effect in March 2016. With a separate new offence of causing a financial institution to fail is also provided for under s 36 of the Act. This is supported by the new set of conduct rules set out in COCON in the FCA Handbook. The objective is to ensure increased accountability of senior managers for specific firm tasks with appropriate disciplinary measures being applicable in the event of any failure to adhere to appropriate good conduct. Firms have to prepare responsibility maps which allocate all relevant Senior Management functions (SMFs) with each manager adding a statement of responsibility (SOR). 

Senior managers would probably have to be involved in this case in some way. Relevant Senior Manager Functions could include Head of Key Business Area (SMF 6); Group Entity Senior Manager (SMF 7); Compliance Oversight (SMF 16); Money Laundering Reporting (SMF 17); Other Overall Responsibility (SMF 18); Head of Overseas Branch (incoming non-EEA branches only) (SMF 19); and Other 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Local Responsibility Function (incoming non-EEA branches only) (SMF 22). Any other members of staff and for subject to the separate certification regime where the may pose a risk of significant harm to the firm or its customers with firms having to certify their fitness and propriety every year. 

d) Institutional liabilities can be considered under the MLR 2017 provisions or general regulatory requirements. The MLR imposes internal systems and control requirements on firms including with regard to customer due diligence (CDD) and monitoring, internal reporting, five year recordkeeping, internal control, risk assessment and management, compliance monitoring, recognition and reporting of suspicious transactions and staff training. Specific FCA Handbook provisions include SYSC 3.2.6 (systems and controls with regard to compliance, financial crime and money laundering), SYSC 3.2.6I (MLRO) with FCA guidance on financial crime being provided in the FC guide. Breach of the MLR requirements is punishable by up to two years imprisonment and an unlimited fine or six months imprisonment and a £5,000 fine before a Magistrates Court. The full range of enforcement actions would be available for breach of relevant Handbook provisions. 

The firm may also have breached COBS 2.1 in failing to act honestly, fairly and professionally (COBS 2.1.1R) and COBS 2.2 in failing to provide appropriate information in a comprehensive form (COBS 2.2.1R). The firm may have breached COBS 4, in particular, in failing to ensure that communications are not fair, clear and not misleading (COBS 4.2.1R), COBS 4.10 in failing to maintain proper systems and controls for approving and communicating financial promotions (COBS 4.10.1 and 2) and COBS 4.11 adequate record keeping (COBS 4.11.1R). The firm may have breached SYSC 3 systems and controls and 6 on compliance. 

The firm may have separately breached CASS 7 on client money rules including treatment of client money (CASS 7.11.1R) and client segregation (CASS 7.13.1, 2 and 3). The firm may also have breached COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (due skill, care and diligence), 5 (market conduct standards), 3 (regulatory cooperation). The firm may also have breached the FCA Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) requirements as originally set out in FSA, Treating customers fairly – towards fair outcomes for consumers (July 2006) with relevant management information (MI) 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

being set out in FSA, Treating customers fairly – guide to management information (July 2007). 

- e) Relevant firm responses might have included: 

   - (i) Review the firm’s new client (or ‘On boarding’) and classification procedures; 

   - (ii) Review the firm’s Money Laundering policy and procedures compliance; 

   - (iii)  Review the role and function of the MLRO in such cases; 

   - (iv)  Review the firm’s specific treatment of UHNW clients and PEPs; 

   - (v) Review the firm’s anti-terrorist financing policy and procedures; 

   - (vi)  Review the firm’s SMF and certification policy and procedures; 

   - (vii)  Review the firm’s internal systems and controls policy and procedure; 

   - (viii)  Review the firm’s internal governance arrangements. 

Reference may also have been made to any of the following: 

- (i) Review the firm's ethics and conduct risk environment; 

- (ii) Review the firm’s internal culture and ethics code; 

- (iii)  Establish a separate ethics or conduct risk committee or sub-committee of the board or, at minimum, assign these functions to a specific internal body; 

- (iv)  Review the firm's compliance policy and function; 

- (v) Review the firm's risk management policy and function; 

- (vi)  Review the firm's internal audit policy and function; 

- (vii)  Review High Level Standards compliance (including PRIN, SYSC, COND, COCON and FIT); (viii) Review the firm's training policy and function under the TC sourcebook; 

- (ix)  Review remuneration policy under SYSC 19C; 

- (x) Review whistle blowing policy; (xiii) Prepare a more specific firm Conduct Risk Framework (including Conduct Risk Appetite and Conduct Risk Strategy); 

- (xii)  Possibly integrate the conduct risk policy within the firm's Enterprise Risk Management Framework (ERFM) or equivalent; 

- (xiii)  Review recruitment policy; 

- (xiv)  Commission independent reports on ethics compliance or conduct risk management to the extent necessary; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

(xv)  Review reputation or risk management; 

(xvi)  Review client cash and securities segregation functions; 

(xvii)  Cooperate closely with the regulators under PRIN 11, FR 7 and COCON 3 and SM 4. 

## **B61.** 

The question raised a series of issues with regard to regulatory compliance, financial ethics and whistleblowing with regard to the activities of the UK subsidiary of an overseas bank engaged with international remittance activities. The FCA had fined the Sonali Bank (UK) Ltd £3,250,600 and prevented it from accepting deposits for new customers for 168 days in October 2016 for anti-money laundering systems failures. The former money laundering reporting officer (MLRO) was also fined £17,900 and prohibited from acting as an MLRO or compliance officer at any regulated firm. The bank of Beirut had been separately find £2.1 million and prevented from accepting new customers from high restrictions for 126 days in March 2015 having provided misleading information with regard to its implementation of anti-money laundering improvements following earlier supervisory visits. Two approved persons at the bank were fined £19,600 and £9,900 for failing to deal with the FCA in an open and cooperative manner. 

- a) The question could have been dealt with either by referring to the specific regulatory concerns that arose or major risks that this created. Specific regulatory concerns arose with regard to:- 

   - (i) The subsidiary is involved with international remittances; 

   - (ii) Remittances are carried out for customers and non-customers; 

   - (iii)  The parent bank is based in a high risk region; 

   - (iv)  The subsidiary's and banks anti-money-laundering procedures appear to be inadequate; 

   - (v) The subsidiary's and the bank's anti-terrorist financing procedures appear to be inadequate; 

   - (vi)  The supervisory visit appeared to disclose relevant inadequacy; 

   - (vii)  The inadequacies necessitated the carrying out of skilled persons report; (viii)  George has set out the inadequacies in a separate internal report; 

   - (ix)  None of the necessary action appears to have been carried out; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (x) the CEO of the subsidiary has misled the FCA with regard to the carrying out of corrective action; 

- (xi)  No further action has been taken in connection with their CEO conduct; 

- (xii)  Neither subsidiary, bank nor bank staff have dealt with the regulators in an open and cooperative manner. 

Specific money laundering risks arise with regard to breach of requirements with regard to:- 

- (i) Customer identification (KYC) and carrying out of customer due diligence (CDD); 

- (ii) Confirming whether clients are politically exposed persons (PEPs); 

- (iii)  Possibly conducting business with clients without physical presence on a non-face-to-face basis; 

- (iv)  Possible use of our correspondence banking relationship; 

- (v) Absence of enhanced due diligence (EDD) procedures for PEPs, nonface to face customers and corresponding transactions; 

- (vi)  Reporting of suspicious transactions (SARS); 

- (vii)  Failure to maintain effective MLRO and supporting complaints procedures. 

Candidates could also have referred to the additional changes brought into effect 

under the Fourth EU Anti-money Laundering Directive 2015/849 which came into effect in June 2017. 

Specific risks involved include:- 

- (i) financial risk; 

- (ii) regulatory risk; 

- (iii)  legal risk; 

- (iv)  operational risk; 

- (v) reputational risk; 

- (vi)  breach of FCA PRIN and PRA Fundamental Rules; 

- (vii)  breach of the CISI Code of Conduct; 

- (viii)  breach of the firm's internal ethical code. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- b) As a compliance officer and MLRO, George should be ensuring full implementation of all of the points made above in connection with general compliance and moneylaundering specific matters. 

- c) George may be held liable for breach of his responsibilities as compliance officer and separately as MLRO. George may also breach other specific anti-moneylaundering strategy provisions. Full details should be provided. 

- d) George is fully aware of the situation. He is also aware of the specific deficiencies having, in particular, prepared the internal report. George cannot fail to take action as compliance officer and as MLRO. George specifically has to act with integrity and with due skill, care and diligence under COCON 1 and 2. 

George should report to his immediate manager in or outside compliance failing which to the board or non-executive directors of the subsidiary and of the bank. If proper action is still not taken, George should consider whistleblowing under the Public Interest Disclosure Act (PIDA) 1998 as amended by the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act (ERRA) 2013. George should also consider reporting under the CISI 'Speak Up' procedure (Prepare, Evaluate, Act and Keep trying). 

- e) The firm has to ensure that all of the general compliance matters and money laundering specific matters referred to in (a) above are complied with. 

The firm is specifically required to act under PRIN 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 3 (proper management and control), 6 (act in the best interests of customers), 8 (avoid conflicts) and 11 (be open and corporative with the authorities) as well as PRA FR 1, 2, 3 (prudent manner), 5 (effective risk strategies and management systems), 6 (responsible and effective organisation and control) and 7. 

The bank must also ensure compliance with its own internal ethical code and whistleblowing procedures. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**B62.** 

**Section B: Answers** 

The question was concerned with the proposed new activities of a medium sized Eastern European bank wishing to set up in the UK. This would include a range of services that may require permission although it is unclear whether the bank will carry them out itself or through a UK partner bank. 

- a) As the entity may be carrying out regulated activities in the UK, it will be subject to the specific requirements imposed under the general prohibition, permission requirement and promotion conditions (ss 19, 20 and 21 FSMA). Full details should be provided. 

- b) The relevant agencies would include the PRA, FCA and CMA although reference may also be made to FOS and FSCS as well as possibly to the Bank of England and FPC. The functions of each should have been outlined. 

- c) Regulated activities are defined under s20 2FSMA and the Regulated Activities Order 2001 as amended and the PRA Regulated Activities Order 2013. These outline specified investments and investment activities. This would include deposits and deposit taking, consumer credit and mortgage services (advising on and arranging finance transactions). Providing payment services do not require authorisation as such although firms providing electronic means of payment are subject to the Payment Services Regulations (PSRs) unless exempt as a small payment institution (SPI). Dealing in foreign exchange is not a regulated activity under the FSMA although foreign exchange dealing as principal would be governed by the Bank of England's Non-Investment Products (NIP) Code (November 2011). 

- d) Given that the UK is no longer an EEA country, the bank will need to apply for direct authorisation in the UK.. The candidate should discuss the process used to apply for a UK licence. 

The FCA provides guidance on the regulatory perimeter under its PERG manual. This includes general guidance on authorisation and regulated activities (PERG2), electronic money (PERG3), mortgage related activities (PERG4) and the Payment Services Regulations (PERG 15). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- e) The rights and relevant procedures applicable to the passporting of regulated activities by EEA authorised institutions across the EEA either on a services or establishment basis should have been explained. 

## **B63.** 

The question was concerned with the proposed activities to be carried out by a new Financial Technology (FinTech) platform in the UK including with specific reference to money laundering compliance. The platform has already been placed within the FCA Regulatory Sandbox. 

- a) A number of specific concerns arise with regard to financial crime issues:- 

      - (i) The platform intends to source funds from across the world without further explanation; 

      - (ii) The platform does not appear to have any specific anti-money laundering controls in place especially with regard to funding sources; 

      - (iii)  No information is provided with regard to the appointment of an MLRO; 

      - (iv)  Relevant sources of funds may include high risk jurisdictions; 

      - (v) The platform may be involved with non-personal non-face to face transaction; 

      - (vi)  The platform may be involved with correspondent banking services; 

      - (vii)  The loans may specifically be used to integrate criminally derived funds into the regular financial system; 

      - (viii)  The involvement of younger people may expose them to becoming involved in criminal activities, for example, in acting as a mules. 

   - Separate ethical issues also arise:- 

      - (i) The platform wishes to lend to potentially vulnerable borrowers; 

      - (ii) These potential clients may not be able to assess the financial terms and conditions on offer effectively; 

      - (iii)  They may consequently not make them most informed choices or incur high levels of debt; 

      - (iv)  The firm must ensure that it is acting in the best interests of its customers and treating them fairly (PRIN 6); 

      - (v) The platform must also ensure that it maintains good financial resources (PRIN 4 and FR 4); 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (vi)  The firm must also ensure that none of its clients become exposed to any criminal activities (above). 

The firm must more generally ensure that it acts with integrity, due skill, care and diligence and maintains effective management and control systems (PRIN 1, 2 and 3 and FR 1, 2 and 6). 

- b) All relevant money laundering offences should be explained including relevant penalties and defences. 

- c) Enhanced due diligence (EDD) may be required due to the involvement of PEPs, non-face-to-face dealings and correspondent banking. The nature of the higher identification requirements imposed under EDD should have been explained. 

- d) The firm must ensure that it maintains effective systems and controls including with regard to money laundering (PRIN 3 and FR 5). Specific obligations on financial crime are imposed under FCA SYSC 6.3 including with regard to money laundering risk, regular assessments, separate statutory obligations, Joint Money Laundering Steering Group compliance, implementation, training and allocation of responsibility (SYSC 6.3.1-8). Firms are also required to appoint an MLRO (SYSC 6.3.9-10). The FCA provides separate guidance on financial crime (SYSC 6.3.11 and FC). The full range of FCA sanctions may be imposed including restriction of promotion, withdrawal of authorisation, public and private censure and fine. 

- e) The platform should take all necessary action to avoid breach of the relevant regulatory, statutory and ethical matters referred to in sub-question (e) above. It should also be open and cooperative with the FCA and PRA (PRIN 11 and FR 7). Financial technology may specifically be used for regulatory compliance (RegTech) purposes in the following ways:- 

   - (i) Carrying out customer identification (KYC) and customer due diligence (CDD); 

   - (ii) Carrying out EDD; 

   - (iii)  Monitoring accounts and identifying any suspicious transactions; 

   - (iv)  Submitting suspicious transaction reports (SRRs); 

   - (v) Monitoring high risk jurisdictions; 

   - (vi)  Monitoring PEPs; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (vii)  Monitoring other customer account activity including servicing and any missed payments; 

- (viii)  Reporting to customers on a regular or specific basis; 

- (ix)     Submitting all relevant regulatory returns to authorities in each 

   - jurisdiction with which the platform is involved over time. 

## **B64.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to inter-bank market activity and specifically the under reporting of positions during the UK financial crisis as part of Bank of England liquidity auctions. The Serious Fraud Office (SFO) in the UK opened an investigation into alleged malpractices in the auctions in March 2015. The Bank's Oversight Committee had earlier published a separate report by Lord Grabiner QC on the Bank of England Foreign Exchange Market Investigation (November 2014) covering foreign exchange activity between 2005 and 2013. 

- a) Specific concerns arise with regard to possible market abuse having been carried out by the bank in the liquidity auctions. Market abuse was previously controlled under s 118 FSMA with this being repealed with the coming into effect of the EU Market Abuse Regulation (MAR) 596/2014 which contains specific directly applicable provisions in this regard. The MAR is implemented through European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) Guidelines and amendments to the FCA Handbook. The MAR came into effect on 3 July 2016 although the question could have been answered with regard to either FSMA or the MAR. Candidates should have referred to the general nature of the offences created and defences and penalties available. Reference may also have been made to the new offences on benchmarking imposed under a 91 of the Financial Services Act 2012 although this was possibly not strictly a benchmarking question. 

- b) Separate regulatory concerns specifically arise with regard to PRIN 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), 3 (proper management and control), 6 (act in the best interests of customers), 8 (avoid conflicts) and 11 (be open and corporative with the authorities) as well as PRA FR 1, 2, 3 (prudent manner), 5 (effective risk strategies and management systems), 6 (responsible and effective organisation 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and control) and 7. Concerns also arise with regard to breach of the CISI Code of Conduct. 

- c) You have to consider escalating the matter within the firm. You should report to your immediate manager in or outside compliance failing which to the board or non-executive directors. If necessary, you could consider using the whistleblowing procedures provided for under the Public Interest Disclosure Act (PIDA) 1998 as amended by the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act (ERRA) 2013. You could also consider reporting under the CISI 'Speak Up' procedure (Prepare, Evaluate, Act and Keep trying). 

- d) The authorities may consider imposing a full range of sanctions on the firm. These include imposing temporary restrictions on activities, permission restrictions, withdrawal of authorisation, public and private censure and fines. 

- e) The bank has to act in an open and cooperative manner in dealing with the FCA and PRA (PRIN 11 and FR 7). The firm should make full disclosure of the internal report to the authorities with a full list of corrective action intended to prevent any similar occurrence in future. This may include taking disciplinary action against the original staff involved as well as the Head of Treasury Department for failing to take necessary action following the completion of your report and failing to disclose this to the authorities. 

## **B65.** 

The question raised a number of compliance and ethical issues in connection with a possible market abuse and potential money laundering situation. It is expressly stated that you are the compliance officer and that you are therefore examining the facts from a compliance perspective. 

Candidates should have carefully read each sub-question set and noted the relevant mark allocation and then moved through the question dealing with each of the issues raised in turn. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) The initial matter to be considered concerned reporting obligations which could either have been dealt with generally in terms of the compliance function as a whole or the specific regulatory breaches concerned. The most complete answers included both. The nature and importance of compliance and the role and function of the compliance officer could initially have been summarised and the compliance officer’s 

responsibility for managing regulatory risk. This could have been dealt with in terms of function or risk or both. Relevant functions relate to: 

- i. Compliance culture 

- ii. Training and communication 

- iii. Advice 

- iv. Monitoring and oversight 

- v. Developing and implementing relevant strategy 

- vi. Issue resolution, problem solving and compliance 

- vii. Managing the firm’s relations with the regulatory authorities 

Risks and risk assessment can be summarised in terms of market, credit, operational, legal and other risks. Specific issues raised in the particular question include potential market abuse(insider dealing and misuse of information) and insider trading as well as possible money laundering as well as breach of a number of provisions in PRIN, SYSC, COCON and FIT. Particular issues raised include: 

- i. Internal and external reporting of possible market abuse including to FCA and criminal authorities by client and corporate broker (insider dealing and misuse of information); 

- ii. Internal and external reporting of possible insider trading by client and corporate broker; 

- iii. Internal and external reporting of possible money laundering by client, including to NCA; 

- iv. Internal and external reporting of possible systems and control failures, including under SYSC 3 and 6 as well as SYSC 4, 5, 7, 9 and 10 for common platform firms; 

- v. Internal and external reporting of possible senior management failures under SYSC 2; 

- vi. Internal and external reporting of possible conflicts of interest, especially at CEO level; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- vii. Possible compliance and reporting systems failures, especially at Head of Risk level; 

- viii. Possible breach of a number of high level principles, including PRIN 1, 2, 3, 5, 8 and 11; 

- ix. Possible breach of a number of provisions in COCON; 

- x. Possible breach of a number of provisions in FIT and COND; 

- xi. Possible breach of a number of provisions in TC; 

- xii. Possible breach of a number of principles within CISI Code of Conduct, including 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and 8; 

## xiii. Possible need to consider ‘Whistle-Blowing’ under Public Interest Disclosure Act. 

Reference may also have been made generally to any of the following: 

- i. Irregular transactions and transaction reporting; 

- ii. Lack of proper communication and follow-up; 

- iii. Possible record failures; 

- iv. Possible oversight failures; 

- v. Possible criminal investigation; 

- vi. Possible disciplinary action against you as compliance officer and approved 

person; 

- vii. Possible regulatory action by FCA against firm and other parties, including breach of s59 FSMA and liability under 71 FSMA. 

All of this has still to be balanced against the amount of actual information you have concerning the underlying regulatory or criminal breaches by the client and your limited resources to investigate, including relevant jurisdictional issues. Again, a strict compliance position has to be balanced against the larger ethical position of the firm and the need to uphold its reputation and professional standards more generally. 

- b) The compliance officer may initially have tried to discuss the concerns raised with the Head of Risk and CEO again to confirm their positions and confirm the information with the Portfolio Control Function. This may involve conducting an internal review of the transactions concerned and the client’s trading performance more generally. Candidates should then have noted further possible reporting lines including: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- i. Immediate line manager (if relevant); 

- ii. Audit Committee; 

- iii. Risk Management Committee; 

- iv. Non-Executive Directors or NedCO; 

- v. Group Compliance and parallel relevant committees and senior personnel; and 

- vi. FCA or CISI where necessary. 

Relevant problems in the particular case nevertheless included: 

- i. Head of Risk’s positions and possible close relationship with CEO; 

- ii. Possible conflict of interest with CEO and possible close relationship with the client; 

- iii. Possible specific conflict of interest with CEO managing client relationship and absence of any independent function; 

- iv. The relative importance of the CEO’s position within the group being a 20% owner of the London operation; 

- v. Relative inexperience of Head of Risk in managing relevant issues having only recently arrived at firm; 

- vi. Limited and inconclusive nature of information available (above); 

- vii. Limited investigation resources available (above); 

- viii. Possible conflicting US and UK jurisdictional issues. 

Candidates may have also included specific comment on the possible relevant offences of market abuse, insider trading and money laundering involved and the extent to which these may have been breached on the basis of the limited information available. 

- c) Relevant other corrective action could have included: 

- i. Conduct internal review of all relevant systems and controls; 

- ii. Conduct internal review of compliance function and reporting lines within firm and group; 

- iii. Conduct internal review of all external reporting lines; 

- iv. Conduct internal review of Portfolio Control Function; 

- v. Conduct internal review of client classification procedures; 

- vi. Conduct internal review of best execution only dealing arrangements; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- vii. Conduct internal review of conflicts of interest policy; 

- viii. Conduct internal review of relations with other professional counterparties involved; 

- ix. Review approved person arrangements; 

- x. Conduct internal review of Training and Competence procedures, especially 

concerning market abuse, insider training and money laundering. 

Reference may also be made to the following: 

- i. Report to FCA on possible breaches and remedial action taken; 

- ii. Review portfolio management functions; 

- iii. Review record keeping and reporting procedures; 

- iv. Review client asset management procedures; 

- v. Review client communication procedures including follow-up processes; 

- vi. Review professional indemnity and insurance arrangements; 

- vii. Review any other outstanding operational or reputational risk management issues that arise. 

- viii. Arrange management meeting to consider reviews; 

- ix. Consider possible suspension of relevant staff in extreme case; 

- x. Consider reopening specific transactions where necessary. 

Relevant FCA enforcement action and penalties and other possible criminal 

sanctions should have been noted with possible reduction under executive settlement scheme. 

Possible breaches of the CISI Code may also have been considered and referral of the matter to the CISI. 

## **B66.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to the establishment of a new ‘Robo advisory’ service in London. Robo advice constitutes part of the range of new financial technology (FinTech) platforms under development at this time. The firm has been placed within the FCA regulatory sandbox as part of its Project Innovate initiative (https://www.thefca.org.uk/firms/fintech-and-innovative-businesses). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- a) The FCA published a Financial Advice Market Review (FAMR) in March 2016 jointly with the Treasury which examined the quality of financial services advice and the ‘advice gap’ in the UK with specific recommendations being made on affordability, accessibility and liability and consumer redress 

(https://www.fca.org.uk/publication/corporate/famr-final-report.pdf). 

Recommendation 2 of the FAMR specifically called for the definition of regulated advice based on personal recommendation following MiFID with other initiatives being adopted to assist the provision of ‘streamlined’ advice subject to appropriate safeguards. The FAMR also considered non-advice services, fact-finding processes and employer and trustee fact sheets (Recommendations 3, 10 and 11). A Financial Advice Working Group (FAWG) was set up following the review (Recommendation 1). 

The FCA set up a separate specialist Advice Unit (FAMR Recommendation 9) to assist firms with regulatory feedback on developing automated models for advice delivery. This includes the provision of individual responses and other tools and resources. The Unit initially focused on investments, pensions (accumulation and decumulation) and protection with this to be extended to mortgages, general insurance and debt sectors. 

- b) All of the principal statutory provisions contained the FSMA as amended would initially apply. This includes the general prohibition (s19 FSMA), permission requirement (s20 FSMA) and restrictions on promotion (s21 FSMA). The firm would have to apply for Part IV permission (s40 FSMA) to become authorised (s31(1)(a) FSMA) and comply with the threshold conditions imposed (s41 and Schedule 6 FSMA as amended and COND). Relevant personnel would have to be approved persons (s59 FSMA), or subject to the new senior managers and certification regimes, and comply with the suitability requirements imposed under FIT and conduct requirements in COCON. The firm would be subject to the rules and guidance imposed by the FCA and PRA (ss137A, 137G and 139A FSMA including the FCA PRIN, PRA Fundamental Rules and all other relevant provisions). 

The firm would have to apply for permission in each of its proposed principal areas of activity. Regulated activities are defined in s22 FSMA and the Regulated Activities Order 2001 (as amended) and the FSMA 2000 (PRA Regulated Activities) Order 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

2013. The 2013 Order divides activities between the FCA and PRA by specifying that anything not specifically assigned to the PRA under the Order is reserved to the FCA. Permissible activities overseen by the FCA will include inter alia providing investment advice (RAO art 53) as well as managing investments (RAO art 37), dealing in investments (RAO arts 53 14 and 21) and arranging deals in investments (RAO art 25). 

- c) The full range of regulatory penalties may be available against the firm including variation of permission (s45 FSMA), suspension of permission (s206a FSMA), cancellation of Part 4 permission (s54 FSMA), withdrawal of authorisation (s33 FSMA), public censure (s205 FSMA), financial penalties (s206 FSMA), requests for information (s165 FSMA and Schedule 12 FSA 2012), independent inquiry (s68 FSA 2012), administration, receivership, winding-up or bankruptcy (Part 24 FSMA, Banking Act 2009 and Schedule 14 FSA 2012), injunctions (s380 FSMA), restitution (ss382 and 384 FSMA), suspension of financial instrument from trading if appropriate (s36 FSA 2012), complaint by a consumer body (super-complaint under s234c FSMA and s43 FSA 2012), regulated person reference (s234d FSMA), OFT investigation (s234h FSMA), action under product intervention rules (s137d FSMA 2000 and s24 FSA 2012) and skilled persons report (s166 FSMA). 

- d) Financial guidance rather than advice would not generally be regulated. HM Treasury published a consultation paper and response on Amending the Definition of Financial Advice in September 2016 and February 2017. Advice would only be regulated where the firm made a personal recommendation. An exemption would be provided to art 53(1) where no personal recommendation was made. This applied the definition under MiFID although the existing provisions are retained in respect of the boundary between regulated and unregulated firms. Regulated firms are to be able to provide more advanced guidance services without these constituting regulated advice with unregulated firms being regulated where they advise on investments. The changes would come into effect from 3 January 2018. 

The FCA issued a Guidance consultation on Streamlined Advice in April 2017 (GC 17/4). This distinguishes full advice and streamlined advice. Streamlined advice is limited to one or more of the client’s specific needs and does not examine circumstances not directly relevant to those needs. This includes simplified advice 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

(with the firm setting out the boundaries) and focused advice (with the client specifying the boundaries). Streamlined advice will include Robo advice and automated services as well as face to face advice. 

Firms are required to obtain necessary information on the client’s knowledge and experience, financial situation and investment objectives under the fact finding process. Fact finding processes would be simplified and made more portable. Additional guidance is provided on generic advice that would not be covered. Employees can also be provided with information by employers and trustees through fact sheets. 

- e) The company would have to apply for permission to cover any new regulated activities. Regulated activities are defined in s22 FSMA and the Regulated Activities Order 2001 (as amended) and the FSMA 2000 (PRA regulated Activities) Order 2013. The 2013 Order divides activities between the FCA and PRA by specifying that with anything not specifically assigned to the PRA under the Order is reserved to the FCA. 

The RAO distinguishes between specified investments and specified activities. Specified investments include investments, deposits, contracts for insurance, shares, instruments creating or acknowledging indebtedness, government and public securities, instruments giving entitlement to investments, certificates representing certain securities, units in a collective investment scheme, rights under a stakeholder pension scheme, options, futures, contracts for differences, Lloyd’s syndicate capacity and membership, funeral plan contracts, regulated mortgage contracts and rights to or interest in investments (RAO arts 73-89). 

Specified activities include accepting deposits, insurance, dealing in investments as principal, dealing in investments as ageing, arranging deals in investments, managing investments, safeguarding and administering investments, sending dematerialised instructions, establishing collective investment schemes, establishing stakeholder pension schemes, advising on investments and other activities relating to Lloyds, funeral plan contracts, regulated mortgage contracts and agreeing to carry on activities (RAO arts 5-65). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B67.** 

The question was concerned with the responsibilities of various parties within a bank where difficulties arose in managing the preparation of an Anti-money laundering review process. The initial report was to be prepared by a specific deadline although this has brought forward with one of the members of staff being on leave and not leaving her section of the report in a shared team folder. IT was misled into agreeing to reset the password access to allow access to confidential files to complete the report in time. 

- a) The parties concerned were responsible as follows: 

   - (i) Henry, the junior compliance officer: 

   - (a) Henry was the person who convinced Jess to call IT and pretend to be 

   - Charlotte and could be considered responsible for gaining access to Charlotte’s folder; 

   - (b) However, he cannot be held responsible for the position he found himself in (having been left alone with a changed deadline for completion of the project); 

   - (c) There were still other choices available to him including: 

      - He could have called Gavin to retrieve the information, even though doing so may have annoyed him; 

      - He could have set out over the phone or (even better) in writing to Grace why he would be unable to get the information to her by 08:30 am on Friday, and given her a new deadline of later in the day when Charlotte would have been back in the office; 

      - He could have provided Grace with the information available to him and let her know that the remaining information would be sent to her when Charlotte returned to the office; 

## (d) He did try to mitigate the situation by changing Charlotte’s password again 

after accessing the relevant information, ensuring that Jess would not be able to gain access again. 

- (ii) Charlotte, the recently promoted compliance manager: 

(a) Charlotte may be considered partly responsible for the situation arising by booking leave at a critical time and not conducting a proper hand over to Henry before she left, ensuring that he had access to all the necessary information; 

- (b) However, her responsibility could be considered mitigated by the fact that she was clearly under pressure and over worked, without receiving the required 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## support from Winter Bank. 

- (iii) Grace, the Compliance Director: 

(a) Assigned a very important project to a junior member of staff and a recently appointed manager, she should have offered more support or involved someone more senior; 

(b) Reconsidered approval of Charlotte’s last-minute leave request, knowing that she would not be back in the office until Thursday with the project needs to be finalised on Friday; 

(c) Not given Henry tacit approval to use any means necessary to get the report to her by Friday morning by saying, ‘I don’t care what you need to do to get this done’ which allowed Henry to justify (to himself, if not others) that getting Charlotte’s password was the right thing to do. Her forceful attitude did not leave Henry with many other choices if he is going to comply with her demands; 

(d) Grace’s position as Compliance Director (with responsibility for overseeing the whole Compliance function within Winter Bank) means that she is ultimately responsible for the actions of her team, particularly under the Senior Managers & Certification Regime, as Grace will most likely hold the SMF16 (Compliance Oversight) function within Winter Bank. 

## (iv) Gavin, the MLRO: 

- (a) Technically, Gavin could be considered responsible because of his position as SMF17 (Money Laundering Function) within Winter Bank; 

- (b) His attitude and uncooperative nature meant that Henry did not feel 

comfortable approaching him with a problem, which is indicative of a poor leadership and management style; 

- (c) While everyone is entitled to a ‘home life’, and it is important to keep a 

distinction between work and home, in many modern companies, some degree of flexibility is expected, particularly of senior managers. Knowing that Charlotte was away, Gavin may be considered partly responsible for Henry gaining access to Charlotte’s computer because he was inflexible and did not offer Henry any additional support with the project. 

- (v) Jess, Henry’s colleague: 

- (a) Jess is responsible in that she acquiesced to Henry’s request to call IT and 

pretend to be Charlotte in order to gain access to her computer and must have 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

known she was doing something wrong; 

(b) The extent to which Jess is responsible may be mitigated depending on whether she has undergone any IT or data security training. If ‘yes’, she would be expected to know better. If ‘no’, questions might be asked about Winter Bank’s responsibility for letting this situation occur by not ensuring staff had received training on data security. 

   - (vi) Sam, IT support: 

   - (a) Did not ask any questions to verify that he was speaking with Charlotte. 

   - (b) Even if one could make an excuse for Jess not having undertaken data security training, the same cannot be said of Sam. As a member of IT staff at Winter Bank, he is at least partly responsible for ensuring security procedures are adhered to. 

- b) With regard to the risks involved, the wider risks and implications were as follows: (i) Staff were overworked and under pressure, which could lead to mistakes and situations where they felt that they needed to bend their values and the rules in order to achieve the objectives; 

   - (ii) Staff felt unable to raise concerns with management, or approach them for guidance and support, which may lead to problems going undetected for long periods of time and become more serious and are not resolved at an early stage; 

(iii) This is a particular risk when you consider that Gavin is the MLRO. People should feel as though they were able to approach him with any Money Laundering concern, although his unapproachable nature would put people off doing so, leaving the company at risk of breaching Anti-Money Laundering rules and regulations; 

(iv) Lack of training and awareness of issues of IT/data/cyber security which could lead to more serious problems such as with staff falling prey to a phishing attack or other cyber/data leak (possibly involving customer details). 

The specific risks for Winter Bank as a result of this occurring were as follows: 

(i) An investigation should be conducted by the Information Commissioners Office into the data breach created when Sam gave Jess Charlotte’s password without verifying who he was talking to; 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

(ii) An investigation could be carried out by the FCA into Grace and Gavin regarding their involvement and their statutory Duties of Responsibility under SM&CR which specifies that the regulators can take action against a senior manager where: 

      - There was a contravention of a relevant requirement by the senior manager’s firm; 

      - At the time of the contravention or during any part of it, the senior manager was responsible for the management of the activities in relation to which the contravention occurred; and 

      - The senior manager did not take such steps as a person in their position could reasonably have been expected to take to avoid the contravention occurring or continuing. 

- c) In terms of preventing this from occurring, the following action could have been taken: 

   - (i) Henry could have raised his concerns about Charlotte being put under too much pressure or Charlotte could have sought support about this herself; Grace could have assigned a more senior staff member to work on and support the project; 

(ii) Grace should also not have approved Charlotte’s last-minute leave request, knowing that doing so would leave Henry (a junior staff member) working on the project on his own right before the deadline; 

(iii) Charlotte could have delayed her annual leave until after the project was completed or at least conducted a proper hand over to Henry before she left in order to double-check that he had all the relevant information available to him; 

(iv) Grace should not have changed the brief (in which she said she needed the report by Monday) at the last minute with her attitude and language leaving Henry with few choices about how to act and even tacitly approving any means to obtain the required information; 

(vii) Jess should have told Henry that she was not comfortable about pretending to be Charlotte in order to have the password reset; 

- (v) Sam should have followed basic security processes and checked to verify that he was speaking with Charlotte before resetting her password; 

(vi) Winter Bank should have ensured that all staff had received appropriate data security training. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- d) In terms of whether Grace and Gavin were more liable than the others: 

   - (i) Grace and Gavin were both hold Senior Manager Functions under the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SMF16 (Compliance Oversight Function) and SMF17 (Money Laundering Reporting Function) respectively); 

   - (ii) As a result, they were held individually accountable for the specific responsibilities outlined. 

(iii) Given that Henry and Charlotte worked in Grace’s team and that the project was a review of Winter Bank’s Anti-Money Laundering processes, both Grace and Gavin could be considered responsible under the SM&CR, for what has occurred depending upon the list of responsibilities each of them has been allocated under Winter Bank’s responsibilities map; 

(iv) COCON 2.2.2R provides that, ‘You must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which you are responsible complies with the relevant requirements and standards of the regulatory system;’ 

(v) A government statement released in October 2015 also explained the Statutory Duty of Responsibility as follows, ‘The same tough underlying obligation will remain on the individual to ensure that they take reasonable steps to prevent regulatory breaches in the areas of the firm for which they are responsible, but the burden will be on the regulators to prove that a senior manager has failed to do this;’ 

(vi) For these reasons, it can be considered that Grace and Gavin are more liable for what has occurred than the other parties involved. 

It might also have been noted that while not a character mentioned referred to expressly, the senior manager responsible for oversight of data protection and IT security may be considered liable if it was found that Winter Bank staff were not properly trained in data protection and IT security as it was this process/policy which was breached by both Jess and Sam. 

## **B68.** 

An overseas bank is considering establishing either a representative office or a branch or subsidiary in the UK to undertake Islamic finance activities. A number of issues are raised. The FSA, for example, examined similar issues in FSA, Islamic Finance in the UK: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Regulation and Challenges (November 2007) with former Chairman, Sir Howard Davies, commenting on Islamic Banking and Finance at a conference in Bahrain in September 2003. 

- a) A representative office is an office established by a bank in an overseas territory for marketing and client development with only non-transactional operations being carried out. A representative office is not separately regulated and cannot engage in regulated activities. The term representative office is not separate defined in the FCA Glossary. 

A bank wishing to undertake a regulated activity in the UK can either do so through the establishment of a branch or a subsidiary. A branch is an office operating under the name of the parent institution with a subsidiary being a separately created company in the host state with separate legal personality.
