- b) The bank would be subject to the general prohibition (s19 FSMA), permission requirement (s20 FSMA) and restrictions on promotion (s21 FSMA). The list of authorised persons is provided under s31 FSMA with applications for Part IV permission being made under s44 FSMA. Islamic finance is not separately defined under the Combined Glossary nor in the Regulated Activities Order (RAO) as amended. Islamic finance would still generally involve one or more other regulated activities including specifically accepting deposits, which would be subject to the general prohibition. Regulated activities are ‘by order’ as set out in the RAO (s22 FSMA) with offences for contravention of the general prohibition being imposed (ss23-25 FSMA) and with agreements being unenforceable against the other part (s26 FSMA). 

- c) The bank would have to apply for Part IV permission (s40 FSMA) to become authorised (s31(1)(a) FSMA). The bank would have to satisfy the revised threshold conditions (s41 and Schedule 6 FSMA as amended and COND). The threshold conditions were amended under the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Threshold Conditions) Order 2013 (SI 2013/555) with each authority being able to issue conditions under s137O FSMA with guidance being provided under the Perimeter Guidance Manual (PERG). The threshold conditions generally cover location of offices, effective supervision, appropriate resources and suitability with 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

an additional business model condition being added (COND 2.7 and para 2F Schedule 6 FSMA). 

The bank would be dual regulated by the PRA and FCA with the application for authorisation being made through the PRA which would coordinate with the FCA. The application forms are now made available on the PRA website although held on the FCA website under the PRA and FCA names. Full and restricted application options are available with applications being considered within 6 months if they are complete and 12 months if they are incomplete. 

The application form generally covers core details; business plan; scope of permission; financial resources, recovery and resolution; human resources; compliance, internal audit & policy; infrastructure; regulatory reporting; fees & levies; and fee, checklist and declaration. Separate pdf documents are available on Banking Authorisation Process (January 2014) and Banking Authorisation Process – Applicant Journey (June 2013). A separate document on ‘Considerations for firms thinking of using third-party technology (off-the-shelf) banking solutions’ (July 2014) is also available. Diagrammatic summaries are provided for the application process in the Applicant Journey document. The PRA and FCA cooperate in the process and either act jointly or with one authority taking the lead. 

- d) As a dual regulated firm, the bank would be subject to prudential regulation by the PRA and conduct regulation by the FCA. Further information on the respective roles and functions of each authority could have been provided. Reference may also have been made to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), Financial Service Compensation Scheme (FSCS), the Complaints Scheme and Complaints Commissioner as well as the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Competition Commission and the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) within the Bank of England although this was not strictly necessary. 

- e) Financial services may be provided in other EU and EEA Member States would require a separate licence and permission granted by an EEA member state. Relevant procedures may have been outlined depending upon whether the firm wish to exercise an establishment or service right. Firms authorised and holding 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

permission in London have no direct rights to provide services or establish in other Member States following the U.K.’s withdrawal. 

## **B69.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to implementation of the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) within the UK. The Data Protection Act 2018 also come into effect in May 2018. A boutique wealth management firm has adopted new GDP are complied processes which include a checklist facility. A number of parties are involved including a student, the head of human resources, the person responsible for data protection compliance and one of the firm’s star fund managers. A number of difficult issues arise with regard to their actions and relations in terms of GDP are an ethical compliance. 

a) Staff members responsible for data protection within Secure Wealth: 

- Ganesh, the Head of Operations 

   - The story mentions that Ganesh is ‘responsible for implementing the data protection process’ at Secure Wealth. 

   - Whilst this is a task that is most likely going to be assigned to the company’s Data Protection Officer (DPO), it is not explicitly mentioned that Ganesh is the DPR for Secure Wealth. 

   - Secure Wealth may not even need to appoint a DPO. A data controller or processor is only obliged to appoint a DPO where: 

- It is a public authority – Secure Wealth is not a public authority. 

- Core activities involve regular and systematic monitoring of individuals on a large scale – As a boutique wealth management firm, one can assume that the company does not have enough clients to be classed as ‘large scale’. 

- Large-scale processing of sensitive data or criminal records takes place – Sensitive data includes information about racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, trade union membership, genetic or biometric information, data concerning health or a person’s sex life or sexual orientation. It is unlikely that Secure Wealth hold this kind of information. The only exception could be if they hold information about their client’s criminal history, obtained through the firm’s due diligence process, in which case they may wish to appoint a DPO. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- A member state’s law requires a DPO to be appointed – UK law does not require a DPO to be appointed. 

   - Nevertheless, Ganesh does seem to be in a position of authority regarding the data protection policies within secure wealth and may well held at least partly responsible for any failing in that process. 

- Nina, the Graduate Trainee 

   - Nina has been asked by Ganesh to ensure all the teams are following the data protection process carefully and diligently. 

   - However, she did not write the policy, is not responsible for assessing its overall effectiveness, and is merely assisting Ganesh with implementation. 

   - Whilst she is therefore involved in the process, she is a junior member of staff employed as a Graduate Trainee and should not be held ultimately and individually accountable for data protection within Secure Wealth. 

- Bob, the Fund Manager 

   - As a Fund Manager, Bob is likely to be held responsible for protecting the data of the clients assigned to him. Should he lose a client file, for instance, that will be his responsibility, and he should be held accountable. 

   - However, it is not easy to make the argument that Bob should be ultimately responsible for data protection across Secure Wealth. 

- Hilary, the Head of HR 

   - As an HR Manager, Hilary will have access to sensitive data about staff members (for example, criminal history, racial or ethnic origin, religious or philosophical beliefs, sexual orientation and data about an employee’s health. She is therefore responsible for the protection of that data regarding the staff members at Secure Wealth. 

   - However, she may not even be able to access the client information folders. Therefore, much like Bob, whilst Hilary is responsible for a subset of the data which is held by Secure Wealth, it does not seem appropriate that she would be ultimately responsible for data protection across Secure Wealth. 

- The CEO/Board Members/The Chairman 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

   - The buck stops with the CEO and/or people in charge of the organisation, such as the Chairman and Board members. 

   - There have been instances where companies have suffered a large-scale data breach, and the CEO has stood down as a result. 

   - The Board (and perhaps Executive Team) are responsible for setting the policy which staff must adhere to on data protection, ensuring staff are properly trained on how to manage client data, and choosing how to respond to threats and breaches. 

   - These senior figures also set the tone from the top, making sure that staff take data protection seriously, as well as creating an open environment where staff feel confident admitting when mistakes are made and raising concerns about processes not working or misconduct by fellow employees. 

   - There are therefore the figureheads of the organisation and could be considered ultimately responsible for data protection at Secure Wealth. 

- All staff members. 

   - All staff members have a role to play when it comes to data protection, and there have been instances where employees have been held individually responsible for data breaches and faced dismissal or legal action as a result. It therefore cannot be said that one sole person is held responsible for data protection, as all staff have a role to play. 

## b) Risks associated with the scenario. 

The wider risks/implications which this fact pattern highlights are as follows: 

- When it comes to data protection, an organisation is only as strong as its weakest link. For that reason, the concerns Nina has raised about Bob’s attitudes towards compliance with the data protection policies are significant and suggest that the company is at risk of non-compliance with data protection rules, including GDPR. 

- Hilary’s response is also concerning. As a senior leader she should be more alert to possible breaches or non-compliance with the Secure Wealth data protection policy and processes and should not respond as she did to Nina (“I’m not really responsible for our data processes... that doesn’t sit within my team.”). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

      - The risk is that, if senior managers do not feel accountable for the data protection process, it leaves Secure Wealth open to risks from certain teams within the organisation. Tone from the top (including team managers) is important. 

      - Additionally, putting too much responsibility onto Ganesh (if, indeed, he is responsible for data protection at Secure Wealth) leaves the organisation open to risk should something happen, and Ganesh be on holiday, unwell, or out of the office for some other reason. 

   - A wider risk can be seen about the attitude to Bob. Hilary says that Bob is reliable, but Nina has witnessed him behaving in a way that is anything but. Does Secure Wealth have an accurate impression of its managers, or, is Bob a bully who everyone is afraid to stand up to? 

   - The clear risks are about whether client’s data is secure (and whether or not there has already been a breach of their data) and, if the data is not secure or has been breached, the regulatory and reputational risk to Secure Wealth. 

      - Under the GDPR, data protection supervisory authorities may impose penalties for non-compliance – set as the greater of €20 million or up to 4% of the total worldwide annual turnover of the company, Additionally, data protection supervisory authorities can require organisations to cease processing personal data, which could stop the organisation from carrying on business. 

- c) The following incidents represent possible breaches of data protection: 

   - Bob has taken documents with his clients’ personal information on them outside of the office to work on whilst commuting. 

      - This could be considered a breach of physical security. Data protection is not just about cyber-attacks and digital information, but also includes physical information that can be removed from an office premises. 

      - It is significant that the information Bob has taken out of the office includes clients’ personal details. 

      - The risk is that he has ever left any of these documents on the train, or in a public space. But, even if he has never lost the documents, there is still the possibility that someone has read the documents over his shoulder or whilst sitting next to him, which could constitute a data breach. 

   - Bob uses his personal email address to communicate with clients as well as his work address. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

   - The use of a personal email address means that Secure Wealth are not able to monitor effectively the security or nature of the information that is being exchanged with clients. This puts Secure Wealth at risk of a data breach. 

   - There is no suggestion here that this activity had led to a data breach, however, as Bob seems to be communicating with his existing clients. 

   - Nevertheless, it is not best practice, and may lead to the firm breaching COBS 4: Communicating with clients, including financial promotions, in that Secure Wealth may not be able to properly record the details of any financial promotion Bob is communicating to his clients via his personal email address. 

- Bob accepted a call from a client’s husband and gave him an update about the status of a particular fund in which his wife had invested money. 

   - He claims to have had verbal consent from his client to share this information but does not appear to have that agreement in writing. 

   - This could well be considered a breach of his client’s data/financial information and is not best practice. 

Overall, the FCA expect businesses to have a culture of security, coming from the board downwards, with a recognition that security is not an IT issue but involves people and processes as well as technology. Therefore, all of the above could be considered a breach of the FCA’s expectations regarding data protection. 

- d) Yes, Nina could possibly be considered to be a whistleblower. Under the Public Interest Disclosure Act, disclosures qualifying for protection is defined as information disclosed by a worker (Nina is employed by Secure Wealth, even if she is just on their grad scheme, so can be considered to be a worker), including that a person has failed, is failing or is likely to fail to comply with any legal obligation to which he is subject. In this case, it appears that Bob has failed, is failing and is likely to fail to comply with Data Protection laws (including GDPR). 

It does not matter whether Bob is actually guilty of the above, what matters is that Nina has a ‘reasonable belief’ that Bob is possibly breaching data protection requirements. Nina has made the disclosure in good faith to her employer (in this case, Hilary), defined as a personal senior to the worker, who has been expressly or implicitly authorised by the employer as having management responsibility over the worker. The story notes that Nina is expected to report directly into Hilary, although it would still have counted as a qualifying disclosure if Nina has raised her concerns with Ganesh. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

This means that, should Nina be dismissed or victimised as a result of raising her 

concerns about Bob’s conduct, she could claim protection under the Public Interest Disclosure Act. 

## e) 

Possible actions Nina could take, and the advantages and disadvantages associated with each: 

- Do nothing: 

   - Nina is not necessarily obligated to take this any further. 

   - This approach is appealing as it might seem to be the one with the least amount of risk involved for Nina. 

   - However, it is the approach which represents the most amount of risk for Secure Wealth. 

- Hilary’s response to Nina implies that she is not going to take this matter any further. 

- Doing nothing means that Bob will continue to behave in the same way, increasing the risk of a data breach. 

   - Additionally, Nina has been asked by Ganesh to ensure that all the teams she works within are following the process carefully and diligently. If she does nothing, she would be failing in this task. 

   - Therefore, this is not the optimal course of action. 

- Ask Hilary to investigate. 

   - Nina could reiterate her concerns and insist that Hilary investigate further. 

   - As Nina’s line manager, it is Hilary’s responsibility to look into any concerns raised by the people who report into her. 

   - However, Hilary is Head of HR and not directly responsible for the implementation of Secure Wealth’s data protection policies. 

   - It also means that Nina would still need to explain to Ganesh why she has not completed the checklist with Bob. 

`o` Therefore, whilst this is a good option, it is not the best course of action. 

- Confront Bob. 

   - Hilary has advised Nina that she should be more insistent about meeting with Bob to go over Ganesh’s checklist. 

   - She could therefore try again and insist that they meet to discuss it. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

   - However, Bob may react badly to this, having already dismissed the idea previously, and he could be less willing to assist Nina with the development of her knowledge about wealth management. 

   - Is this really Nina’s responsibility? Ganesh delegated completion of the checklist to Nina, but he is responsible for oversight of the whole project and is more senior than Nina. 

   - It also puts Nina in a difficult position regarding what she has witnessed Bob doing with client information so far. 

   - It is therefore not the best course of action. 

- Report to Ganesh. 

   - This seems like the optimal approach. Hilary herself has advised Nina to make a report to Ganesh if she is seriously concerned that there is been a breach of data protection. 

   - As noted previously, Ganesh is responsible for implementing the data protection process. As Head of Operations, he would also probably wish to be informed  about the way which Bob is handling customer data. 

   - He has both the seniority and authority to take this further. He can also advise Nina about what he thinks she should do in this situation. 

   - Reporting to Ganesh is therefore the best next step Nina can take in the circumstances. 

- Raise concerns elsewhere. 

   - There is also an option for Nina to report her concerns elsewhere, either to another member of staff or even to the FCA or ICO. 

   - Other members of staff – she could report to the Head of Compliance, for example, or even the CEO, but it has already been noted that this is Ganesh’s area of responsibility, and he should therefore be informed first. 

   - FCA and/or ICO – reporting to a regulator is a significant step, particularly since no significant breach of data has been identified yet. Should managers at Secure Wealth find it appropriate to inform one or more regulatory authority about what has occurred, doing so would not be Nina’s responsibility. 

Neither of these options are therefore the optimal approach. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **B70.** 

The question was concerned with the implications for a UK securities firm following the UK withdrawal from the EU Single Market. The firm has a representative office in Ireland and can consider transferring operations to Dublin although this would require obtaining appropriate local authorisation. It can also consider obtaining authorisation in other Member States either directly or through the extended Dublin office. 

_[Tutor’s Note: The issue of how the UK has withdrawn from the EU’s Single Market has now been resolved following Brexit. However, I have left this question in the bank as an example of how the Examiner likes to examine ‘hot topics’. Questions similar to this one appeared in several consecutive exams. It also contains useful background knowledge.]_ 

- a) As an authorised firm with its head office in the UK, the securities firm would be able to exercise passport rights under the core European financial directives. The right to carry on securities related activities in other Member States is provided for under the Capital Requirements Directive 4 (CRD 2013/36/EU) and the Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR 575/2013) and Markets in Financial Instruments Directive 2 (MIFiD 2014/65/EU) and Markets in Financial Instruments Regulation (MIFiR 600/2014/EU). These apply to institutions which include both credit institutions and investment firms (art 3(3) CRR) with passport rights for investment firms and credit institutions being provided for under art 34(1) MIFiD2. The firm in this case would be an EEA firm under Schedule 3 FSMA in relation to the MIFiD2 and be able to exercise separate underlying Treaty Rights as a Treaty firm under Schedule 4 FSMA to the extent necessary. Before the UK’s withdrawal, the firm would be able to passport activities from the UK into other Member States of the EU and EEA through its parent operation in the UK. 

   - b) Relevant procedures would vary depending upon whether the firm wished to exercise service or establishment rights in other territories. Firms are required to notify the PRA of their intention to exercise passport rights. The PRA will seek assurance concerning the risks posed to its objectives by UK firms wishing to operate elsewhere within the EEA with the review being proportionate to the possible impact on the PRA objectives. The PRA is required to assess the adequacy of the firm's resources and administrative structure and whether it complies with the requirements set out in the relevant directive. EU banks passport 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

under the CRD IV and securities firms under MiFID2. The Treasury issued regulations concerning the exercise of outward and inward passport rights in 2001 (FSMA (EEA Passport Rights) Regulations 2001/2511). Separate Commission technical standards were set out in an Implemented Regulation (EU) 926/2014 of 27 August 2014. 

- (c) All of the principal statutory provisions contained the FSMA as amended would continue to apply. This includes the general prohibition (s19 FSMA), permission requirement (s20 FSMA) and restrictions on promotion (s21 FSMA). The firm would have to ensure that its Part IV permission (s40 FSMA) to become authorised (s31(1)(a) FSMA) was up-to-date and comply with the threshold conditions imposed (s41 and Schedule 6 FSMA as amended and COND). Relevant personnel would have to be approved persons (s59 FSMA) and comply with the suitability requirements imposed under FIT and conduct requirements in COCON. 

As an investment firm, it would principally be regulated by the FCA. The firm would be subject to the rules and guidance imposed by the FCA and PRA (ss137A, 137G and 139A FSMA including the FCA PRIN and all other relevant provisions). The question strictly referred to principal statutory restrictions although candidates may have referred to rules and guidance manuals as these are imposed under the provisions contained in the FSMA. 

Reference may also have been made to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), Financial Service Compensation Scheme (FSCS), the Complaints Scheme and Complaints Commissioner as well as the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Competition Commission and the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) within the Bank of England although this was not strictly necessary. 

- (d)  A UK authorised London subsidiary would have no automatic rights following the UK’s withdrawal from the EU unless replacement arrangements were agreed. If replacement rights were continued, the subsidiary may be able to exercise services and establishment rights across Europe. The relevant procedures would vary depending upon whether the firm wished to exercise service or establishment rights in other territories (above). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

In the absence of specific arrangements, the firm would have three options following the UK withdrawal from the EU. It would have to retain its existing UK authorisation and permission to continue to operate within the UK. A representative office is only an overseas operation used for marketing and client development purposes without local authorisation to conduct transactional business. A representative office would have no rights to provide services or establishment in other Member States either before or after the UK’s withdrawal. If the firm wishes to conduct regulated activities within the EU, it would then have to restructure its representative office as a full operation and apply for authorisation from the Irish authorities or apply for authorisation in another Member State either through a branch or separately incorporated subsidiary. A branch operates under the legal identity of the parent firm in the UK without any separate local legal status. A subsidiary constitutes a newly formed corporate entity within the UK or elsewhere. 

- e) Passport rights only apply in relation to the provision of financial services on a services or establishment basis within the EU. The firm would not automatically be able to carry on financial services in any other non-EU country. The extent to which the firm would have to make a full new original application would depend upon the relationship between the separate third country and either the UK or the EU depending upon whether the new services were to be carried on from London or Ireland. 

Special rules apply under the MIFiD II in respect of assessing equivalence requirements with regard to 3rd country firms coming into the EU and establishing branches (MIFiD II art 39) which replace the earlier national provisions. The UK set out its implementation approach to these provisions in an HM Treasury consultation document in March 2015. The rights that the firm would otherwise have to carry on financial services in other non-UK and non-EU countries would depend on the arrangements agreed with the specific country concerned and the UK or EU respectively. 

The establishment of non-EU branches by EU firms was referred to by the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) in July 2017 with national competent authorities being required to ensure that such use was based on objective reasons where services 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

were provided in the non-EU jurisdiction and did not result in non-EEA branches performing material functions or services back into the EU (para 49 ESMA Opinion to support supervisory convergence in the area of investment firms in the context of the United Kingdom withdrawing from the European Union (13 July 2017 ESMA 35-43-762)). 

## **B71.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to the effects on a fund management firm in the UK of developments in the Financial Technology (FinTech) area. The firm provides retail services principally through telephone dealing, website and platform relations. The FCA has set up a dedicated section on Fintech and innovative businesses on its new website (https://www.the-fca.org.uk/firms/fintech-and-innovative- businesses). This specifically explains the nature and operation of Project Innovate including the new Innovation Hub and Regulatory Sandbox which are intended to support the development of innovative financial products and services in the UK. 

- a) Financial technology could impact on the firm’s activities in a number of ways. These include the following: 

   - Distributed Ledger Technology (DLY) or Blockchain Distributed Ledger Technology is a system of replicated and synchronised digital records geographically spread over multiple sites, countries or institutions. There is no centralised administrator or centralised data storage. One form of distributed ledger design is the blockchain system, which can be either public or private. A blockchain is a continuously growing list of records called blocks, which are linked and secured using cryptography. Each computer linked to the network, (“node”) receives a copy of each block as it is created. Each block contains an encrypted reference to the previous block called a hash. Once recorded, the data in any given block cannot be altered retrospectively without the alteration of all subsequent blocks, which requires collusion of the network majority. The blockchain provides an open, distributed record of transactions or other information shared between two or more parties which is efficient and verifiable. 

   - Robo-Advice or Automated Advice 

   - Robo-advice comprises financial advice provided with limited or zero human intervention by software applications that combine digital user interfaces and decision- 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

making algorithms. Such advisors may be standalone firms or platforms or can be the in-house applications of mainstream financial institutions. A typical robo-advisor collects information from clients about their financial situation, investment goals and risk tolerance, and then uses the data to offer advice and/or automatically invest client funds. 

- Chatbots 

Chatbots use artificial intelligence to simulate a human conversation. They employ a technique called natural language processing to interpret human inputs (voice or text) within a defined context and respond accordingly. Chatbots can be used to answer customer queries and to action customer trade execution orders. 

- Crowdfunding 

Crowdfunding is a method of raising finance to fund a business, project or venture, by asking a large number of people for a small amount each. 

Traditionally, financing has involved asking a few people for large sums. Crowdfunding uses the internet to contact thousands of potential investors typically using social media and then directing investors to a website set up for the purpose. 

- Artificial Intelligence 

Artificial intelligence is the theory and development of computer systems which can perform tasks that normally require human intelligence. These tasks include natural language processing, visual perception, speech recognition, decision making and translation between languages. This is supported by processes including machine learning (the automated acquisition of information and the inference of rules for using that information), and the application of algorithms to reach approximate or definite conclusions. 

- Analytics or “Big Data” 

Big data refers to very large volumes of structured or unstructured data that can be analysed, or mined, to identify patterns or trends in, for example, customer behaviour or market activity. This data can be collected from a range of sources including news reports, comments on social networks, personal electronic devices and applications, questionnaires, and financial data feeds. 

- Cloud Computing 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Cloud computing is the delivery of IT services from the internet via web-based tools and applications, rather than using a direct connection to an in-house server. Information is kept in a remote database accessed through the internet, rather than a local hard drive, and can be accessed from any location. Software- based services can be accessed and charged for on an on-demand basis rather than being purchased or licenced. 

- RegTech 

RegTech (regulatory technology) describes software applications used for regulatory reporting and compliance purposes by financial institutions. The term also refer to firms that offer such applications. RegTech offers firms the ability to deliver accurate regulatory information to regulators at reduced cost based on the consistent application of standardised reporting requirements. 

- SupTech 

SupTech (supervisory technology) is the use of innovative tools and systems by financial regulators to improve their supervision of regulated individuals, firms and markets and/or to reduce costs. 

- b) The firm’s business model may be disrupted by their adopting, or their competitors 

adopting, FinTech services which potentially offer the following: 

## • Improved Marketing 

The use of big data processing can be used to identify appropriate new customers or information about existing customers can be used to improve products and their delivery. 

The use of social media, and particularly links with existing tech firms such as Amazon and Facebook, using the information they hold on their customers, can be used to target sales and marketing messages at the appropriate individuals. 

- Enhanced Fund Management 

The use of big data processing can provide additional information about the performance and activities of investment targets which may improve fund performance. One example being the analysis of satellite photographs of retail stores’ carparks to assess their trade volumes. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Artificial intelligence can be used to manage trading either in the automated selection of investment opportunities based on a defined strategy, or in managing the timing and size of trades to achieve the best price. 

- Innovative Distribution 

The use of mobile phone apps, perhaps combined with access to consumers’ bank accounts under Open Banking, may replace the existing retail distribution platforms that the firm currently relies on. 

Existing, or emerging internet firms, such as Amazon and Google, may enter the market as distribution channels leveraging their large customer base and the detailed information they hold on them. Such entrants could take significant market share away from traditional distribution channels. 

- More Efficient Back Office Processing 

The use of blockchain links between investors, fund managers, banks, brokers and custodians could reduce costs and errors in back office processing and potentially reduce the risk of fraud. 

Chatbots could be used to respond to customer queries and requests more cheaply than human support staff. 

- Improved Customer Service 

The use of robo-advisors to suggest investment strategies to customers based on analysis of their financial position, objectives and risk tolerance, could provide the benefits of a tailored wealth management service without the costs associated with traditional human service. 

- c) Relevant FCAs initiatives to support FinTech innovation within the regulatory regime include the following: 

   - Project Innovate 

The FCAS launched Project Innovate in October 2014 to help encourage and support innovation in the financial services industry. 

This work is described as central to delivering against the FCA’s competition objective – it aims to encourage innovation in the interests of consumers, and to promote 

competition through disruptive innovation that offers new services to consumers and challenges existing business models. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

The FCA uses Project Innovate to establish constructive engagement with innovative firms, from small start-ups to mass market new business models, and seeks to remove unnecessary barriers to innovation. 

The project helps innovative firms gain access to timely and frank feedback on the regulatory implications of their concepts, plans and choices. It also seeks to tackle the structural issues that impede the progress of innovators entering the market. 

- The Innovation Hub 

A central component of Project Innovate is the Innovation Hub which provides support to new and established businesses (both regulated and unregulated) who seek to launch innovative products and services into the market. 

There are no restrictions on the areas of financial services covered by the Innovation Hub: firms innovating in mortgages, capital markets and pensions can all access the Hub for help in introducing the products to the market. 

The Innovation Hub also performs a horizon-scanning role by identifying new technologies and areas where the regulatory framework needs to adapt to enable further innovation in the interests of consumers. 

It provides an end-to-end experience for new entrants. Firms that receive initial support from the Hub have their applications for authorisation handled by a specialised authorisation process. 

The FCA provides firms with dedicated supervisory support, usually for one year post – authorisation, which gives firms a seamless regulatory experience and minimises the risk of unnecessary delay. 

- International Engagement 

Through engaging with international firms and overseas regulators, Project 

Innovate supports the FCA’s competition objective by promoting the UK as a 

centre for innovation in financial services. 

It does this by: 

- Facilitating the entry of innovative overseas firms into the UK, thereby increasing innovation and competition in the UK financial services markets, and 

- Facilitating the expansion of UB-based innovative firms into overseas markets, potentially making the m more sustainable challengers in the UK. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

As at May 2018, the FCA has signed Co-operation agreements and frameworks with 

regulators in Australia, Singapore, Hong Kong, Canada, China, Japan, US and Korea. 

- Regulatory Sandbox 

Launched in May 2016, this allows firms to test innovative products, services, business 

models and delivery mechanisms in the real market, with real consumers without incurring all the normal regulatory consequences of engaging in those activities 

The Sandbox provides firms with: 

   - The ability to test products and services in a controlled environment 

   - Reduced time to market at potentially lower cost 

   - Support in identifying the appropriate consumer protection safeguards to build into new products and services 

- The FCA Advice Unit 

The FCA Advice Unit provides regulatory feedback to firms who are developing 

automated advice models for consumers. 

This could include: 

- An initial meeting with the Advice Unit, and other specialized areas of the FCA if needed, to discuss the proposition 

- For firms requested ongoing engagement, a dedicated point of contact in the Advice Unit to provide regulatory input at agreed milestones 

- Specific feedback on the regulatory implications of the model – this could include follow-up meetings with relevant FCA staff to discuss specific issues, informal steers and individual guidance as appropriate 

- Advice on how to apply for authorization if the firm is currently unauthorized. 

The advice and feedback provided will focus on helping the firm to understand the regulatory implications of their model. 

- d) The challenges that FinTech developments could create to the FCA’s statutory objectives include the following: 

   - **Impact on** the **Consumer Protection Objective** 

Poorly designed robo-advice algorithms, or their use without adequate Know- Your Customer information gathering, may result in large numbers of customers receiving inappropriate advice of being sold inappropriate products of services. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

The use of cryptographic and/or biometric identification may improve the security of financial transactions and reduce the opportunities for fraud. 

Consumers dealing with firms over the internet may unwittingly be transacting with unregulated firms or be taken in by fraudsters. 

- **Impact on** the **Integrity Objective** 

RegTech has the potential to increase the efficiency and effectiveness of market supervision by providing regulators with near real time data access and automated regulatory reporting. 

Big data has the potential to improve supervisor’s monitoring of regulated individuals and firms through insights gained from automated analysis of data, for example through the application of natural language processing to the analysis of news reports, annual reports and accounts, broker research, market trade data and press releases 

Unregulated third party service providers providing critical services to large numbers of financial institutions may pose systemic risk to the financial system. They may also have access to large volumes of client and market data which could be used to support market abuse activities. 

- **Impact on the Competition Objective** 

Smaller firms may be disadvantaged relative to larger firms which have bigger technology budgets, access to in-house technical expertise and who can realise efficiencies through economies of scale. This could result in reduced competition and the market domination of a few large players. 

Alternatively, the entry of new players competing with incumbent fund managers and distribution channels could result in lower costs for customers and reduce the systemic risk associated with the dominance of large investment houses and distribution platforms. 

## **B72.** 

This was an optional question. It raised several issues with regard to money laundering systems and controls and financial ethics in relation to a London bank. The compliance department of the bank has become concerned about a few cash deposit-related issues. In total, 27 candidates took this question. The overall average mark was 13.7 from a maximum of 20 marks. This is slightly above the average for the entire examination paper. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

There was a large range of 12.5 marks see in this question, indicating that some candidates did much better than others. 

a) Specific concerns arise with regard to: 

(i) The large cash deposits made, including in such a clear and direct manner. 

(ii) The fact that the businesses had stated that it would make deposits using cheques and bank and credit cards. 

(iii) The connected nature of the businesses. 

(iv) The coincidence of the largest deposits following weekend raves where drugs could have been sold or other illegal activities carried out. 

(v) The possible lack of proper identity checks including Know Your Customer (KYC) screening. 

(vi) The possible need for Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) 

(vii) The possible involvement of Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs) as clients; 

(viii) The possible involvement of Ultra High Net Worth (UHNW) persons as clients 

(ix) The possible size and number of transactions. 

(x) The lack of explanation of the proper source of the funds and possible secretive nature of the operations concerned. 

(xi) The possible failure to comply with standard procedures and requirements. 

(xii) The possible limited number of personnel and senior personnel involved. 

(xiii) The possible involvement and collusion of the local compliance officer. 

(xiv) The possible whistleblowing under the Public Interest Disclosure Act 1998 (PIDA). 

(xv) The failure to have taken any appropriate action in the present case. 

(xvi) The failure to have complied with the FCA directions to improve systems and controls. 

(xvii) The failure to have notified the FCA and the PRA of the concerns that had arisen. 

(xviii) The failure to have acted in an open and cooperative manner with the FCA and the PRA. 

Additional issues may also have arisen from an ethical perspective: 

(i) Potential breach of PRIN 1, FR 1 and APER 1 (integrity). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

(ii) The need to report suspicions under the Proceeds of Crime Act (POCA) 2002. 

(iii) The need to cooperate with the regulators under PRIN 11, FR 8 and APER 4. (iv) The need to comply with other codes of conduct, including that of the CISI. 

b) The full range of relevant offences, defences and penalties under the POCA should have been noted. The principal UK money laundering offences are now restated under Part VII of POCA. POCA creates three main offences relating to concealing (s 327), arranging (s 328) and acquisition, use and possession (s 329), as well as attempting, conspiring or inciting to commit any such offence (s 340(11)(a) and (b)). Money laundering includes aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring the commission of any of these offences (s 340(11)(c) and (d)). Two separate offences are also created, namely failing to disclose (ss 330-332) and tipping-off (s 333). 

Revisions to POCA were made under the Serious Organised Crime and Police Act (SOCPA) 2005, with separate reference possibly being made to reporting under s 21A of the Terrorism Act 2000 and to the Bribery Act 2010. Reference should also have been made to the Money Laundering Regulations 2007 as amended. 

Separate provisions with regard to anti-terrorist requirements are contained in the Terrorism Act 2000, the Terrorism Act 2006 and the Counter-Terrorism Act 2008. Other relevant legislation includes the Anti-terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001, which introduced after 11 September 2001, the Prevention of Terrorism Act 2005, which introduced control orders, and the Terrorism (United Nations Measures) Order 2009 and Terrorist Asset-Freezing (Temporary Provisions) Act 2010. 

Identification and record keeping requirements are imposed under the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance and Money Laundering Regulations 2007, including with regard to Customer Due Diligence (CDD), KYC and identification requirements, as well as Simplified Due Diligence (SDD) and EDD for higher risk entities, as well as PEPs. 

A consolidated list of the persons and countries against whom financial sanctions are in place is maintained by HM Treasury and the JMLSG. Separate lists of uncooperative jurisdictions are also maintained by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). 

c)    Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD) is provided for under Regulation 14 (1) and Section 5.5 of the Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance. EDD is specifically to be used in higher risk situations including the customer is not physically 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

present when identities are verified, correspondent banking or with PEPs. Additional documents, data or information have to be collected. 

EDD is to be applied on a risk sensitive basis were insufficient information has been obtained under standard due diligence (JMLSG Section 5.5.1). Firms should obtain sufficient information concerning the circumstances and business of customers and beneficial owners to support the risk assessment process and monitor customer activity and transactions (JMLSG Section 5.5.2). The relevant additional information will depend on the nature of that money laundering or terrorist financing risk JMLSG Section 5.5.3). 

PEPs consists of high ranking non-UK public officials, Members of Parliament and immediate families and close associates. 

Reference could also have been made to the Consolidated List of persons or countries against whom financial sanctions are in place is maintained by the HM Treasury and Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG). Separate lists of uncooperative jurisdictions are also maintained by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF). 

b) Institutional liabilities can be considered under the MLR 2007 provisions or general regulatory requirements. The MLR imposes internal systems and control requirements on firms including with regard to customer due diligence (CDD) and monitoring, internal reporting, five year recordkeeping, internal control, risk assessment and management, compliance monitoring, recognition and reporting of suspicious transactions and staff training. Specific FCA Handbook provisions include SYSC 3.2.6 (systems and controls with regard to compliance, financial crime and money laundering), SYSC 3.2.6I (MLRO) with FCA guidance on financial crime being provided in the FC guide. Breach of the MLR requirements is punishable by up to two years imprisonment and an unlimited fine or six months imprisonment and a £5,000 fine before a Magistrates Court. The full range of enforcement actions would be available for breach of relevant Handbook provisions. 

The firm may also have breached COBS 2.1 in failing to act honestly, fairly and professionally (COBS 2.1.1R) and COBS 2.2 in failing to provide appropriate information in a comprehensive form (COBS 2.2.1R). The firm may have breached COBS 4, in particular, in failing to ensure that communications are not fair, clear and not misleading (COBS 4.2.1R), COBS 4.10 in failing to maintain proper systems and controls for approving and communicating financial promotions (COBS 4.10.1 and 2) 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and COBS 4.11 adequate record keeping (COBS 4.11.1R). The firm may have breached SYSC 3 systems and controls and 6 on compliance. 

The firm may have separately breached CASS 7 on client money rules including treatment of client money (CASS 7.11.1R) and client segregation (CASS 7.13.1, 2 and 3). The firm may also have breached COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (due skill, care and diligence), 5 (market conduct standards), 3 (regulatory cooperation), and the senior manager rules. 

The firm may also have breached the FCA Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) requirements as originally set out in FSA, Treating customers fairly – towards fair outcomes for consumers (July 2006) with relevant management information (MI) being set out in FSA, Treating customers fairly – guide to management information (July 2007) 

e) National Westminster was fined £264,772,619.95 by Mrs Justice Cockerill at Southwark Crown Court on 13 December 2021 following convictions for three offences in failing to comply with money laundering regulations. This was despite the bank not having been complicit in money laundering directly. This was the first occasion in which the FCA has commenced a criminal action in relation to money laundering. The bank had failed to properly monitor the activities of a commercial customer, Fowler Oldfield, a jewellery business, in Bradford between 8 November 2012 and 23 June 2016. 

Standard Chartered Bank (SCB) was separately fined £46.55 million by the PRA on 17 December 2021 for failing to be open and cooperative with the PRA and for failings in its regulatory reporting governance and controls in relation to a tailored PRA liquidity expectation. This was the highest fine imposed in a PRA-only enforcement case. 

The bank should take all necessary action to avoid a breach of the relevant regulatory, statutory and ethical matters referred to in sub-sections (a) and (b) above. It should also be open and cooperative with the FCA and the PRA (PRIN 11 and FR 7). It should generally ensure that the following are properly secured: 

(a) Customer identification, i.e. KYC and CDD. 

- (b) EDD. 

- (c) High-risk jurisdictions monitored. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (d) Any PEPs monitored. 

- (e) Accounts are monitored and any suspicious transactions identified. 

- (f) Suspicious transaction reports (SRRs) submitted. 

- (g) Carry out necessary improvements and submit all relevant regulatory returns to authorities 

- (h) Have any specific concerns considered by the MLRO. 

- (i) Refer any other matters to the Financial Intelligence Unit within the National Crime Agency (NCA). 

- (j) Refer any possible internal beaches to the FCA and the PRA under the duty to cooperate. 

## **B73.** 

The question raises a number of issues with regard to responsibility of various members of staff in a UK wealth management firm. A new junior member of staff has been assigned to assist a senior manager on a large client account. Errors have arisen in relation to the execution of certain documents which have to be corrected. 

a) Whose fault is it? 

- (i) Holly – It does appear as though Holly is at fault for this situation arising, because she 

   - actively inserts her signature in the space where Mrs Green’s signature should have been, effectively witnessing something she was not present for. She knows that this action was wrong, and even if she did it because of other pressures/reasons (Steven’s instructions to do whatever is required to fix the mistake, her recent bereavement, thinking that she was acting in the best interest of the client), these reasons would not justify her taking this course of action – which was effectively fraudulently witnessing the signature of a trustee. Furthermore, her actions were also misjudged on another account, as on receiving the returned documents from Mr Harris’ son, she is discomforted by the attached note about the state of Mr Harris’ health, and puts the documents to one side for a day before reviewing them. If she had reviewed them sooner, she may have been able to do something to rectify the problem. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (ii) Lucy – Lucy is Holly’s line manager, and is also Head of Wealth Management at Health & Wealth. If the ‘buck stops’ with anyone, it would be Lucy. Additionally, Lucy was the one to allow Holly to take some time off following the death of her grandmother, and therefore should have had an understanding of the personal pressures Holly was under at the time. However, it is clear that Lucy was not even aware of what had happened until returning from annual leave, and it seems unlikely that she could have done anything to prevent this from happening or rectify the issue at an earlier stage. Being at fault is different from being responsible, and whilst one could argue that she is ultimately responsible for what happens within her team, she cannot be said to be at fault here. 

- (iii) Steven – The Harris family are Steven’s key client account, and it is Steven who has been 

   - setting up various forms of trust arrangement for the family. Furthermore, he set the ‘tone’ for how Holly should manage this client relationship very early on, when he told her to “ensure 

that the required paperwork was achieved with as little disturbance of the family as possible”. Finally, his aggressive reaction when Holly approached him with the issue of Mrs Green’s missing signature was disproportionate, and put pressure on Holly to fix the problem by using unethical or unusual means. Whilst he was not the one to insert his signature in the space designed for the witness, his behaviour contributed to that outcome, and as a senior manager responsible for the client, some blame should be assigned to him for allowing this problem to arise. 

- (iv) Mr Harris’ son – Mr Harris’ son has a responsibility for ensuring that his signature is properly witnessed, especially on such an important document (that has already had to be returned once because of a missing signature). However, it is clear that he, and the Harris family, were going through a very difficult time, and perhaps understandable that mistakes would be made. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Furthermore, it appears that he made every effort to ensure the documents were returned in a timely manner, and kept Health & Wealth up to date regarding the state of his father’s health. For these reasons, it does not appear that Mr Harris’ son would be considered to be most at fault. 

- (v) Mrs Green – Mrs Green’s missing signature is the crux of the issue here, and one could suggest that her failure to properly witness all of Mr Harris’ son’s signatures is the reason this situation has arisen at all – thereby making it her fault. However, it was noted that Mrs Green “completed the rest of the form correctly and inserted her signature at all other necessary points”, and Holly herself believes the problem to be an “honest mistake”. Furthermore, Mr Harris’ son should retain some responsibility for ensuring that all his signatures were properly witnessed on such an important document. For this reason, it seems most unlikely that a reasonable person would conclude that Mrs Green is most at fault. 

   - Who is most at fault? - All the characters in this story are culpable/at fault to some degree. It was a combination of failings (Mrs Green missing the signature, Mrs Harris’ son failing to spot the oversight, Holly for missing the signature of the witness to Mr Harris’ son first time round and not checking the documents immediately when they were received), pressures (Lucy being away, Holly’s recent bereavement, Mr Harris’ illness, time pressures) and misconduct (Steven’s reaction and subsequent instruction to fix the problem by whatever means necessary, Holly’s misguided decision to insert her own signature in the witness space) which lead to the situation arising. Nevertheless, the characters who made active, rather than passive, choices are Holly and Steven. Holly signed as a witness to a signature she was not present to observe, and Steven informed her to “Just fix it… I don’t care how!”; However, Lucy’s failure to appoint a replacement for Holly was a serious contribution too. For this reason, they should all be considered at fault. 

- b) The character who is ultimately responsible, in a regulatory sense, for this problem is Lucy. The Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SM&CR) does not currently apply to Health & Wealth, as although they are an FCA- 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

regulated firm, they are not a bank, building society, credit union, or PRAdesignated investment firm. As a solo-regulated firm, the SM&CR will only apply to Health & Wealth from 9 December 2019. For an extra mark – if the SM&CR had been in force Lucy could also be held to account by the regulator as the senior manager responsible in her job description. This includes taking all reasonable steps in her role – which she may have failed to do by failing to appoint a replacement for Holly in her absence, and for herself in her absence. 

As Head of Wealth Management, it is likely that Lucy will be responsible for this area of the firm’s business functions and activities, and therefore be allocated ‘overall responsibility’ for wealth management at Health & Wealth. Furthermore, as a senior manager, she will need to be deemed to be fit and proper to do her job. However, as stated, this will not formally apply to her until December 2019. [My wording above could replace this wording. Since the SM & CR does not come into force until December, it is not strictly relevant for marking before then] 

Nevertheless, as the senior manager in the team, Holly’s line manager, and Steven’s manager the regulator would consider her responsible for any misconduct allowed to occur within her department, and additionally she would be responsible for setting the correct tone/culture within her area of the business. 

- c) There are three main points in the scenario which give an indication about the company culture at Health & Wealth: 

   - (vi) Grievance policy and procedures There is a grievance policy in place which allows employees time off work following the loss of a family member: “Lucy granted [Holly’s] request to take a few days off as allowed by Heath & Wealth’s grievance procedure”. Having a grievance policy/procedure in place is important for the mental health and wellbeing of staff, and the fact that Holly was permitted time off following the death of her grandmother suggests that there is an understanding and caring company culture. However, there is no suggestion in the scenario that there was any additional support or care for Holly following her return to work after taking grievance leave. In fact, she was assigned back to the Harris family account, despite the fact that the company knew Mr Harris was unwell and 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

suffering from a similar illness to her grandmother. As a result, one could infer that Health & Wealth have proper policies in place, but do not go beyond the minimum standard in supporting staff members or colleagues who have recently suffered a bereavement. 

- (vii) Pressure from senior management (Steven) 

Steven’s reaction to Holly, when she tells him about Mrs Green’s missing signature, is 

indicative of a culture of pressure from senior management: “Holly arranges a meeting to 

discuss the situation with Steven, who is furious about the oversight. He tells Holly “this is all your fault! I can’t believe you could have cost me my most important client! Just fix it… I don’t care how!”” This could be unique to Steven, however, if he is permitted to continue with this kind of behaviour – either through being incentivised for the results it yields, or simply by not being reprimanded for the way in which he spoke to Holly – this culture could spread throughout Health & Wealth, as the tone is often set from the top (and Steven is a senior wealth manager). One may even go so far as to say that this pressure verges on bullying, which would be an important issue to address. 

- (viii) Ability to raise concerns/Speak Up (when Holly explains to Lucy that there had been a missing witness signature, and she had inserted her own signature to confirm the authenticity of the original) 

As soon as Lucy returns from leave, Holly informs her about what has happened: “On returning to the office, Lucy and Holly meet for an update meeting. Holly tells Lucy about the situation with Mr Harris [and] explains to Lucy that there had been a missing witness signature, and she had inserted her own signature to confirm the authenticity of the original.” This suggests that Holly, a junior staff member, is comfortable owning up to mistakes and raising concerns with her manager (who is considerably more senior). This may just be indicative of a good relationship between Holly and Lucy, however, Holly also first approached Steven with the problem – actually suggesting that there may be a culture where staff members are not afraid to own up to mistakes, or raise concerns about issues arising, with senior management. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Could changes to the culture prevent a similar issue from occurring in the future 

   - (i) The most significant threat to a positive company culture would be if senior managers use pressure (and potentially bullying) to motivate staff and get results. If Steven, or another –manager, were to react in a similar manner to the way described in the scenario, one of two things (or both) may occur: 

   - Staff would be encouraged/pressured (and therefore more likely) to use unethical methods to resolve problems, as Holly did when she inserted her own signature. 

   - Staff would no longer feel comfortable approaching their managers with a problem, thereby preventing problems from coming to light at an early stage and perhaps allowing them to escalate. 

   - (ii) Therefore, ensuring that action is taken against Steven for the way he acted would send a strong message that this is not part of the desired culture at Health & Wealth, and could prevent a similar issue from occurring in the future. 

- d) The following options are available to Lucy regarding Holly: 

   - (i) Fire Holly for gross misconduct (she was not a witness to the trustee’s signature, and therefore fraudulently inserted her own name in the document) and carelessness in checking the signatures. 

   - Pros – Sends a strong message (not just to Holly, but all staff) that this behaviour will not be tolerated; removes risk that Health & Wealth will be liable for the fraud committed by Holly. 

   - Cons – Is disproportionate given the circumstances; may prevent staff from coming forward about mistakes made in the future. 

   - (ii) Take no further action (acknowledge that Holly was left in a tough position, and did what she thought was right in the circumstances). 

   - Pros – Staff will feel reassured that Health & Wealth are a supportive employer. 

   - Cons – If no action is taken, this may send the message that mistakes will go unpunished, thereby creating a lax/sloppy culture. 

   - (iii) Take positive steps to ensure this does not happen again, such as requiring Holly to take further training, and/or perhaps have all her work strictly supervised for a set time. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

   - Pros – Sends a message that this behaviour is not what is expected of Health & Wealth staff; manages risk by giving Holly the additional training necessary to ensure this does not happen again; is mindful of the pressures that Holly was under at the time. 

   - Cons – This could be expensive, and create a drain on resources (e.g. the manager supervising her will need to take time out of their day to double-check her mistakes). 

- (iv) Holly should face disciplinary action, but the sanction should fall short of dismissal. 

   - Pros - Sends a strong message (not just to Holly, but all staff) that this behaviour will not be tolerated; demonstrates to the regulator that Health & Wealth take a strong stance against fraud. 

   - Cons – Does not take into account Steven’s role in the scenario; could be deemed disproportionate given the circumstances. 

Similar options are also available to Lucy regarding Steven: 

- a) Fire Steven for his bullying behaviour. 

- Pros - Sends a strong message to all staff that this behaviour (e.g. bullying) will not be tolerated, and that senior managers are not exempt from the disciplinary process when they behave in this manner. 

- Cons - Is disproportionate given the circumstances 

- b) Take no further action (as Steven could not necessarily have known what action Holly would take to rectify the problem, and he may not have intended her to take unethical steps). 

- Pros – Steven will feel supported as a manager, who is able to make decisions about how to manage his team. 

- Cons – If no action is taken, this may send the message that aggressive behaviour will go unpunished, thereby creating a culture where this is seen as acceptable. 

- c) Require Steven to take further training on appropriate management techniques. 

- Pros – Sends a message that this behaviour is not what is expected of Health & Wealth staff; will ensure that Steven thinks twice before reacting in a similar manner in the future; demonstrates the value of continuing development/learning/training, even for senior managers. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- Cons – This could be expensive; Steven may feel as though his skill as a manager is being called into question, and react negatively. 

- d) Steven should face disciplinary action, but the sanction should fall short of dismissal. `o` Pros - Sends a strong message that this behaviour will not be tolerated; demonstrates to the regulator that Health & Wealth take a strong stance against bullying. 

- Cons – Could be deemed disproportionate given the circumstances; could demotivate Steven, and prevent him from making decisive choices in the future (something necessary for a senior manager). 

The following options are available to Lucy regarding the Harris family: 

- a) Inform them about what has happened, apologise, and try and resolve the problem. 

- Pros – Is honest/open/transparent/fair; is the only way to try and correct the problem. Normally it is a factual question as to whether Mrs Green did or did not witness Mr Harris’ son’s signature at the relevant time. If she did, she could confirm that she had done so by formal written Declaration. However some documents require witnesses signatures to be effective. 

- Cons – Could annoy/upset the Harris family, potentially with the risk that they will leave Health & Wealth in favour of another firm. If the document was of a type that required a contemporary written witness signature to be effective, the firm could have a large professional indemnity bill. 

- b) Do not inform them, as doing so would only add further stress at a difficult time, and allow Holly’s signature to remain in the space designed for a witness. 

- Pros – Would avoid adding stress to the family at a difficult time. 

- Cons – Is not honest/open/transparent/fair; is a short-term solution, not a long-term solution; sends the message that this behaviour is implicitly permitted. 

## **B74.** 

The question raised a number of issues with regard to the regulation of algorithmic trading under the FSMA. A new programme has been developed by digital platform which is to be purchased by an existing authorised UK securities firm. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

- (a) The platform would have to be authorised under s 19 FSMA and hold relevant permission under 20 FSMA to the extent that it carried on a regulated activity in the UK. Regulated activities are defined in s22 FSMA and the Regulated Activities Order 2001 (as amended) and the FSMA 2000 (PRA Regulated Activities) Order 2013. The 2013 Order divides activities between the FCA and PRA by specifying that anything not specifically assigned to the PRA under the Order is reserved to the FCA. Permissible activities overseen by the FCA would include inter alia managing investments (RAO art 37), dealing in investments (RAO art 21) and arranging deals in investments (RAO art 25). This would also include sending dematerialised instructions (RAO art 45). The RAO distinguishes between specified investments and specified activities. Specified investments include investments, deposits, contracts for insurance, shares, instruments creating or acknowledging indebtedness, government and public securities, instruments giving entitlement to investments, certificates representing certain securities, units in a collective investment scheme, rights under a stakeholder pension scheme, options, futures, contracts for differences, Lloyd’s syndicate capacity and membership, funeral plan contracts, regulated mortgage contracts and rights to or interest in investments (RAO arts 73-89). 

- (b) Algorithmic trading is defined in the FCA Glossary as trading in financial instruments with a computer algorithm automatically determining individual parameters of orders (including whether to initiate the order, the timing, price and quantity of order or how to managed order after submission) with limited or no human intervention excluding systems only used for routing orders to one or more trading venues and simple processing, confirmation or post-trade execution activities. Authorisation and permission would be required to the extent that the activities of the platform involved the activities referred to above (including investment management, arranging and dealing and sending dematerialised instructions) in relation to the investments as defined. The FCA published a report on _Algorithmic Trading in Wholesale Markets_ (12 February 2018) with the PRA issuing a Consultation Paper on _Algorithmic Trading_ (12 Feb 2018). The FCA document reviews activities and themes with the PRA paper outlining governance and risk management expectations in relation to algorithmic trading. A number of recommendations are made with regard to management approval of suitable governance frameworks, sound approval processes for algorithms, algorithm and risk 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

control testing, algorithm and risk control inventory maintenance and effective oversight of algorithmic control functions and risk management. 

- (c) All of the principal statutory provisions contained the FSMA as amended would apply to the specific regulated activities involved. This includes the general prohibition (s19 FSMA), permission requirement (s20 FSMA) and restrictions on promotion (s21 FSMA). The firm would have to apply for Part 4A permission (s40 FSMA) to become authorised (s31(1)(a) FSMA) and comply with the threshold conditions imposed (s41 and Schedule 6 FSMA as amended and COND). Relevant personnel would have to be approved persons (s59 FSMA), or subject to the new senior managers and certification regimes, and comply with the suitability requirements imposed under FIT and conduct requirements in COCON. The platform would be subject to the rules and guidance imposed by the FCA and PRA (ss137A, 137G and 139A FSMA including the FCA PRIN, PRA Fundamental Rules and all other relevant provisions). 

- (d) The full range of regulatory penalties may be available against the firm including variation of permission (s45 FSMA), suspension of permission (s206a FSMA), cancellation of Part 4 permission (s54 FSMA), withdrawal of authorisation (s33 FSMA), public censure (s205 FSMA), financial penalties (s206 FSMA), requests for information (s165 FSMA and Schedule 12 FSA 2012), independent inquiry (s68 FSA 2012), administration, receivership, winding-up or bankruptcy (Part 24 FSMA, Banking Act 2009 and Schedule 14 FSA 2012), injunctions (s380 FSMA), restitution (ss382 and 384 FSMA), suspension of financial instrument from trading if appropriate (s36 FSA 2012), complaint by a consumer body (super-complaint under s234c FSMA and s43 FSA 2012), regulated person reference (s234d FSMA), OFT investigation (s234h FSMA), action under product intervention rules (s137d FSMA 2000 and s24 FSA 2012) and skilled persons report (s166 FSMA). 

- (e) The platform or acquiring securities firm would have to apply to have the scope of permission extended to include any other additional activities and forms. This may include advising on investments (RAO art 53), safeguarding and administering investments (RAO art 40), establishing collective investment schemes (RAO art 52) and agreeing to carry on activities (RAO art 64) as well as possibly accepting deposits (RAO 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

art 5), effecting and carrying out insurance contracts (RAO art 10), issuing regulated mortgage contracts (RAO art 61), funeral plan contracts (RAO art 59) and Lloyds related activities (RAO art 56). These would apply in relation to any of the investments referred to above. 

## **B75.** 

This question provided a case study related to the selection of a new CEO for a UK-based fintech firm that has grown rapidly in recent years. The case study provided in formation in relation to two candidates for the CEO position: candidate A, and candidate B. 

(a) The Senior Managers & Certification Regime (SM&CR) was introduced as part of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013, following recommendations from the Parliamentary Commission on Banking Standards (PCBS). The regime was established with the aim of enhancing accountability and governance within financial institutions, specifically targeting the roles and responsibilities of senior management. It is designed to promote a culture of responsibility and integrity at the top levels of financial firms. The SM&CR framework is structured around three core components: the Senior Managers Regime (SMR), the Certification Regime, and the Conduct Rules. 

The SMR focuses on the most senior individuals in a firm who hold key decisionmaking roles. Under this regime, senior managers are required to have clearly defined responsibilities and must be approved by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) or the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) before they can perform their roles. Each senior manager is assigned a "Statement of Responsibilities" that outlines their specific duties and areas of accountability within the firm. This ensures that there is a clear understanding of who is responsible for each aspect of the firm’s operations, which enhances transparency and accountability. 

The Certification Regime applies to employees who are not senior managers but whose roles could cause significant harm to the firm or its customers. Unlike the SMR, individuals in these roles do not require approval from regulators. Instead, the responsibility lies with the firm to assess and certify that these individuals are ‘fit and proper’ to perform their roles. This certification must be renewed annually, and it covers aspects such as competence, honesty, integrity, and financial soundness. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

The Conduct Rules set out basic standards of behaviour that apply to all employees within a regulated firm. These rules are intended to ensure that individuals act with integrity, due skill, care, and diligence, and in the best interests of customers. The rules are divided into two tiers: the first tier applies to all employees, while the second tier includes additional rules specifically for senior managers. Firms are required to train their staff on these rules and ensure compliance. 

The SM&CR was specifically designed to address weaknesses in corporate culture and governance that contributed to past financial crises. By imposing stringent accountability measures, the regime aims to instil a culture of responsibility among senior managers and key employees, reducing the likelihood of misconduct and promoting ethical behaviour. The requirement for firms to certify the fitness and propriety of individuals in significant roles reinforces the emphasis on high standards of conduct throughout the organisation. 

(b) The Chief Executive function (SMF1) is the function of having responsibility, under the immediate authority of the governing body, alone or jointly with others, for carrying out the management of the conduct of the whole of the business (or relevant activities) of a firm. The role of the CEO in a financial services firm, particularly within a UK subsidiary of a foreign bank, is one of the most critical Senior Management Functions (SMFs) as defined under the Senior Managers & Certification Regime (SM&CR). The CEO holds overarching responsibility for the firm’s operations, strategy, and governance, ensuring that the firm complies with regulatory requirements and operates in a manner that promotes the interests of its clients and the integrity of the financial markets. 

The CEO is responsible for the day-to-day management of the firm’s business activities, ensuring that operations are conducted efficiently, effectively, and in compliance with all relevant laws and regulations. This includes overseeing the implementation of strategic objectives, managing resources, and ensuring that the firm’s financial performance meets the expectations of the Board and shareholders. The CEO must provide strong leadership, setting the tone from the top regarding the firm’s culture, values, and commitment to ethical conduct. This involves ensuring that the firm’s governance framework is robust, with clear lines of accountability and decision-making processes that support the firm’s strategic goals while maintaining 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

high standards of corporate governance. The CEO is also responsible for fostering a culture of compliance and risk management, ensuring that the firm’s policies and procedures align with regulatory expectations and that all employees understand their roles in maintaining the firm’s integrity. 

As the primary point of contact between the firm and its regulators, the CEO is responsible for ensuring that the firm adheres to all applicable regulatory requirements.
