• effective and comprehensive training to staff involved in the monitoring, detection and identification of orders and transactions that could constitute insider dealing, market manipulation or attempted insider dealing or market manipulation, in particular the provision of training on a regular basis and ensuring this is appropriate and proportionate in relation to the scale, size and nature of the business. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

One mark was awarded for each valid point made up to four marks. 

## **B97.** 

## **a) (5 marks)** 

## **What are the expectations of the financial firm when managing its agents? What concerns do you observe in this case?** 

In many regards the treatment of agents from a regulatory perspective is similar to that of all employees. The firm must ensure that there are appropriate internal systems, policies and procedures existing for all employees and agents. It is also important for a firm to ensure an appropriate business culture operates for all their agents. Like all employees, agents should operate with honesty and competence and have the skills, knowledge and expertise necessary to fulfil their responsibilities. The firm is responsible for establishing the policies and procedures to ensure compliant behaviour by their agents for financial compliance and for countering financial crime. 

Concerns in this case include the agent defining their own policies, treating the customers inappropriately and not following appropriate complaint handling procedures. More widely there is an issue of not treating customers fairly arising in the case, such as the use of irrelevant information in how the customer is judged (such as describing them as a troublemaker). It is expected that firms have appropriate written procedures for handling expressions of dissatisfaction. These procedures should be followed regardless of whether the complaint is oral or written, whether the complaint is justified or not, as long as it relates to the firm’s provision of a financial service. This should occur whether the business is sourced directly or through an agent. 

One mark was awarded for each valid point made up to five marks. 

## **b) (5 marks)** 

## **What actions should the firm undertake in this case in relation to the agent?** 

The firm needs to undertake several actions in relation to the agent. First it needs to explain to the agent that they do not define the policies and procedures for the sale and administration of the firm’s financial services. Rather they are obliged to follow the procedures of the firm. The complaint and contacts from the customer need to be recorded and responded to appropriately, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

following the firm’s procedures. In practice the firm needs to deal with complaints promptly and fairly with effective and transparent procedures. Once the firm has received a complaint, it must investigate competently, diligently, and impartially, obtaining additional information as necessary. As it is important to consider the root cause of the complaints through analysing the causes of complaints, an investigation of the agent may be considered. If a complaint is made it is good practice to consider if other products and customers have been affected. Therefore, a wider review of the business undertaken by the agent may be appropriate. It is important to determine if more cases similar in nature to the one raised has occurred, through the agent or indeed other agents working for the firm. 

It is important to keep records as to all complaints. The record keeping of complaints by the agent also needs to be investigated. Records must also be kept as to the complaints, for regulatory monitoring and to comply with any future contacts with the FOS. These records should include the name of the complainant, the substance of the complaint, and details of any correspondence between the firm and the complainant, including details of any redress offered by the firm. Potentially the agent may be encouraged to join a professional body and undertake further training. Training can also be provided by the firm. In light of worrisome circumstances seen in this case the firm may also wish to reassess its employment of this agent. 

One mark was awarded for each valid point made up to five marks. 

## **c) (5 marks)** 

## **What actions should the firm undertake in this case in relation to the customer?** 

The firm needs to contact the customer, appropriately investigate the complaint, and notify the customer of their right to go to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) if appropriate. To bring the case to a suitable conclusion, the firm should follow the following process. Once a complaint has been received by the firm, a prompt written acknowledgement should be provided assuring the customer the complaint has been received and is being dealt with. The firm must keep the customer informed as to the progress of the complaint investigation and resolution. The complaint needs to be addressed in eight weeks. If the firm rejects the complaint, they must enclose a copy of the FOS standard explanatory leaflet and inform the customer that if they remain dissatisfied, they may refer the complaint to the FOS within six months. In this case, it must be determined whether the payout from the product should be made and, if this is justified, the payment should be made promptly. Potentially, and considering the issues 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

raised in the complaint, the firm may also consider providing redress to the customer if this is deemed appropriate. 

One mark was awarded for each valid point made up to a maximum of five marks. 

## **d) (5 marks)** 

## **Why are fair and unbiased complaints handling procedures important for the financial services industry?** 

Effective complaints handling is an essential aspect of the financial services industry and critical to fulfilling many of the regulatory principles underlying the UK form of financial regulation. These complaints handling procedures are critical in ensuring that customers are treated fairly, and that financial services business is undertaken in a transparent and fair environment. Crucial in this process is ensuring that trust exists in this industry and when interacting with financial firms. This question is open ended, and many more ideas and issues were raised, which were allocated marks as appropriate. 

## **B98.** 

## **a) (6 marks)** 

## **What administration, regulatory and compliance roles might be appropriately outsourced?** 

In practice a wide range of a firm’s activities may be outsourced to a third party. Many concerns can arise in this outsourcing process. If an operational function is regarded as critical to the firm and might impair regulatory compliance, then this may not be a suitable course of action. This is very much the case for certain types of financial firm. For example, banks are strongly discouraged by international regulators from the outsourcing of their compliance function. Similarly, a firm needs to ensure that any outsourcing does not impede compliance with financial regulation and communications with regulators. 

Areas where concerns should not arise include those of advisory services, such as legal advice, training, billing, security, using standardised services and services which are not part of the firm’s relevant services. Of course, while activities can be outsourced, responsibility cannot be outsourced and is retained by the firm. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

SYSC 8 of the FCA Handbook sets out rules and guidance in relation to outsourcing. This includes the requirement that firms must take reasonable steps to avoid undue additional operational risk; and must not undertake the outsourcing of important operational functions in such a way as to impair materially: 

a) the quality of its internal control 

b) the ability of the regulator to monitor the firm’s compliance with all obligations under the regulatory system and, if different, of a competent authority to monitor the firm’s compliance with all obligations under UK MiFID. 

An operational function is regarded as critical or important if a defect or failure in its performance would materially impair the continuing compliance of a common platform firm with the conditions and obligations of its authorisation or its other obligations under the regulatory system, or its financial performance, or the soundness or the continuity of its relevant services and activities. 

One mark was awarded for each valid point made to a maximum of six marks. 

## **b) (6 marks) Outline the regulatory expectations when outsourcing administrative, regulatory and compliance activities of the firm.** 

When entering into an outsourcing agreement there are many expectations. Firms must ensure the service provider has the ability, capability, and appropriate authorisations to undertake the work reliably and professionally. This work needs to be supervised, monitored, and assessed to ensure it is undertaken to a sufficient standard. The firm needs to have staff who can undertake this supervision. If work of a sufficient quality is not provided it is the responsibility of the firm to act, such as by terminating the outsourcing contract. The firm is also reliant on the service provider to provide appropriate information to interested parties such as the regulator or auditor and to keep the firm informed of any problems or issues which arise. The service provider must also protect any confidential information relating to the firm and its clients. Lastly, the firm and the service provider must establish, implement, and maintain a contingency plan for the outsourced service. For different areas of business such as investment services there may be different and extended requirements which need to be fulfilled in relation to an outsourced service provider. 

One mark was awarded for each valid point made to a maximum of six marks. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **c) (4 marks)** 

## **Describe the risks the firm might face when undertaking this outsourcing strategy.** 

The outsourcing relationship creates a range of risks for the firm. While the firm is responsible for the actions of the service provider and needs to supervise these relationships there are issues if this relationship breaks down. For instance, the service provider may not follow policies and procedures as expected. For example, this could lead to a breach in confidentiality or insufficient information being provided to third parties. 

Even though the firm has outsourced activities, if a failure occurs in critical areas, then the firm will be held accountable. Critical in this process is ensuring there are robust internal control systems to ensure monitoring of outsourced work is undertaken appropriately. 

One mark was awarded for each valid point made to a maximum of four marks. As this is an open-ended question, other appropriate responses may be awarded marks. 

## **d) (4 marks)** 

## **On balance, is outsourcing a useful strategy for smaller financial services firms? Will this solution reduce the workload that you are currently facing?** 

On balance, outsourcing provides a useful solution to some of the demands facing smaller financial firms. Outsourcing may provide compliance functions with better access to information and lead to greater efficiency of operation. These outcomes can also be very helpful if the firm does not have an independent person available who is not involved in the provision of other services. Such independence is itself an important attribute of better compliance. 

While the outsourcing decision is a trade-off between the amount of work undertaken within the firm and outside by a third party, it should not be a transfer of responsibility or control. The responsibility and accountability remain within the firm. In general, firms which outsource activities remain responsible for the outsourced actions and are required to monitor and review the operations of any third party critically to ensure regulatory obligations are fulfilled. As outlined in the workbook: 

1. The outsourcing must not result in the delegation by senior personnel of their responsibility. 

2. The relationship and obligations of the firm towards its clients under the regulatory system must not be altered. 

3. The conditions with which the firm must comply in order to be authorised, and to remain so, must not be undermined. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

4. None of the other conditions subject to which the firm’s authorisation was granted must be removed or modified. 

One mark was awarded for each valid point made to a maximum of four marks. As this is an open-ended question other appropriate responses may be awarded marks. 

## **B99.** 

## **a) (4 marks)** 

The Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 (POCA), which defines money laundering and establishes offences for concealing, arranging, acquiring, using, and possessing criminal property. It 

mandates that firms report suspicions of money laundering, with failure to disclose constituting an offence. 

The Terrorism Act 2000 (TA), which criminalises the financing of terrorist activities and requires the reporting of suspicions. 

The Anti-Terrorism, Crime and Security Act 2001, which provides additional powers to combat terrorism and enforce anti-terrorism measures. 

The Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 (MLRs 2017), which require firms to conduct risk assessments, implement controls, and perform customer due diligence. 

The Serious Organised Crime and Police Act 2005 (SOCPA), which amended POCA to address activities legal in their origin country but illegal in the UK, 

clarifying reporting obligations and providing defences for unknowing involvement in such activities. 

Additionally, candidates could have mentioned: 

The FCA’s SYSC Sourcebook, which mandates that firms have systems and controls to manage money laundering risks, with senior management accountable for compliance and a Money Laundering Reporting Officer overseeing these activities. 

The FCA's Financial Crime Guide, which consolidates guidance on countering financial crime risks, offering practical examples for firms to reduce their financial crime risk and enhance compliance. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

Joint Money Laundering Steering Group (JMLSG) Guidance, which provides industry guidance on implementing anti-money laundering regulations, outlining best practices for risk management, customer due diligence, and reporting suspicious activities, serving as evidence of compliance for the FCA and courts. 

These laws and regulations ensure that UK financial services firms can prevent, detect, and report money laundering and terrorist financing activities to maintain the integrity of the financial system. 

## **b) (4 marks)** 

POCA establishes five offences, namely: 

**1. Concealing** – it is an offence for a person to conceal or disguise criminal property. 

**2. Arrangements** – it is an offence to be involved in an arrangement which the person knows, or suspects, facilitates the acquisition, retention, use or control of criminal property for another person. These arrangements may be widely interpreted and includes advising on a transaction. 

**3. Acquisition, use and possession** – acquiring, using or having possession of criminal property. 

These three offences are punishable by a jail term of up to 14 years and a fine. 

**4. Failure to disclose** – three conditions need to be satisfied for this offence: 

   - The person knows or suspects that another person is laundering money. 

   - The information giving rise to the knowledge or suspicion came to her/him whilst undertaking business in a regulated sector. 

   - The person does not make the required disclosure as soon as is practicable. 

The offence of failure to disclose suspicions of money laundering is committed not only when the person knows or suspects money laundering, but also when there are reasonable grounds to know or suspect money laundering: i.e. would a ‘reasonable person’ known or been suspicious? This offence is punishable by a jail term of up to five years and a fine. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

**5. Tipping off** – providing another person with information likely to prejudice a money laundering investigation. 

This offence is punishable by a jail term of up to two years and a fine. 

## **c) (8 marks)** 

Terrorist financing threatens the financial services system by using legitimate financial channels to move and store funds, often through small transactions that are difficult to detect, thereby compromising the integrity of these institutions. The exploitation of global financial networks by terrorists poses significant regulatory challenges and increases compliance costs for financial institutions, which face severe penalties for non-compliance. This undermines public trust in the financial system, leading to potential economic instability and loss of confidence among customers and investors. 

CDD is an acronym of customer due diligence – this term is related to the process through which a client is identified and checked to ensure they are who they say they are, and that there are no legal reasons preventing a business relationship being developed between the firm and the individual. In some situations, this process has developed into a risk-based assessment to determine any particular risks associated with a client and/or business relationship. For a corporate client it is important to undertake customer due diligence to determine the beneficial ownership of the corporation. 

EDD is an acronym of enhanced due diligence. In an EDD process a firm conducts additional checks and measures relating to an individual or beneficial owner deemed or assessed to be higher risk, and when the information collected in the CDD process is insufficient. This includes when there is a high risk of terrorist financing, where a transaction or business relationship is established in a high-risk third country; or any other circumstances where complex/large/unusual transactions occur without an obvious economic or legal purpose. As part of EDD measures, firms should examine the background or purpose of the transaction and increase the degree of client monitoring. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

SDD is an acronym of simplified due diligence – this arises in low-risk situations. For some organisations checks on public registers such as the FCA’s Register can be used in a SDD process. 

## **d) (4 marks)** 

Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs) are individuals who are or have been entrusted with a prominent public function at any time in the preceding year, excluding middle-ranking or junior officials. This definition includes heads of state, government ministers, senior judicial or military officials, senior executives of state-owned corporations, and important political party officials. When dealing with PEPs, their relatives, and close associates, financial institutions must exercise enhanced due diligence due to the heightened risk of involvement in bribery, corruption, and other illicit activities. 

Firms must implement robust risk management systems to identify whether a client or beneficial owner is a PEP, a family member, or a known close associate, requiring senior management approval to establish or continue business relationships with such individuals. 

It is essential to take adequate measures to establish the source of wealth and source of funds involved in business relationships or transactions with PEPs. This involves conducting enhanced ongoing monitoring to detect and report suspicious activities effectively. 

Given the potential for significant control or influence by PEPs, firms must adopt a proportional, risk-based approach, adjusting their due diligence measures based on the level of risk posed by each PEP. 

Domestic PEPs are generally treated as a lower risk than foreign PEPs. 

## **B100.** 

## **a) (4 marks)** 

The firm should examine any suspicious transactions on a case-by-case basis to determine if the transaction includes elements which may constitute market abuse. 

If there are reasonable grounds for suspecting a transaction involves market abuse, the firm must notify the FCA without delay. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

A further part of this process is knowledge of the client/customer and the business relationship the client has with the firm. This will help the firm understand what types of transactions the client should be reasonably and normally undertaking. This information may be used to develop a suspicious activity report (SAR) and/or suspicious activity and order report (STOR), which should include all relevant information about the individual(s) or entity believed to be acting suspiciously. Therefore, the key to knowing when to report suspicious activity is to have a good understanding of your customer- from understanding their business, building relationships with them, to understanding the activities and transactions that they will, and do, undertake. 

As UK MAR also applies to unexecuted orders and requests for quotes it is important to examine current transactions, past transactions, unexecuted orders and requests for quotes. 

## **b) (8 marks)** 

First it is helpful to acknowledge process to tackle market abuse varies according to the risk of the financial firms and business activity it undertakes, i.e. an investment bank will have more developed processes than a small financial firm. 

The scope of investigations includes both transactions and unexecuted orders and requests for quotes. Processes for tackling market abuse need to allow individual and/or comparative analysis of every transaction executed, and orders placed, modified, cancelled or rejected. This analysis should indicate which activities require further assessment to detect market manipulation or abuse. 

This analysis requires human involvement in the monitoring, detection and identification of transactions and orders that could constitute actual or attempted insider dealing and/or market manipulation. 

An important aspect of this process is to ensure staff are appropriately trained to undertake such monitoring, detection and identification of orders and transactions. The training needs to be ongoing. In cases where an employee may be involved in the market abuse the firm requires systems to manage any conflicts of interest. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

## **c) (4 marks)** 

The Market Abuse Regulation (MAR) is a key piece of legislation that aims to prevent market abuse and protect the integrity of financial markets. It covers various forms of market abuse, including insider dealing, unlawful disclosure of inside information, and market manipulation. MAR requires firms to implement measures to detect and report suspicious transactions and activities. 

The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (FSMA) provides the framework for the regulation of financial markets and services in the UK. It includes provisions to 

combat market abuse and empowers the FCA to investigate and take enforcement action against individuals and firms involved in market abuse. 

Part V of the Criminal Justice Act 1993 specifically addresses insider dealing. It makes it a criminal offence for individuals to deal in securities on the basis of inside information, or to encourage others to do so, and imposes severe penalties for breaches. 

The FCA Handbook, including the Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls (SYSC) Sourcebook, outlines requirements for firms to establish effective systems and controls to prevent market abuse. This includes having appropriate policies, procedures, and training in place to ensure compliance with market abuse regulations. 

## **d) (4 marks)** 

Market abuse undermines the integrity of financial markets. Ensuring fair and transparent market practices helps to build and maintain investor confidence, which is essential for the proper functioning of financial markets. Firms that actively work to prevent market abuse contribute to a stable and trustworthy market environment. 

Market abuse, such as insider trading and market manipulation, can lead to significant financial losses for investors. By preventing such practices, firms protect their clients from unfair practices and potential losses, fostering a sense of security and trust among their customer base. Addressing market abuse is essential for the long-term sustainability of financial markets. By promoting fair practices, firms help to create a level playing field, which is crucial for the long-term health and sustainability of the financial sector. This also contributes to the overall economic stability and growth. 

A firm’s reputation is one of its most valuable assets. Involvement in market abuse can severely damage a firm’s reputation, leading to a loss of client trust 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section B: Answers** 

and potential business. Firms that are seen to uphold high ethical standards and actively prevent market abuse are more likely to attract and retain clients and business partners. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

## _**Each Question carries 15 marks in total.**_ 

- **C1.** Explain the mechanisms through which the FCA is made accountable for its activities under the regulatory regime set up under the FSMA. 

- **C2.** The purposes of FSMA would be equally well achieved by abolishing the conduct of business rules and retaining only authorisation and compensation rules. Discuss. 

- **C3.** Outline the disciplinary powers of the FCA and comment on the policy behind the process, considering its fairness for firms and individuals as well as its perceived effectiveness, or otherwise, in the eyes of investors. 

- **C4.** There have been a number of examples of disciplinary action being taken against compliance officers. Consider the duties and obligations of a compliance officer. What practical steps must be taken by compliance officers to reduce their exposure to disciplinary action? 

- **C5.** The regulators are coming to grips with the development of the internet as a global financial services marketplace. In what way does the current regulatory regime apply to internet advertisements and how far are the regulators capable of enforcing the rules? Consider what other opportunities may arise for electronic delivery of financial services and how should the regulators be planning to protect investors? 

- **C6.** Compare and contrast a screen-based market making system with an open outcry market and discuss the merits and demerits of each from the point of view of the investor.  Also, consider the pros and cons of an order-driven as opposed to a quote-driven market for trading securities. 

- **C7.** Outline the main markets and trading systems on the London Stock Exchange, together with the main dealing and reporting requirements that apply to each. 

- **C8.** Identify the principal types of risk that financial intermediaries have to manage and the main requirements or standards that apply with regard to their control. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C9.** Explain the principal changes brought into effect under the Financial Services Act 2021 and comment on the extent to which they will improve financial regulation in the UK. 

- **C10.** Outline the key enforcement powers available to the FCA in dealing with financial or economic crime. 

- **C11.** Assess the extent to which the conflicting objectives of increased market competition and efficiency can be reconciled with the maintenance of high ethical standards in modern markets and proper financial regulation. 

- **C12.** Discuss the role of Threshold Conditions in the authorisation of firms by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA). How do these conditions ensure that only suitable firms are allowed to operate within the UK financial services market? 

- **C13.** Analyse the challenges financial institutions face in applying a risk-based approach to Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs). How do the FCA’s proposed amendments to FG17/6 help address these challenges, and what changes could firms make to enhance their PEP risk management frameworks? 

- **C14.** Outline the principal provisions that apply with regard to maintaining business continuity and operational resilience in the UK and comment on the effectiveness of these measures in securing financial stability, in particular, in the event of a major market disruption including military intervention. 

- **C15.** “The recent explosive growth of sovereign wealth fund investment presents no significant new regulatory obstacles for the FCA.” Discuss. 

- **C16.** In your role as compliance officer to an FCA authorised firm you are concerned that, in spite of your efforts to educate staff on the impact of Money Laundering Regulations, there is still some ignorance among staff. Prepare a memorandum to all staff giving practical guidance on when it is and is not necessary to verify the identity of UK institutional and corporate investors and how to do so. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C17.** “The effectiveness of the Financial Promotions regime has been achieved at the expense of unacceptable complexity.” Discuss. 

- **C18.** Discuss the extent to which remuneration and bonus packages raise ethical issues that have to be dealt with by regulated firms and their non-executive boards of directors. 

- **C19.** Assess the ability of the FCA to deal with the regulatory challenges presented by the recent substantial growth in the use of alternative investment management (AIM) facilities including hedge funds and private equity vehicles. 

- **C20.** Analyse how the UK’s Bribery Act 2010 mitigates the risk of bribery and outline the responsibilities of financial services firms under this legislation. Additionally, discuss the major challenges that global financial services organisations face in combating bribery. 

- **C21.** Assess the effectiveness of the range of enforcement powers available to the authorities in the UK commenting specifically on any changes introduced following the UK, European and international financial crises or shocks. 

- **C22.** Explain the role and function of the principal international regulatory committees that operate in the financial area and comment on their value and effectiveness. 

- **C23.** Discuss the extent to which financial ethics should be subject to further statutory and regulatory control, beyond the protections already provided for under the FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance and the common law. 

- **C24.** Comment on the extent to which the FCA has been able to respond to the challenges created by the emergence of electronic commerce and electronic money activities in recent years, both in terms of allowing innovation but constraining risk and exposure. 

- **C25.** Comment on the extent to which an appropriate regulatory balance has been constructed in providing for appropriate incentives and rewards for key personnel in the financial services sector, and with maintaining high ethical standards. 

- **C26.** Comment on the value and effectiveness of the strengthened enforcement policy adopted by the FCA in recent years and the extent to which this has improved behaviour in markets or simply generated additional penalty fines income for the FCA. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C27.** Assess the extent to which financial firms have to consider the effects of possible climate risk or other extreme events in maintaining appropriate ethical and cultural standards and frameworks. 

- **C28.** Discuss the principal options that may be available for UK financial firms to allow them to continue providing regulated activities across Europe following the withdrawal of the United Kingdom from the European Union in 2020 and comment briefly on their relative desirability. 

- **C29.** Explain the nature and function of the Recovery and Resolution Plans (RRPs) that financial firms are required to maintain in the event of future market instability or crisis. 

- **C30.** Critically evaluate the role of organisational culture in influencing conduct within financial institutions. How does the FCA’s approach to culture and governance aim to enhance market integrity, and what steps can firms take to align their culture with regulatory expectations? 

- **C31.** Outline the principal provisions that apply with regard to data protection in the UK and comment on the effectiveness of these measures in securing privacy rights. 

- **C32.** Assess the extent to which the regulatory regime brought into effect under the Financial Services Act 2012 assists in preventing future financial crises in the UK, or only imposes disproportionate new regulatory obligation and cost compared to the previous regime. 

- **C33.** Discuss the extent to which financial firms must consider the impact of future exogenous (or external) shocks in designing or reviewing their internal ethical and cultural frameworks, with specific focus on major geopolitical events, such as the Russian entry into Ukraine in February 2022. 

- **C34.** Comment on the effectiveness of the regime set up under the FSMA (as amended) to prevent or contain any abuse of power or procedure by the regulatory authorities. 

- **C35.** Assess the effectiveness of the macro-prudential oversight regime set up under the Financial Services Act 2012, commenting specifically on the role and function of the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) within the Bank of England. 

- **C36.** What conditions are required for an effective and transparent system of compliance within a financial firm and how should senior management encourage this? 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C37.** Assess the extent to which recent financial reforms at the national, European or international levels have been able to secure an appropriate balance between financial regulation and market function or have only imposed unnecessary burdens and costs to the ultimate disadvantage of consumers and other recipients of financial services. 

- **C38.** Comment on the need for the establishment of separate financial conduct and prudential regulators and the relative effectiveness of the UK institutional regulatory framework set up under the Financial Services Act 2012. 

- **C39.** Comment on the need for and extent to which high ethical standards can be promoted by the Banking Standards Review Council (BSRC), as recommended by Sir Richard Lambert in his review of banking standards, in the City of London. 

- **C40.** Discuss how financial regulation in the USA is organised compared with the UK and the relative merits of these systems. 

- **C41.** Discuss the extent to which the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SMCR) assists to ensure that key personnel within financial firms carry out their functions responsibly without any threat to the interests of their clients or the financial stability of the firms concerned. 

- **C42.** Explain the nature of the FCA’s approach to conduct risk and comment on the extent to which it has been able to create a clear and meaningful regulatory framework to contain this over time. 

- **C43.** Assess the extent to which financial firms have to consider the effects of possible biological pandemics and other extreme events that may impact financial markets in reassessing ethical and cultural frameworks following the Global Financial Crisis and Coronavirus Crisis. 

- **C44.** Assess the effectiveness of the revised provisions on misleading statements and impressions introduced under the Financial Services Act 2012 and comment on their significance as part of the larger UK statutory regime to control market abuse and manipulation. 

- **C45.** What are the major themes within the FCA’s Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) strategy? Why do these themes exist and are they important for financial regulation? 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C46.** Explain the nature of financial technology (Fintech) and comment on the extent to which regulatory authorities can manage additional risks and exposures that may arise. 

- **C47.** Explain the current landscape for regulating crypto assets in the UK, including any consultations to date and proposed regulation in the future. In your answer, refer to the management of risks that arise from the trade in crypto assets. 

- **C48.** Outline the general purpose and content of the Capital Requirements Directive (CRD) IV package of measures and comment on their possible effectiveness in preventing future financial instability and crisis within the UK and Europe. 

- **C49.** Comment on the extent to which a separate Rulebook for the PRA and Handbook for the FCA increases or decreases the effectiveness of financial regulation and supervision in the UK. 

- **C50.** Comment on the extent to which the UK regulatory and supervisory framework and measures adopted following the global financial crisis are capable of managing the additional risks and exposures that may arise with regard to external or exogenous shocks such as a biological pandemic or natural disaster. 

- **C51.** Assess the effectiveness of the new joint Complaints Scheme set up under FSMA 2000 (as amended) to allow parties to make complaints against the conduct of the regulatory authorities. 

- **C52.** Discuss the extent to which the promotion of financial ethics within regulated firms has been impacted by crisis events such as the recent Coronavirus (Covid 19) pandemic in the UK and elsewhere. 

- **C53.** Discuss the general purpose and content of the MiFID II and associated regulation (MiFIR) measures and comment on their possible value in improving the efficiency and stability of securities and capital markets, both in the UK and across Europe. 

- **C54.** Assess the effectiveness of the recent changes announced in connection with the strengthening of capital and liquidity standards in national and international markets, in maintaining financial stability. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C55.** Comment on the effectiveness of the work programme of the Banking Standards Board (BSB) to increasing ethical standards in the banking and financial area within the UK. 

- **C56.** Explain the principal changes brought into effect under the Bank of England and Financial Services Act 2016 commenting on their need and the extent to which they may improve financial regulation in the UK. 

- **C57.** Recent policy announcements have indicated that ring-fencing for UK-based banking may be phased out. Explain what the ‘ring fence’ is in a prudential regulatory sense and why this measure was introduced. Discuss the implications of the removal of ring-fencing. 

- **C58.** Is a fine just the price of doing business for many firms? Discuss the cost of noncompliance for financial firms. 

- **C59.** Explain the function of the Takeover Panel. Discuss the importance of the Panel’s role from a financial regulation perspective. 

- **C60.** Discuss what is meant by the term ‘a fit and proper person’ in the context of financial regulation and how a firm should assess employees and potential employees. (In your answer reference for example SMCR, APER and COCON as appropriate). 

- **C61.** “The regulatory regime established under the FSMA is, to a significant extent, based on internal management accountability and liability rather than external supervision and sanction.”  Discuss. 

- **C62.** Evaluate the role of Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) enforcement actions in deterring financial misconduct and maintaining market integrity. Have fines and regulatory interventions been effective in changing corporate behaviour? Are there areas where enforcement could be improved? 

- **C63.** As Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) concerns continue to shape financial markets, UK regulators are increasing their focus on ESG. Discuss how financial firms are being impacted by ESG regulations and whether the current regulatory framework is effective in ensuring transparency, accountability, and long-term sustainability. What challenges do firms face in implementing ESG policies, and how might regulation evolve in the future? 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

## **Answers** 

**C1.** The accountability of the FCA is secured through a combination of the following: 

- HM Treasury appoints the Chairman and governing body of the FCA. 

- HMT receives a formal annual report from the FCA. 

- HMT reviews of the economy, efficiency and effectiveness of discharge of the FCA’s functions. 

- HMT inquiries into defined events (systems threat or listing failure) with power to appoint, obtain information and report. 

- Internal corporate governance with non-executive directors and effective internal delegation, monitoring, complaints and record systems. The FCA is expected to meet generally applicable standards of corporate governance such as the UK Corporate Governance Code to the extent that it is applicable. 

- Annual public meeting and meeting report. 

- Internal complaints arrangements. 

- Duty to establish and to consult various panels (FCA Practitioner, Smaller Business Practitioner, Markets Practitioner and Consumer) and to consider the panels’ opinions. 

- Appeal against FCA decisions is possible to the Tribunal. 

- Use of policy statements, discussion papers and consultation papers. 

- Judicial review under general law or misfeasance in public office. 

Reference should also have been made to the FCA’s statutory objectives and to the principles of good regulation. 

- **C2.** The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000, as it was originally enacted, was in essence a piece of consumer protection legislation aimed principally at protecting the private investor. It does this by requiring that all persons wishing to engage in regulated activities as defined by the Act are authorised to do so and that, once authorised, they are obliged to follow certain conduct of business rules, which concern their dealings with customers. If an authorised firm goes out of business owing its customers money which cannot be recovered through the insolvency laws, there is a compensation scheme set up by the FCA that will compensate investors (up to certain limits). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The requirement to become authorised has certainly been effective over the years in putting out of business those firms that do not satisfy the threshold conditions (currently to be found at FSMA, Sch. 6, and COND). This is obviously a very fundamental protection for investors. Any unauthorised regulated activity (such as unauthorised investment business) is a criminal offence, and the perpetrator is liable to a maximum two-year jail sentence and a fine. In addition, any contracts entered into are unenforceable against the customer. 

Whilst this is obviously very important, it is not good enough for a firm to demonstrate that it was once able to pass a threshold (‘fit and proper’) test. It is important that it continues to behave itself correctly and, as an adjunct to this, it is liable to punishment under the FCA disciplinary rules if it fails to achieve the required standard in the conduct of its business with customers. To this end, the FCA has established 11 Principles for Businesses (PRIN), other High Level Standards (SYSC, etc) and extensive, detailed Conduct of Business (COBS) Rules. 

In addition to this, a firm is expected to reach a minimum standard of financial security. This is achieved through the capital adequacy (financial resources) rules (PRU), which ensure that all firms meet certain minimum capital requirements depending on the nature of their activities. Furthermore, the client money (and other assets) rules (CASS) require a firm to keep its clients’ money separate from that of the firm at all times. 

If, in spite of this, the firm becomes insolvent owing customers money (whether through management incompetence or fraudulent practice, or for some other reason), then the Financial Services Compensation Scheme comes into play for eligible claimants (as defined). This allows for payment to eligible claimants of (at least part of) their otherwise unrecoverable losses. 

There are those who think that the costs of compliance are excessive and that compensation scheme payouts are inadequate. In consequence, it could be argued that it would be better to do away the conduct of business rules entirely and pump a proportion of the costs saved into a better compensation scheme. 

What this argument misses, though, is the fact that a payout from the compensation scheme is only available when a firm has become insolvent and not when a customer is dissatisfied 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

with a firm for any other reason. The compensation scheme does not protect investors who have suffered from mismanagement or poor advice at the hands of a firm. 

Institutional investors may need only limited protection, but private investors would be likely to lose confidence in the integrity of the markets if the conduct of business rules 

were abolished. The consequence could be that fewer retail investors would wish to enter the market if there was no formal regulatory control over the way in which transactions were conducted. 

## **C3.  Disciplinary powers of the FCA** 

In the event of a breach of the rules by an authorised firm or approved person, the following powers are available to the FCA: 

- Private warning or public statement of misconduct 

- Appropriate financial penalties 

- Issue of restitution order 

- Withdrawal of authorisation or approval. 

In cases of market abuse the FCA’s powers extend more widely to catch any market user (including, for example, retail investors). 

Any penalty has to be preceded by a **warning notice** , which sets out the terms of any public statement or the amount of any financial penalty. Thereafter, any decision to impose a penalty will be accompanied by a **decision notice** . 

## **Restraints on the powers of the FCA** 

The FCA’s powers over authorised firms and approved persons are subject to the following restraints: 

- The Upper Tribunal (Tax and Chancery Chamber) is available for those who wish to appeal against a decision by the FCA. 

- The FCA is required to publish a statement of its policy on financial penalties; and a copy of this statement must be provided to the Treasury. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

## **Fairness and effectiveness** 

In devising an effective disciplinary process, the FCA must seek to balance the need to be firm but fair towards authorised persons with the obligation to provide adequate protection for investors. 

One basic element of fairness is the opportunity for those disciplined by the FCA to appeal if they are dissatisfied with a penalty. 

As regards effectiveness, the FCA operates on the basis of civil standards of evidence (a case has to be proved “on the balance of probability”) rather than criminal standards (“beyond all reasonable doubt”). This means that allegations of misconduct can be dealt with more quickly and efficiently than by recourse to the criminal courts (where the State has shown a reluctance to prosecute in certain areas – for example, insider dealing or market rigging – because of the difficulty of obtaining a conviction). 

Unlike the courts, however, there is no obligation on the FCA to “follow precedent”, which can give rise to concerns about inconsistency in the FCA’s decisions. 

## **Conclusion** 

The imposition of large fines imposed by the FCA on major financial institutions is likely to lead ultimately to a much more expensive marketplace in which investors buy products and services. The target is for the FCA to wield its disciplinary powers in an efficient manner in order to discourage wrongdoing by firms; but it must also be cost-effective in order to avoid running up costs that will ultimately be borne by consumers. 

## **C4. Duties and obligations of compliance officers** 

The FCA’s High Level Standards require that the firm’s management appoint a director or senior manager to take responsibility for: 

- Oversight of the firm’s compliance 

- Reporting to the firm’s governing body on compliance. 

The term ‘compliance’, which is central to the whole regulatory structure, is nowhere formally defined. It is generally understood to mean the institution and enforcement of procedures which are designed to enable a firm to comply with its regulatory obligations. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Although the titular compliance officer within a firm has the task of establishing and monitoring systems of compliance, the prime responsibility for ensuring compliance with all the relevant regulatory requirements rests with the directors (or partners) of the firm, and in particular with the chief executive. 

In order to fulfil their responsibilities, the compliance officer must ensure that the compliance function is so **organised** that: 

- The structure of the compliance function and the lines of responsibility are clearly set out in writing. 

- Compliance officers have access to, and are demonstrably supported by, the chief executive; and the senior compliance officer has access to any audit committee of the board and to the non-executive directors. 

- Compliance officers are in a position to be independent and objective. 

- Adequate resources (of staff, skills, computer equipment and training) are available for compliance work. 

- Compliance officers have access to all records and paperwork, however confidential, within the firm. 

- Compliance officers have appropriate status, authority and personality. 

The compliance officer must ensure that the **range** of his activities includes: 

- Adequate staff training 

- Well defined operating procedures and maintenance of written records 

- Appropriate in-house rules (about personal dealing, confidential information, conflicts of interest, etc) 

- Efficient in-house advisory services 

- Regular monitoring of conduct of business 

- Correct handling of customer complaints 

- Full and timely disclosure to the FCA of routine returns and ad hoc requests for information. 

## **Practical steps** 

The following steps will assist the compliance officer in ensuring that his objectives are fulfilled: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- Identify where the firm lacks a culture of compliance; highlight these areas to senior management and, on implementation of improvements, assist the staff in understanding and adapting to the changes. 

- Ensure that a clear training programme is established, with the objective of ensuring that everyone understands his or her obligations and the costs of non-compliance. 

- Ensure that the compliance manual sets down clear policies on, for example: Chinese walls, money laundering, independence, inducements and complaints procedures. 

- Highlight the key areas of risk to the business and use this analysis for monitoring purposes. 

- Remember the role of independence: this will at times involve reporting certain matters to senior management and, on occasion, to the FCA. 

**C5.** Any material circulating on the internet or otherwise which constitutes a financial promotion (as defined by FSMA) may only be issued or approved for issue in the UK by an authorised person. A financial promotion (e.g. an investment advertisement) on a firm’s internet site, even if entered on the site from an overseas branch, will be considered to have been issued to persons in the UK if it is accessible via a computer screen in the UK. 

Under the Financial Promotions Order, communications via the internet are regarded as nonreal time communications. Therefore, firms must ensure that they comply with the detailed COBS requirements regarding clarity, fairness and expertise. 

Firms must, for example, ensure that: 

- The promotional purpose is not disguised or misrepresented. 

- The facts on which comparisons are made are verified. 

- The accuracy of all material statements of fact can be substantiated. 

The detailed rules on advertisement contents do not apply to financial promotions circulated abroad which clearly state that the advertisement (and the investment product or service it advertises) is directed only at persons abroad. However, unless a firm can restrict access to its website (e.g., by issuing passwords) such that only non-UK investors can have access, the advertisement will have to comply fully with the detailed rules 

. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

## **Enforcement** 

In determining whether a regulatory issue arises the FCA must consider the following: 

- The extent to which UK investors can access the site 

- Whether the site contains appropriate disclaimers and warnings 

- Whether the content of the site makes it clear (if that is the case) that it is not directed at UK investors 

- Whether the site has been notified as a UK site to search engines 

- Whether the existence of the site has been advertised in the UK. 

The FCA still has to deal with the difficulty of policing extra-territorial business where the investment company is not UK-based. Here, the only practical way forward is to secure the co-operation of any relevant overseas regulatory authority. 

## **Further opportunities** 

There is no doubt that the electronic delivery of financial services will expand and diversify rapidly. With this will come increased use of the medium for document delivery (with attendant risks for security) and the gradual removal of humans from the advisory and execution-only share dealing business (possibly even discretionary transactions). 

In view of such significant future developments the FCA needs to consider their implications seriously. The FCA needs to ensure that it has the framework of regulation, the resources and the expertise to be able to monitor software as it is developed. In addition, there has never been a stronger need for international co-operation between 

regulators: one possible development might be a ‘kitemark’ issued by each national regulator to identify and endorse those investment products which it considers to meet basic minimum international standards. 

Above all, however, there is an overriding need to educate the public in how to recognise and select appropriate products and avoid the rogues. Such public education should be a central part of the FCA’s overall programme to promote open regulation. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

## **C6.** 

- a) Screen-based vs. open outcry trading 

## **Introduction** 

Of the London securities and derivatives markets, the LSE, LIFFE and ISDX all now use screen-based trading. Certain other international exchanges (particularly commodity exchanges) still make some use of open outcry trading. 

## **The case for open outcry trading** 

1. A pit trade can be done instantly; this means that customer orders can be completed in a timely and efficient manner. 

2. It is argued that prices are determined more efficiently, since all market participants are in physical proximity to one another. However, retail customers may feel that this method of price determination is too esoteric. 

3. In view of the speed at which transactions can be executed, there is increased liquidity in the market. 

4. There is clear transparency about the prices at which deals are done. 

5. No technological risk. 

## **The case for screen-based trading** 

1. Participants do not need to be in physical proximity; thus, anyone can participate irrespective of their geographical location. 

2. Less physical infrastructure is required – there is no need for a trading floor, and this may reduce costs. 

3. There are no restrictions on the number of participants or on the market hours. 

4. The above factors must be balanced against the risk of catastrophe in the event of: {i) loss of telephone or electronic systems; or (ii) unauthorised access to the system by a firm’s employee entering (accidentally or by design) ‘rogue’ transactions, which could lead to either massive losses or the collapse of the system. 

b) Order-driven vs. quote-driven trading 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

## **Introduction** 

In London, in general, the most liquid stocks are dealt with on an order-driven system (SETS) while less liquid stocks are dealt on a combined (hybrid) order-driven and quotedriven system (SETSqx). 

## **The case for order-driven trading** 

1. Participants can clearly see the price at which deals are done, thus assisting transparency. However, this is tempered by the fact that not all deals in SETS stocks are done via SETS. 

2. The non-involvement of market makers cuts out the middleman and this results in reduced dealing costs, which can be passed on to the customer. 

3. The minimum order size is one share, which ensures that deals in any size can be done at minimal cost without the need to phone round for prices, etc. 

4. The system is the only practical system for illiquid securities in which market makers are not prepared to make firm two-way prices. 

## **The case for quote-driven trading** 

   1. Market makers are obliged to quote firm two-way prices during the mandatory period (8.00am to 4.30pm). This helps to ensure liquidity. 

   2. The publication of market makers’ prices enables all market participants to determine the current prices of securities at all times. 

   3. Competitiveness is encouraged, particularly in those stocks that are the most liquid. Thus, spreads are narrowed, minimising costs for the investor. 

   4. Where deals are not done via a market maker, the firm must improve on the best market makers’ prices. Again, this can only operate to the advantage of the investor. 

- **C7.** The principal markets making up the LSE should be noted, including the main market systems and AIM. The nature and operation of each of the main LSE trading systems should have been referred to. 

Separate reference should have been made to trade reporting, transaction reporting and the main alternative markets such as Aquis [formerly ISDX], as well as any Multilateral Trading Facilities (MTFs) or other systems available. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

No specific answer was required, only command of the main markets, regulatory issues and trading systems in place. 

- **C8.** The risk question was only attempted by a small number of candidates. The objective was simply to test candidate’s ability to identify the main types of risk that arise and how these are managed in practice. 

The main financial risks include credit risk, market or position risk, interest rate risk, foreign exchange risk and settlement or delivery risk. The other main risks that should have been referred to include legal risk as well as operational risk. Reference may also have been made to other management or business-related risks such as reputational, strategic or management risks. 

The relevant requirements or standards that apply could either have been explained in terms of the general regulatory regime set up under the FSMA or the more specific compliance systems set up within financial institutions. Considerable flexibility was allowed in dealing with this general question. 

**C9.** The Financial Services Act 2021 received Royal Assent on 29 April 2021. This enables the introduction by the FCA of a dedicated or tailored Investment Firms Prudential Regime (IFPR) due to the differences that arise between banks and investment firms, and the effects of this on prudential regulation. Investment firms do not accept deposits or grant traditional loans but provide investment services and carry out investment activities. Regulation can, therefore, be disproportionate, inappropriate and impose unnecessary administrative and compliance burdens in many cases. 

The IFPR will apply to investment firms prudentially regulated by the FCA and their holding companies, with systemically important designated investment firms being subject to PRA oversight and CRD 4. This will allow the UK to maintain a more effective prudential regime for systemic and non-systemic investment firms. The IFPR will principally be established through FCA rules which will set firm level requirements. Specific areas to be covered are set out in the Act including capital, liquidity, exposure to concentration risk, reporting, public disclosure, governance arrangements and remuneration policies. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

A separate enhanced accountability framework is set up for the FCA to reflect its additional responsibilities in this area. Specific important public policy considerations are to be taken into account including: relevant international standards, the relative standing of the UK as a place for internationally active investment firms to carry on activities, the net zero emissions target set under the Climate Change Act 2008; financial services equivalence and any other matters set out in the HM Treasury regulations. 

The Act separately enables HM Treasury and the UK regulators to implement remaining Basel 3.5 implementation or the additional ‘finalisation’ standards agreed in 2017 to the extent not already given effect to with a parallel accountability framework set up for the PRA regarding these new powers. Further powers are conferred in relation to LIBOR transition arrangements and outstanding contracts. 

This follows a recommendation for the adoption of a legislative solution by the Working Group on Sterling Risk-Free Rates in the ‘Tough Legacy Report’ (Working Group on Sterling Risk-Free Reference Rates, ‘Paper on the Identification of Tough Legacy Issues’ (May 2020)). 

The Chancellor of the Exchequer confirmed in a written statement to Parliament in June 2020 that necessary amendments would be made to the EU Benchmarks Regulation (BMR) (Chancellor of the Exchequer, ‘Financial Services Regulation’, 23 June 2020). The FCA is given additional powers to manage an orderly wind-down of critical benchmarks, such as LIBOR, following a formal representativeness assessment and to prohibit supervised entities from using a specific benchmark. A new permanent market access regime (referred to as the Gibraltar Authorisation Regime (GAR)) was established to allow reciprocal market access between the UK and Gibraltar. A new Overseas Funds Regime (OFR) was set up to allow overseas collective investment schemes (CISs) to be marketed to UK investors, including retail investors, retail CISs and money market funds. The UK’s MiFIR equivalence regime for third country investment firms has been amended. Technical revisions were made to the onshored European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR) including to improve clearing services access. The Act contains other miscellaneous reforms including in relation to the Variation or Cancellation of Part 4A FSMA permissions, Consultation on the Introduction of a Duty of Care on Authorised Firms, Money Laundering, Help to Save, Buy-Now-Pay-Later Products, as well as financial collateral payment services and cash provision. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C10.** The question was concerned with the enforcement powers available to the FCA specifically in connection with financial and economic crime. In the best answers, candidates referred to both the general FCA powers of enforcement as well as the specific offences and remedies available in the financial and economic area. 

Specific FCA enforcement powers include: 

- **(i)** Fines; 

- **(ii)** Private or public censure; 

- **(iii)** Injunction; 

- **(iv)** Varying or withdrawing approval; 

- **(v)** Varying or cancelling permission; 

- **(vi)** Information gathering; 

- **(vii)** Investigations; 

- **(viii)** Reports by skilled persons; 

- **(ix)** Restitution; 

- **(i)** Bankruptcy or insolvency. 

Candidates could also then have referred to the particular statutory remedies available to 

the FCA in connection with: 

- **(i)** Market Abuse (MAR); 

- **(ii)** Market Manipulation (Part 7 FSA 2012); 

- **(iii)** Insider Trading (CJA); 

- **(iv)** Money Laundering (POCA and SOCPA, ML Regulations, SYSC and JMLSG); and 

- **(v)** Counter terrorism (CTA). 

Candidates could also have referred to the more aggressive ‘proactive’ and ‘credible deterrence’ approach to enforcement adopted by the FCA following the financial crisis. 

- **C11.** Candidates were asked to consider the relationship between competition and efficiency with the maintenance of high ethical standards. This could have been answered in a number of ways. The simplest may have been to have referred to the regulators’ basic statutory objectives and the supporting supervisory principles in FSMA. Competition and efficiency are specifically included within the statutory regulatory framework set up under the FSMA. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Competition is specifically dealt with regard to international character, distortion and intrafirm competition. 

The regulatory objectives identified should then have been contrasted with the need to maintain high ethical standards at all times. The FSA October 2002 Discussion Paper 18 on Ethics may have been referred to. Of more importance was the need to refer to the structure and content of the high-level standards imposed on all regulated institutions under Block 1 of the FCA Handbook. More specific reference may have then been made to particular sections within Block 1 to the extent that time was available. This specifically includes PRIN, COCON and SYSC as well as FIT and COND. Reference should also have been made to other supporting codes of standards such as the Code of Conduct issued by the CISI. 

**C12.** This question asked candidates to discuss the role of Threshold Conditions in the authorisation of firms by the FCA and PRA; and to evaluate how these conditions ensure that only suitable firms are allowed to operate within the UK financial services market. This was an open-ended question in which a lot of material might be included. The first part of the response should consist of a discussion around the Threshold Conditions. The response should then address how, and to what extent, the Threshold Conditions ensure that only suitable firms are allowed to operate within the UK financial services market. Reference should have been made to the six revised General Principles and to the takeover timetable. 

The Threshold Conditions (COND) set out the fundamental regulatory requirements firms must meet to gain and maintain authorisation from the FCA or PRA to operate in the UK financial services market. These conditions are designed to ensure that firms are suitable, financially sound, and capable of operating in a way that supports the stability and integrity of the financial system, while protecting consumers. 

The Threshold Conditions form a gateway for firms seeking to operate within the UK's financial market. They set minimum standards in several key areas: 

• **Location of Offices** (COND 2.2): Firms must have their head office and principal place of business in the UK if they carry on regulated activities in the UK. This ensures that regulators can exercise effective oversight. For a firm to be supervised adequately, it must be within reach of UK authorities, allowing them to monitor and enforce compliance easily. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

• **Effective Supervision** (COND 2.3): Firms must be capable of being effectively supervised by the FCA or PRA. Firms must have systems in place that enable regulatory bodies to assess their compliance with the rules and respond promptly to any supervisory requests. This helps ensure transparency and cooperation between the firm and regulators, mitigating risks posed by inadequate oversight. 

• **Appropriate Resources** (COND 2.4): Firms must have sufficient financial and nonfinancial resources to run their operations safely and effectively. This includes capital adequacy, systems, control functions, and competent personnel. By ensuring firms have the necessary resources to meet obligations, this condition helps prevent firms from becoming financially distressed, reducing risks to customers and the market as a whole. 

• **Suitability** (COND 2.5): The firm and its senior management must be ‘fit and proper’, which includes having the necessary honesty, integrity, competence, and regulatory compliance history. This condition is designed to minimise the risk of misconduct and ensure that those running financial institutions are capable of managing the firm in a compliant and ethical manner. 

• **Business Model** (COND 2.7): The firm must demonstrate that its business model is suitable for the regulated activities it seeks to carry out, and that it does not pose a risk to the FCA’s objectives, which include protecting consumers and ensuring market integrity. The PRA will focus on whether the business model is sustainable in terms of financial soundness, particularly for firms like banks and insurers, where stability is crucial. 

These conditions collectively seek to ensure that firms entering the UK market have the necessary foundations to operate in a manner that supports market stability, minimises risk, and protects consumers. While the Threshold Conditions provide a strong initial barrier to entry for unsuitable firms, there are questions about whether they fully ensure that only suitable firms operate in the market. One potential issue is that the conditions focus heavily on firms at the point of authorisation. While firms must continue to meet these conditions on an ongoing basis, enforcement tends to rely on the regulators identifying issues through supervisory processes. In some cases, firms may deteriorate after gaining authorisation, without immediate intervention from regulators, creating a window of risk. 

Moreover, COND 2.3 (Effective Supervision) and COND 2.4 (Appropriate Resources) are somewhat subjective, and their interpretation can vary depending on the complexity of a firm's business model. For example, in fintech, where technology changes rapidly, ensuring effective supervision and that resources are ‘appropriate’ may be challenging due to the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

evolving nature of risks in areas such as cybersecurity and data privacy. Regulators may find it difficult to assess or respond to these risks quickly enough, potentially allowing unsuitable firms to slip through the cracks. 

Additionally, while the Suitability (COND 2.5) condition aims to ensure that firms are managed by individuals with the necessary integrity and competence, past failures in governance, such as the collapses of financial institutions like BCCI or scandals at firms like Enron, suggest that even stringent fit-and-proper tests are not always foolproof. Dishonest or incompetent behaviour may not be apparent until a firm has been operational for some time, leading to consumer harm or market disruption. 

The regulators have taken steps to address some of these issues by placing an emphasis on continuous monitoring and supervision as well as requiring firms to update regulators on significant changes that could affect their ongoing compliance with the Threshold Conditions. Additionally, SM&CR has been introduced to complement the Threshold Conditions by assigning personal accountability to senior managers, thus reinforcing individual responsibility for meeting regulatory standards. 

However, there remains a question as to whether the current level of scrutiny and ongoing supervision is sufficient, particularly for firms in fast-moving sectors such as fintech. Stresstesting of business models and more dynamic risk assessments could be further developed to ensure that firms remain suitable as they grow or as market conditions change. While the Threshold Conditions serve as a gateway for ensuring firm suitability, they are not perfect. Firms that pass the initial authorisation may still pose risks over time if ongoing supervision is not sufficiently proactive. Continuous improvement in regulatory monitoring, particularly in sectors with rapid innovation, is essential to ensure that the Threshold Conditions fulfil their purpose in the long term. 

- **C13.** This question asked candidates to analyse the challenges that financial institutions face in applying a risk-based approach to Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs) and to give their opinion on how the FCA’s proposed amendments to FG17/6 help address these challenges, as well as the changes that firms could make to enhance their PEP risk management frameworks. This was an open-ended question in which a lot of material could be included. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Financial institutions face significant challenges in applying a risk-based approach to Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs). These challenges stem from the inherent risks associated with PEPs, the complexity of accurately assessing those risks, and the need to balance regulatory requirements with practical business considerations. The FCA’s proposed amendments to FG17/6 aim to address some of these challenges by providing clearer guidance on the proportionality of risk management, particularly concerning domestic PEPs. However, there are additional measures that firms can implement to further enhance their PEP risk management frameworks. 

One of the primary challenges is accurately identifying PEPs, including their close associates and family members. PEP status can change frequently, and maintaining up-todate information is resource-intensive. Moreover, distinguishing between domestic and foreign PEPs, and applying the appropriate level of scrutiny, adds another layer of complexity. The risk associated with PEPs varies widely depending on factors such as the individual’s role, the country of origin, and the nature of their transactions. Institutions must assess the risk on a case-by-case basis, which requires a deep understanding of the political and economic environment in which the PEP operates. This assessment can be subjective and may lead to inconsistencies in how PEPs are treated across the institution. Enhanced due diligence (EDD) is required for PEPs, but applying EDD uniformly to all PEPs can be overly burdensome and may not be proportionate to the actual risk. The challenge lies in balancing the need for thorough scrutiny with the principle of proportionality, ensuring that resources are focused on higher-risk PEPs without unnecessarily disrupting business operations or client relationships. 

The FCA’s proposed amendments to FG17/6 provide clearer guidance on the treatment of PEPs, particularly domestic PEPs, and emphasise a more proportionate risk-based approach. The amendments suggest that not all PEPs should be treated as high risk by default, especially domestic PEPs who may hold positions with limited or no influence over financial matters. This change helps institutions apply a more nuanced approach, reducing the burden on firms and allowing them to focus their resources on higher-risk PEPs. The amendments also encourage firms to consider the specific circumstances of each PEP, such as their role, the nature of their transactions, and the potential risk they pose. This guidance aligns with the broader principles of a risk-based approach, allowing firms to tailor their due diligence processes to the actual level of risk presented by each PEP. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

To improve the identification and monitoring of PEPs, firms should invest in advanced screening technologies that utilise artificial intelligence and machine learning. These tools can help automate the identification of PEPs, track changes in their status, and provide real-time alerts for any potential risks. Firms could implement a process for regularly updating their risk assessments for PEPs. This includes staying informed about changes in the political landscape, understanding the evolving risks associated with different regions, and adjusting the level of due diligence accordingly. Ensuring that staff across the institution are well-trained in identifying and managing PEP risks is crucial. Regular training sessions could be conducted to keep employees up-to-date on the latest regulatory requirements and best practices for PEP risk management. Firms should also establish clear internal guidelines that differentiate between domestic and foreign PEPs, with a focus on applying proportionality in their risk-based approach. These guidelines should be regularly reviewed and updated to reflect the latest FCA guidance and the firm’s own risk appetite. Robust internal controls and governance structures are essential for effective PEP risk management. Firms should ensure that there is a clear line of accountability for PEPrelated decisions and that senior management is actively involved in overseeing PEP risk management processes. 

While the FCA’s proposed amendments to FG17/6 offer valuable direction for managing PEP risks, it’s essential to strike a balance between prescriptive requirements and a holistic understanding of risk. Overly rigid approaches can lead to inefficiencies, while a more flexible, risk-based approach allows firms to focus on genuinely high-risk scenarios. Less prescriptive measures can empower institutions to use their judgement, leading to more effective and nuanced risk management. Ultimately, less may be more when it comes to regulatory guidance, enabling firms to better tailor their responses to the specific risks they face. 

- **C14.** Candidates were asked to assess the extent to which financial firms within the UK may be able to manage and contain future global market shocks and maintain the stability of the UK financial system or further measures and what revisions are required. This may include some form of military action or disruption. Firms have to ensure that they have the necessary systems, controls as well as other continuity and business disruption arrangements and programmes.
