The COVID-19 crisis of 2020-2021 highlighted the need for firms to be able to withstand any form of shock. Many of the same requirements and systems’ conditions will apply. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Firms must ensure that they can operate on a continuing basis in the event of any major market or social disruption arising. This is concerned with maintaining appropriate systems and controls with business continuity dealt with under SYSC 3.2.19 and operational risk dealt with under SYSC 13 including external events and other changes in SYSC 13.8. Firms should have in place appropriate arrangements, having regard to the nature, scale and complexity of the business, to ensure that they can continue to function and meet the regulatory obligations in the event of unforeseen interruption with arrangements being regularly updated and tested to ensure effectiveness (SYSC 3.2.19). 

Firms must maintain appropriate Business Continuity Planning (BCP) arrangements which allow them to continue operating, including through remote staff working, as well as ensure continued compliance with all relevant regulatory obligations (SYSC 13.8). Firms should consider the likelihood and impact of a disruption on the continuity of operations from unexpected events. This may include disruptions arising through the loss or failure of internal and external resources (including people, systems and assets), information loss or corruption and other external events (including vandalism, war and acts of God) (SYSC 13.8.5). 

Firms should adopt the appropriate arrangements to maintain the continuity of their operations and reduce the likelihood of disruption (including through succession planning, systems resilience and dual processing) and the impact of the disruption (including through contingency arrangements and insurance) (SYSC 13.8.6). The strategy for maintaining operational continuity and plans for communicating and testing the strategy should be properly documented. This would include formal business continuity plans to deal with short-, medium- and long-term disruptions (including resource requirements, recovery priorities and communication arrangements), escalation and invocation plans (including implementation processes and contact information), information integrity validation and appropriate review and update processes with regard to the firm’s operations and risk profile (SYSC 13.8.7). 

Firms should assess the appropriateness of any alternative sites used including with regard to the location, speed of recovery, adequacy resources and any issues that arise with regard to shared use (SYSC 13.8.8). Separate provisions apply with regard to outsourcing (SYSC). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Other continuity arrangements may apply. The Bank of England monitors business continuity and operational resilience within the UK’s financial sector with the FCA and Treasury, with responses coordinated through the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) and National Crime Agency (NCA) (https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/financialstability/financial-sector-continuity). The objectives are to ensure that retail and wholesale markets remain open and functioning, with there being an orderly and early return to trading in the event of closure, involve relevant infrastructure providers and market participants in decision-taking and be ready to facilitate other market initiatives. The UK has also adopted the Civil Contingencies Act 2004 which replaced the Civil Defence Act 1948. 

Firms will have to comply with any sanctions imposed by the government or other regulatory restrictions imposed on countries or firms from identified countries. A number of other regulatory adjustments and concessions may be announced by the authorities which have to be adhered to. Firms must maintain effective monitoring systems to follow all directions and the necessary systems and controls to give effect to these in practice. Candidates could also have noted that the measures adopted in response to the global financial crisis beginning in 2008-2009 ensured that financial markets and firms were more stable and resilient, and capable of withstanding such shocks as the COVID-19 crisis. Other market and regulatory initiatives have been adopted in response to COVID-19 specifically while other existing systems, controls and continuity planning arrangements allowed firms to continue to operate. Candidates could have referred to any of the above. 

**C15.** The question was concerned with Sovereign Wealth Funds (SWFs). SWFs are among the largest single investors currently and, despite the downturn in many aspects of legal work, are still generating a substantial amount of transaction activity within the major law firms. 

There is no standard definition of ‘Sovereign Wealth Fund’ at this time although reference may be made to any of the major funds including in the Middle East as well as Scandinavia and China (each of which generally has its own website). Reference may also have been made to the current work being undertaken in this area by various organisations and committees including ESMA (the European Securities and Markets Authority) and the International Organisation of Securities Commissions (IOSCO) as well as the Basel Committee and the European Commission. The issue has also been discussed within the US Congress. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The specific regulatory issues that arise include the following: 

- 1)   Disclosure and transparency; 

- 2)   Corporate governance; 

- 3)  Management autonomy within target firms; 

- 4)  National security (although specific definition issues arise in defining relevant interests with this possibly extending to include energy companies, transport and freight (such as the New York Port Authority) in addition to military and armaments interests); 

- 5)   Money laundering; 

- 6)  Possible market distortion or disruption; 

- 7)  Moral hazard (with the implied sovereign support or guarantee provided);  and 

- 8)  Potential corruption and political interference in certain countries. 

Reference could also, for example, specifically have been made to the FCA’s statutory objectives and the extent to which SWFs could cause specific difficulties for the FCA in regulatory terms. 

## **C16.** 

## **Memorandum** 

## To: **All staff** 

## From: **Compliance Officer** 

Date: 

## Re: **Money Laundering Scope** 

Set out below is guidance on when it is necessary to verify the identity of institutional and corporate customers, and how verification procedures should be carried out. 

## **When procedures are required** 

Whenever a business relationship is to be established, or a one-off transaction or series of linked transactions of Euro 15,000 or more is undertaken, the identification procedures must be followed. Once identification procedures have been satisfactorily completed, then as long as records are maintained in accordance with the guidance notes, no further evidence is needed when subsequent transactions are undertaken. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Exemptions are: 

- Transactions with any UK or EU credit or financial institution. 

- Introduction from another financial institution in the EU or one of the countries in the Financial Action Task Force or Jersey, Guernsey or the Isle of Man. This concession only applies to one-off transactions. 

- Postal or electronic business from an account held in the applicant’s name at a UK or EU credit institution. In such situations record need be made of only the account number and branch sort code, if there is no apparent inconsistency between the name on the cheque (if applicable) and the name in which the account is held. 

Irrespective of the size and nature of the transactions and the exemptions set out above, identity must be verified in **all cases** where money laundering is known or suspected. 

## **Procedures required – UK registered companies and partnerships** 

No further steps, over and above the existing business practice, to verify identity will normally be required where the applicant for business is: 

- A company quoted on the London Stock Exchange or another recognised or designated 

- investment exchange. 

- Known to be a subsidiary of such a company. 

- A private company or partnership, one or more of whose directors/partners are already known to the firm. 

Where the applicant for business is an unquoted company or a partnership and none of the directors/partners is already known, the firm should identify one or more of the principal director/partners and/or shareholders as applicable. In addition the following documents should normally be obtained: 

- Copy of the latest report and accounts (audited where applicable). 

- Copy of the certificate of incorporation/certificate of trade or equivalent. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The firm may also: 

   - Make a credit reference agency search or take a bankers reference; or 

   - For smaller companies, or partnerships, verify the identity of one or more of the directors/partners in line with the requirement for personal customers referred to earlier. 

- **C17.** Certainly, there is some overlap in the sources of rules surrounding financial promotions. Ignoring general case law and the requirements of the Advertising Standards Authority there are three main sets of rules governing Financial Promotions. 

In FSMA, s.21, there is a legal restriction on the issue of advertisements, making it illegal to issue a financial promotion without being authorised or having the advertisement approved by an authorised person. 

The Financial Promotion Order then contains some exemptions to s.21 for example the annual report and accounts exemption and exemptions to articles by journalists and promotions aimed only at investment professionals. 

The FCA’s Conduct of Business Sourcebook then sets out various rules relating to financial promotions, covering such matters as the fair, clear and not misleading rule, special requirements for communicating with retail clients, rules on disclosure of information about the past, simulated past and future performance of investment products, and rules on direct offer financial promotions and cold calls. 

There is certain logic to the structure of the regulations. There is little detail in FSMA on the form and content of promotions. This is due to the inherently time-consuming nature of lawmaking – it is much more flexible for the Treasury to update the Financial Promotions Order (FPO) on the types of promotions that are or are not covered by the regulations and for the FCA to adapt their rules on the restrictions and relaxations in COBS than it would be for parliament to debate and write amendments into law. 

Additionally, there is little overlap in the two main sources of detail – if you need to see if a type of promotion is exempt you turn to the FPO. If the promotion is covered then you look 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

to the COBS to find information on the specific form and content permitted for that type of promotion. 

Are the rules complex? Undoubtedly yes. Could the rules be written in a less complex manner that remained equally effective? Perhaps not. The three sources of legislation are each of a manageable size and written within a structure that means they can be appropriately amended should the need arise. 

**C18.** The Conservative Government under Liz Truss ended the controls over bank bonuses that had been put into place in the wake of the Great Financial Crisis, and as such, this is a highly relevant and current topic that you can expect to be tested from time to time over the years to come. 

Regulatory concerns arise with regard to the possible risk distorting effects that unbalanced incentive packages can have on risk management with consequent effects on firm and market instability. 

The question could have been dealt with in a number of ways. Reference could, for example, have been made to the FCA’s statutory objectives and supervisory principles in FSMA as well as to the high-level standards contained in PRIN, SYSC and COCON, COND, FIT and the CISI Code of Conduct. 

Specific provisions include PRIN 1 (integrity), 3 (management and control), 6 (customers’ interest), 8 (conflicts of interest) and 11 (relations with regulators); SYSC 2 (senior management arrangements), 3 (systems and controls including remuneration policies), 7 (risk controls) and 10 (conflicts of interest); 

COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence) and 3 (regulatory relations); FIT 1 (honesty, integrity and reputation) and 2 (competence and capability); and CISI 1 (honesty and fairness), 2 (integrity), 5 (conflicts of interest) and 8 (high personal standards). 

Reference may also have been made to COBS 9 (suitability), 10 (appropriateness), 12 (conflicts of interest), 2.3 (inducements), 11.6 (dealing commissions), 11.2 (best execution) and possibly 11.7 (personal account dealing). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Reference could have been made to the evolution of the rules that Truss’s government repealed, including the regulator’s CEO letter concerning remuneration packages in banks and the draft Code issued in March 2009 and possibly to the recent additional issues that arose with the partial nationalisation of major UK financial institutions in 2008. The FCA policy would generally appear to be to leave this to be dealt with within specific financial institutions in particular at the board level or through existing management arrangements for other senior staff. Firms must nevertheless avoid increasing or aggravating firm risks and exposures which fall within their risk management capability. 

Candidates should also have included discussion on relevant ethical rather than purely legal or regulatory aspects of the problem. Reference could have been made to the regulator’s general discussion papers and general approach in this area. Reference may also have been to the risk of conflicts of interest or cross-referencing to relevant high-level standards. 

- **C19.** The general issue raised was the extent to which the FCA can respond to changes in financial markets, in this case with specific reference to the growth of the Alternative Investment Management (AIM) market. General introductory issues could have included the nature of the delegated and rules based regulatory system set up under the FSMA. Relevant advantages include flexibility, low cost and speed of revision, ease of amendment or adjustment, market specialisation and its evolutionary nature over time. 

The question specifically referred to hedge funds and private equity although reference may also have been made to other aspects of modern asset management including collective investment schemes (and UCITS) as well as the structured finance market and possibly sovereign wealth funds (SWFs). General issues include definitions, market growth, structure and operation and advantage and disadvantage. 

Candidates should have dealt with the specific regulatory challenges raised in the question. This could either have been dealt with in terms of specific AIM markets or the AIM sector more generally. 

Difficulties specifically arise with regard to transparency and opacity, information collection and dissemination, confidentiality, regulated activities, regulatory scope and perimeter, prudential regulation (including capital, liquidity, risk management and systems and 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

controls), leverage, investment strategies (including short selling), governance, valuation, conflicts of interest, retail client protection and fees and remuneration. 

The EU has proposed a Directive on Alternative Investment Managers which has been severely criticised with the FSA and other bodies issuing other papers in connection with hedge funds, private equity, asset management more generally and SWFs. Each of these could have been noted. 

[Tutor’s note: The Alternative Investment Fund Managers Directive was implemented in the UK in July 2013.] 

Reference may also have been made to particular scandals including Long-Term Capital Management (LTCM) and GLG Partners LP and Philippe Jabre and to the FSA Discussion Paper on hedge funds in August 2002. Hector Sants subsequently referred to relevant issues in September 2006 as including serious market disruption and erosion of confidence, market abuse, insider trading and manipulation, control and operational issues, valuation and preferential treatment. 

The relevance of the crisis may also have been referred to with regard to the extent to which the AIM market may have increased market volatility and the response documents adopted. 

## **C20.** 

This question asked candidates to analyse how the UK’s Bribery Act 2010 mitigates the risk of bribery and outline the responsibilities of financial services firms under this legislation. Candidates were additionally asked to discuss the major challenges that global financial services organisations face in combating bribery. Like all questions under this section, this was again an open-ended question in which a lot of material might be included. 

The Bribery Act 2010 is considered one of the most stringent anti-bribery laws in the world. It was introduced to combat bribery and corruption, not just domestically but also on an international scale. It was enacted to provide a comprehensive framework for preventing bribery in both the public and private sectors. The Act criminalises several forms of bribery, including offering, promising, or giving a bribe; requesting, agreeing to receive, or accepting a bribe; bribing a foreign public official; and the failure of a commercial organisation to 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

prevent bribery. The Act’s extraterritorial reach means that UK companies, as well as individuals associated with them, can be prosecuted for bribery offences committed anywhere in the world. 

One of the key ways in which the Act mitigates bribery risk is through the introduction of the corporate offence of failing to prevent bribery. This offence holds companies liable if an associated person (such as an employee, agent, or subsidiary) commits bribery on their behalf, unless the company can demonstrate that it had adequate procedures in place to prevent such conduct. This provision incentivises companies to implement robust antibribery measures, as the absence of such procedures can lead to significant penalties, including unlimited fines. Furthermore, the Act requires transparency in corporate dealings and promotes a culture of integrity by encouraging companies to develop and enforce strong compliance programmes. The threat of severe penalties and reputational damage serves as a powerful deterrent against engaging in or tolerating bribery. 

Financial services firms have specific responsibilities under the Act to ensure that they do not engage in or facilitate bribery. Financial services firms must establish and maintain robust anti-bribery policies and procedures. The Ministry of Justice has provided guidance on what constitutes ‘adequate procedures’, including top-level commitment, risk assessments, due diligence, communication and training, monitoring and review, and enforcement of anti-bribery policies. Firms are required to conduct regular and thorough risk assessments to identify and evaluate the bribery risks they face. This includes understanding the specific risks associated with the markets in which they operate, the services they provide, and the third parties with whom they interact. Furthermore, financial services firms must perform due diligence on their business partners, agents, and intermediaries to ensure they are not engaging in corrupt practices. This due diligence extends to understanding the anti-bribery policies of third parties and ensuring that these policies are aligned with the firm’s own standards. Firms have a responsibility to educate their employees about the Act and the firm’s anti-bribery policies. Regular training sessions should be conducted to ensure that all employees, especially those in high-risk roles, are aware of their responsibilities and the potential consequences of engaging in bribery. 

Despite the robust framework provided by the Bribery Act 2010, global financial services firms face several significant challenges in combating bribery. Firms often operate in multiple jurisdictions, each with its own anti-bribery laws and regulations. Navigating this 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

complex regulatory environment can be challenging, particularly when the requirements in different countries may conflict or when local customs may tolerate practices that are considered bribery under the Bribery Act. Cultural norms regarding business practices vary widely across the world. What is considered a customary business gift in one country may be viewed as a bribe in another. Global financial services firms must balance respecting local customs with ensuring compliance with anti-bribery laws, which can be a delicate and challenging task. Firms often rely on a network of third parties, including agents, consultants, and intermediaries. Managing the bribery risks associated with these third parties is difficult, especially in regions with high levels of corruption. Firms must ensure that their due diligence processes are rigorous and that third parties are held to the same anti-bribery standards. The enforcement of anti-bribery laws has become more aggressive in recent years, with regulators in multiple jurisdictions increasingly cooperating to tackle bribery. Global financial services firms are at risk of facing significant penalties and reputational damage if they are found to be in violation of anti-bribery laws, even if the bribery occurred in a different country. Beyond legal and financial penalties, the reputational damage associated with bribery allegations can be severe. Global financial services firms must work to protect their reputation by maintaining high ethical standards and demonstrating a commitment to combating bribery in all forms. 

**C21.** The question asked for comment on the effectiveness of the regulatory enforcement powers available to the authorities with specific reference to new measures. Candidates could initially have referred to earlier general powers including financial penalties (s206 FSMA), variation of permission (s45 FSMA), cancellation of Part 4 permission (s54 FSMA), withdrawal of authorisation (s33 FSMA), public censure (s205 FSMA), injunctions (s380 FSMA) and restitution (ss382 and 384 FSMA) and skilled persons’ reports (s166 FSMA). 

Candidates should have specifically referred to the revised or extended powers made available following the UK financial crisis. These include suspension of permission (s206A FSMA and s9 FSA 2010), removal of the restriction on imposing a financial penalty and cancelling authorisation (s10 FSA 2010), penalties for performing a controlled function without approval (s63A FSMA and s11 FSA 2010), suspending or restricting an approved person (s66 FSMA and s12 FSA 2010), publication of decision notices (s391 FSMA and s13 FSA 2010), requests for information (s165 FSMA and Schedule 12 FSA 2012), administration, receivership, winding-up or bankruptcy (Part 24 FSMA, Banking Act 2009 and Schedule 14 FSA 2012), complaint by a consumer body (super-complaint under 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

s234C FSMA and s43 FSA 2012), requiring firms to comply with a consumer redress scheme (s404 FSMA and s14 FSA 2010), requiring information in relation to financial stability (s165A FSMA and s18 FSA 2010), suspension of a financial instrument from trading (s36 FSA 2012), complaint by a consumer body (super-complaint under s234C FSMA and s43 FSA 2012), regulated person references (s234D FSMA), OFT investigation (s234H FSMA) and product intervention rules (s137D FSMA and s24 FSA 2012). 

Reference may also have been made to the new powers for the Treasury to arrange independent inquiries (s68 FSA 2012), duties of the FCA and PRA to investigate and report on possible regulatory failure (ss73 and 74 FSA 2012), publication of inquiry and investigation reports (s82 FSA 2012) and revised offences on misleading statements and impressions with regard to benchmarks (ss89-91 FSA 2012). Separate new provisions were also introduced in relation to complaints against the regulators (ss84-88 and Part 6 FSA 2012). 

Separate reference may also have been made to the new requirements imposed on authorised firms in connection with recovery and resolution plans (RRPs) (s139B FSMA and s7 FSA 2010) and power to prohibit or require disclosure of short selling (s131B FSMA and s8 FSA 2010). 

Reference may have been made to each of the additional statutory provisions adopted following the global financial crisis including the Financial Services Act 2010, Financial Services Act 2012, Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 and the Bank of England and Financial Services Act 2016. 

Candidates may also have referred to other European or international measures adopted following the crisis although the question strictly referred to UK powers adopted following the crisis. Candidates should have specifically commented on the effectiveness of the powers. This may have included discussing the Supervisory Enhancement Programme (SEP) adopted by the FSA and ‘credible deterrence’ enforcement policy as well as increased financial fine policy adopted by the FCA in recent years and the overall effectiveness of these measures. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C22.** Candidates had the opportunity to identify as many as possible of the principal international 

regulatory committees currently in operation and to describe their key role and function. Relevant committees include: 

- a) Basel Committee on Banking Supervision; 

- b) International Organisation of Securities Commissions (IOSCO); 

- c) International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS); 

- d) Committee on Payment and Settlement Systems (CPSS); 

- e) Committee on the Global Financial System (CGFS); 

- f) Joint Forum of Financial Conglomerates; 

- g) Financial Stability Board (FSB); 

- h) Financial Action Task Force (FATF). 

As much information as possible should have been provided on the work and importance of these committees including any recent papers, such as the Basel Committee Basel III capital adequacy provisions following the recent financial crisis. 

Reference could also have been made to other international accounting organisations such as the International Accounts Standards Committee (IASC), International Accounting Education Standards Board (IAESB) and International Public Sector Accounting Standards Board (IPSASB) and the International Accounting Standards Board (IASB). 

While not strictly committees, candidates could have also have referred to other international financial institutions (IFIs) such as the International Monetary Fund, World Bank, Bank for International Settlements (BIS), Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) and other development banks (such as the Asia Development Bank (ADB), African Development Bank (AfDB), Latin American Development Bank and Inter-American Development Bank). 

- **C23.** The question was concerned with the extent to which financial ethics are sufficiently dealt with under the FCA Handbook or whether additional legislative or statutory requirements are necessary. 

Candidates could initially have referred to the extent to which financial ethics are promoted under the current FCA Handbook and supporting guidance and other FSA/FCA papers. This includes the draft Code of Ethics in CP09/18, CP10/12 on Competence and Ethics and 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

the Conduct Rules under COCON. Reference may also have been made to Hector Sants’ speech on ‘Regulatory Culture’ in October 2010 and FSA discussion paper on ethics in October 2002. 

Specific reference could have been made to particular FCA Handbook provisions or policies including PRIN, SYSC and COCON as well as the TCF, RDR and new product regulation rules. 

A statutory basis for ethical conduct is also provided under the Approved Persons Requirement (s59 FSMA), Code of Conduct (s64 FSMA) and Action for Damages (s71 FSMA). As well as more general action for statutory damages for breach of an FCA rule under s138D FSMA, both sets of damages under ss71 and 138D are nevertheless narrowly defined and would limit more general discretionary recovery under a loose unethical behaviour principle. 

A number of difficulties would then arise in attempting to formulate a statutory rule for ethical conduct. Specific difficulties would arise regarding definition, certainty, defences and exceptions, compliance and proof, penalties and enforcement. It is also arguable that a general unspecified requirement would limit firms’ legitimate practices, stifle innovation and impose unnecessary regulatory and legal costs and burdens. 

**C24.** The question raises the issue of the delivery of financial services through electronic means and the FCA’s response in this regard. This then raises general issues with regard to financial innovation but also specific FCA policy in implementing the EU Electronic Commerce Directive (ECA) and the Electronic Money Directive (and new E-Money Directive) and the Electronic Money Regulations. 

Issuing e-money is a regulated activity under the RAO with authorised firms being referred to as Electronic Money Institutions (EMIs) or registered small EMIs. 

The Electronic Commerce Directive applies to Electronic Commerce Activities (ECAs) which are defined in terms of ‘information society services’. These apply to services provided electronically, at a distance, for remuneration and at the request of the individual. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

These were given effect to by the FCA under the Electronic Money Sourcebook (ELM) and the Electronic Commerce Directive (ECO) sourcebook with ELM being disapplied from April 2011 and ECO from October 2007. The second E-Money Directive (2EMD) came into effect in May 2011 with new Electronic Money Regulations (EMRs) in 2011. The FCA’s approach to electronic money was set out in a paper issued in March 2011 with 2EMD to be implemented with the Payment Services Directive (PSD) under the EMRs. 

As much relevant information as possible should have been provided with no specific answer being expected. 

## **C25.** 

The question was essentially concerned with the relationship between remuneration and incentive packages with the need to maintain appropriate ethical standards within financial firms. Candidates should have based their discussion around their opinion as to whether or not they believe the decision of the Conservative Government under Liz Truss to end the controls over bank bonuses that had been put into place in the wake of the Great Financial Crisis was a wise move, or not. As usual, the marks here are for justifying your belief, and demonstrating arguments both for and against that decision, rather than worrying about or trying to anticipate whether the examiner agrees or disagrees with the decision Truss made. 

The principal papers on remuneration following the Great Financial Crisis should have been referred to briefly. Possible materials you could have used include the review by Sir David Walker in 2009 and FCA Remuneration Code as well as relevant ‘CEO’ letters. The Remuneration Code included within SYSC 19 was updated in January 2011 partly to implement the EU Capital Requirements Directive (CRD 3) and Walker Review recommendations and provisions in the Financial Services Act 2010. The 12 principles cover governance, performance measurement and remuneration. A more proportionate approach was adopted with regard to implementation based on the new disclosure requirements contained in CRD 3. 

Candidates could then have attempted to link this work with the FSA’s/FCA’s separate initiatives on ethics. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Relevant documents include FSA discussion paper on ethics in October 2002 and the draft Code of Ethics in CP09/18, CP10/12 on Competence and Ethics and COCON conduct rules. Reference may also have been made to relevant speeches. Specific reference could have been made briefly to the Handbook provisions or policies including PRIN, SYSC, COCON, RDR and the Consumer Duty. 

Remuneration could then have been considered as one specific moral or ethical area in which the authorities have been able to take more specific action. 

This is a highly relevant and current topic that you can expect to be tested from time to time over the years to come. 

- **C26.** The FCA has adopted a more aggressive ‘proactive’ and ‘credible deterrence’ approach to enforcement following the financial crisis. This was highlighted by a speech by Hector Sants in March 2009. Candidates could have reviewed all of the enforcement powers available to the FCA and additional matters dealt with under the Financial Services Act 2010 especially in terms of suspension, non-approved persons and short selling penalty powers. Reference may also have been made to the FCA’s five-step process (based on disgorgement, discipline, mitigation, deterrence and discount). The high recent levels of fine, prohibition and conviction may also have been noted with reference to some of the specific penalties imposed in individual cases. 

Candidates could then have specifically commented on the effectiveness of this policy in terms of deterrence and the ability of the FCA to secure its statutory objectives. Relevant issues include possible retroactive effect, lack of due process, limited crisis relevance and longer-term ineffectiveness in terms of continuing regulatory compliance and supervision. Higher retrospective penalties cannot compensate for ineffective ongoing supervision and regulation by the authorities. Reference may also, for example, have been made to the FSA Northern Rock Report and separate NAO reviews as well as the most recent RBS Report in December 2011. 

- **C27.** Candidates were asked to consider the extent to which financial firms have to consider the impact of climate risk on ethical and cultural frameworks. This follows the UN Conference of the Parties on Climate (COP) 26 held in Scotland in November 2021. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Climate and ethics were relevant to the extent that firms have to implement all relevant objectives and obligations imposed by regulatory authorities both in terms of securing strict compliance and more general adherence to the purposes intended. This was also necessary to support customers in terms of the nature and quality of the services received and customers’ own objectives and choice in this area. 

The FCA has stressed that climate change and other environmental, social and governance (ESG) matters have become increasingly significant to the activities of listed companies, regulated firms and consumers (FCA, ‘Climate Change and Sustainable Finance (22 April 2021)). The Chancellor of the Exchequer has required the FCA to support the Government’s commitment to securing a net zero economy by 2050 working in cooperation with the Bank of England’s FPC, with the Bank of England acting in its capacity as the Prudential Regulation Authority (Remit Letter to Mikhil Rathi, FCA Chief Executive (24 March 2021)). The FCA would ensure that its regulatory approach supported market participants manage the risks concerned and capture opportunities to benefit consumers. Mikhil Rathi had delivered a speech at the COP 226 event on ‘A Strategy for Positive Sustainable Change’ (3 November 2021) which highlighted the FCA’s new strategy for positive change and the need to mobilise the capital required to manage emissions. Former FCA CEO, Andrew Bailey, gave a speech on ‘Tackling Climate for Real: the Role of Central Banks’ before the Reuters Responsible Business Event 2021 (1 June 2021). The FCA’s ESG strategy is based on five key themes of Transparency, Trust, Tools, Transition and Team (FCA, ‘Our Strategy for Positive Change: Our ESG Priorities (3 November 2021)). The FCA would implement the FSB’s Task Force on Climate Related Financial Disclosures’ (TCFD’s) 11 recommendations on disclosures covering the four pillars of governance, strategy, risk management, metrics and targets. The UK’s joint Government regulator the TCFD Taskforce published an Interim Report with A Roadmap Towards Mandatory Climate-related Disclosures in November 2020. The FCA finalised its new disclosure rules for UK premium listed companies in December 2021. 

UK authorities were also committed to implementing the measures to be produced by the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) working with the International Accounting Standards Board (IASB) under the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Foundation and the International Organisation of Securities Commission’s (IOSCO) Sustainable Finance Taskforce. The FCA would separately implement the UK Government’s Roadmap to Sustainable Financing (October 2021) and the ‘Sustainable 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Disclosure Requirements’ (SDR). The FCA has adopted other guiding principles in considering authorisation applications by sustainable investment funds to set out expectations concerning the design, delivery and disclosure of retail responsible and sustainable funds with a separate follow-up Chairs’ Letter in July 2021. 

The FCA and PRA had set up a Climate Financial Risk Forum (CFRF) in 2019 to exchange experience in managing climate-related risks and opportunities, and to develop capability and capacity. The FCA was assisting development of other solutions to support firms manage the net zero transition. Specific work has been undertaken through the FCA’s TechSprint programme and digital sandbox pilot with a separate Green Fintech Challenge initiative to assist new start-ups, incumbents and technology providers. 

The PRA published a PRA Climate Change Adaptation Report 2021 on 28 October 2021 which states its response to dealing with climate change risks in relation to its operations and policy functions. This explains the role of the Bank of England and PRA in relation to climate-related exposures and outlines the work undertaken on improving resiliency, climate related risks and supervisory approach adjustments. The PRA would embed climate change within its supervisory approach and incorporate this into supervisory expectations in dealing with firms. The Bank of England considers that climate change is relevant to its core mission of promoting the good of the people of the UK by maintaining monetary and financial stability. (https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/climate-change.) 

Climate scenario analysis and stress testing was incorporated within the PRA’s toolkit to assess the impact and exposures of climate pathways on the financial system. This was incorporated into the Climate Biennial Exploratory Scenario (CBES) which would assess physical and transition risks in relation to early, late and no additional action scenarios published by the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS). The microeconomic and financial stability effects of climate change would also be incorporated into the Bank of England’s Agenda for Research. Firms have to be aware of all of these policies and initiatives and incorporate them within their own climate-related frameworks and wider systems and control compliance arrangements. 

Candidates could have separately outlined the FCA’s and PRA’s existing treatment of financial ethics in modern financial markets. Candidates could have explained the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

background to PRIN and the Fundamental Rules (FRs) within the larger handbooks and listed each of these with the key requirement being to act with integrity. While no separate guidance is provided on this by the FCA or PRA with regard to firms, additional information was provided under APER and now COCON 4 in terms of the meaning of the main equivalent provisions in connection with individuals. 

Firms would generally have to comply with all existing relevant provisions including PRIN 1 (integrity), PRIN 6 (customers’ interests), PRIN 7 (communications), PRIN 8 (avoiding conflicts of interest) and PRIN 9 (trust) as well as possibly PRIN 10 (protecting client assets). They must also continue to treat customers fairly at all times. Parallel measures are imposed under the PRA’s Fundamental Rules. Individuals would be subject to equivalent provisions under COCON. 

Rather than focus on ethics, the FCA has shifted its examination to firm culture. Tracey McDermott gave a speech on ‘Wholesale Conduct Risk’ before the British Bankers’ Association on 13 June 2015. Andrew Bailey, former FCA Chief Executive Officer between July 2016 and March 2020 and previously Chief Executive of the PRA, delivered a speech on ‘Culture in Financial Services – A Regulator’s Perspective’ at the City Week 2016 Conference on 9 May 2016. Bailey stressed the importance of culture with it being of ‘the utmost importance to financial regulators’. Culture is dependent on a range of contributory forces including management, governance, remuneration, incentive structure, management quality and effectiveness as well as personnel adoption and adherence. Many of the recent major prudential or conduct scandals arose from cultural failures in governance, remuneration, risk management and leadership. 

Bailey has stated that the authorities could not ‘determine the culture of firms’, which would not work, and could not ‘write a regulatory rule that settles culture’ with this being the product of many factors that the regulators could influence including governance, remuneration and risk management. There was ‘no magic bullet to change culture’ although the new senior managers and certification regime (SMCR) would assist in embedding ‘the notion of personal responsibility for the affairs of the firm at the level of senior management’. It was ‘not the job of regulators to enforce culture and to change culture’ with firm incentives determining culture and with the authorities attempting ‘to align those incentives with our public interest objectives as well as the private interest of the firm’. Bailey separately addressed the HKMA Annual Conference for Independent Non-Executive 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Directors in Hong Kong on 16 March 2017, the Transforming Culture in Financial Services Conference in London on 19 March 2018 and the Investment Association’s Culture Conference at Mansion House, London on 6 November 2018. 

Candidates could have referred to other FCA work on culture and governance. The FCA expects financial leaders to manage the drivers of behaviour in their firms to create and maintain cultures which reduce the potential for harm (FCA, ‘Culture and Governance’ (3 August 2020 available https://www.fca.org.uk/firms/culture-and-governance). Culture is stated to be at ‘the heart’ of how the FCA authorises and supervises firms. Culture is referred to as ‘the habitual behaviours and mindsets that characterise an organisation’ with the FCA focusing on four key drivers of purpose, leadership, rewards and management and governance. The FCA published a collection of 28 papers on ‘Transforming Culture in Financial Services’ in March 2018 (DP 18/2) which examined what good culture might look like, the role of regulation and regulators, how firms might promote incentives and how to change behaviour for the better. This was followed by a Transforming Culture Conference and publication of an ‘Event Summary and Next Steps paper on 19 March 2018. 

The FCA’s Business Plan for 2020/21 has highlighted the importance of the crisis. Five key priorities were listed for the following one to three years based on transforming how the FCA works and regulates, enabling effective consumer investment decisions, ensuring that credit markets work well, making payments safe and accessible while delivering fair value in a digital age (FCA Business Plan for 2020/21 (7 April 2020)). The FCA’s crisis response had been intended to ensure that markets continue to function in an orderly manner during a major repricing event, issue emergency guidance to allow government schemes to operate, support consumers, assist public access to essential banking services and protect the most vulnerable within society. The FCA would continue to update its operations in response to unfolding events and inform stakeholders appropriately. 

Megan Butler, FCA Executive Director of Supervision – Investment, Wholesale and Specialists, gave a speech on the crisis on 4 June 2020 (‘The FCA’s response to COVID-19 and expectations for 2020’ at the PIMFA’s Virtual Festival). While advisers and wealth managers had responded well to the crisis, attention had to shift from ‘incident response’ to the longer term impact with the FCA focusing on operational resilience, financial resilience and acting with integrity. The FCA specifically criticised firms that had attempted to avoid customer liabilities by closing down companies and setting up new ones. Certain specific crisis response measures were identified based on ensuring a good level of operational 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

resilience, understanding firms’ financial resilience to allow them to fail in an orderly manner, ensuring that markets functioned effectively through price formation and orderly trading, treating customers fairly and ensuring that customers were aware of, and protected from, scams. 

Candidates could also have referred to earlier material in this area. A number of general documents and speeches have been issued on ethics over time. The FSA's initial general approach to ethics was set out in the FSA’s ‘An Ethical Framework for Financial Services’ (October 2002) with competence being examined in the FSA’s, Competence and Ethics (June 2010) CP 10/12. Ethics was considered as part of the Retail Distribution Review (RDR) with the FSA recommending the creation of an enforceable Code of Ethics for investment advisors in the FSA’s Retail Distribution Review (November 2008) Feedback Statement 08/06. This was referred to again in the FSA’s Distribution of Retail Investments: Delivering the RDR (June 2009) CP 09/18 with eight general principles proposed. These can be summarised in terms of acting honestly and fairly at all times, integrity, legal and regulatory compliance, market integrity standards, conflict and professional competence, declining to act and upholding the highest personal and professional standards. FSA Chairman, Howard Davies, discussed ethics in ‘Ethics in Regulation’ in 2001. 

This is a difficult and new area with no specific answer required. It is nevertheless clear that climate risk management will be increasingly incorporated and embedded into financial regulation and compliance. Candidates were only required to identify the main issues raised and provide some comment and assessment. 

## **C28.** 

**[Author’s Note:** _Obviously, Britain has now fully left the EU following the end of the Transitional Period. You would not be asked a question exactly like this one again. However, we have retained this 2021 question, and its post-exam report, within the pack to give you the ability to see how ‘current issues’ are tested, and how the Examiner would have expected candidates to tackle questions such as this one. The model answer also gives you some useful background knowledge.]_ 

The citizens of the UK voted in a National Referendum on 23 June 2016 to leave the EU on a 51.9% to leave the EU with the selected Article 50 departure date announced on 29 March 2017 being 29 March 2019. The British Government published a White Paper on 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

the _Future Relationship Between the UK and EU_ (the Chequers Plan) on 12 July 2018. The Government negotiated a _Draft Withdrawal Agreement_ and _Outline Political Declaration_ with the EU with final terms being concluded on 14 November 2018. This would have included a transitional period until 31 December 2020 during which the UK remained a member of the European Economic Area (EEA), the Single Market and external EU Customs Union although it also contained a potentially limitless continuation (backstop) clause. The November Draft Agreement and Declaration were rejected by the House of Commons in a ‘Meaningful Vote’ on 15 January 2019 on a 432 to 202 vote against the proposed withdrawal arrangements with no clear alternative arrangements being agreed. Further compromise amendments were produced in Parliament on 29 January 2018 including a direction to the Government to renegotiate the backstop provision. 

Financial firms principally enjoy passport rights to carry on activities in other EU Member States under specific financial Directives (including CRD4, MiFID 2 and Solvency 2) or under underlying Treaty rights as set out in the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU) (originally the European Economic Community (EEC) Treaty 1958) which is supported by the Treaty on European Union (TEU) (initially adopted as the Maastricht Treaty in 1992). These rights are based on the underlying law of free movement and principally cover the right of branch establishment and services as well as the free movement of goods, workers and capital. 

The following possible options can be distinguished for financial firms on the U.K.’s withdrawal from the EU: 

- 1) Existing establishment and services rights may be continued on directive passport or Treaty basis during the proposed 18 month or possibly extended transition period; 

- 2) Establishment and services rights may then be continued further under a final UK EU international treaty trade agreement provided for under the Draft Withdrawal Agreement and Political Declaration; 

- 3) Separate equivalence regimes may be exercised in the event of UK firms acquiring third country status although this is only available under specific directives such as MiFID, the Market Abuse Regulation, Solvency 2 Directive, Prospectus Directive and Transparency Directive (https://ec.europa.eu/info/business-economy-euro/banking-andfinance/international- relations/recognition-non-eu-financial-frameworks-equivalencedecisions_en ); 

- 4) Where no passport, Treaty or equivalence rights are available, UK firms can apply for 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

new full original authorisation in another EU Member State which would then allow them to carry on activities (passport) into other Member States; 

- 5) Firms with branches in other Member States may be able to apply for country specific authorisation under third country reciprocity arrangements; 

- 6) Certain firms may be able to benefit from internal group business transfers where they have extended group structures that include separate banking, securities and insurance authorisation within the EU; 

- 7) Some firms are considering setting up various types of ‘back to bank’ lending or other trading arrangements to allow them to copy the economic effects of business transfers within the UK and EU (although the ECB has indicated that limitations will be imposed on such activities by 2022); 

- 8) Firms may also consider establishing representative offices within other Member States in the EU although this would only be for marketing and client development purposes with any authorised activities having either to be returned to the UK or only carried out through EU authorised institutions. 

Candidates may have referred to the separate possible future Treaty arrangements that may be available although the specific financial options are as set out above. Any possible trade relations apart from a WTO opt-in would necessarily involve agreement on some form of _Draft Withdrawal Agreement_ and _Outline Political Declaration_ . Future Treaty relations could include: 

- a) Norway (or Norway Plus) EEA model including Single Market access and EU Customs Union Membership (with consultation rights and EEA Joint Committee involvement and the requirement for national domestic law implementation); 

- b) Switzerland (European Free Trade Association (EFTA)) model based on bilateral trade agreements; 

- c) Turkey (Andorra and San Marino) non-EEA and non-EFTA Customs Union model (excluding agricultural goods and services); 

- d) Canada Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement (CETA) model for free trade including single market entry (but excluding certain food substances and services) or an EU Japan Economic Partnership Agreement (EPA) model; 

- e) Singapore and Hong Kong unilateral free trade model; 

- f) WTO default position in the absence of any other trade arrangement (with the EU being prevented from discriminating against UK firms and with border controls limited under the WTO Trade Facilitation Agreement) and with possible reliance on Article 24 which allows 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

tariff free trade to be continued for exceptional reasons to allow parties to negotiate new trade relations. 

- **C29.** One of the most significant gaps in the earlier regulatory frameworks before the Global Financial Crisis was the ability of authorities to deal with domestic and Global Systemically Important Firms (GSIFs) in difficulty. Many authorities lacked necessary resolution as well as market support (lender of last resort) powers. 

To add depth to your answer, you might have referred to some of the evolution of the regulations around these plans. New laws (under the Financial Services Act 2010 and ss 137J and K FSMA 2000 as amended) requiring Recovery and Resolution Plans (RRPs) were created as a result of this, with all major financial firms being required to maintain effective RRPs to manage both their pre- and post-crisis condition and either to protect their stability or facilitate a managed winding-down of their activities and an eventual winding-up. The Bank of England published its ‘Approach to Resolution’ in 2014 which refers to three separate phases of stabilisation, restructuring and exit. The PRA issued a consultation paper on this in 2014 (CP13/14) with a policy statement response in January 2015 (PS 13/14). The PRA’s expectations on firm and group recovery plans were set out in Supervisory Statement 18/13 and resolution planning in Supervisory Statement 19/13 as amended. 

Recovery Plans generally operate in a pre-crisis situation and allow the firm, for example, to raise additional liquidity or capital, dispose of assets or subsidiaries and transfer activities. Resolution Plans are intended to assist authorities manage the subsequent winding-up of the institution in the event of the failure of the Recovery Plan at the same time as protect the provision of critical functions and limit contagious effects and public costs. Resolution Plans generally operate with other special resolution procedures such as those set up under the Banking Act 2009 in the UK. 

The PRA rulebook contains separate rules on Recovery Plans and Resolution Packs. Firms must prepare and maintain an appropriate resolution pack again submitted to the PRA online (PRA Resolution Pack rules 2.3 and 2.4). This should contain sufficient information and analysis to facilitate the planning for or taking of action in the event that circumstances arise that may lead to the failure of the business of the firm or the business of the firm’s 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

failure (rule 2.5). Equivalent provisions apply with regard to the preparation of group resolution packs by banks, building societies and designated investment firms (rule 3). The resolution packs must be kept up-to-date and the PRA notified of any material changes (rule 4). Firms must take into account the nature, scale and complexity of their businesses and establish and maintain appropriate internal processes to govern the resolution pack with an executive director being responsible for this. The firm’s governing body must also assess, approve and oversee the production of the resolution pack with these arrangements being periodically reviewed by the audit committee (rule 5). 

Candidates should have commented on the effectiveness of these arrangements. These should allow all major firms to manage any pre-crisis situation effectively with authorities being able to take necessary action in the event that the firm’s internal RRPs fail to contain the crisis. Implementation of higher capital standards, new liquidity measures and leverage restrictions under Basel III with the new proposed RRP constitute the most important postcrisis responses and make many other measures redundant, with the other measures on risk management and balanced remuneration provision. Effective support (lender of last resort) arrangements are nevertheless still be necessary in the most extreme situations with effective macro-prudential oversight being required at all times. 

- **C30.** This question asked candidates to critically evaluate the role of organisational culture in influencing conduct within financial institutions. How does the FCA’s approach to culture and governance aim to enhance market integrity, and what steps can firms take to align their culture with regulatory expectations? Again, this was an open-ended question in which a lot of material could be included. 

Organisational culture plays an important role in influencing conduct and decision-making within financial institutions. Recognising this, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) places significant emphasis on culture and governance as key factors in promoting market integrity and consumer protection. Organisational culture comprises the shared values, beliefs, and norms that guide behaviour within an institution. A positive culture encourages ethical behaviour, accountability, and a focus on customer outcomes, while a poor culture can lead to misconduct, systemic failures, and significant harm to consumers and markets. 

Key ways in which culture influences conduct include: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

• Tone from the Top – Leadership behaviour sets the standard for acceptable practices. Leaders who prioritise ethical behaviour and transparency foster a culture where misconduct is less likely to occur. 

- Incentive Structures – Reward systems that align with long-term, customer-focused goals discourage short-termism and risky behaviours. 

- Communication and Training – Effective communication and regular training reinforce the importance of compliance and ethical conduct. 

- Accountability Mechanisms – Clear accountability ensures individuals are responsible for their actions, which deters misconduct. 

Historically, cultural failures have been central to financial scandals, such as the LIBOR manipulation and the mis-selling of financial products, underscoring the critical need for strong cultural frameworks. The FCA does not prescribe a one-size-fits-all culture but focuses on outcomes, encouraging firms to cultivate environments that prioritise good conduct. The Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SM&CR) for example is intended to enhance individual accountability by requiring senior managers to have clearly defined responsibilities, promoting a culture of ownership and ethical leadership. The FCA has also noted that a firm's governance structures should effectively support its purpose and values, ensuring decisions align with desired cultural outcomes. Remuneration and incentive systems should be designed to ensure they do not inadvertently encourage misconduct. 

Through regular supervisory interactions, the FCA assesses cultural indicators, such as employee feedback mechanisms, decision-making processes, and the handling of misconduct. The FCA regularly publishes speeches, discussion papers, and guidance that highlight the importance of culture, fostering industry-wide dialogue and awareness. By adopting this multifaceted approach, the regulator aims to encourage firms to embed cultures that naturally drive good conduct. 

Firms should articulate a clear set of values that prioritise ethical behaviour and customer welfare, ensuring these are communicated effectively throughout the organisation. Senior management should exemplify the desired culture, demonstrating commitment through their actions and decisions. Remuneration and promotion criteria should reward behaviours that align with the firm's values and penalise misconduct. Regular training programmes should reinforce the importance of compliance, ethical decision-making, and understanding of regulatory expectations. Systems that allow employees to report concerns without fear of 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

reprisal are vital. Firms should actively encourage and act upon such feedback. Cultural considerations should be embedded into all business processes, from product development to customer interactions. Using tools like employee surveys, conduct metrics, and independent audits, firms should continuously evaluate their cultural health. The FCA's approach, which emphasises accountability, governance, and outcome-based assessments, seeks to foster cultures that inherently discourage misconduct. However, it is important for firms to proactively cultivate and maintain cultures that align with both regulatory expectations and ethical standards. Striking a balance between prescriptive requirements and a holistic understanding of risk is essential – sometimes, less prescriptive measures can empower firms to use their own judgement, leading to more effective and nuanced risk management. Ultimately, less may indeed be more when it comes to regulatory guidance in relation to culture, enabling firms to better tailor their responses to the specific risks they face. 

**C31.** The question was concerned with the purpose and content of the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) 2016/679/EU and the UK implementing measures with UK Data Protection Act (DPA) 2018. The EU adopted the GDPR in 2016, and it came into effect on 25 May 2018. It replaced the Data Protection Directive (DPD) 95/46/EC which was implemented in the UK under the Data Protection Act 1998. The right to respect private and family life, and home and correspondence was earlier protected under Article 8 of the 1950 European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) and the seven principles agreed by the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) “Recommendations Concerning Guidelines Governing the Protection of Privacy and Trans-border Flows of Personal Data” in January 1999 (which covered notice, purpose, consent, security, disclosure, access and accountability). Controls on automatic processing were provided for under the 1981 Council of Europe Convention for the Protection of Individuals regarding Automatic Processing Personal Data. 

The GDPR applies to personal data, which is information relating to a living individual which can be identified directly from the information or through matching with other information that is, or is likely to be, in the possession of the organisation or entity concerned. Obligations are imposed on data collectors and data processors. Public entities and firms that regularly and systematically process personal data must employ a Data Protection Officer (DPO). Data is generally to be protected by design and by default in systems processing (Article 25). The GDPR contains eight revised data protection principles based 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

on fair and lawful processing, for legitimate purposes, with data being adequate, relevant and non-excessive, accurate and up-to-date, not kept for longer than necessary, processed in accordance with individual rights, secure and properly transferred. Data subjects have eight principal rights under the GDPR, consisting of the right: 

- to be informed 

- of access (Article 15) 

- to rectification (data correction) 

- to data erasure (Article 17) 

- to restrict processing 

- to data portability 

- to object to the processing of their data in certain circumstances 

- related to automated decision making (including profiling). 

Firms in breach of the GDPR can be fined up to €20 million or 4% of annual global turnover. European Member States must establish an independent Supervisory Authority (SA), with activities being coordinated through a European Data Protection Board (EDPB). The GDPR is implemented following the UK’s withdrawal from the EU under the Data Protection, Privacy and Electronic Communications (Amendments etc) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019/419. 

The GDPR has extended the personal rights initially conferred under the DPA 1995 in a number of important respects. Firms must consider data subjects’ interests in managing personal data. The GDPR does not apply with regard to national security, police or judicial use, statistical and scientific analysis and personal use. Security and judicial matters are dealt with under Parts 3 and 4 of the DPA 2018 in the UK, with equivalent GDPR measures being applied to other areas outside the regulation under Part 2. The Information Commissioner (IC) is established under Part 5 and enforcement dealt with in Part 6. It is an offence knowingly or recklessly to obtain or disclose personal data without the consent of the data controller, procuring such disclosure or retaining data without consent and for selling or offering to sell personal data (which implements the requirements set out in the Enforcement of Intellectual Property Rights Directive (IPRED) 2004/48/EC). The GDPR does not attempt to confirm the nature of data or personal data in law and the rights of companies and data subjects outside the terms of the regulation. Separate concerns have arisen regarding the nature of consent and the cost and burden of compliance. Cyber security has had to be dealt with separately under the Network Information Systems (IDS) 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Directive 2016/1148/EU, which came into effect in August 2016, and was implemented on 9 May 2018. The government is currently reviewing the UK’s approach to data protection and relevant legislative and regulatory measures. 

**C32.** _**[Tutorial Note:** The question asked for an assessment of the effectiveness of the regulatory regime brought into effect under the FSA 2012. The question could have been dealt with in a number of different ways with_ _**no specific answer being expected** . Although these changes took place under the Coalition government some time ago, it is quite likely that the government will seek to make changes to this regime in order to adapt the regulatory regime to the changing macroeconomic and political environment, particularly since Brexit._ _**Therefore it is a good idea for candidates for this exam to be aware of the background to the current regime, so that in future exams they can comment on future changes to this regime in an authoritative way.]**_ 

The changes adopted under the FSA 2012 were principally institutional in nature with the establishment of the Financial Policy Committee (FPC), within the Bank of England to assume a new macro-prudential function, and with the replacement of the FSA by a separate bank subsidiary, the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA), and an independent Financial Conduct Authority (FCA). 

The PRA is principally responsible for the prudential regulation of banks, insurance companies, larger designated investment firms, credit unions and building societies. The FCA is responsible for the prudential regulation of all other institutions and the conduct regulation of all institutions. FSMA 2000 has been amended to divide the statutory powers and functions of the two institutions with the FSA Handbook being split to reflect these changes. All of the necessary statutory amendments were brought into effect under the FSA 2012 which amended FSMA 2000 as well as include additional specific provisions on Treasury, Bank of England, FCA and PRA collaboration and crisis management (Part 4), inquiries and investigations (Part 5), regulatory complaints (Part 6), offences (Part 7) and Banking Act 2009 amendments (Part 8). 

While the UK model has been referred to as a ‘Twin Peaks’ system, following the Australian regime set up after the Financial System (Wallis) Inquiry in Australia in June 1996, a number of inconsistencies remain, in particular, with the FCA being the prudential regulator of 23,000 and the PRA the prudential regulator of only 1,700 firms (above). The original 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Twin Peaks papers also did not consider the central bank involvement nor need for separate macroprudential oversight. 

Although not referred to in the Treasury or other regulatory documents supporting the establishment of the new regime, this can principally be justified on the basis of involving the creation of a central bank based macroprudential model with the ‘subsidiarisation’ of systemic function within the central bank and delegation of non-systemic activities to an independent agency. 

The advantages of the new regime can be summarised in terms of separate focus, priority or mandate, distinct objectives, separate needs and outcomes with dedicated and specialist staff. These have to be set against the disadvantages of substantially increased cost, significant increase in complexity, inevitable regulatory fragmentation (with the production of separate FCA and PRA Handbooks replacing the earlier FSA and later Combined Handbook), regulatory overlap and duplication, substantial increase in regulatory obligation within firms, substantial increase in management demand within firms, policy conflict and confusion and inconsistent enforcement and sanction. 

The FSA 2012 regulatory regime that came into effect in 2013 is far more complex that before and imposes substantial additional regulatory burden and cost on financial firms. These costs will inevitably be passed on to business and household clients. The principal changes are institutional in nature although the crisis was not caused by institutional failings as such. 

The principal advantage of the regime introduced in FSA 2012 is that it created a central bank based macro-prudential model. Ever since, however, arguments have raged as to whether the benefits of the associated institutional changes created outweigh the longer term costs and burdens imposed. Institutional reform and structural regulation inevitably increase regulatory costs and obligation with any further additional stability advantages being unclear as successive requirements are imposed. The continuing and additional regulatory burdens imposed on firms have to be balanced against the correspondingly limited marginal benefits that may follow in terms of increased financial stability. Difficult questions of proportion and balance arise. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

You can expect such arguments to be tested from time to time in future exams in the years to come. 

## **C33.** 

In this question, candidates were asked to assess to what extent they had to consider the impact of current and future exogenous (or external) shocks, including major geopolitical events, such as with the Russian entry into Ukraine in February 2022, in designing or reviewing their internal ethical and cultural frameworks. This may also include biological (e.g., the COVID-19 pandemic) events, natural and climatic disasters, terrorism and cyber terrorism, warfare and nuclear threats. 

The FCA has issued specific guidance on the events in Ukraine and their impact on financial markets (https://www.fca.org.uk/news/statements/events-ukraine-impact-financialmarkets), noting the impact of the events and financial sanctions imposed. Firms engaged in securities activities were reminded of their obligations under the UK Market Abuse Regulation (MAR) and to disclose inside information as soon as possible, unless they have a valid reason to delay disclosure. The market was to be kept fully informed of any information or changes that are required to be disclosed under MAR. 

Companies’ disclosure obligations continued even where trading of securities had been suspended. Jon Cunliffe, Deputy Governor for Financial Stability at the Bank of England, gave a speech on ‘Monetary and Financial Stability and the Invasion of Ukraine’ at the European Economics & Financial Centre on 4 April 2022 in which he discussed the impact that the Russian invasion had on the UK economy and on UK financial stability. 

The FCA issued separate guidance in relation to the COVID-19 crisis (https://www.fca.org.uk/coronavirus), with information also having been made available for consumers dealing with financial difficulties during the pandemic (https://www.fca.org.uk/consumers/dealing-financial-difficulties-coronavirus). 

Candidates should have noted that firms have to ensure that they can continue to comply with all relevant regulatory obligations at all times and to maintain the necessary systems and controls and business continuity arrangements which can withstand any major disruption and shock. More obligations, such as those imposed under FCA PRIN and the PRA Fundamental Rules, become of even more importance at such times with financial ethical policies more generally. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Firms would generally have to comply with all existing relevant provisions including PRIN 1 (integrity), PRIN 6 (customers’ interests), PRIN 7 (communications), PRIN 8 (avoiding conflicts of interest) and PRIN 9 (trust), as well as possibly PRIN 10 (protecting client assets). They must also continue to treat customers fairly at times. Parallel measures are imposed under the PRA Fundamental Rules. Individuals would be subject to equivalent provisions under COCON. 

Candidates could have outlined the FCA’s and the PRA’s existing treatment of financial ethics in modern financial markets. They could have explained the background to PRIN and the Fundamental Rules within the larger handbooks and listed each of these, with the key requirement being to act with integrity. While no separate guidance is provided on this by the FCA or the PRA with regard to firms, additional information was provided under COCON 4, with regard to the meaning of the main equivalent provisions in connection with individuals. 

Rather than focus on ethics, the FCA has shifted its examination to firms’ culture. Tracey McDermott gave a speech on ‘Wholesale Conduct Risk’ before the British Bankers’ Association on 13 June 2015. Andrew Bailey, former FCA Chief Executive Officer between July 2016 and March 2020, and previously Chief Executive of the PRA, delivered a speech on ‘Culture in Financial Services – A Regulator’s Perspective’ at the City Week 2016 Conference on 9 May 2016. He stressed the importance of culture, with it being of ‘the utmost importance to financial regulators. Culture is dependent on a range of contributory factors, including management and governance, remuneration and incentive structure, management quality and effectiveness, and personnel adoption and adherence. Many of the recent major prudential or conduct scandals arose from cultural failures in governance, remuneration, risk management and leadership. 

Andrew Bailey has stated that the authorities could not “determine the culture of firms”, which would not work, and could not “write a regulatory rule that settles culture” with this being the product of many factors that the regulators could influence, including governance, remuneration and risk management. There was “no magic bullet to change culture”, although the SM&CR would assist in embedding “the notion of personal responsibility for the affairs of the firm at the level of senior management”. It was “not the job of regulators to enforce culture and to change culture”, with firms’ incentives determining culture and with the authorities attempting “to align those incentives with our public interest objectives as well as the private interest of the firm”. Andrew Bailey separately addressed the HKMA Annual Conference for Independent Non-Executive Directors in Hong Kong on 16 March 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

2017, the Transforming Culture in Financial Services Conference in London on 19 March 2018, and the Investment Association Culture Conference at the Mansion House in London on 6 November 2018. 

Candidates could have referred to other FCA work on culture and governance. The regulator expects financial leaders to manage the drivers of behaviour in their firms to create and maintain cultures which reduce the potential for harm (FCA, ‘Culture and Governance’, 3 August 2020 available https://www.fca.org.uk/firms/culture-andgovernance). Culture is stated to be at “the heart” of how the FCA authorises and supervises firms. It is referred to as “the habitual behaviours and mindsets that characterise an organisation”, with the FCA focusing on four key drivers of purpose, leadership, rewards, and management and governance. The FCA published a collection of 28 papers on ‘Transforming Culture in Financial Services’ in March 2018 (DP 18/2) which examined what good culture might look like, the role of regulation and regulators, how firms might promote incentives and how to change behaviour for the better. This was followed by a Transforming Culture Conference and publication of an ‘Event Summary and Next Steps’ paper on 19 March 2018. 

The FCA Business Plan for 2020/21 has highlighted the importance of the crisis. Five key priorities were listed for the following one to three years based on transforming how the FCA works and regulates, enabling effective consumer investment decisions, ensuring that credit markets work well, making payments safe and accessible and delivering fair value in a digital age (FCA Business Plan for 2020/21, 7 April 2020). The FCA’s crisis response had been intended to ensure that markets continue to function in an orderly manner during a major repricing event, issue emergency guidance to allow government schemes to operate, support consumers, assist public access to essential banking services and protect the most vulnerable within society. The FCA would continue to update its operations in response to unfolding events and inform stakeholders appropriately.
