Megan Butler, FCA Executive Director of Supervision – Investment, Wholesale and Specialists, gave a speech on the crisis on 4 June 2020 (‘The FCA’s Response to COVID19 and Expectations for 2020’) at the Personal Investment Management & Financial Advice Association’s (PIMFA’s) Virtual Festival. While advisers and wealth managers had responded well to the crisis, attention had to shift from ‘incident response’ to longer-term impact, with the FCA focusing on operational resilience, financial resilience and acting with integrity. The regulator specifically criticised firms that had attempted to avoid customer liabilities by closing down companies and setting up new ones. Certain specific crisis 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

response measures were identified based on ensuring a good level of operational resilience, understanding firms’ financial resilience to allow them to fail in an orderly manner, ensuring that markets functioned effectively through price formation and orderly trading, treating customers fairly and ensuring that customers were aware of, and protected from, scams. 

Candidates could also have referred to earlier material in this area. A number of general documents and speeches have been issued on ethics over the last 20 years. The FSA's initial general approach to ethics was set out in An Ethical Framework for Financial Services (October 2002) with competence being examined in Competence and Ethics (June 2010) CP 10/12. Ethics was considered as part of the Retail Distribution Review (RDR), with the FSA recommending the creation of an enforceable Code of Ethics for investment advisers in Retail Distribution Review (November 2008) Feedback Statement 08/06. This was referred to again in Distribution of Retail Investments: Delivering the RDR (June 2009) CP 09/18, with eight general principles proposed. These can be summarised in terms of acting honestly and fairly at all times, integrity, legal and regulatory compliance, market integrity standards, conflict and professional competence, declining to act, and upholding the highest personal and professional standards. FSA Chairman, Howard Davies, discussed ethics in Ethics in Regulation in 2001. 

Candidates could have referred to any of the above. 

- **C34.** Accountability is generally secured through the extension of the earlier accountability regime set up under the FSMA to apply to the new regulatory authorities under the amendments brought into effect under the FSA 2012. These are essentially concerned with HM Treasury powers (appointment, annual report, reviews and independent enquiries), annual general meetings, corporate governance requirements, revised complaints procedure, competition scrutiny, Practitioner Panel and Consumer Panel consultation with Upper Tribunal appeals and Judicial Review. 

Regulators have to comply with the formal warning and decision notice procedure with notices being brought before the Regulatory Decisions Committee (RDC) with appeal before the Upper Tribunal. All of the regulators are also subject to questioning before the Parliamentary Treasury Committee. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

FSA 2012 specifically requires the Bank, FCA and PRA to maintain arrangements for the investigation of complaints against them under a revised complaints scheme. Complaints relate to any expression of dissatisfaction concerning the manner in which the regulators have carried out, or failed to carry out, any relevant functions. Anyone directly affected by any of the regulators' actions or inactions may lodge a complaint. Complaints are investigated by the regulators either jointly or individually as appropriate with a complainant having a right to have the complaint referred to the Office of the Complaints Commissioner (OCC). The Bank, FCA and PRA issued a document on Complaints against the regulators that sets out the relevant procedures with forms being available on the regulators' websites. 

In commenting on the effectiveness of the new regime, candidates could have noted that the new regulatory structure is even more concentrated with many regulatory powers specifically being exercised by the Bank directly or through the PRA indirectly. The Bank is responsible for monetary policy, macro-prudential policy, payments and infrastructure, bank resolution and market liquidity (including lender of last resort). 

Under the earlier Tripartite Committee arrangements, the Bank, FSA and Treasury had equal status. Under the new arrangements, the position of the FCA has effectively been diluted with the Bank only reporting to the Treasury every six months and with the Treasury function effectively being limited to approving emergency support funds. 

**C35.** Candidates were asked to comment on the effectiveness of the macro-prudential regime set up under FSA 2012. This has been raised many time in previous examinations. Candidates should have referred to the establishment of the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) within the Bank of England which has specific responsibility for macro-prudential oversight. Similar macro-prudential bodies have been set up in the US with the Financial Services Oversight Council (FSOC) and with the EU European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB)). 

The Bank of England Act 1998 was amended (under s2 FSA 2012) to provide that the Bank's Financial Stability Objective would be to protect and enhance the financial stability of the UK financial system with the Bank's Court of Directors to determine the Bank's strategy on Financial Stability (s9A). The amendments also provide for the establishment of the Financial Policy Committee (FPC) (s9B BEA 1998) although the FPC had operated on an 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

interim basis since February 2011. The FPC replaced the earlier Financial Stability Committee (FSC) set up under the Banking Act 2009 (s238 BA 2009 inserting a new s2B BEA 1998). 

The purpose of macro-prudential oversight is to monitor the potential build-up of any risk or exposure across the financial system as a whole. This was understood to be one of the principal failings leading to the UK and global financial crisis in 2008-2009. The functions of the FPC are to monitor the stability of the UK financial system to identify and assess systemic risk, give directions, make recommendations and prepare Financial Stability Reports or carry out any other functions determined by the Bank's Court. The FPC may give directions to the FCA or PRA in the exercise of their functions. The FPC may make recommendations including on the provision of financial assistance to financial institutions. The FPC is to continue to publish the Bank's Financial Stability Reports. 

The FPC is being given a number of macro-prudential tools. The Bank and FSA issued a discussion paper in December 2011 which referred to a number of possible balance sheet, lending and market structure options. A first Macro-prudential Measures Order was issued in March 2013 to allow the FPC to impose additional own funds to cover residential and commercial property exposures or financial sector exposures. 

Candidates did not need to refer to any of the specific statutory provisions although they should have explained the general purpose of the FPC and the nature of the powers to be exercised. 

**C36.** This question asked candidates to discuss the conditions required for an effective and transparent system of compliance within a financial firm, **and** how senior management should encourage this. This is an open-ended question in which a lot of material might be included. Whilst this was a popular question for candidates many candidates missed out on full marks by not addressing the role that senior management play in building and maintaining an effective and transparent system of compliance. 

With regards to the conditions required for an effective and transparent system of compliance within a financial firm, within a strong answer we would expect the following: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- Firms should have written compliance policies and procedures outlining principles and processes for managing compliance risks, and these should be in place, and regularly reviewed. 

- The firm should ensure that the compliance function is independent and has formal status within the organisation. 

- Regular risk assessments should be performed to identify and evaluate compliance risks at least annually. 

- Regular and risk-based compliance assurance and testing should be undertaken to ensure adherence to policies and procedures. 

- Compliance reports and management information should be provided to relevant stakeholders on a regular basis, summarising risks, corrective measures, and actions taken. 

- Clear and effective communication channels should be in place for compliance matters within the organisation. 

- Regular compliance training and education should be provided to all employees to ensure they understand and adhere to policies and procedures. 

- Compliance staff should have the necessary skills, knowledge, and expertise needed for their roles. 

- Horizon scanning is in place to effectively identify and address relevant changes and developments in the regulatory requirement. 

- Roles and responsibilities for risk identification and risk management are clearly articulated. 

- Arrangements are in place for the segregation of duties within the firm to article, identify and manage conflicts of interest. 

- Effective systems and controls are in place to counter financial crime, proportionate to the nature, scale, and complexity of the firm’s activities. 

- An internal audit function separate from other functions should be in place, responsible for evaluating the adequacy and effectiveness of systems, internal control mechanisms, and arrangements. 

- Complaints handing processes are in place, and complaints are used as a relevant source of information and improvement. 

Concerning how senior management should encourage this, a strong answer could note that senior management should: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

   - Lead by example by adhering to and promoting the firm’s compliance policies and ethical behaviour. 

   - Actively review management information and compliance reports and ensure timely implementation of corrective measures. 

   - Promote a speak-up culture, and create an environment where staff feel comfortable reporting compliance concerns without fear of retaliation. 

   - Encourage continuous improvement in compliance processes and stay updated with regulatory changes. 

   - Ensure the compliance function is provided with adequate resources, including qualified and experienced staff. 

   - Embed compliance as part of the firm’s culture and an integral part of business activities. 

- **C37.** Candidates were asked to comment on the extent to which a correct regulatory balance had been achieved at the national, European and international levels. The question strictly referred to national, European ‘or’ rather than ‘and’ international levels with candidates being able to comment on one, two or all three perspectives. To score lots of marks, it might have been easier to review regulatory developments in recent years at all three levels. 

The objective was to assess the relative advantages of improved market regulation and function as against excessive burden and cost. The question did not specifically refer to the global, European or UK financial crises as such although candidates could have referred to this by way of useful background information. 

Candidates should then have reviewed the more significant developments at each level and then comment on the extent to which these could be considered to be necessary or proportionate. This could have been dealt with in a number of different ways with no specific answer being expected. Necessary revisions following the crisis clearly include the strengthening of capital and liquidity, tightening of leverage and debt levels (under Basel III and EU CRD IV), strengthening governance, improving supervision, introducing new forms of bank resolution (such as under the UK Banking Act 2009 or proposed EU Bank Recovery and Resolution Directive (BRRD), extended forms of market or lender of last resort (LLR) and new full system macro-prudential oversight mechanisms. The new focus on promoting 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

higher ethical standards has also to be fully supported especially through the enhanced enforcement of existing provisions, in particular, within PRIN, SYSC, COCON and now PRA FR 1-4 (for CRR, non-CRR and SII and non-SII firms). These deal with the core conditions that arose especially in terms of weak regulation, supervision, resolution, support and oversight and conduct. 

Whether many of the other more specific institutional and regulatory changes adopted have also been necessary is less clear. The institutional reforms undertaken in the UK with the replacement of the FSA by the FCA and PRA cannot be justified on the basis of the supposed ‘twin peaks’ moral (due to the substantial inconsistencies involved) although it is understandable in terms of the desire to create a new central bank based macro-prudential model with the delegation of non-systemic prudential and conduct functions to an independent agency. Other more specific reforms are also less clearly justifiable including excessive restrictions on remuneration packages (beyond risk insulation), structural regulation and ring-fencing (under the Independent Commission on Banking (ICB), EU Liikanen Report and US Volcker Rule and proposed UK banking standards in addition to the existing principles for businesses. Other more specific detailed provisions within CRD IV or the US Dodd Frank Act 2010 for non-prudential nature could also have been referred to. 

It is inevitable following a severe crises and substantial loss that there are significant political, public and media calls for reform. Such reforms are necessary provided that they are targeted and proportionate. Unnecessary, irrelevant and disproportionate amendments will simply undermine market function and innovation and increase cost and limit choice to the substantial disadvantage of customers, market counterparties and society as a whole. 

No specific answer was expected provided some of these issues were discussed and informed opinions expressed. 

- **C38.** The question focused on the difference between prudential and conduct regulation. This reflects the earlier ‘twin peaks’ division which was accepted by the Financial System Inquiry (Wallis Inquiry) in Australia in June 1996 which led to the establishment of the Australian Prudential Regulation Commission (later Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA)) and Corporations and Financial Services Commission (later Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC)). This was reflected in the UK with the creation of the PRA and FCA under the Financial Services Act 2012 with effect from 1 April 2013. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The core objective is to split prudential and conduct regulation on the basis that they are considered inconsistent and better managed by separate authorities focusing on the separate functions involved. Prudential regulation was principally concerned with stability and focuses on capital, liquidity, systems, governance and resolution. Conduct focuses on behaviour and customer or consumer protection. This division can be justified on the basis of separate focus, priority or mandate, inconsistent objectives, different needs and outcomes, dedicated and focused treatment and different disciplinary and enforcement requirements. The disadvantages include cost, complexity, obligation, management demand, duplication, policy conflict and inconsistent enforcement and sanction. Regulatory separation leads to overall regulatory fragmentation which fails to reflect the increasingly integrated and interconnected nature of modern financial markets. 

The UK model is also inconsistent in theoretical terms with the PRA only being responsible for the prudential regulation of banks, building societies, credit unions, insurance companies and designated investment firms with the FCA managing the prudential regulation of all other firms as well as the regulation of markets and exchanges in addition to other conduct matters. 

If the objective of the UK institutional reform was simply to create more effective and focused prudential and conduct regulation, this could have been more easily achieved by establishing a separate Prudential Regulation Division (PRD) and Conduct Regulation Division (CRD) within the FSA. This would have avoided the policy operational limitations referred to as well as the considerably more complex content of the FSMA following all of the statutory amendments required to split the FCA and PRA functions. Many of the other reforms considered necessary following the crisis had already also been adopted by the FSA and outgoing Labour administration before the Coalition government came into office in May 2010. 

While it is difficult to justify the split between the PRA and FCA in purely regulatory terms, the new UK model can be understood in light of the desire to establish a central bank based macro-prudential model with integrated systemic regulation. The decision was taken to replace the Financial Stability Committee (FSC) created under the Banking Act 2009 with the Financial Policy Committee established under the Financial Services Act 2012 and to transfer the larger prudential regulatory functions of the FSA to the PRA set up as a 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

subsidiary of the Bank of England. While a strong macro-prudential unit could have been established within the Bank of England and closer operational relations created between the Bank and the FSA, including possibly separate FSA prudential and conduct divisions, the decision was taken to re-transfer the prudential regulation of depository institutions from the FSA to the Bank with insurance companies and larger securities firms. 

This can then be explained in terms of a central bank based macro-prudential model with integrated systemic prudential regulation and the transfer of non-systemic functions to the independent FCA. One difficulty that had arisen was that the FSA board had become overloaded with non-prudential matters although this could have been dealt with separate prudential and conduct board committees with only final decisions being referred to the full board. 

Conduct regulation has become of increasing importance following the crisis with the large number of significant revelations of misconduct including with regard to payment protection insurance (PPI), interest rate swaps, money laundering breaches, sanction breaches, LIBOR, foreign exchange and alleged gold market fixing. This requires the development of a clear and coherent conduct risk policy. Whether this needs a separate institutional base is nevertheless still unclear. Institutional reform of itself rarely creates better regulation which can only be achieved through the adoption of appropriate supervisory and regulatory approaches with necessary funding and training of all relevant staff. 

No set answer was expected. These are difficult issues with many arguments for and against. It is also a subjective matter to form any conclusive opinion as to the effectiveness of the UK regulatory regime. 

- **C39.** Candidates were asked to comment on the potential value of the BSRC in promoting high ethical standards. The BSRC is to be established following the review conducted by Sir Richard Lambert with a consultation document being issued in February 2014 and final report in May 2014. This had been referred to in the Parliamentary Commission on Banking Standards (PCBS) final report on Changing Banking for Good (12 June 2013) with higher professional standards to be promoted in the banking area including through the development of a new set of core standards with the BSRC not acting as a lobby body. The BSRC would be funded by the industry although act in an independent manner. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Sir Richard Lambert recommended that participating institutions commit to a programme of continuous improvement of culture, competence and customer outcomes with various recommendations being made with regard to the BSRC specifically. It should establish standards of good practice, report annually, meet with nonexecutive directors and risk or reputation committee chairs, work with industry and stakeholders to develop a new code of practice, identify and encourage good practice and learning, development and leadership, assist compliance and work with professional bodies to improve training and qualifications. Nine specific recommendations were made with regard to purpose, governance, funding and reporting, arrangements, engagement, institutional relations, regulatory cooperation, raising standards of conduct over time, stakeholder involvement, training and avoidance of duplication of function. 

The principal advantage of the BSRC is that it will operate on a self-regulatory basis and benefit from industry experience, engagement, commitment, funding and voluntary enforcements which may promote public trust and confidence and placate media and political criticism. Difficulties nevertheless remain in terms of additional cost, regulatory overlap, standards conflict, consistency, weak enforcement and limited overall effectiveness. 

The question specifically asked for comment on the ability of the BSRC to increase ethical standards with a substantial body of ethical provisions already having been provided for under FCA PRIN, PRA Fundamental Rules, SYSC and COND, COCON and FIT, COBS, BCOBS and MCOB as well as with the statutory prohibitions imposed on market abuse, misleading statements and impressions and insider trading and general criminal and civil law. Many of the regulatory obligations imposed are principally based on integrity which is concerned with complying with expected high standards of professional conduct in any particular situation. Separate codes of ethics and standards are also applied by, for example, the CISI, Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA) and Association for Financial Professionals (AFP) and other professional bodies. 

While the PCBS had intended that ethical standards were applied by firms and individuals, it has been confirmed that the BSRC will only be involved with firm rather than individual standards. The establishment of separate standard in the banking as opposed to other financial areas will create significant inconsistency. The need for a separate standards body may also appear to be inconsistent with the specific policy of establishing the FCA as a 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

conduct regulator. Transferring banking standards to a separate body appears to contradict the basis for the new regulatory division and regime established under the Financial Services Act 2012. 

Unfortunately, neither the PCBS nor Banking Standards Review reports considered the meaning, nature, source and content of financial ethics and the relationship between the existing provisions currently applicable. The simple solution would have been for the FCA to prepare an Ethical sourcebook within its Handbook (possibly referred to as ETHIC) to clarify these essential points and to provide additional guidance on the application of these provisions in specific areas either in the new Ethics sourcebook or separately. 

No specific answers were expected with candidates only having to outline the various issues and arguments involved. 

- **C40.** This question asked candidates to discuss how financial regulation in the USA is organised compared with the UK, and the relative merits of these systems. This is an open-ended question in which a lot of material might be included. Within a strong answer we would expect the following: 

The financial regulatory system in the USA is characterised by its complexity and multiplicity of regulators, each with overlapping responsibilities. 

Key regulatory bodies and their areas of responsibility include: 

- The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), which oversees the securities markets, protecting investors, maintaining fair and efficient markets, and facilitating capital formation. 

- The Federal Reserve (Fed), which regulates and supervises bank holding companies, state-chartered banks that are members of the Federal Reserve System, and other institutions. 

- The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC), which is responsible for the regulation and supervision of national banks and federal savings associations. 

- The US Treasury, an executive agency responsible for promoting economic prosperity and ensuring the financial security of the US. 

- Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA), which is the largest independent regulator for all securities firms doing business in the US. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), which is responsible for the protection of consumers in the financial sector, overseeing banks, credit unions, securities firms, payday lenders, mortgage-servicing operations, foreclosure relief services, debt collectors, and other financial companies. 

- The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), which regulates commodity futures and options markets. 

This fragmented approach allows for a diverse and sometimes innovative regulatory environment, but it also leads to challenges such as regulatory gaps, overlapping jurisdiction, and increased complexity for financial institutions operating across state lines and different financial products. 

The second element of the answer should cover how financial services regulation is structured in the UK, noting that the UK’s financial regulatory system is more streamlined with fewer but more centralised regulators. 

The Financial Services and Markets Act (FSMA) 2000 is the cornerstone of financial regulation in the UK. The Act ensures a more coherent regulatory framework with clear demarcation of responsibilities and streamlined processes for regulatory oversight. 

Key regulatory bodies and their areas of responsibility should be mentioned, including: 

- The FCA, which regulates the conduct of financial firms to ensure markets function well, consumer protection, and competition. 

- The PRA, which is responsible for the prudential regulation and supervision of banks, building societies, credit unions, insurers, and major investment firms. 

- The Bank of England, which in addition to its monetary policy role, also oversees financial stability and macroprudential regulation through the Financial Policy Committee (FPC). 

- The Payment Systems Regulator (PSR), which has been tasked to ensure that payment systems are operated and developed in a way that considers and promotes the interests of all the businesses and consumers that use them. 

With regards to the relative merits of the US and UK system, it could be noted that: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The USA system, with multiple regulators, allows for specialised oversight tailored to different financial sectors (e.g., SEC for securities, CFTC for commodities). The complexity and gaps within the system can sometimes allow for innovative financial products and practices. However, overlapping responsibilities of regulators can lead to inefficiencies, regulatory arbitrage, and increased compliance costs for financial institutions. Differing regulatory standards and enforcement practices can also result in inconsistencies across states and financial sectors. 

The UK system is more centralised and having fewer regulators reduces complexity and makes regulatory processes more transparent and easier to navigate. It provides uniform standards and regulations across the financial sector, ensuring consistent enforcement and compliance. Clear division of roles between the FCA, PRA, and BoE ensures efficient regulation and oversight without significant overlap. However, the centralisation of regulatory power can sometimes lead to slower responses to emerging financial innovations and crises. 

There should be an acknowledgment that each system has its unique strengths and weaknesses, shaped by their respective regulatory philosophies and market needs. Reference could be made to The Edinburgh Reforms, which aim to make the UK’s regulatory framework more agile, competitive, and responsive to new challenges. 

**C41.** The creation of the new Senior Managers and Certification Regimes was recommended by the Parliamentary Commission for Banking Standards (PCBS) in “Changing Banking for Good” in June 2013. The new regime was provided for under Part 4 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013. This replaces the earlier approved persons regime for banks, building societies, credit unions and PRA-designated investment firms, although this would be extended to other firms subsequently. The new regime came into effect on 7 March 2016. The new offence of causing a financial institution to fail was also provided for under s 36 of the 2013 Act. The objective of the new senior managers regime is to ensure that senior managers are held accountable for any misconduct within their specific areas of responsibility. 

Firms have to prepare “responsibility maps” to allocate all relevant Senior Management functions (SMFs), with each manager having a statement of responsibility (SOR) (s60(2A) FSMA). Authorised persons must vet candidates before putting them forward for approval to ensure that they are fit and proper in terms of relevant qualifications, training, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

competence and personal characteristics (s60A). Conditions or time limits may be attached to approvals (s61(2B)) and approval may be varied subsequently (s 63ZB). Dual regulated firms are assessed by the PRA initially, although the PRA and FCA must both be satisfied that any applicant complies with the standards set out in the FSMA. Approved persons were earlier considered in terms of whether they were fit and proper having regard to whether they acted with honesty, integrity and reputation, competency and capability and financial soundness. 

The regime applies to persons approved under s59 FSMA to carry out a designated senior management function (SMF) as provided for under s 59ZA FSMA as amended. The PRA has inter alia designated the following senior management functions: Chief Executive (SMF 1); Chief Finance (SMF 2); Executive Director (SMF3); Chief Risk (SMF 4); Head of Internal Audit (SMF 5); Head of Key Business Area (SMF 6); Group Entity Senior Manager (SMF 7); Credit Union Senior Manager (SMF 8); Chairman (SMF 9); Chairman of Risk Committee (SMF 10); Chair of Audit Committee (SMF 11); Chair of Remuneration Committee (SMF 12); Chair of Nomination Committee (SMF 13); Senior Independent Director (SMF 14); Compliance Oversight (SMF 16); Money Laundering Reporting (SMF 17); Other Overall Responsibility (SMF 18); Head of Overseas Branch (incoming non-EEA branches only) (SMF 19); and Other Local Responsibility Function (incoming non-EEA branches only) (SMF 22) (PRA March 2016). 

A separate certification regime for deposit taking firms (other than credit unions) is created for other personnel that may pose a risk of significant harm to the firm or a customer. Firms are to certify the fitness and propriety of staff every year (s 63E FSMA) without pre-approval as under the earlier approved persons regime. The same conditions generally apply regarding certification as approval, including qualifications, training, competence and characteristics (s 63F). 

A new set of Conduct Rules (COCON) have been created (under s 64A FSMA), although these largely replicate the earlier APER rules. COCON contains five individual conduct (IC) rules covering integrity (COCON 2.1.1), skill, care and diligence (COCON 2.1.2), openness and co-operation with regulators (COCON 2.1.3 earlier APER 3), due regard to customers’ interests (COCON 2.1.4) and observance of proper standards of market conduct (COCON 2.1.5 earlier APER 3). COCON also contains four senior manager (SM) conduct rules covering taking reasonable steps to ensure business is controlled effectively (COCON 2.2.1 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

earlier APER 5), reasonable steps to ensure business complies with relevant regulatory requirements (COCON 2.2.3 earlier APER 7), reasonable steps to ensure appropriate delegation and oversight (COCON 2.2.3) and disclosure of all appropriate information to the FCA or PRA (COCON 2.2.4). Firms must ensure that all persons are properly notified of the COCON rules and take all reasonable steps to ensure that they understand how the rules apply to them (COCON 2.3.1), including through suitable training (COCON 2.3.2). COCON contains additional detailed guidance on the meaning of each of the rules (COCON 4), which expands the earlier guidance provided in the APER of Conduct of Practice (APER 3 and 4). 

Candidates could have commented on the potential value of the new regime in improving conduct standards. While this is intended to make senior managers more accountable, the senior manager pool is considerably smaller than under the approved persons regime. Certification also transfers responsibility for other approvals from the authorities to firms. International banks have argued that the additional personal liability will act as a significant disincentive to attracting the most appropriate talent in London. All senior managers have been advised to ensure that they are insured and fall within banks’ directors’ and officers’ insurance, and possibly to ask for indemnities in connection with potential regulatory action against the firm. All other significant staff have to be certified annually, with employment histories having to be considered for up to 6 years. This may, again, act as a significant disincentive to attracting high-quality staff. 

**C42.** Candidates were asked to explain the nature of the FCA’s approach to conduct risk and comment on the extent to which it has been able to create a clear and meaningful regulatory framework to contain this over time. The question could have been dealt with by explaining the statutory functions of the FCA and possibly contrasting these with the PRA. Relevant papers concerning the FCA's approach to conduct risk could have been noted and the significant uncertainties that remain in this regard. Reference could specifically been made to the FCA’s new policy risk model and relevant drivers as well as relevant priority areas of focus. Candidates could also have referred to other relevant high-level regulatory provisions on conduct risk contained in the FCA Handbook. 

The FCA has generally been made responsible for conduct regulation with the PRA managing prudential regulation under the revised institutional structure established under the FSA 2012. The FCA is specifically responsible for conduct risk although this is a difficult 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

issue to define precisely. The term conduct risk is not referred to in the FSMA as amended. Conduct risk is not separately defined in the FCA Glossary nor is the term risk separately defined. The objectives of the FCA are referred to under the FSMA in terms of acting as a financial function authority with the specific operational objectives of promoting consumer protection, system integrity and competition (s1B (2) and (3) FSMA). Conduct risk is not separately defined and it would generally appear that this refers to anything that is not prudential regulation. 

The FCA has been developing its approach to conduct risk under its 2013 and 2014 Risk Outlook documents with a series of priority actions being set out in its Business Plans for 2013 and 2014. While the 2013 Risk Outlook expressly referred to ‘conduct risk’, this is simply referred to in terms of risk in the 2014 Risk Outlook and Risk Outlook included within the 2015 Business Plan. The Risk Outlook was incorporated into the FCA Business Plan 2015/16 released on 24 March 2015. The FCA explains its prudential responsibility and prudential risk in its 2014 Risk Outlook at 65-67. 

The new policy risk model is generally made up of two parts with Drivers of Risk (Part A) consisting of Underlying drivers (inherent factors and Structures and business conduct features) and Environmental developments) and Evolving risk landscape (Part B) consisting of Cross-market pressures and related risks and Forward-looking areas of focus (2014 Risk Outlook P7). The key drivers are expanded in the circular diagram provided by the FCA in the 2013 and 2014 Risk Outlooks. This refers to Inherent risks (Information asymmetries, Biases (rules of thumb and mental shortcuts) and the growing importance of financial capability), Structures and business conduct (market structures, culture and incentives and conflicts of interest) and Environmental drivers (economic and market, technological and policy and regulatory) (FCA, Risk Outlook (2013) P10; and FCA, Risk Outlook (2014) P8. 

Seven forward looking areas of focus were identified in 2014 (technological developments, poor cultures and controls, firms to act against customers' best interest, retirement income products, consumer credit growth, complex terms and conditions and house price growth) which were revised in the 2015 Business Plan (to include technology, poor culture and controls, large back-books and pensions as well as consumer credit affordability, unfair contract terms and systems and controls with regard to financial crime). A number of more specific areas of concern are referred to under these headings. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Candidates may also have referred to relevant high level regulatory provisions covering conduct risk including PRIN 1 (integrity), 5 (market conduct), 6 (customers' interest), 7 (client communication), 8 (conflicts of interest), 9 (trust) and 10 (asset protection) as well as COCON 1 (integrity) and 3 (market conduct), SYSC 10 (conflicts of interest) and 19 (remuneration) as well as COND and FIT more generally. Training requirements may also have been referred to under TC as well as Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) and the Retail Distribution Review (RDR). 

This is a difficult area and no specific answer was expected. 

- **C43.** Candidates were asked to assess the extent to which financial firms have to consider the effects of possible biological pandemics and other extreme events that may impact financial markets in reassessing ethical and cultural frameworks, following the global financial crisis and the Coronavirus (COVID-19) crisis. 

Firms would generally have to comply with all existing relevant provisions including PRIN 1 (integrity), PRIN 6 (customers’ interests), PRIN 7 (communications), PRIN 8 (avoiding conflicts of interest) and PRIN 9 (trust), as well as possibly PRIN 10 (protecting client assets). They must also continue to treat customers fairly at all times. Parallel measures are imposed under the PRA Fundamental Rules. Individuals would be subject to equivalent provisions under COCON. 

The FCA has issued specific guidance in relation to the COVID-19 crisis (https://www.fca.org.uk/coronavirus). The FCA has explained its expectations of firms (https://www.fca.org.uk/coronavirus/information-firms) with information having been made available for consumers dealing with financial difficulties during COVID-19 

(https://www.fca.org.uk/consumers/dealing-financial-difficulties-coronavirus). Separate information has been provided by the Bank of England and PRA 

(https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/coronavirus/information-for-firms). The Financial Stability Board (FSB) sent a letter to the G20 on FSB which sets out action to maintain financial stability during COVID-19 (15 July 2020), with a separate report on the COVID-19 pandemic: Financial stability implications and policy measures taken – Report to the G20 (15 July 2020 and subsequently updated). Various statements have been produced by the FSB and other international bodies (see generally https://www.fsb.org/work-of-thefsb/covid-19-maintaining-financial-stability/). A substantial amount of material has since 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

been published, with one of the principal difficulties being to ensure that this is sufficiently easy to access, understand and apply in practice. Candidates could have referred to some of this in further detail. 

Candidates could have outlined the FCA and PRA existing treatment of financial ethics in modern financial markets. Candidates could have explained the background to PRIN and the Fundamental Rules (FRs) within the larger handbooks and listed each of these with the key requirement being to act with integrity. While no separate guidance is provided on this by the FCA or PRA regarding firms, additional information was provided under APER and now COCON 4 with regard to the meaning of the main equivalent provisions in connection with individuals. 

Rather than focus on ethics, the FCA has shifted its examination to firm culture. Tracey McDermott gave a speech on ‘Wholesale Conduct Risk’ before the British Bankers Association on 13 June 2015. Andrew Bailey, former FCA Chief Executive Officer between July 2016 and March 2020 and previously Chief Executive of the PRA, delivered a speech on ‘Culture in Financial Services – A Regulator’s Perspective’ at the City Week 2016 Conference on 9 May 2016. Andrew Bailey stressed the importance of culture, with culture being of “the utmost importance to financial regulators”. Culture is dependent on a range of contributory forces including management and governance, remuneration and incentive structure, management quality and effectiveness and personnel adoption and adherence. Many of the recent major prudential or conduct scandals arose from cultural failures in governance, remuneration, risk management and leadership. 

Andrew Bailey has stated that the authorities could not “determine the culture of firms”, which would not work, and could not “write a regulatory rule that settles culture” with this being the product of many factors that the regulators could influence including governance, remuneration and risk management. There was “no magic bullet to change culture”, although the new senior managers and certification regime would assist in embedding “the notion of personal responsibility for the affairs of the firm at the level of senior management.” It was “not the job of regulators to enforce culture and to change culture”, with firm incentives determining culture and with the authorities attempting “to align those incentives with our public interest objectives as well as the private interest of the firm.” Andrew Bailey separately addressed the HKMA Annual Conference for Independent NonExecutive Directors in Hong Kong on 16 March 2017, the Transforming Culture in Financial 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Services Conference in London on 19 March 2018 and the Investment Association Culture Conference, Mansion House, London on 6 November 2018. 

Candidates could have referred to other FCA work on culture and governance. The FCA expects financial leaders to manage the drivers of behaviour in their firms in order to create and maintain cultures which reduce the potential for harm (FCA, ‘Culture and governance’ (3 August 2020 available https://www.fca.org.uk/firms/culture-and-governance). Culture is stated to be at “the heat” of how the FCA authorises and supervises firms. Culture is referred to as “the habitual behaviours and mindsets that characterise an organisation” with the FCA focusing on the 4 key drivers of purpose, leadership, rewards and management and governance. The FCA published a collection of 28 papers on “Transforming Culture in Financial Services” in March 2018 (DP 18/2) which examined what good culture might look like, the role of regulation and regulators, how firms might promote incentives and how to change behaviour for the better. This was followed by a Transforming Culture Conference and the publication of an “Event summary and next steps” paper on 19 March 2018. 

The FCA Business Plan for 2020/21 has highlighted the importance of the crisis. Five key priorities were listed for the following 1-3 years: transforming how the FCA works and regulates, enabling effective consumer investment decisions, ensuring that credit markets work well, making payments safe and accessible and delivering fair value in a digital age (FCA Business Plan for 2020/21 (7 April 2020)). The FCA’s crisis response had been intended to ensure that markets continue to function in an orderly manner during a major repricing event, and to issue emergency guidance to allow government schemes to operate, support consumers, assist public access to essential banking services and protect the most vulnerable within society. The FCA would continue to update its operations in response to unfolding events and inform stakeholders appropriately. Megan Butler, FCA Executive Director of Supervision – Investment, Wholesale and Specialists, gave a speech on the crisis on 4 June 2020 (“The FCA’s response to Covid-19 and expectations for 2020” at the PIMFA’s Virtual Festival). While advisers and wealth managers had responded well to the crisis, attention had to shift from “incident response” to longer term impact, with the FCA focusing on operational resilience, financial resilience and acting with integrity. The FCA specifically criticised firms that had attempted to avoid customer liabilities by closing companies and setting up new ones. Certain specific crisis response measures were identified based on ensuring a good level of operational resilience, understanding firms’ financial resilience to allow them to fail in an orderly 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

manner, ensuring that markets functioned effectively through price formation and orderly trading, treating customers fairly and ensuring that customers were aware of and protected from scams. 

Candidates could also have referred to earlier material in this area. A number of general documents and speeches have been issued on ethics over time. The FSA's initial general approach to ethics was set out in ‘FSA, An ethical framework for financial services’ (October 2002) with competence being examined in ‘FSA, Competence and ethics’ (June 2010) CP 10/12. Ethics was considered as part of the Retail Distribution Review (RDR) with the FSA recommending the creation of an enforceable Code of Ethics for investment advisors in ‘FSA, Retail Distribution Review’ (November 2008) Feedback Statement 08/06. This was referred to again in ‘FSA, Distribution of retail investments: Delivering the RDR’ (June 2009) CP 09/18 with eight general principles proposed. These can be summarised in terms of acting honestly and fairly at all times, integrity, legal and regulatory compliance, market integrity standards, conflict, professional competence, declining to act and upholding the highest personal and professional standards. FSA Chairman, Howard Davies, discussed ethics in “Ethics in Regulation” in 2001. 

**C44.** Candidates were asked to comment on the significance of the new statutory provisions adopted on misleading statements and impressions under the FSMA as amended. The earlier provisions contained in s397 on misleading statements and practices have been replaced by the new offences of misleading statements and misleading impressions in ss89 and 90 Financial Services Act 2012 as well as the new provisions on misleading statements and impressions in relation to benchmarks in s91 FSA 2012. 

s89 applies with regards to making statements known to be false or misleading in a material respect, being reckless as to whether the statement is false or misleading or dishonestly concealing any material facts in relation to the statement (s89 (1) and (2)). It is also an offence to make a statement or conceal facts to induce another person to enter or not enter into a relevant agreement or exercise rights under a relevant investment. Offences are available for price stabilisation, control of information and buyback programmes. s89 no longer refers to promises or forecasts nor to deceptive statements. Separate offences apply with regards to misleading impressions under s90 where a person intends to create a false or misleading impression with regards to the market in relevant investments or the price or value of the investments (s90 (1) FSA)). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

This is extended to apply to intent to acquire, dispose of or subscribe for or underwrite investments or exercise rights under the investment or not and where the impression may generate a gain or cause loss to another person (s90 (2) and (3)). Exemptions are provided or under (ss89 (3) and 90 (9)). 

A new offence is created of making false or misleading statements in connection with benchmark arrangements with the intention that the statements are used to set the benchmark knowing that or being reckless as to the statement being false or misleading (s91 (1)). A separate offence is created where a person acts or engages in any course of conduct which creates a false or misleading impression as to the price or value of an investment or the appropriate interest rate (s91 (2)). Equivalent exemptions again apply (s91 (3)). A person guilty of any offence under Part 7 is liable for up to ten years imprisonment on indictment or a fine or both (s92 (1)) – this penalty increasing with the passing of FSA 2021 from the original seven years. 

Reference could also have been made to the civil provisions on market abuse contained in the Market Abuse Regulation and separate offences on insider dealing in the Criminal Justice Act 1993. A short overview of the relevant offences, defences and penalties should have been provided. Candidates should also have referred to the different burdens of proof with the civil market abuse and criminal misleading statements and impressions and insider dealing offences. 

Market manipulation would also clearly be contrary to FCA PRIN 1 (integrity), PRA FR 1, COCON 1 and COCON 5 as well arguably as PRIN 5 (market conduct), 6 (customers' interests) or PRIN 12 (Consumer Duty), possibly 8 (conflicts of interest), 9 (trust) and possibly 10 (client assets). 

**C45.** The FCA’s Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) strategy was published in November 2021. This strategy was to build further trust and transparency in the area of ESG investing and other associated areas. The strategy is based on five core themes. For the workbook these are recorded as: 

- Transparency – promoting transparency on climate change and wider sustainability along the value chain. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- Trust – building trust and integrity in ESG-labelled instruments, products, and the supporting ecosystem. 

- Tools – working with others to enhance industry capabilities and support firms’ management of climate-related and wider sustainability risks, opportunities, and impacts. 

- Transition – supporting the role of finance in delivering a market-led transition to a more sustainable economy. 

- Team – developing strategies, organisational structures, resources, and tools to support the integration of ESG into FCA activities. 

These principles are important as they are part of the CISI Code of Conduct in Principle 7. This states a CISI member needs to “Respect Others and the Environment – to treat everyone fairly and with respect, supporting opportunity for all, embracing diversity and inclusion and ensuring that the environmental impact of your work is considered.” More generally moving towards more sustainable and green methods of doing business reflects wider global challenges faced by businesses across the world. Despite this importance there are substantial concerns that ‘green washing’ can occur in the representation, development and marketing of financial assets linked to ESG objectives. This misrepresentation of the green credentials of financial assets is seen both in the UK and globally. This outcome has been attributed to a corrosive influence on this market for green and sustainable financial assets undermining trust and constraining growth in these markets. 

It may be helpful to consider further regulatory publications in the areas such as the FCA discussion paper on sustainability disclosure requirements and investment labels 2021, and the 2021 FCA policy statement on enhanced climate-related disclosures for asset managers, life insurers and FCA-regulated pension providers. One mark was awarded for each valid point raised up to 15 marks. Other relevant content was also awarded marks. 

- **C46.** FinTech refers to the use of new computing and digital technology to support and improve the structure and operation of banking and financial markets and the delivery of banking and financial services. This includes digital banking and payment, electronic securities trading (including algorithmic and high-frequency trading), digital insurance (including robo advice) as well as the increased use of automation and robotics (including chatbots), digital and cloud delivery, big data analytics, machine reading and learning and artificial 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

intelligence (AI). This also includes distributed ledger technology (DLT) and blockchain with digital currencies such as Bitcoin. DLT is principally based on decentralisation and disintermediation, access cryptography and transaction hashing and verification mining and reconciliation based on computer code rather than traditional forms of law and regulation. 

Regulatory technology (RegTech) refers to the use of technology for compliance purposes (including risk management and reporting) with authorities also using technology to improve supervisory contact and conduct (SupTech). The FCA launched Project Innovate October 2014 to promote the development of technological innovation within the financial services area. This includes supporting firms through its Innovation Hub, Advice Unit and Regulatory Sandbox (opened in May 2016) which allows firms to test innovative new products, services, business models and delivery mechanisms in real market conditions without the threat of regulatory sanction. Potential advantages arise through increased efficiency with lower costs, improved marketing, enhanced performance and fund management, innovative distribution, more efficient back office processing and improved customer service more generally. 

A significant number of new challenges nevertheless arise from a technology, business model, consumer, user and stakeholder, markets, regulation and governance, infrastructure, central banking and monetary policy and financial stability perspective. These include technology risk and business and model failure, excessive outsourcing, technology dependence, network collapse, business dislocation and fragmentation, supervisory and regulatory dilution and defect and the possibility of increased speed and volume of loss transmission and overall financial instability and systemic collapse. More specific regulatory threats can be identified in terms of the impact of new technology on the FCA’s consumer protection, integrity and competition objectives and the PRA’s soundness, policyholder and competition objectives. The degree of disruption that may arise in the financial services industry cannot be predicted with certainty with the UK regulatory and supervisory framework having been set up in response to past non-technology related threats. It remains unclear to what extent the authorities would be able to respond to any future technology induced or aggravated crises that arise. 

No specific answers were expected with candidates only having to outline the various issues and arguments involved. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C47.** Currently the regulation of the trade in crypto assets is in a state of flux, with widespread consultation as to regulatory changes both in the UK and internationally. This market has raised major consumer protection concerns particularly in relation to the inappropriate sales and promotion of crypto assets. These activities have been linked with significant consumer detriment. The market is also poorly defined with crypto assets including a wide range of different and distinct products, which have changed frequently over time. 

Moreover, there are concerns that some of the trade in some crypto assets may be associated with organised crime and could raise money laundering concerns. These are reflected in the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 where businesses carrying out certain crypto asset activities will need to comply with the regulations in relation to those activities and register with the FCA. There is also EU law in this area including the Sixth Money Laundering Directive (6MLD). 

In January 2022, the FCA published a consultation paper on strengthening the financial promotion rules for high-risk investments, which include crypto assets, and in August 2022, the FCA published its final rules. 

In its consultation, the FCA proposed to make changes in the following areas: 

- Classification of high-risk investments 

- Readily realisable securities 

- Restricted mass market investments 

- Non-mass market investments 

- The consumer journey into high-risk investments 

- Strengthen the role of firms approving and communicating financial promotions 

- Applying the financial promotion rules to qualifying crypto-assets. 

From 1 December 2022, the following requirements apply in relation to risk warnings for financial promotions of high-risk investments: 

- Promotion of non-mass market investments (NMMI) is banned to retail investors 

- Promotion of restricted mass market investments (RMMI) is subject to certain restrictions to retail investors 

- Non-readily realisable securities and peer-to-peer agreements 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- Crypto-assets were also envisaged being in this category, but this will follow at a later stage 

- Enhanced requirements in terms of risk warnings (where the customer journey into high-risk investments begins) 

- Risk warnings: stating that investors could lose all of their money invested 

- Risk summaries: prescribed risk summaries – should take no more than two minutes to read 

- Firm can diverge from this if there are valid reasons for doing so 

- Banning incentives to invest: the ban of monetary or non-monetary benefits (ie refer a friend or new joiner bonuses) 

- Cooling off period: minimum 24-hour cooling off period for first-time investors with a firm 

- FCA would like to see firms applying higher standards than the minimum regulatory standards 

- Personalised risk warnings: first-time investors with a firm must be provided with a personalised risk warning that must include the client’s name 

- Client categorisation: retail clients must self certify they meet the criteria to receive promotions on high-risk investments. Retail clients must provide evidence as to why they believe they meet the relevant criteria, ie, a statement on their income/assets. 

The following Handbook guidance applied from 1 February 2023: 

- Enhanced requirements for firms approving and communicating financial promotions 

- Displaying the name of the approver and date of approval on the financial promotion 

- Potential for use of firms' FRN if due to limits on space 

- Approvers must have appropriate levels of competence and expertise in the type of investment/service being promoted 

- Approvers must take an active role in ensuring that the promotion remains compliant while being used 

- Approvers take reasonable steps to ensure compliance with appropriateness rules periodically through the life of the promotion 

- Enhanced conflict of interest requirements for approvers 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- Approvers must undertake preliminary suitability assessments before NMMIs are promoted to retail investors. 

One mark was awarded for each valid point raised up to 15 marks. 

**C48.** The question was concerned with the characters and content of the EU Capital 

Requirements Directive (CRD) IV package of measures. CRD 4 consists of the CRD 2013/36/EU and Regulation EU575/2013. These generally implement the Basel III capital, liquidity and leverage revisions within the EU and which will, in turn, be implemented within all of the EU member states. This directive was ‘onshored’ into UK law through the EU (Withdrawal) Act 2018. 

Basel 3 was finalised in 2010 and contains the new Basel Committee provisions governing capital, liquidity and leverage. Capital adequacy is generally increased from 2% to 4.5% with additional capital buffers been provided for including a conservation buffer (2.5%), countercyclical buffer (0–2.5%) and systemic risk buffer (1–3.5%). The new liquidity requirements include a short Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) and longer-term Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR). The leverage ratio consists of a 3% of own funds limit on total debt exposure. 

This followed earlier Basel 1 in 1988, which established the minimum 8% ratio of capital to risk-adjusted assets for all internationally active banks, and Basel 2, which established the three pillars of minimum capital requirements (including credit risk (standardised, foundation and advanced internal ratings-based (IRB) approaches), market risk (standardised and models) and operational risk), supervisory review and market disclosure. These were revised following the global financial crisis to create the Basel 3 amendments. 

These are implemented under the CRD and CRR with provisions involving national implementation being set out in the CRD and the more detailed common provisions which require no domestic adjustment contained in the CRR. These will be expanded through European Banking Authority (EBA) implementing measures. The CRD measures require separate UK implementation under the PRA Rulebook (with separate measures for CRR and non-CRR firms) and FCA Handbook (BIPRU) with the CRR being directly applicable as UK law and simply implemented on a copy out basis. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The CRD and CRR will form a key part of the European Banking Union (EBU) and Capital Markets Union (CMU). A number of steps have been taken to establish the EBU following the European financial crisis in 2009–2010 with this generally being based on the creation of a single Banking Rule Book (EBR) supported by a Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM) and Single Resolution Mechanism (SRM), which includes the Bank Recovery and Resolution Directive (BRRD) 2014/59/EU, supported by the revisions to the Deposit Guarantee Schemes Directive (DGSD) 94/19/EC. The EBR essentially consists of the CRD and CRR and relevant implementing EBA technical standards. 

Candidates could also have commented on the value of the new increased capital, liquidity and leverage measures. The purpose of capital is to provide a means of loss absorption for firms to prevent them from becoming insolvent. Liquidity ensures that firms maintain sufficient cash or readily realisable assets on an ongoing basis to cover ongoing liabilities. Leverage limits total debt exposure, which was a significant factor in the global financial crisis. The effect of these requirements is substantially to improve the stability of financial firms and prevent contagion. This regulatory adjustment is supported by enhanced supervision with new resolution procedures, emergency support facilities and wider macro prudential oversight. All of this should limit the possibility of a future crisis arising or, at minimum, the more stable and effective management of the crisis. 

- **C49.** The question focused on the effects of the production of a separate PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook. The background to this is that the PRA and FCA had applied the earlier FSA Handbook of Rules and Guidance on a combined basis from the establishment of the new regulatory regime under the Financial Services Act 2012 in April 2013 until the production of the PRA Rulebook in 2015. The Rulebook and Handbook are created through the production of ‘rule-making instruments’ by the PRA and FCA under s 138G of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 as amended with the FCA having an express rulemaking power under s 137A and the PRA under s 137G. 

The creation of a separate PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook is considered to be a natural extension of the split between prudential and conduct regulatory functions under the current UK regime. This can be justified on the basis that prudential and conduct regulation are considered inconsistent and better managed by separate authorities focusing on the separate functions involved. Prudential regulation is principally concerned with stability and 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

focuses on capital, liquidity, systems, governance and resolution. Conduct focuses on behaviour and customer or consumer protection. 

This division can be justified based on separate focus, priority or mandate, inconsistent objectives, different needs and outcomes, dedicated and focused treatment and different disciplinary and enforcement requirements. The disadvantages include increased cost, complexity, obligation, management demand, duplication, policy conflict and inconsistent enforcement and sanction. 

The other difficulty that arises is that regulatory separation may lead to more general regulatory fragmentation, which fails to reflect the increasingly integrated and interconnected nature of modern financial markets. This may specifically arise with regard to dual-regulated firms, which are subject to the PRA eight fundamental rules (FR) and twelve FCA principles for businesses (PRIN). While this could become a problem in practice where common provisions are interpreted differently, this may not create any difficulty if the PRA and FCA adopt a common interpretation. 

Care also has to be taken in assessing the degree of possible fragmentation elsewhere within the separate substantive provisions applied. PRA-regulated firms are subject to the PRA provisions with FCA firms having to comply with the FCA’s measures. The distinct sets of firms concerned are subject to separate prudential requirements. Distinct prudential requirements are therefore applied to dual regulated and non-dual regulated firms. Many of these are also now based on other prudential regulatory requirements, including the directly applicable CRR measures and equivalent Solvency II insurance rules, which further limit contradiction and inconsistency. 

The principal difficulties may then only remain in non-prudential areas for dual-regulated firms, which correspond with the conduct risk scope of FCA jurisdiction. As noted, this may principally arise with regard to the overlapping FR and PRIN provisions although this may again not cause any substantial difficulties in practice providing the regulators adopt a common interpretation. 

No set answer was expected. These are difficult issues with many arguments for and against – and due credit is given to candidates setting out and justifying their views on such arguments. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- **C50.** The Coronavirus crisis 2020 highlighted the importance of the need to manage endogenous (internal) as well as exogenous (external) shocks. These may include biological (pandemic) events, natural disasters (earthquakes and tsunami), climatic impacts (floods and droughts) as well as terrorism and cyber terrorism, warfare and nuclear threats. The question could either have been answered either by referring to the general responses adopted following the Global Financial Crisis or specific measures targeted at the coronavirus crisis. 

More general earlier responses included the FSA’s Supervisory Enhancement Programme (SEP) and credible deterrence enforcement initiative, enhanced capital, liquidity and leverage (under Basel 3 and EU CRD 4 and CRR), College Supervision, Resolution and Recovery Planning (RRPs) and Special Resolution Regimes (SRRs), management accountability and liability (including under the UK Senior Managers and Certification Regime) and new micro-prudential regimes (including with the UK Financial Policy Committee (MPC), European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB) and US Financial Stability Oversight Committee (FSOC)). Reference could also have been made to the revision of the FSMA and establishment of the new institutional structure within the UK, with the creation of the FCA and PRA, as well as possibly with the introduction of structural regulation (ring fencing) although separate issues arise with this. 

As much detail as possible could have been provided for the highest marks including reference to the specific legislative measures involved. It is arguable that all these measures substantially assisted ensure that financial markets and firms were more stable and resilient and capable of withstanding such shocks as the Coronavirus crisis. Other market and regulatory initiatives were adopted in response to the Coronavirus specifically while other existing systems and controls and continuity planning arrangements allowed firms to continue to operate. 

While a number of regulatory adjustments and concessions were announced by the authorities, in particular, to allow the Government’s market support measures to operate, markets and firms to ensure that they could continue to operate. The UK government announced a number of market support measures including the Corporate Financing Facility, Business Interruption Loan Scheme, Large Business Interruption Loan Scheme, Job Retention (Furlough) scheme, Term Funding Scheme, Business Rate Reductions, 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Small Business Grant Fund, retail, hospitality and leisure support, Business Support grants, Future Fund and Post-16 Provider Relief as well as ‘Eat Out to Help Out’ scheme. A series of regulatory initiatives were announced. These included maintaining earlier Systemic Risk Buffer (SRB) rates, modification of leverage ratios, reporting in disclosure amendments, governance concessions for senior managers including on furloughing functions and delayed implementation of Basel III.1 by one year (Bank of England, ‘Our response to coronavirus (Covid-19): regulatory measures for PRA firms’ available https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/coronavirus/information-for-firms). Other measures were announced by the FCA including in relation to consumer credit relief, listed company financing, pension and retirement income and client asset controls (FCA, ‘Coronavirus (Covid-19)’ available https://www.fca.org.uk/coronavirus). 

The Coronavirus crisis also highlighted the need for firms to ensure that they can operate on a continuing basis in the event of any market or social disruption arising. This is concerned with maintaining appropriate systems and controls with business continuity dealt with under SYSC 3.2.19 and operational risk dealt with under SYSC 13 including external events and other changes in SYSC 13.8. Firms should have in place appropriate arrangements, having regard to the nature, scale and complexity of the business, to ensure that they can continue to function and meet the regulatory obligations in the event of unforeseen interruption which arrangements are regularly updated and tested to ensure effectiveness (SYSC 3.2.19). 

Firms must also maintain appropriate Business Continuity Planning (BCP) arrangements which allow them to continue operating, including through remote staff working, as well as ensure continued compliance with all relevant regulatory obligations (SYSC 13.8). Firms should consider the likelihood and impact of a disruption on the continuity of operations from unexpected events. This may include disruptions arising through the loss or failure of internal and external resources (including people, systems and assets), information loss or corruption and other external events (including vandalism, war and acts of God) (SYSC 13.8.5). 

The firm should adopt appropriate arrangements to maintain the continuity of its operations and reduce the likelihood of disruption (including through succession planning, systems resilience and dual processing) and the impact of the disruption (including through contingency arrangements and insurance) (SYSC 13.8.6). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The strategy for maintaining operational continuity and plans for communicating and testing the strategy should be for properly documented. This would include formal business continuity plans to deal with short, medium and long term disruptions (including resource requirements, recovery priorities and communication arrangements), escalation and invocation plans (including implementation processes and contact information), information integrity validation and appropriate review and update processes having regard to the firm’s operations and risk profile (SYSC 13.8.7). Firms should assess the appropriateness of any alternative sites used including with regard to the location, speed of recovery and adequacy resources and any issues that arise with regard to shared use (SYSC 13.8.8). Separate provisions apply with regard to outsourcing (SYSC). The Bank of England monitors business continuity and operational resilience within the UK financial sector with the FCA and Treasury with responses coordinated through the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) and National Crime Agency (NCA) (https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/financial-stability/financial-sector-continuity). The objectives are to ensure that retail and wholesale markets remain open and functioning, there is an orderly and early return to trading in the event of closure, involve infrastructure providers and market participants and relevant decision taking and facilitate market initiatives. The UK has also adopted the Civil Contingencies Act 2004, which replaced the Civil Defence Act 1948. 

Candidates could have referred to any of the above. No specific answer was expected. 

- **C51.** The FCA and PRA are required under Part 6 of the Financial Services Act 2012 to maintain a complaints scheme for the investigation of complaints arising in connection with the exercise of, or failure to exercise, any of their relevant functions and to appoint an independent person (referred to as the Complaints Commissioner) to be responsible for the conduct of investigations in accordance with the complaints scheme (the Scheme) (http://www.fscc.gov.uk/). The Scheme came into effect on 1 April 2013. 

The Office of the Complaints Commissioner is headed by the Financial Regulators Complaints Commissioner. The Complaints Commissioner considers grievances against the FCA, PRA, the Payment System Regulator and the Bank of England’s oversight of clearing houses and settlement systems. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

Complaints can be made by anyone who is directly affected by the way in which the regulators have carried out their functions, or anyone acting directly on such a person’s behalf, if the complaint meets the requirements of the Scheme. To be eligible to make a complaint under the Scheme, a person must be seeking a remedy (which for this purpose may include an apology) in respect of some inconvenience, distress or loss, which the person has suffered because of being directly affected by the regulators’ actions or inaction (http://fscc.gov.uk/complaints-scheme/ ). Complaints should be made within 12 months of the date on which the complainant first became aware of the circumstances giving rise to the complaint. Complaints made later than this will be investigated under the Scheme only if the complainant can show reasonable grounds for the delay (http://fscc.gov.uk/complaints-scheme/). 

Complaints Commissioner publishes a response to each complaint which begin on 17 December 2013 and which are made available to the public with the regulator’s response (see, for example, https://www.fca.org.uk/about/complain-about-regulators/complaintscommissioners-final-reports). Complaints Commissioner also publishes Annual Reports (https://frccommissioner.org.uk/publications/annual-reports/ ). 

Most complaints relate to the conduct of the FCA with 90% of complaints being settled with her been referred to the Complaints Commissioner with the Commissioner upholding the FCA’s action in the majority of instances. 275 complaints were dealt with in 2019/20. 63 complaints substantive responses were issued with 49 regulatory decisions upheld and seven not upheld with five been partially upheld. 26 recommendations were made for corrective action, 14 for compensation and five for an apology with 33 of these were accepted by the FCA and three rejected (Complaints Commissioner, Reviewing how the financial services regulators consider complaints Annual Report (2019/20) 7 and 8). Only two complaints were considered against the PRA and one against the Bank of England. Matters considered included the accuracy of registers, firm supervision and delays in regulatory processes and lack of candour on failings and impact. 

The Commissioner has stated that the scheme is not considered to have been operating satisfactorily in all respects (Annual Report (2019/20) 4). The FCA complaints’ function was not able to cope with the volume and complexity of work, significant regulatory issues had not been identified despite investigations with recommendations for improvement had 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

been adopted especially in terms of compensation and prioritising complainants’ needs. Work was being undertaken to correct the omissions. 

There had also been no effective prompt and public system to ensure quality assurance. The Commissioner recommended making the scheme more readily understandable to complainant’s and clarifying compensation policy, providing the Commissioner with a wider role in ensuring quality assurance and publication of the work undertaken by the FCA to review its functions and deal with concerns. 

Complaints about financial firms rather than one of the regulators should be referred to the company concerned and then the Financial Ombudsman Service.
