The purpose of the Ombudsman scheme under the FSMA is to provide for the resolution of ‘certain disputes’ quickly and with minimum formality by an independent person (s 225(1)) The scheme is to be administered by a body corporate with further direction being provided with regard to jurisdiction and operation in Part XVI and Schedule 17 FSMA. 

Complainants should generally use the financial institution’s internal scheme first before making a separate reference to the Ombudsman. The Ombudsman may direct financial institutions to take any necessary action or make financial awards up to £355,000. The Ombudsman scheme is generally dealt with under the Dispute Resolution: Complaints sourcebook (DISP) with compensation under COMP within the Redress block of the FCA Handbook. 

- **C52.** Candidates were asked to discuss the extent to which the promotion of ethical standards in the UK has bene impacted by recent crisis including the Coronavirus crisis 2020. Candidates should have explained the background to PRIN and the Fundamental Rules (FRs) within the larger handbooks and listed each of these. Candidates might have noted that some of these requirements are more prudential or asset rather than conduct rule based with the requirements most principally concerned with ethics including integrity, skill, care and diligence, interest trust, conflicts and communication disclosure (PRIN 1, 2, 6, 7, 8 and 9 and FR 1 and 2). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The key requirement in the ethics area is with regard to integrity. While no separate guidance is provided on this by the FCA or PRA with regard to firms, additional information is provided under COCON with regard to the meaning of the main equivalent provisions in connection with individuals. 

Rather than focus on ethics, the FCA has shifted the examination to firm culture. Tracey McDermott gave a speech on ‘Wholesale Conduct Risk’ before the British Bankers Association on 13 June 2015. Andrew Bailey, former FCA Chief Executive Officer between July 2016 and March 2020 and previously Chief Executive of the PRA, gave a speech on ‘Culture in Financial Services – A Regulator’s Perspective’ at the City Week 2016 Conference on 9 May 2016. Andrew Bailey stressed the importance of culture with culture being of ‘the utmost importance to financial regulators’. Culture arose from a range of contributory forces including management and governance, remuneration and incentive structure, management quality and effectiveness and personnel adoption and adherence. All of the recent major prudential or conduct scandals arose from cultural failures in governance, remuneration, risk management and leadership. 

Andrew Bailey stated that the authorities could not ‘determine the culture of firms’, which would not work, and could not ‘write a regulatory rule that settles culture’ with this being the product of many factors that the regulators could influence including governance, remuneration and risk management. There was ‘no magic bullet to change culture’ although the new senior managers and certification regime would assist in embedding ‘the notion of personal responsibility for the affairs of the firm at the level of senior management.’ It was ‘not the job of regulators to enforce culture and to change culture’ with firm incentives determining culture with the authorities attempting ‘to align those incentives with our public interest objectives as well as the private interest of the firm.’ Andrew Bailey separately addressed the HKMA Annual Conference for Independent NonExecutive Directors in Hong Kong on 16 March 2017, the Transforming Culture in Financial Services Conference in London on 19 March 2018 and the Investment Association Culture Conference, Mansion House, London on 6 November 2018. The FCA expects financial leaders to manage the drivers of behaviour in their firms to create and maintain cultures which reduce the potential for harm (FCA, ‘Culture and governance’ (3 August 2020 available https://www.fca.org.uk/firms/culture-andgovernance). Culture is stated to be at ‘the heart’ of how the FCA authorises and supervises firms. Culture is referred to as ‘the habitual behaviours and mind-sets that 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

characterise an organisation’ with the FCA focusing on four key drivers of purpose, leadership, rewards and management and governance. The FCA published a collection of 28 papers on ‘Transforming Culture in Financial Services’ in March 2018 (DP 18/2) which examined what good culture might look like, the role of regulation and regulators, how firms might promote incentives and how to change behaviour for the better. This was followed by a Transforming Culture Conference and publication of an ‘Event summary and next steps; paper on 19 March 2018. 

The Coronavirus crisis 2020 could influence culture in relation to how firms treat customers, other financial institutions and possibly their own staff. The FCA and PRA issued a number of statements on regulatory adjustments and concessions as a result of the crisis (FCA, ‘Coronavirus (Covid-19) available https://www.fca.org.uk/coronavirus; and Bank of England, ‘Our response to Coronavirus (Covid-10)’ available https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/coronavirus). 

The FCA published an Insight article on ‘Conduct, culture and covid-19’ on 10 June 2020 (available https://www.fca.org.uk/insight/conduct-culture-and-covid-19). This noted that the crisis had changed the world and the way that people work within our short time with people having to adapt to the changes. While this created challenges and risks, this was also an opportunity to move from a crisis management approach to considering how businesses should be run more effectively with a strong focus on culture. Firms that failed to do so would produce poor conduct, low staff morale and weak future performance. Those that succeeded would generate positive outcomes for customers, employees and investors. A number of key themes were identified including clear purpose and direction, leadership integrity, risk and opportunity identification, psychological safety, continued diversity and inclusion, support employees and think, plan, experiment and adapt. 

The FCA Business Plan for 2020/21 highlighted the importance of the crisis. Five key priorities were listed for the following 1-3 years based on transforming how the FCA works and regulates, enabling effective consumer investment decisions, ensuring that credit markets work well, making payments safe and accessible and delivering fair value in a digital age (FCA Business Plan for 2020/21 (7 April 2020)). The FCA’s crisis response had been intended to ensure that markets continue to function in an orderly manner during a major repricing event, issue emergency guidance to allow government schemes to operate, support consumers, assist public access to essential banking services and 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

protect the most vulnerable within society. The FCA would continue to update its operations in response to unfolding events and inform stakeholders appropriately. 

Megan Butler, FCA Executive Director of Supervision – Investment, Wholesale and Specialists, gave a speech on ‘The FCA’s response to Covid-19 and expectations for 2020’ at the PIMFA’s Virtual Festival on 4 June 2020. While advisers and wealth managers had responded well to the crisis, attention had to shift from ‘incident response’ to longer-term impact with the FCA focusing on operational resilience, financial resilience and acting with integrity. The FCA specifically criticised firms that had attempted to avoid customer liabilities by closing down companies and setting up new ones. Certain specific crisis response measures were identified based on ensuring a good level of operational resilience, understanding firms’ financial resilience to allow them to fail in an orderly manner, ensuring that markets functioned effectively through price formation and orderly trading, treating customers fairly and ensuring that customers were aware of and protected from scams. 

Candidates could also have referred to earlier material in this area. A number of general documents and speeches have been issued on ethics over time. The FSA's initial general approach to ethics was set out in FSA, An ethical framework for financial services (October 2002) with competence being examined in FSA, Competence and ethics (June 2010) CP 10/12. Ethics was considered as part of the Retail Distribution Review (RDR) with the FSA recommending the creation of an enforceable Code of Ethics for investment advisors in FSA, Retail Distribution Review (November 2008) Feedback Statement 08/06. This was referred to again in FSA, Distribution of retail investments: Delivering the RDR (June 2009) CP 09/18 with eight general principles proposed. These can be summarised in terms of acting honestly and fairly at all times, integrity, legal and regulatory compliance, market integrity standards, conflict and, professional competence, declining to act and upholding the highest personal and professional standards. FSA Chairman, Howard Davies, discussed ethics in ‘Ethics in regulation’ in 2001. 

FSA CEO Hector Sants switched the focus from ethics to culture in ‘Do regulators have a role to play in judging culture and ethics?’ in June 2010 and ‘Can culture be regulated?’ in October 2012. Hector Sants noted in the first speech that when he joined the FSA, he was told by senior management that ‘the FSA does not do ethics.’ Howard Davies had earlier stated that the FSA could not ‘seek to act as the conscience of the square mile.’ Rather 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

than focus on ethics, the ‘better focus for regulators would be culture, which no doubt reflects the ethical framework of the organisation.’ Sants considered that the work ethics was ‘not precise enough’ and carried ‘too much baggage for regulators.’ There was nevertheless ‘already a consensus of what constitutes the right behaviour’, which included through the codes produced by the CISI and Worshipful Company of International Bankers which had ‘broad acceptance in the community.’ Firms should have ‘a culture which encourages individuals to make judgements and deliver the outcomes we want’ and that ‘at all times we want an undertaking to act with integrity.’ The regulators had therefore to focus on ‘what an unacceptable culture looks like and what outcomes that drives.’ 

A number of speeches were made by FCA Chief Executive, Martin Wheatley (such as ‘Competing on integrity’ (November 2013); ‘Ethics and Economics’ (March 2014); ‘The fairness challenge’ (October 2013); and ‘Good conduct and market integrity’ (June 2014)) and other FCA staff (including Clive Adamson, FCA Director of Supervision, ‘The importance of culture in driving behaviours of firms and how the FCA will assess this’ (April 2013); and ‘Trust and confidence – ensuring firms' ethics are built around their customers’ (November 2013); and later Mark Steward, ‘Culture and governance’ (November 2015)). 

Culture was referred to in the FCA Business Plan 2015/16 as a key driver in behaviour with an effective culture being one that supports a business model and business practices that have the fair treatment of customers and clients at their core and support market integrity. Culture began at the top with the FCA ensuring that firms and boards dealt with conduct issues as a priority. Boards had to engage in conduct issues by specifically investigating products or business lines, understanding cross-selling product strategies, possible loss and suitable product sales. Customer focus had also to be translated into how business decisions were made, the treatment of consumer responses, individual behaviour, issue escalation and remuneration and incentive awards. 

The FCA had published a separate statement on ‘Culture in banking’ following the decision not to proceed with the conduct of thematic review on culture in wholesale banking as announced in its 2015/16 Business Plan (https://www.fca.org.uk/static/documents/foi/foi4350-information-provided.pdf). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The emerging UK ethical framework is reflected in the more specific provisions contained within the High Level Standards and other more specific laws and regulations designed to ensure ethical outcomes. No separate ethical sourcebook has been prepared at this time although the issue of banking standards is to be considered by the new Banking Standards Board (BSB) set up following the Banking Standards Review (May 2014) by Sir Richard Lambert after the earlier recommendations for the establishment of a professional body by the Parliamentary Commission on Banking Standards (PCBS) final report on Changing banking for good (12 June 2013) Vols I and II. 

Candidates could also have referred to the specific requirements imposed under FCA PRIN, SYSC, COCON (and PRA FR) as well as COND for firms and FIT for individuals. While a number of PRIN are more prudential based including 3 (management and control), and 4 (financial prudence), others are more ethical and conduct based including 1 (integrity), 6 (customers' interests), 7 (communication), 8 (conflicts of interest), 9 (trust) and asset protection (10) as well as skill, care and diligence within PRIN 2. This is also reflected in COCON 1 (integrity), 2 (skill, care and diligence), and 5 (proper standards of market conduct). Conflicts of interest are dealt with under SYSC 10 and remuneration under SYSC 19A. Elements of this are also reflected under more specific COBS including 3 (categorisation), 4 (communication and financial promotions), 6 (disclosure), 8 (agreements), 9 (suitability), 10 (appropriateness), 11 (dealing and managing), 12 (investment research), 13 and 14 (product information), 15 (cancellation) and 16 (reporting). 

Reference may also have been made to the business standards sourcebooks including Conduct of Business (COBS), Insurance: Conduct of Business (ICOBS), Mortgages and Home Finance: Conduct of Business (MCOB) and Banking: Conduct of Business (BCOBS). 

A number of other statutory provisions can also be considered to include an ethical element such as the prohibition on market abuse under the Market Abuse Regulation (MAR) EU/596/2014 and misleading statements and impressions in ss89-90 FSA 2012 as well as separate insider dealing offences in the CJA. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

**C53.** MiFID I was adopted in April 2004 and came into effect in November 2007. It created a common regulatory regime across the EU in investment services with the specific objectives of increasing competition and consumer protection replacing the earlier Investment Services Directive (ISD) 1993. 

MiFID I distinguished between core investment services and activities and non-core ancillary services. The directive contained a number of provisions governing authorisation, regulation and passporting, client classification, client order handling, pre-trade and posttrade transparency, best execution and systemic internalisation. 

The European Commission published a consultation document on MiFID II in September 2010 with a draft directive being produced in October 2011 for an MiFID II Directive and Markets in Financial Instruments Regulation (MiFIR) to amend the European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR) which came into effect in August 2012. EMIR contains specific provisions imposing reporting obligations on OTC derivatives, clearing obligations for eligible OTC derivatives and common rules for central counterparties (CCPs) and trade repositories (TRs). 

The European Parliament adopted MiFID II (2014/65/EU) in April 2014 that would come into effect in January 2017 with the MiFIR (EU/600/2014). 

The FCA issued a Discussion Paper and Consultation Paper in May 2014 covering such issues as authorisation, passporting and best execution, equity transparency and equities trading, non-equity transparency and derivatives trading, high frequency and algorithmic trading and other market microstructural issues, data reporting service providers and data dissemination, access to CCPs, trading venues (including Regulated Markets (CMs), Multilateral Trading Facilities (MTFs), and Organised Trading Facilities (OTFs) as well as Systemic Internalises (SIs)) and benchmarks, commodities and transaction reporting and retention of order data. 

The FCA has summarised the principal changes in terms of scope and commodity derivatives, transparency, high frequency trading, market structure, organisational requirements, trade reporting, conduct of business rules revision and transaction reporting. Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Markets in Financial Instruments) Regulations (MiFIR) were published in 2017 (SI 2017/701). 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The general objectives of MiFID II were to make financial markets safer, more transparent and more efficient with increased investor confidence and competition. It specifically extends earlier provisions to deal with new market structures, transparency, research and inducements and governance with additional price transparency, volume limits on dark pools, separation of analysis and commission payments and higher standards on investment products. Algorithmic and high frequency trading must be registered and the use of circuit breakers with capital adequacy being extended to commodity traders. MiFID II can be considered to have been effective to the extent that it introduces new requirements in these areas. Against this, it imposed substantial new obligations and costs on firms and has arguably fragmented rather than integrated markets across the EU. Trading will be removed from telephone to electronic markets with less analysis being produced. Data production and data transfer and storage costs have also been increased substantially. 

Candidates should have commented on the value and purpose of the revisions made under MiFID II and the MiFIR as referred to in the question. No specific answer is expected here. 

- **C54.** Candidates had to assess the effectiveness of the recent changes brought into place in connection with strengthening capital and liquidity standards. Higher capital, liquidity and leverage standards were imposed at the international level under the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision’s Basel III documentation with these being implemented within the EU under the Capital Requirements Directive (CRD) IV package of measures which consists of the Capital Requirements Directive 2013/36/EU and Regulation EU575/2013. 

Basel III was finalised in 2010 with capital adequacy generally being increased from 2% to 4.5% and with additional capital buffers been provided for including a conservation buffer (2.5%), countercyclical buffer (0-2.5%) and systemic risk buffer (1-3.5%). The new liquidity requirements include a short Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) and Longer Term Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR). A separate leverage ratio was imposed made up of a 3% of own funds limit on total debt exposure. 

Basel III followed the earlier Basel 1 standards in 1988, which established the minimum 8% ratio of capital to risk-adjusted assets for all internationally active banks, and Basel 2, which established the three pillars of minimum capital requirements (including credit risk 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

(standardised, foundation and advanced internal ratings-based (IRB) approaches), market risk (standardised and models) and operational risk), supervisory review and market disclosure. These were revised following the global financial crisis to create the Basel III amendments. 

Basel III is implemented in the EU under the CRD and CRR with provisions involving national implementation being set out in the CRD and the more detailed common provisions, which require no domestic adjustment, contained in the CRR. These are being expanded through European Banking Authority (EBA) implementing measures. The CRD measures require separate UK implementation under the PRA Rulebook (with separate measures for CRR and non-CRR firms) and FCA Handbook (BIPRU) with the CRR being directly applicable as UK law and simply implemented on a copy out basis. 

Candidates could also have commented on the value of the new increased capital and liquidity standards and separately referred to the new supporting leverage measures. The purpose of capital is to provide a means of loss absorption for firms to prevent them from becoming insolvent. Liquidity ensures that firms maintain sufficient cash or readily realisable assets on an ongoing basis to cover ongoing liabilities. Leverage limits total debt exposure, which was a significant factor in the global financial crisis. The effect of these requirements is substantially to improve the stability of financial firms and prevent contagion. This regulatory adjustment is supported by enhanced supervision with new resolution procedures, emergency support facilities and wider macro prudential oversight. All of this should limit the possibility of a future crisis arising or, at minimum, the more stable and effective management of the crisis. 

**C55.** Candidates were asked to comment on the effectiveness of the new Banking Standards Board (BSB) in increasing ethical standards in the banking and financial area within the UK following the production of its Annual Report for 2016-2017 and other documents. 

The BSB assumed the functions of the Banking Standards Review Council (BSRC), which was set up following the review conducted by Sir Richard Lambert with a consultation document being issued in February 2014, and final report in May 2014. This had been referred to in the Parliamentary Commission on Banking Standards (PCBS) final report on Changing Banking for Good (12 June 2013) with higher professional standards to be promoted in the banking area including through the development of a new set of core 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

standards. The BSRC was not to act as a lobby body and would be funded by the industry while acting in an independent manner. 

Sir Richard Lambert recommended that participating institutions commit to a programme of continuous improvement of culture, competence and customer outcomes with various recommendations being made with regard to the BSRC (and later BSB) specifically. It should establish standards of good practice, report annually, meet with non-executive directors and risk or reputation committee chairs, work with industry and stakeholders to develop a new code of practice, identify and encourage good practice and learning, development and leadership, assist compliance and work with professional bodies to improve training and qualifications. Nine specific recommendations were made with regard to purpose, governance, funding and reporting, arrangements, engagement, institutional relations, regulatory cooperation, raising standards of conduct over time, stakeholder involvement, training and avoidance of duplication of function. 

The principal advantage of the BSB is that it would operate on a self-regulatory basis and benefit from industry experience, engagement, commitment, funding and voluntary enforcements which may promote public trust and confidence and placate media and political criticism. Some concerns nevertheless arose in terms of additional cost, regulatory overlap, standards conflict, consistency, weak enforcement and limited overall effectiveness. 

The BSB specifically operates through research, action proposals and the production of good practice guidelines in the form of Statements of Good Practice and supplementary Supporting Guidance. The BSB has released a Statement of Good Practice on fitness and proprietary with further guidance to follow on Certification and Regulatory References. 

The 2016-2017 was the first Annual Report produced by the BSB and was based on the responses received to the BSB 28,000 Employee Survey and other comments made. The Review confirms that a large number of good practices had been adopted and positive developments had occurred in raising standards of behaviour and competence although changes were required in attitudes and behaviour that were detrimental to the interests of customers and clients. Progress was required in the areas of responsiveness, accountability, personal resilience and openness. The BSB confirmed that the review was not a single event with annual reviews to be conducted to ensure that culture operated in 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

the interests of consumers. A number of specific priority themes were identified, in particular, with regard to alignment between values and businesses, creating a culture of responsibility and accountability and promoting personal resilience and well-being among employees. 

The BSB has separately provided reports to 22-member banks on their internal performances with the results being discussed with the specific firms. The firms were asked to demonstrate the extent to which they implemented the BSB’s Assessment Framework, which was based on nine principles (consisting of honesty, respect, openness, accountability, competence, reliability, responsiveness, personal and organisational resilience and shared purpose). Assessments would be carried out annually. 

The BSB delivered earlier provisional comments (report cards) for the seven founding members (Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds Banking Group, Royal Bank of Scotland, Santander, Standard Chartered and the Nationwide Building Society) and three other banks sitting on its board. These constituted provisional assessments of behaviour and culture within the major banks. The institutions were asked to explain their goals and ambitions and specifically the purpose, objectives, methods of achieving the objectives and means of assessing these achievements. The BSB has accordingly made an important provisional attempt to assess the extent to which the banks achieve their own internal objects rather to set separate single common standards of behaviour. 

The question asked for comment on the ability of the BSB to increase ethical standards. A substantial body of ethical provisions already having been provided for under FCA PRIN, PRA Fundamental Rules, SYSC and COND, COCON and FIT, COBS, BCOBS and MCOB as well as with the statutory prohibitions imposed on market abuse, misleading statements and impressions and insider trading and general criminal and civil law. Many of the regulatory obligations imposed are principally based on integrity that is concerned with complying with expected high standards of professional conduct in any particular situation. Separate codes of ethics and standards are also applied by, for example, the CISI, Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA) and Association for Financial Professionals (AFP) and other professional bodies. 

The FCA had earlier stated in its 2015 Business Plan that it would conduct an internal review into banking culture and specifically whether a, promotion and centres have 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

contributed to scandals within the UK and abroad. The FCA later announced on 31 December 2015 that this review would be abandoned with two separate reviews of incentive structures in financial products sales and the use of customer information by insurance companies. The FCA stated that a traditional thematic review would not achieve its desired outcomes with the FCA focusing on encouraging the delivery of cultural change within individual institutions. The Treasury denied involvement although the Government had appeared to have adopted a more supportive approach to the banking industry with the earlier reduction in the bank levy. 

The development of a new ethical standards framework remains a difficult issue in the UK and elsewhere. A substantial amount of work has been carried out although it is arguable that a clear framework has yet emerged. The new BSB may be able to make a further more substantial contribution over time although it is too early to judge. 

Candidates could specifically have commented on the extent which the BSB can supplement this other work and assist promote appropriate standards across the banking area. No specific answers were expected with candidates only having to outline the various issues and arguments involved. 

**C56.** Candidates were required to explain the principal changes brought into effect under the Bank of England and Financial Services Act (BEFSA) 2016 and comment on their need and value in improving financial regulation. The purpose of the BEFSA 2016 Act was generally to strengthen governance and accountability, update resolution planning and crisis management and extend the new Senior Managers and Certification Regimes to all authorised persons within the financial services area. A number of specific amendments were adopted in this regard. 

The Treasury is given specific power to alter the title or add or remove Deputy Governors of the Bank under s1A Bank of England Act 1998 (s1). A new Deputy Governor for Banking and Markets is created as a member of the Court and of the FPC. The term of office of nonexecutive directors may be extended by up to six months (s2) with the Oversight Committee being abolished (s3) and with oversight functions being transferred to the Court. A new Prudential Regulation Committee (PRC) is created within the Bank and the status of the PRA as a subsidiary of the Bank abolished (s12). The Comptroller and Auditor General can carry out examinations of the economy, efficiency and effectiveness with which the Bank 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

uses its resources (s9). The FPC is made a committee of the Bank rather than a subcommittee of the Court with the number of annual MPC meetings being reduced to eight. 

FCA Chief Executives are required to appear before the Treasury Select Committee before appointment (s18). The Treasury may make recommendations to the FCA concerning aspects of economic policy that the FCA should consider in implementing its objectives (s19). The regulatory principles are amended to include a reference to diversity (s20). Provision is made in Schedule 4 to extend the Senior Managers and Certification Regimes to all authorised persons under Part 5 FSMA (s21). The definition of misconduct is revised to require that the regulators prove that the senior manager has not taken reasonable steps reversing the earlier burden of proof (s25). Provisions governing the enforceability of credit agreements are amended (ss27-28) with the Treasury being given power to provide financial assistance to take action against illegal money laundering with the cost being covered by a new industry levy and other money laundering disclosures to be made (ss2930). 

The Treasury may make regulations relating to transformer vehicles that are used in connection with insurance linked securities (s31). The Pension Wise service is extended to include the sale of annuities with the FCA issuing new rules on transfers (ss32-35). The Bank is to inform the Treasury where firms' resolution strategies have been developed or updated with the Treasury being able to request information on the Bank's assessment of public fund risks with regard to potential firm failure (36). The Treasury may make regulations with the consent of the Bank allowing banks within the same group as an existing issuer to issue banknotes in Scotland and Northern Ireland (s38). 

While a number of these revisions are more technical, the most significant change was to confirm the PRA as an internal division within the Bank of England rather than as a separate subsidiary. This is supported by the creation of a Prudential Regulation Committee (PRC) within the Bank and a Deputy Governor for Banking and Markets. The subsidiary PRA’s status was always anomalous and possibly based on the need to transfer its functions again if the institutional structure adopted under the FSA 2012 did not operate successfully. As the PRA has been effectively absorbed into the Bank’s other operations, there was no need to continue its separate subsidiary position. The other most significant change was to extend the Senior Managers and Certification Regimes to all authorised 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

persons that would otherwise have created an unlevel playing field between banks and other financial institutions. 

- **C57.** The ring-fencing requirement arose from the great financial crisis (GFC) of 2007–08 and as a response to the threat of contagion in the UK and internationally. Concerns had arisen in the GFC that risk could easily spread from institution to institution and between different financial sectors and national financial sectors. Furthermore, there were concerns before the GFC that many financial institutions were allowed to undertake a very wide range of financial activities rather than being restricted to undertake a number of restricted activities associated with a particular type of institution (banking, insurance, trading etc.): i.e., many institutions were allowed to undertake a wide range of different activities some of which may have increased the individual risks to institutions. To limit this contagion after the GFC it was proposed to split the retail operations including taking deposits away from trading activities in many banks. This was termed ring fencing in the UK whereby different activities could exist in the same institution yet would be ring fenced from each other. Different approaches to address this problem were also seen in the USA and the EU and were often further reaching than ring fencing seen in the UK. In the UK this policy was forwarded by the Independent Commission on Banking (Vickers report) and implemented by the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013. 

Removing the ring fence can have many outcomes. It will allow banks to reduce the expense of complying with these regulations. There may also be considerable commercial benefits of undertaking trading activities and retail activities in tandem. Of course, these benefits need to be tempered by the concern that ring fencing was introduced to reduce systemic risk seen in the UK and the world financial system. Removing ring fencing may increase the risk of individual financial institutions failing and the risks to the financial system. Lastly, removing the system of ring fencing or similar arrangements is only currently being discussed in the UK. This removal may make the UK relatively less regulated internationally, with both benefits and drawbacks to this move. 

- **C58.** There is a long-standing concern in all areas of regulation that enforcement actions may not always deter and limit the level of offending or breach of regulations. Fines are a particular area of concern with examples where fines may be seen as a cost of doing business in some regulated areas. In the responses, we would like to see a general discussion of the 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

costs of non-compliance and the use of fines in the UK financial system. An initial point is that fines are one of many regulatory punishments. These punishments are diverse and influence the firms’ or individuals’ ability to work in the financial services industry. 

Considering some of these punishments and other costs of non-compliance is useful. For example, it would be helpful to detail outcomes such as prohibition or variation of regulatory permissions. Similarly, the focus of punishment has also shifted towards individuals such as senior managers and compliance staff. This is undertaken to alter corporate viewpoints of non-compliance, from considering the cost of the fine to considering personal and career influencing outcomes of non-compliance. 

The scale of financial regulation fines in the UK has also increased over time and reflects the concern that the cost of the offending and the probability of being caught for a breach of regulation should be matched by the scale of fines levied. This increase in the level of fines is aimed at enhancing what might be viewed as an effective deterrent. These fines are levied using a range of different criteria yet should reflect the profits or benefits of the offending firm/individual from the offending through a process of disgorgement. Such fines also reflect other contributory factors and the seriousness of the breach of regulation. There are also penalty discounts which may be applied, for example, for settling the dispute with the regulator in a timely manner and conceding liability. For example, in the associated text for the workbook it outlines the level of fines and includes the following level of fines in recent years. This reflects a movement in regulatory punishments: 

- 2017 – £229,515,303 (13 fines) 

- 2018 – £60,467,212 (15 fines) 

- 2019 – £392,303,087 (21 fines) 

- 2020 – £192,570,018 (11 fines) 

- 2021 – £567,765,219 (10 fines) 

**C59.** The Takeover Panel was established in 1968 as a private and voluntary body to ensure takeovers, mergers and associated transactions occurring between firms from the UK and associated areas were undertaken in an appropriate manner. This voluntary panel developed issuing a code outlining the procedures and how such transactions should be 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

undertaken. Critical in this role was the concern that only some shareholders may benefit from a takeover action. The Panel and the associated code were enacted to limit these concerns and enable all shareholders to have a say in the proposed transaction, be offered broadly equal treatment, and generally be treated fairly. The treatment of minority shareholders in takeover/merger transactions was a particular concern to be tackled by this code. Lastly, the code established procedures to ensure that such a takeover or merger transaction is undertaken following an appropriate administrative process and can be completed in a timely and legal manner. The panel can also signal if a particular transaction has wider economic and societal concern such as those related to competition. This would result in the transaction being referenced to the antitrust/competition regulator or another appropriate regulator. 

Since the establishment of the panel its role has expanded and been formalised. To date, the actions and the role of the Panel have been formalised together with the expectations of the takeover code and have been provided with legal backing, for example in Chapter 1 of Part 28 of the Companies Act 2006. This role is also supported by the European Directive on Takeover Bids. 

As detailed in the workbook text the role of the panel is to: 

   - provide advice and guidance on the application and interpretation of the relevant rules to offerors, offerees, advisers and, where relevant, market participants 

   - monitor the progress of an actual or contemplated takeover bid, for breaches of compliance with the relevant rules, and 

   - enforce the code, by reaching decisions on issues arising, or seeking enforcement through the courts. 

- **C60.** UK financial regulation has long focused on the requirement for a person acting in a regulated role to have the required competence and honesty and integrity to undertake the role appropriately . 

For example, under the Approved Persons (APER) regime the financial regulator approves individuals to undertake specified controlled functions. Part of this form of 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

regulation is a requirement to adhere to the FCA’s Statements of Principle for Approved Persons and Code of Practice for Approved Persons. 

For instance, the four general principles for approved persons expect an individual to: 

1. act with integrity in carrying out his controlled function 

2. act with due skill, care and diligence in carrying out his controlled function 

3. observe proper standards of market conduct in carrying out his controlled function 

4. deal with the FCA and other regulators in an open and cooperative way and disclose appropriately any information which the FCA would reasonably expect. 

_**[Tutor note: The examiner’s reference to APER is curious, to say the least, given the fact that APER is now obsolete for all but a handful of appointed representatives of authorised firms. APER has been replaced by SMCR for all authorised firms from 2019. Note that the question requirement did not require reference to APER, but suggested that you could have referred to APER if you so chose].**_ 

The Conduct rules (COCON) are expected levels of behaviour seen across the financial industry by all relevant employees. These five rules, termed first tier rules, are applied to all employees of firms regulated by the financial regulator. The first three rules also apply to firms regulated by the prudential regulator: 

Rule 1 You must act with integrity. 

Rule 2 You must act with due skill, care and diligence. 

Rule 3 You must be open and cooperative with the FCA, the PRA and other regulators. Rule 4 You must pay due regard to the interests of customers and treat them fairly. Rule 5 You must observe proper standards of market conduct. 

This requirement was established in the Approved Persons Regime and continued in the Senior Managers Regime. These requirements outline that a person undertaking a regulated or controlled function needs to demonstrate a range of personal criteria to be suitable and acceptable for such a role. A person with acceptable criteria would be fit and proper for the role. Such personal criteria would include but are not limited to: 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

- honesty, integrity and reputation 

- competence and capability 

- financial soundness 

These characteristics can be wide-ranging. For instance, honesty, integrity, and reputation would include checks as to whether the person has past convictions, is subject to any past or ongoing complaints, has been or is insolvent or has breached legal or regulatory norms in the past. Competence and capability would relate to whether the person has appropriate qualifications, training, and work history to be allowed to undertake a regulated role. This would also include the personal suitability and the potential medical lifestyle concerns which may limit the competence of an individual to undertake a regulated role. Lastly financial soundness would indicate if someone is solvent or subject to a debt order or similar. These financial criteria are important as the financial condition of an individual can influence decision making in some circumstances and situations. 

In assessing the utility of this criteria, it is useful to consider examples; for instance, Paul Flowers working as the chairman at the Cooperative Bank is a suitable example. This appointment breached fit and proper person requirements in terms of the level of training (which was insufficient) and also in terms of lifestyle whereby Mr Flowers had troublesome drug addiction issues which might have been expected to impair his decision making. 

Discussion of the process and procedures through which fit and proper status may be attributed is also useful to discuss. 

## **C61.** 

The main obligations imposed on internal management should be discussed and contrasted with the external powers of supervision available to the FCA under the FSMA and Handbook of Rules and Guidance. 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

The main obligations applicable with regard to management are set out in the Block 1 High Level Standards including the requirements with regard to principles for businesses (PRIN), systems and controls (SYSC), code of conduct (COCON) and fit and proper test for approved persons (FIT). Each of the relevant requirements should have been outlined and discussed. General issues of liability and sanction should have been referred to including potential personal liability (s138D). 

The importance of internal management should then be contrasted with the external supervisory powers available to the FCA. The powers of the FCA should be referred to (including information gathering and investigatory powers, reports by skilled persons, authorisation and permission, redress, disciplinary action and private warnings, public statements of misconduct, fines, variation or cancellation of permission and approval).Relevant decision-taking procedures and requirements could also have been referred to. 

There is no need to come to any specific conclusion but only to identify the relevant provisions that apply with regard to both internal management and external supervision and sanctions. 

## **C62.** 

This question asked candidates to evaluate the role of the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) in using enforcement actions to deter financial misconduct and uphold market integrity. Candidates are expected to assess the effectiveness of fines and regulatory interventions in changing corporate behaviour, and to consider whether there are areas where the FCA’s enforcement approach could be improved. Answers missed marks by failing to evaluate the effectiveness of enforcement critically; particularly the debate over whether fines alone change corporate culture or simply serve as a cost of doing business. 

The FCA plays an important role in maintaining market integrity through enforcement actions. These include investigating misconduct, imposing fines, banning individuals from financial services, and taking criminal enforcement action when necessary. Such interventions are intended not only to punish wrongdoing but also to act as a deterrent to future misconduct by firms and individuals. There have been high-profile cases where enforcement has proven effective, such as significant fines issued for market manipulation, mis-selling of financial 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

products, or AML failings. These outcomes signal that the FCA is willing to act decisively, reinforcing its commitment to protecting consumers and ensuring fair, transparent markets. Enforcement also provides redress where appropriate, which can help restore consumer confidence. 

However, critics argue that for large firms, fines may be viewed as a cost of doing business. This raises questions about whether enforcement leads to genuine cultural change. The Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SMCR) was introduced partly in response to this, aiming to improve personal accountability. But questions remain over whether senior individuals are routinely held accountable when systemic failings occur. The FCA has recognised the need to improve in this area. In 2024, it announced plans to increase the speed and transparency of enforcement cases. This includes streamlining investigation processes, publishing more updates on ongoing cases, and ensuring decisions are communicated clearly. These reforms are intended to bolster public trust and reinforce the credibility of enforcement actions. 

A further proposal that has drawn public attention is the idea of ‘naming and shaming’ firms under investigation before a final outcome has been reached. While this proposal has been controversial and may not be in place when the exam is taken, it reflects broader concerns about how effective enforcement is in influencing firm behaviour. Should candidates raise this point, they should consider arguments on both sides, including its potential to improve transparency versus the risk of reputational damage prior to due process. FCA enforcement is a vital regulatory tool but is not without limitations. While it does deter some misconduct, enforcement alone may not bring about lasting cultural change unless supported by proactive supervision, clear accountability, and meaningful individual consequences. 

Improvements in transparency and speed, alongside further debate around initiatives like naming and shaming, suggest enforcement is evolving to meet public and regulatory expectations. 

## **C63.** 

This question asked candidates to discuss how financial firms are being affected by the growing regulatory focus on Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) issues in the UK. It requires an evaluation of whether the current regulatory framework effectively promotes 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

transparency, accountability, and long-term sustainability, as well as an analysis of the challenges firms face in implementing ESG policies. Candidates were also expected to consider how ESG regulation may develop in the future. While responses showed a good understanding of the growing importance of ESG in financial services, marks were missed by failing to reference specific regulatory initiatives such as TCFD, SDR, or the ISSB framework. 

ESG considerations are playing an increasingly prominent role in the UK financial sector. Regulatory initiatives aim to improve transparency, accountability, and promote sustainable investing. The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) and other regulators are introducing requirements for climate-related disclosures, enhanced governance standards, and rules around greenwashing. These changes are designed to help investors make informed decisions and to support the UK’s transition to a more sustainable economy. Firms are being impacted through the need to update internal systems, gather reliable ESG data, and improve disclosures. 

The FCA’s requirements under Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) apply to many listed companies and asset managers, who must report on governance, strategy, risk management, and metrics related to climate risks. The introduction of the Sustainability Disclosure Requirements (SDR) will further raise the bar, introducing labels for sustainable investment products to prevent misleading claims. Challenges for firms include data availability, the cost of compliance, and aligning global ESG standards. Many firms struggle to obtain consistent and reliable ESG data from third-party providers, and there is a lack of standardisation across jurisdictions. Implementation requires investment in staff training, systems development, and integrating ESG factors into investment and risk management frameworks. 

On the international stage, there is increasing momentum towards a harmonised global framework. At COP29, global leaders reaffirmed their commitment to the work of the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), which is working to develop consistent global sustainability disclosure standards. The UK has endorsed this direction, which may help reduce fragmentation and make ESG reporting more consistent across borders. However, the geopolitics of ESG remain complex. In the United States, there has been some backtracking on ESG commitments, particularly in politically conservative states where ESG 

**==> picture [153 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**Section C: Answers** 

investing is viewed with scepticism. This divergence can create uncertainty for multinational firms and underscores the importance of clear UK guidance and leadership in this space. Looking ahead, the UK’s regulatory framework is expected to evolve in alignment with international standards, while continuing to address domestic priorities around climate risk, biodiversity, and social responsibility. Regulators will likely require more granular disclosures, and firms must demonstrate not just intent, but measurable ESG outcomes. A well-regulated ESG environment will improve investor confidence and help mobilise capital towards longterm, sustainable economic goals.
